info.title: PDF Digital Signatures — A Technical Reference

preface.title: Preface
preface.simpara: PDF digital signatures are one of those technologies that seem straightforward until you actually have to implement them. You read the spec, write some code, run a test — and the signature validates in your tool but fails in Adobe Acrobat. Or it works in Acrobat but fails the EU’s validation service. Or it works everywhere on Monday and breaks on Friday because a timestamp authority renewed its certificate.
If you’ve been there, this book is for you.

This is a technical reference for engineers, developers, and security researchers who need to understand PDF digital signatures at the deepest level — from the raw bytes in the file to the legal framework that gives those bytes legal standing across 27 EU member states. Every claim is backed by precise references to ISO 32000-2:2020, the ETSI PAdES standards, relevant RFCs, and the eIDAS regulation. Code examples use Apache PDFBox 3.0.0 and BouncyCastle 1.80 on Java 21, because that’s what most of us actually use in production.

section.title: Who This Book Is For
section.simpara: You are a software engineer, a security researcher, or a technically curious person who needs to understand PDF signatures, not just use them. Maybe you’re building a signing service. Maybe you’re debugging why a customer’s signature shows "invalid" in one viewer but "valid" in another. Maybe you’re the person who got handed a government compliance checklist that says "PAdES-B-LTA" and nobody in your organization knows what that means.

We assume you know how to program, that you’ve encountered public-key cryptography before (even if the details are fuzzy), and that you’re comfortable reading data structures. We do not assume you know anything about PDF internals, CMS, or the European regulatory landscape. Parts I and II build that foundation from scratch.

section.title: What This Book Is Not
section.simpara: This is not a tutorial. There are no "Hello World" signing examples in Chapter 1. If you need to sign a PDF in the next 20 minutes, copy the code from Chapter 28 and come back later to understand why it works.

This is not a product manual. We use PDFBox and BouncyCastle because they’re open-source, widely deployed, and well-documented. The concepts apply equally to iText, OpenPDF, or any other library — the PDF specification doesn’t care what language you write in.

This is not a legal guide. We explain the eIDAS regulation and its technical requirements in detail, but we are engineers, not lawyers. When legal precision matters — and with qualified electronic signatures, it always does — consult someone with a law degree.

section.title: How This Book Is Organized
section.simpara: The book is structured in six parts, each building on the previous:

Part I: Cryptographic Foundations (Chapters 1–6) starts from first principles. If you can explain the difference between RSA and ECDSA, between a certificate and a key pair, between CMS and PKCS#7 — skip ahead. If any of those distinctions are hazy, start here. We cover just enough cryptography to make the rest of the book make sense, with a focus on the specific algorithms and structures you’ll encounter in PDF signatures.

Part II: PDF Internals (Chapters 7–11) dissects the PDF file format itself. You’ll learn how objects, cross-reference tables, and incremental updates work — because you cannot understand PDF signatures without understanding incremental saves. You’ll also learn about AcroForm fields, annotations, and coordinate systems, because signature fields are form fields, signature appearances are annotations, and the coordinate system will trip you up.

Part III: PDF Digital Signatures (Chapters 12–18) is the heart of the book. This is where we take apart the /Sig dictionary key by key, trace the byte range byte by byte, and walk through the CMS structure field by field. We cover approval and certification signatures, visual appearances, validation logic, PAdES profiles, and long-term validation. If you only read one part of this book, read this one.

Part IV: eIDAS & Qualified Signatures (Chapters 19–23) enters the regulatory world. The European eIDAS regulation created a legal framework where a digital signature can have the same legal standing as a handwritten one — but only if it meets very specific technical requirements. We explain what those requirements are, how the EU Trusted List works, what a Qualified Trust Service Provider does, and how remote signing via the Cloud Signature Consortium API fits into the picture.

Part V: Security, Incidents & Outlook (Chapters 24–27) covers what can go wrong — and what has gone wrong. The Ruhr University Bochum research team published devastating attacks against PDF signature validation in 2019–2021. We analyze every attack technically, explain why it worked, and show how to defend against it. We also cover certificate authority breaches, industry oddities, and the emerging EUDI wallet.

Part VI: Code & Implementation (Chapters 28–31) is where theory meets production. Complete, tested Java code for signing, CMS construction, verification, and analysis — not snippets, but full classes you can drop into a project. We also provide test cases and reference PDFs for every scenario discussed in the book.

section.title: Conventions Used in This Book
section.simpara: PDF dictionary keys are written in monospace with a leading slash: /SubFilter, /ByteRange, /Contents. When we show a full dictionary, it’s presented as a code listing with callout annotations explaining each key.

Spec references follow this pattern: "ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1" means ISO 32000-2:2020, section 12.8.1. RFC references: "RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §5" means RFC 5652, section 5. We use these abbreviations consistently throughout.
Code examples are Java 21 using PDFBox 3.0.0 and BouncyCastle 1.80. Every code listing compiles and runs — the source is in the book’s repository alongside test cases that verify the examples.
Admonition blocks carry specific meaning:

note.simpara: Additional context, background, or alternative approaches.

tip.simpara: Practical advice from experience — the kind of thing a senior colleague would tell you over coffee.

important.simpara: Something you must understand to avoid a specific class of bugs.

warning.simpara: A common mistake or misunderstanding that leads to broken signatures.

caution.simpara: A security-relevant issue that could lead to signature bypasses or compliance failures.

section.title: A Note on the PDF Specification
section.simpara: The PDF specification has a complicated history. Adobe published it as a proprietary format in 1993, released it as ISO 32000-1 in 2008, and then ISO 32000-2:2020 superseded it in 2020 with significant changes to the digital signature sections.

Throughout this book, we reference ISO 32000-2:2020 as the authoritative source. Where the behavior of older PDF versions matters (and for digital signatures, it often does — plenty of documents in the wild still use PDF 1.7 features), we note the differences explicitly.

The PAdES standards (ETSI EN 319 142) add an additional layer of requirements on top of ISO 32000-2. They don’t replace the PDF spec — they constrain it. A PAdES-compliant signature is always a valid ISO 32000-2 signature, but not every valid ISO 32000-2 signature is PAdES-compliant. This distinction matters enormously in the EU, and increasingly, everywhere else.

section.title: Three Decades of PDF: A Timeline
section.simpara: PDF was born as a page description language for printing. Digital signatures were an afterthought — bolted on six years later in PDF 1.3, using a cryptographic framework (PKCS#7) that predated PDF itself. Understanding why PDF signatures work the way they do — and why they break the way they break — requires understanding the history that shaped them.

The following table traces every major milestone in the evolution of PDF and, specifically, the digital signature infrastructure built on top of it. Some of these are format changes. Some are regulatory events. Some are security incidents that forced the entire industry to rethink assumptions it had held for years. All of them left marks on the code you will write or debug.

table.title: The evolution of PDF and PDF digital signatures, 1993–2026

row.entry: What happened and why it matters

entry.simpara: Adobe published the Portable Document Format as a proprietary specification for reliable cross-platform document rendering. No forms, no interactivity, no signatures — just pages, text, images, and fonts. The file structure established here (header, body, cross-reference table, trailer) survives unchanged to this day. Every PDF you sign in 2026 still starts with %PDF-1.x or %PDF-2.0 and ends with %%EOF.

Introduced AcroForm — interactive form fields that users could fill in. This matters for signatures because signature fields are form fields. The /AcroForm dictionary, the field tree, widget annotations — all of the infrastructure that Chapter 9 dissects — was born here. PDF 1.2 also added Unicode support and Flate compression, both of which are ubiquitous in modern PDFs.

The moment PDF gained the ability to carry cryptographic signatures. Adobe defined the /Sig dictionary type, the /ByteRange array, the /Contents hex string for the signature value, and the /SubFilter key with its initial value adbe.pkcs7.detached. The design decision to embed the CMS signature inside the PDF file (rather than as a detached sidecar) and to define the signed region as a byte range with a gap for the signature value itself — these were architectural choices made in 1999 that we still live with. Every quirk in Chapter 12 traces back to this release.

Added transparency (the imaging model’s biggest change ever), 128-bit RC4 encryption, and tagged PDF for accessibility. For signatures: PDF 1.4 formalized the adbe.pkcs7.sha1 SubFilter (where the SHA-1 hash of the entire document is embedded as the CMS content, rather than using a detached signature). This SubFilter is now deprecated but still appears in legacy documents. Acrobat 5 also introduced certification signatures (/DocMDP transform) — the distinction between "I approve this document" and "I certify this is the authoritative version" starts here.

Introduced object streams and cross-reference streams — a complete reworking of how PDF files store their internal structure. Instead of the classic ASCII cross-reference table, PDF 1.5 allows binary cross-reference data compressed inside stream objects. This matters for signatures because the byte range must cover the entire file structure, and cross-reference streams change where objects physically live in the file. PDF 1.5 also added XFA forms (Adobe’s XML-based form format), which would become a persistent source of interoperability problems for signatures on XFA documents — a problem so annoying that ISO 32000-2:2020 deprecated XFA entirely.

AES-128 encryption replaced the weak RC4-based encryption from earlier versions. Embedded file streams (attachments) and 3D content were added. For signatures: PDF 1.6 refined the interaction between encryption and signing — a signed, encrypted PDF requires careful handling to ensure the signature covers the encrypted content correctly. The /NeedAppearances flag behavior was also clarified, which matters because a missing appearance stream on a signature field causes some viewers to show a blank signature block.

The last version of PDF that Adobe defined unilaterally before handing the specification to ISO. PDF 1.7 solidified the AES-256 encryption scheme and finalized the AcroForm/XFA coexistence model. The vast majority of signed PDFs in the wild today are PDF 1.7 documents — not because of any technical limitation of PDF 2.0, but because template authors, form generators, and enterprise software have not yet migrated. When you debug a signature failure in production, you are almost certainly looking at a PDF 1.7 file.
ISO 32000-1:2008 — PDF becomes an ISO standard

Adobe donated the PDF 1.7 specification to ISO, making it an open international standard. The technical content was essentially identical to Adobe’s PDF Reference 1.7, but the governance changed: future changes would go through the ISO committee process, not Adobe’s product team. For signatures, this was the moment PDF digital signatures gained the credibility of an ISO-backed specification — a prerequisite for government and regulated-industry adoption.

The European Telecommunications Standards Institute published the first PDF Advanced Electronic Signatures standards. PAdES defined profiles for how CMS signatures should be embedded in PDF to meet European legal requirements: which signed attributes are mandatory (ESS signing-certificate-v2), which SubFilter values to use (ETSI.CAdES.detached), and how to embed timestamps, revocation data, and long-term validation information. PAdES didn’t replace ISO 32000 — it constrained it. A PAdES-compliant signature is always a valid ISO 32000 signature, but the reverse is not true. This distinction — and the interoperability problems it causes when a PDF viewer validates against ISO 32000 rules but not PAdES rules — is a recurring theme in this book (Chapters 17 and 26).

A Dutch certificate authority, DigiNotar, was completely compromised by an attacker who issued over 500 fraudulent certificates — including certificates for google.com, mozilla.org, and intelligence agencies. The certificates were used in man-in-the-middle attacks against Iranian dissidents. Every major browser vendor revoked trust in DigiNotar within weeks; the company went bankrupt within months. For PDF signatures: DigiNotar was a wake-up call about the fragility of the certificate trust model that all digital signatures depend on. If a CA can be compromised, any signature issued by that CA’s certificates becomes suspect. This incident accelerated the adoption of Certificate Transparency logs and tighter CA audit requirements — both of which affected the trust infrastructure behind PDF signatures. Chapter 25 covers this and other CA breaches in detail.

Adobe formalized its AATL program — a curated list of root certificates that Acrobat trusts for signature validation. If your signing certificate chains up to an AATL root, Acrobat shows a green checkmark. If it doesn’t — even if the certificate is perfectly valid, issued by a reputable CA, and chains to a trust anchor in your OS trust store — Acrobat shows a warning. This created a de facto monopoly on "valid" PDF signatures: to get a green checkmark in the world’s dominant PDF viewer, you need an AATL-listed certificate, which costs significantly more than a standard code-signing certificate. The AATL remains one of the most contentious aspects of the PDF signature ecosystem (Chapter 26).

The European Union passed the Electronic Identification, Authentication and Trust Services regulation, creating a legal framework where digital signatures can have the same legal standing as handwritten signatures — but only if they meet strict technical requirements. eIDAS defined three tiers: electronic signatures (any electronic indication of signing intent), advanced electronic signatures (uniquely linked to the signatory, capable of identifying them, under their sole control, linked to the data so any change is detectable), and qualified electronic signatures (advanced + created by a qualified device + based on a qualified certificate issued by a qualified trust service provider). Only QES has automatic legal equivalence to a handwritten signature across all EU member states. PAdES became the primary technical mechanism for creating eIDAS-compliant signatures in PDF. Chapters 19–23 cover the entire eIDAS ecosystem.

The eIDAS regulation became fully effective across all EU member states, with the EU Trusted List (EUTL) operational. The EUTL is a machine-readable, cryptographically signed list of every Qualified Trust Service Provider in the EU — the authoritative source for determining whether a signing certificate qualifies for the highest legal tier. For developers: if your application needs to validate whether a PDF signature is a qualified electronic signature, you must check the signing certificate against the EUTL, not just against a generic trust store. The EUTL XML schema, its signature validation, and the LOT (List of Trusted Lists) hierarchy are covered in Chapter 20.

The first major revision of the PDF specification under ISO governance. PDF 2.0 deprecated XFA forms, removed several legacy features, and made significant changes to the digital signature sections. Notably: the adbe.pkcs7.sha1 SubFilter was deprecated (SHA-1 is cryptographically broken for collision resistance since the SHAttered attack of February 2017), and the specification added the ETSI.RFC3161 SubFilter for document-level timestamps. PDF 2.0 also clarified the byte range coverage rules and tightened the requirements around incremental updates after signing — changes that were partly motivated by early reports of the signature validation problems that Bochum would systematically expose two years later.

The Ruhr University Bochum team published their landmark paper at ACM CCS 2019, systematically breaking PDF signature validation in every major viewer. They identified three attack classes: Universal Signature Forgery (USF) — crafting a signed PDF with a garbage or empty signature value that viewers accepted as valid; Incremental Saving Attack (ISA) — appending unsigned changes to a signed PDF that viewers failed to flag; and Signature Wrapping Attack (SWA) — relocating the signature’s byte range to point at attacker-controlled data. Of 22 desktop viewers tested, all were vulnerable to at least one attack. The paper title referenced the fact that signed PDFs are used for tax refund claims, insurance payouts, and financial contracts — if signatures could be forged, the financial exposure was enormous. This research is the subject of Chapter 24.

The second edition of PDF 2.0 incorporated corrections and clarifications, including tightened language around signature validation motivated by the Bochum attacks. In the same year, Adobe issued CVE-2020-9592 and CVE-2020-9596 and patched Acrobat against USF, ISA, and SWA. Foxit, Nitro, LibreOffice, Okular, and every other major viewer released their own patches. The PAdES standards were also updated (ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1) to align with the second edition of ISO 32000-2.

The Bochum team published a follow-up: Shadow Attacks. Unlike the 2019 attacks (which modified a PDF after signing), Shadow Attacks prepared malicious content before the document was signed. The attacker created a PDF with hidden content (invisible text, overlapping images, replaced form fields) that the signer could not see during signing — but that became visible when the recipient opened the document. The signature validated correctly because the signed bytes were genuinely signed by the legitimate signer. The attack exploited the gap between what the signer saw and what the bytes contained — a fundamental problem in any visual-document signing model. Three variants were demonstrated: Hide (hiding content behind overlapping elements), Replace (swapping visible content via interactive form features), and Hide-and-Replace (combining both). Again, all major viewers were vulnerable.

ETSI updated the PAdES standard to align with ISO 32000-2:2020 and to incorporate lessons from the Bochum attacks. The update clarified requirements around signature scope validation, incremental update handling, and the relationship between the byte range and the visible document content. It also formalized the PAdES-B-LTA profile’s requirements for archival timestamps in the DSS (Document Security Store) — the mechanism that enables long-term validation decades after the signing certificate has expired.

The EU adopted the revised eIDAS regulation, which introduced the European Digital Identity (EUDI) Wallet — a smartphone-based identity wallet that every EU member state must offer to its citizens by 2026. The EUDI wallet will carry government-issued identity attestations (PID — Person Identification Data) and qualified electronic attestations of attributes (QEAA), enabling citizens to sign documents with qualified electronic signatures from their phone. This represents the most ambitious attempt to make QES mainstream — moving it from specialized HSM-backed signing services to consumer smartphones. The Architecture Reference Framework (ARF) defines the technical specifications. Chapter 27 covers the EUDI wallet and its impact on PDF signing.

EU member states are conducting large-scale pilot programs for the EUDI wallet across sectors: banking (identity verification), travel (digital travel credentials), education (diplomas), healthcare (prescriptions), and government services. The pilots test the wallet’s signing capabilities, including PAdES-B-LTA signature creation from mobile devices, remote QSCD integration, and cross-border signature validation. The Cloud Signature Consortium (CSC) API — the standard interface for remote signing — is being adapted for wallet integration. The outcome of these pilots will determine whether QES becomes a mainstream consumer technology or remains a specialized enterprise tool.

section.simpara: The table omits minor point releases and focuses on the events that changed what developers need to know. If you are working with PDF signatures today, every row in that table has left its fingerprint on the code you are writing — whether it’s the byte range gap from 1999, the PAdES constraints from 2009, the trust model questions raised by DigiNotar in 2011, or the validation rewrites forced by Bochum in 2019–2021.

section.title: Acknowledgments
section.simpara: This book would not exist without the open-source communities that make PDF signature work accessible to everyone. The Apache PDFBox team — particularly Andreas Lehmkühler, Tilman Hausherr, and the many contributors who maintain the signing infrastructure — have built the most widely used open-source PDF library in the Java ecosystem. The Legion of the Bouncy Castle (yes, that’s their actual name) gave us BouncyCastle, which provides the CMS and X.509 implementations that most Java signing code depends on, whether it knows it or not.

Special acknowledgment goes to the security researchers at Ruhr University Bochum — Vladislav Mladenov, Christian Mainka, Karsten Meyer zu Selhausen, Martin Grothe, and Jörg Schwenk — whose systematic analysis of PDF signature validation between 2019 and 2021 exposed fundamental flaws in how every major PDF viewer handled signatures. Their papers — "1 Trillion Dollar Refund: How To Spoof PDF Signatures" (CCS 2019), "Shadow Attacks: Hiding and Replacing Content in Signed PDFs" (NDSS 2021), and several follow-ups — demonstrated that attackers could forge signatures, hide content changes behind valid signatures, and bypass validation in Adobe Acrobat, Foxit Reader, and dozens of other viewers. The industry patched, but the research fundamentally changed how we think about PDF signature security. Their work is cited throughout this book, and Chapter 24 is dedicated to a technical analysis of their attacks.

What they found was this: PDF viewers did not actually verify what they claimed to verify. The Bochum team identified three major attack classes. Universal Signature Forgery (USF) showed that some viewers only checked whether a signature existed — not whether its cryptographic value was correct. An attacker could craft a PDF with an empty or garbage /Contents value, and viewers like Adobe Acrobat DC (before the patch) would display it as "signed and valid." Incremental Saving Attack (ISA) exploited the incremental update mechanism that PDF signatures depend on: an attacker could append a new revision to a signed PDF — changing text, adding pages, replacing content entirely — and because viewers did not properly check whether the appended changes fell outside the signed byte range, the signature still showed as valid. Signature Wrapping Attack (SWA) relocated the signed content within the file so that the byte range pointed at attacker-controlled data while the original signature remained structurally present. The follow-up Shadow Attacks paper (NDSS 2021) went further: an attacker could prepare a PDF before it was signed, embedding hidden content that would only become visible after signing — the signer would see one document, but the recipient would see another, and the signature would validate on both views.

The team tested 22 desktop PDF viewers and 7 online validation services. Every single one was vulnerable to at least one attack class. Adobe issued CVE-2020-9592 and CVE-2020-9596 and patched Acrobat in May 2020. Foxit, Nitro, LibreOffice, and every other major viewer followed with their own patches. The research forced a fundamental rethinking of PDF signature validation — in particular, how viewers handle incremental updates after signing, how byte range coverage is verified, and how the relationship between the CMS signature and the actual document bytes is validated. Before Bochum, most viewers treated signature validation as a checkbox. After Bochum, it became a security-critical code path that required adversarial thinking.

The European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) deserves credit for the PAdES standards that brought PDF signatures into the regulated world. Whatever one may think about the complexity of ETSI EN 319 142, it solved the real problem of making PDF signatures legally interoperable across borders.

And the PDF specification itself is the product of hundreds of engineers at Adobe, ISO, and ETSI who somehow managed to build a digital signature framework into a page description language originally designed for printing. That it works as well as it does is a testament to their effort. That it has the quirks it has is a testament to the fact that three decades of backward compatibility leaves marks.

partintro.simpara: This chapter builds the cryptographic foundation that everything else in this book depends on. We start with the key distribution problem that asymmetric cryptography was invented to solve, work through RSA and elliptic curve algorithms at a conceptual level, and end with practical Java code that generates the key pairs you will use to sign PDFs.

If you can already explain why RSA-2048 and ECDSA with P-256 provide roughly equivalent security, and you know why BouncyCastle exists alongside the JDK’s built-in providers, skip to Chapter 2. Everyone else: this is where your journey into PDF signatures begins.

chapter.title: The Key Distribution Problem
chapter.simpara: Before we talk about RSA, elliptic curves, or any of the algorithms that make PDF signatures possible, we need to understand the problem they were invented to solve.

Symmetric cryptography — where the same key encrypts and decrypts — has been around for millennia. Caesar ciphers, Enigma machines, AES-256: they all share a single fundamental weakness. Both parties must possess the same secret key. If Alice wants to send an encrypted message to Bob, she and Bob must first agree on a shared secret. In person, this is trivial — write the key on a piece of paper and hand it over. Over a network, it is a nightmare.
This is the key distribution problem: how do you securely share a secret with someone you have never met, over a channel that an adversary controls?

For most of human history, the answer was "you don’t — you use a trusted courier." Governments ran key distribution networks. Military organizations employed dedicated personnel whose sole job was transporting codebooks. Banks used armored vehicles. None of this scales to the internet, where you need to establish secure communications with millions of strangers, instantly, without any prior arrangement.

In 1976, Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman published "New Directions in Cryptography," describing a system where two parties could agree on a shared secret over a public channel without ever having communicated before. A year later, Ronald Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman published the RSA algorithm — the first practical public-key cryptosystem. (Clifford Cocks at GCHQ had independently discovered an equivalent system in 1973, but it was classified until 1997. Cryptography has a habit of being invented multiple times.)

The insight behind all of these systems is the same: mathematical one-way functions. Operations that are easy to perform in one direction but computationally infeasible to reverse. Multiplying two large primes takes microseconds; factoring their product takes longer than the age of the universe. This asymmetry — easy forward, hard backward — is the foundation of everything that follows.

chapter.title: Key Pairs: The Fundamental Asymmetry
chapter.simpara: An asymmetric key pair consists of two mathematically related keys:

listitem.simpara: A private key — kept secret, never shared, never transmitted
A public key — derived from the private key, freely distributable

chapter.simpara: The relationship between them has two critical properties:

listitem.simpara: It is computationally infeasible to derive the private key from the public key. Given a public key, there is no known efficient algorithm to compute the corresponding private key. The security of the entire system rests on this.

Operations performed with one key can only be reversed with the other. Data encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted with the private key. Data signed with the private key can only be verified with the public key.

chapter.simpara: This second property is what makes digital signatures possible. When you sign a PDF, your private key produces a cryptographic value that is mathematically bound to both your key and the document content. Anyone who has your public key — which is embedded in your certificate, which is embedded in the PDF itself — can verify that the signature was produced by someone holding the corresponding private key.
They cannot forge a new signature. They cannot modify the document without invalidating the signature. They cannot extract your private key from the signature or the public key.
This is the fundamental asymmetry that gives asymmetric cryptography its name, and it is the reason PDF digital signatures work at all.

note.simpara: The terms "encrypt" and "decrypt" are technically imprecise when applied to digital signatures. Signing is not encryption in the classical sense — you are not hiding data; you are producing a proof. The distinction matters in formal cryptographic contexts and in legal frameworks like eIDAS (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS)).

Throughout this book, we use "sign" and "verify" for signature operations, reserving "encrypt" and "decrypt" for actual confidentiality operations. Many older references (and some library APIs, including parts of the JCA) conflate the two. Do not let the terminology confuse you.

chapter.title: RSA: The Workhorse
chapter.simpara: RSA, named after Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman, remains the most widely deployed asymmetric algorithm in the world. Every PDF viewer supports it. Every certificate authority issues RSA certificates. Every signing library implements it. If you encounter a signed PDF in production, there is an overwhelming probability that it was signed with RSA.

section.title: The Mathematical Basis
section.simpara: The security of RSA rests on the integer factorization problem: given the product of two large primes, recover the original primes. Multiplication is trivial; factoring is believed to be computationally infeasible for sufficiently large numbers. Here is how this translates into a key pair.

listitem.simpara: Choose two large primes, p and q. These primes are generated independently using a cryptographically secure random number generator. For RSA-2048, each prime is approximately 1024 bits long. Finding primes of this size is itself nontrivial — it requires probabilistic primality tests (Miller-Rabin is the standard), and several candidates may be tested before a probable prime is found. This is why RSA key generation is noticeably slower than ECDSA key generation.

Compute the modulus n = p * q. This product n appears in both the public and private keys. For RSA-2048, n is a 2048-bit number. Computing n from p and q takes microseconds. Recovering p and q from n is the hard problem.

Compute the totient. Euler’s totient function for n is phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1). This value is used to derive the private exponent and is then discarded. It must never be stored or revealed — knowing phi(n) is equivalent to knowing the factorization of n.

Choose the public exponent e. In practice, e is almost always 65537 (0x10001). This value was chosen because it is prime, it has a low Hamming weight (only two bits set), and it makes modular exponentiation during verification fast. You will see 65537 in virtually every RSA certificate you examine. Some ancient systems used e = 3, which is faster but has known vulnerabilities when combined with naive padding.

Compute the private exponent d. d is the modular inverse of e modulo phi(n): d * e = 1 mod phi(n). This is computed using the extended Euclidean algorithm. Knowing d requires knowing phi(n), which requires knowing p and q.
The public key is the pair (n, e).

The private key is the pair (n, d). In practice, the private key also stores p, q, and several precomputed values (the CRT — Chinese Remainder Theorem — parameters) that make signing four times faster.

section.title: Why Large Primes Matter
section.simpara: The security of RSA depends on the inability of an attacker to factor n and recover p and q. The difficulty of factoring grows super-polynomially with the size of n. The best known classical factoring algorithm is the General Number Field Sieve (GNFS), whose complexity is roughly:

This means that doubling the key size does not merely double the work — it increases it by many orders of magnitude. Factoring a 768-bit RSA modulus was achieved in 2009 by a team of researchers using hundreds of machines over two years. Factoring a 1024-bit modulus is estimated to be within the reach of a well-funded nation-state. Factoring a 2048-bit modulus is estimated to require more computation than humanity can currently muster.

section.title: RSA Key Sizes vs Security Levels
section.simpara: The size of an RSA key is the bit length of the modulus n. Larger modulus means harder factoring means more security — but also slower operations.

table.title: RSA key sizes and their practical characteristics

entry.simpara: Broken. NIST disallowed after 2013. Do not use for any purpose. You will still encounter these in legacy PDFs — they were common before 2010.
Current minimum. NIST SP 800-57 minimum through 2030. This is what most CAs issue today. Adobe AATL requires at least 2048-bit RSA.
Recommended for new deployments. Provides 128-bit security — the level NIST considers adequate through at least 2030 and likely well beyond.

Conservative choice. Noticeable performance impact for high-volume signing. Some government requirements mandate this. The additional security margin over 3072 is modest relative to the performance cost.

warning.simpara: RSA-1024 is not merely "weak" — it is factored. In 2010, a team factored a 768-bit RSA modulus. The computational cost of factoring 1024-bit moduli is within reach of well-funded adversaries. If you encounter a signed PDF with an RSA-1024 certificate, the signature provides no meaningful security guarantee, even if the viewer shows it as "valid." Many viewers do not check key size during validation.

section.title: Performance Impact of Key Size
section.simpara: RSA has an unusual asymmetry in its operation costs: verification is fast; signing is slow.

This is because the public exponent e is small (65537), making the modular exponentiation during verification much cheaper than the exponentiation with the large private exponent d during signing. For PDF signatures, this works in our favor: documents are signed once but verified many times.

However, RSA’s performance degrades sharply as key sizes increase. The signing operation scales roughly as O(k3) where k is the key size in bits. Doubling the key size makes signing approximately eight times slower. For a desktop application signing a single document, this is irrelevant. For a server processing thousands of signing requests per second, the difference between 2048-bit and 4096-bit RSA is significant.

section.title: Why RSA-1024 Persists in the Wild
section.simpara: If RSA-1024 is broken, why do you still encounter it in signed PDFs? The answer is a combination of institutional inertia, cost, and the peculiar nature of long-lived documents.

Government agencies issued RSA-1024 certificates throughout the 2000s for document signing infrastructure. Many of those signed documents still exist, still circulate, and still need to be validated. Some organizations operate signing infrastructure on hardware security modules (HSMs) that cannot be easily re-keyed — the cost of replacing an HSM fleet across dozens of offices can be enormous. Other systems operate in air-gapped environments where updating cryptographic infrastructure requires physical site visits.

The practical consequence: your validation code must handle RSA-1024 gracefully, even if it correctly flags it as insecure. Refusing to parse a legacy document because the key size is too small is unhelpful. Displaying it as "valid" without qualification is irresponsible. The right approach is what PDFalyzer does: show the signature, show the key size, and let the user draw the appropriate conclusion.

simpara.emphasis: How to check the key size of a certificate in a signed PDF:

tip.simpara: In PDFalyzer, open the Signatures tab and look at the "Public Key" section of any signature. The key size is displayed directly.
From the command line, extract the certificate and use OpenSSL:
In Java with BouncyCastle, it is one line:
If you see 1024 or less, the certificate was issued before modern standards were enforced, and the signature’s cryptographic value is nil.

chapter.title: Elliptic Curve Cryptography: Smaller Keys, Same Security
chapter.simpara: In the mid-1980s, Neal Koblitz and Victor Miller independently proposed using elliptic curves over finite fields as the basis for public-key cryptography. The core idea: the elliptic curve discrete logarithm problem (ECDLP) is believed to be harder than integer factorization for equivalent key sizes. This means we can achieve the same security level with dramatically smaller keys.

section.title: Elliptic Curve Basics
section.simpara: An elliptic curve, for cryptographic purposes, is the set of points (x, y) satisfying an equation of the form y2 = x3 + ax + b over a finite field. The "finite field" part is important: we are not working with real numbers and smooth curves. We are working with integers modulo a large prime p, and the curve is a discrete set of points.

The cryptographic operation on these points is point addition: given two points on the curve, there is a geometric rule for computing a third point. Repeated point addition (scalar multiplication) gives us the one-way function: given a point G and a scalar d, computing Q = d * G is efficient. Recovering d from Q and G (the ECDLP) is computationally infeasible.

You do not need to understand the geometry to use ECDSA effectively. What matters is the practical consequence: the ECDLP is harder than integer factorization for the same key size, which is why a 256-bit elliptic curve key provides the same security as a 3072-bit RSA key.

section.title: The NIST Curves: P-256, P-384, P-521
section.simpara: The standard curves for ECDSA, defined by NIST in FIPS 186-4:

entry.simpara: The workhorse. Universally supported. Adobe Acrobat, PDFBox, iText, BouncyCastle — everyone supports P-256. If you are choosing an ECDSA curve for PDF signing, this is the default choice.
Required by certain government profiles (e.g., NSA Suite B, CNSS Policy 15). Broader support than P-521 but less than P-256.

Maximum security. The name is not a typo — it is 521 bits, not 512. Rarely used in practice for PDF signatures because the security margin over P-384 is excessive for most threat models, and some tools have spotty support.

important.simpara: The curve names are a source of endless confusion. "P-256" is the NIST name. "secp256r1" is the SECG (Standards for Efficient Cryptography Group) name. "prime256v1" is the OpenSSL name. They are all the same curve. You will encounter all three names in certificate metadata, library APIs, and documentation. When PDFBox reports a signature algorithm, it uses the JCA name; when BouncyCastle constructs the CMS structure, it uses OIDs; when you read the certificate in OpenSSL, it says "prime256v1." They are all P-256.

section.title: Why ECDSA Signatures Are Smaller
section.simpara: The practical consequence of the ECDLP’s difficulty is striking. RSA-3072 provides the same security as ECC-256, but the RSA key is twelve times larger. This size difference cascades through every part of the system: smaller keys mean smaller certificates, smaller CMS structures in the PDF’s /Contents field, faster network transmission of signed documents, and less storage overhead.

table.title: Equivalent security levels across algorithms (NIST SP 800-57 Part 1, Rev. 5)

section.simpara: For PDF signatures specifically, the /Contents hex string must be large enough to hold the entire CMS SignedData structure, including the signer’s certificate chain. With RSA-4096 certificates and a chain of three certificates, this blob can exceed 10 KB. With ECDSA P-256, the same chain might be 3 KB. Chapter 13 covers why the size of /Contents matters — it determines the byte range gap, which must be estimated before signing.

section.title: ECDSA Adoption in PDF Signatures
section.simpara: ECDSA adoption in PDF has been slower than in TLS, where P-256 became the dominant algorithm years ago. The reasons are institutional rather than technical.

Certificate authorities have been issuing RSA signing certificates for two decades, and existing workflows are built around RSA. Many enterprise signing solutions — particularly those using HSMs provisioned in the 2010s — have RSA keys burned into hardware. The Adobe AATL program includes ECDSA certificates from several providers, but the majority of AATL roots remain RSA.

That said, new deployments increasingly choose ECDSA. The eIDAS 2.0 Architecture Reference Framework (Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0)) lists ECDSA as a recommended algorithm for the EUDI wallet. If you are starting a new signing project and you have control over the certificate procurement process, ECDSA with P-256 is the technically superior choice.

caution.simpara: The random nonce k in ECDSA is a notorious source of catastrophic failures. If the same k is used for two different messages, the private key can be algebraically recovered. This is not a theoretical concern — it has happened in production. Sony’s PlayStation 3 code-signing key was extracted in 2010 because they used a constant value for k. The Bitcoin Android wallet vulnerability in 2013 was caused by a weak random number generator producing predictable k values. Modern implementations use deterministic nonce generation (RFC 6979) to eliminate this class of bugs entirely. BouncyCastle 1.80 supports RFC 6979 deterministic signatures.

chapter.title: EdDSA and Ed25519: The Future Direction
chapter.simpara: Edwards-curve Digital Signature Algorithm (EdDSA) is a modern signature scheme designed by Daniel J. Bernstein and colleagues, published as RFC 8032 in 2017. It addresses several practical shortcomings of ECDSA while providing equivalent or better security.

section.title: Bernstein’s Work and Design Philosophy
section.simpara: Daniel J. Bernstein — "djb" to everyone in the cryptographic community — designed Curve25519 in 2005 and the Ed25519 signature scheme in 2011 with a very specific philosophy: eliminate every source of implementation error.

The NIST curves (P-256, P-384) have been criticized for their opaque parameter generation — NIST never fully explained how the "random" seed values were chosen, which fueled suspicion (especially after the Dual_EC_DRBG backdoor scandal in 2013, where the NSA was found to have inserted a backdoor into a NIST-standardized random number generator). Bernstein designed Curve25519 with fully transparent, verifiable parameters — every constant has a documented justification.
More importantly, Ed25519 was designed to be implementable without the subtle pitfalls that plague ECDSA:

listitem.simpara: Deterministic signatures. EdDSA does not use a random nonce. The nonce is derived deterministically from the private key and the message. This eliminates the entire class of nonce-reuse vulnerabilities. There is no Sony PS3 scenario with EdDSA — it is structurally impossible.

Constant-time by design. The algorithm was designed from the ground up to run in constant time. There are no data-dependent branches or memory accesses that could leak information about the private key through timing, power analysis, or cache behavior.
No parameter negotiation. You use Ed25519 (128-bit security) or Ed448 (224-bit security). That is it. No curves to choose, no hash functions to configure, no padding schemes to get wrong.

section.title: Ed25519 and Ed448
section.simpara: EdDSA defines two concrete instances:

listitem.simpara: Ed25519: Based on Curve25519 (a twisted Edwards curve). Provides ~128-bit security. Signatures are 64 bytes, public keys are 32 bytes. This is the one everyone uses.

Ed448: Based on Curve448 (the "Goldilocks" curve, named by Mike Hamburg because 448 = 224 * 2, a "just right" security level). Provides ~224-bit security. Signatures are 114 bytes, public keys are 57 bytes.

section.title: EdDSA and PDF: Adoption Status
section.simpara: Here is where the good news ends, at least for PDF signatures.

As of 2026, EdDSA support in the PDF signature ecosystem is minimal. ISO 32000-2:2020 does not explicitly mention EdDSA. The PAdES standards (ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04)) reference ECDSA but not EdDSA. Adobe Acrobat does not support EdDSA signatures — a PDF signed with Ed25519 will show as "unknown algorithm" or "signature invalid" in Acrobat.

simpara.emphasis: Do not use EdDSA for PDF signatures in production today.

warning.simpara: While the algorithm itself is excellent, the ecosystem does not support it. A signature that cannot be validated by the dominant PDF viewer is, for all practical purposes, useless.

That said, EdDSA adoption is accelerating in the broader PKI ecosystem (TLS, SSH, code signing), and it is a matter of when — not if — PDF viewers add support. The eIDAS 2.0 Architecture Reference Framework (Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0)) includes EdDSA in its list of acceptable algorithms for the EUDI wallet. When EUDI wallet-based PDF signing becomes mainstream (Chapter 27), EdDSA support in PDF viewers will follow.

table.title: Algorithm comparison for PDF digital signatures (as of 2026)

entry.simpara: All except P-384 provide equivalent security.
Raw signature value before CMS packaging.
RSA scales poorly with key size.
EdDSA OIDs are not in the PDF spec.

table.title: Algorithm support across PDF viewers and libraries (as of early 2026)

chapter.title: Connecting to PDF: OIDs and SignerInfo
chapter.simpara: When a PDF signature is created, the signing algorithm is encoded as an ASN.1 Object Identifier (OID) inside the CMS SignedData structure (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)). Specifically, it appears in the SignerInfo.digestEncryptionAlgorithm field (the CMS name for the signature algorithm — "digestEncryptionAlgorithm" is a historical misnomer that has caused confusion for two decades, but we are stuck with it).

table.title: Common algorithm OIDs you will encounter in PDF signatures

entry.simpara: Legacy signatures. The hash algorithm is specified separately in digestAlgorithm.
Most common in modern PDF signatures.
Probabilistic padding. Hash specified in parameters.
Not yet seen in production PDF signatures.

tip.simpara: When you encounter the OID 1.2.840.113549.1.1.1 (plain RSA) in the digestEncryptionAlgorithm field, do not panic. This does not mean the signature used no hash. It means the CMS was constructed in the older style where the signature algorithm OID says "RSA" and the hash algorithm is determined solely by the digestAlgorithm field in the SignerInfo. Both styles — the combined OID like 1.2.840.113549.1.1.11 (SHA-256 with RSA) and the split approach — are valid per RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS). PDFBox and BouncyCastle handle both transparently.

chapter.title: Java’s Cryptographic Provider Model and BouncyCastle
chapter.simpara: Before we write any code, we need to understand how Java organizes its cryptographic implementations, because this directly affects how you generate keys, create signatures, and interact with BouncyCastle.

section.title: JCA, JCE, and the Provider Architecture
section.simpara: The Java Cryptography Architecture (JCA) defines abstract interfaces — KeyPairGenerator, Signature, MessageDigest, Cipher — and a provider model where concrete implementations are plugged in at runtime. The Java Cryptography Extension (JCE) extends JCA with additional capabilities. In modern Java (since JDK 9), the distinction is mostly historical.

The JDK ships with built-in providers: SUN, SunRsaSign, SunEC, SunJCE. These cover the basics — RSA, ECDSA with NIST curves, AES, SHA-2 hashes. For many applications, the built-in providers are sufficient.
For PDF signatures, they are not.

section.title: Why BouncyCastle Exists
section.simpara: The Legion of the Bouncy Castle (yes, that is the organization’s legal name, registered in Australia) provides the most comprehensive open-source cryptographic library for Java. BouncyCastle is not an alternative to the JCA — it is a JCA provider that plugs into the same framework.
We need BouncyCastle for PDF signatures for several reasons:

listitem.simpara: CMS/PKCS#7 implementation. The JDK does not include a CMS library. BouncyCastle’s bcpkix module provides CMSSignedDataGenerator, CMSSignedData, and everything else needed to construct and parse the CMS SignedData structures that PDF signatures require (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)).
Algorithm breadth. BouncyCastle supports algorithms the JDK does not, including EdDSA (Ed25519, Ed448), certain elliptic curves, and legacy algorithms that appear in older signed PDFs.
ASN.1 library. BouncyCastle includes a complete ASN.1 parsing and generation library — essential for working with X.509 certificates, CMS structures, and OCSP responses at the byte level.
Certificate generation. The bcpkix module includes X509v3CertificateBuilder for creating X.509 certificates programmatically — something you cannot do with the JDK alone.

section.title: Provider Registration

tip.simpara: In a Spring Boot application, register the BouncyCastle provider in a @PostConstruct method of a @Configuration class. This ensures it is available before any bean that needs cryptographic operations is initialized. PDFalyzer does this in BouncyCastleConfig.java. If you forget this step, you will get NoSuchAlgorithmException or NoSuchProviderException at runtime — and the error message will not tell you that a missing provider registration is the cause.

section.title: RSA Key Pair Generation
section.simpara: The output will show a 3072-bit key with public exponent 65537, the public key in X.509 (SubjectPublicKeyInfo) format, and the private key in PKCS#8 format. These format names — X.509 for public keys, PKCS#8 for private keys — are the standard encodings that appear in PEM files. Chapter 5 covers key material formats in detail.

section.title: ECDSA Key Pair Generation
section.simpara: Note the curve name: "secp256r1", not "P-256". The JCA uses SECG names in ECGenParameterSpec. BouncyCastle accepts both, but for portability, use the SECG name.

section.title: Performance Comparison
section.simpara: On a typical machine, RSA-3072 key generation takes 200-1500 ms (it varies because finding large primes is probabilistic), while ECDSA P-256 key generation completes in under 5 ms. The encoded key sizes will show the RSA public key at roughly 400 bytes versus 90 bytes for the EC public key.

These numbers matter in practice. If you are building a self-signed certificate generator for testing (as we do in Chapter 29), RSA key generation is the bottleneck. If you are deploying a signing service that provisions per-user keys, the hundred-fold speed advantage of ECDSA key generation is worth considering.

chapter.title: Choosing an Algorithm for PDF Signatures
chapter.simpara: Given all of the above, what should you actually use?

For maximum compatibility: RSA-2048 or RSA-3072 with SHA-256. Every PDF viewer, every library, every validation service supports this combination. If your signed PDFs need to be validated by the widest possible audience — and they usually do — RSA with SHA-256 is the safe choice. Use 3072-bit keys for new deployments; 2048-bit is acceptable for existing systems but provides only 112-bit security.

For smaller signatures and modern infrastructure: ECDSA with P-256 and SHA-256. If you control the validation environment (e.g., an internal document management system) or if you are targeting modern viewers (Adobe Acrobat 2020+, PDFBox 3.x, iText 8.x), ECDSA P-256 gives you 128-bit security with much smaller key material. The CMS blob in /Contents will be noticeably smaller, which simplifies byte range estimation.

For future-proofing: watch EdDSA, but do not deploy it yet. EdDSA is the superior algorithm by every technical measure, but the PDF ecosystem has not caught up. When Adobe Acrobat adds EdDSA support — likely driven by EUDI wallet adoption — it will become the preferred choice.

simpara.emphasis: The algorithm choice affects your certificate, not your code.

tip.simpara: The PDF signing code in PDFBox does not care whether you give it an RSA key or an ECDSA key. The SignatureInterface.sign(InputStream content) method receives the document bytes and returns a CMS blob. Whether that blob contains an RSA signature or an ECDSA signature depends entirely on what key material you provide.
This means you can switch algorithms by changing your certificate and keystore — the signing code remains identical. We demonstrate this in Chapter 29.

chapter.title: Summary
chapter.simpara: Asymmetric cryptography provides two operations that make digital signatures possible: signing with a private key, and verifying with the corresponding public key. The security of the system rests on mathematical problems — integer factorization for RSA, the elliptic curve discrete logarithm for ECDSA and EdDSA — that are easy to set up but computationally infeasible to reverse.

RSA remains the dominant algorithm for PDF signatures due to universal support across all viewers and libraries. ECDSA with P-256 offers equivalent security at 128 bits with dramatically smaller keys and faster operations. EdDSA (Ed25519, Ed448) is technically superior but lacks ecosystem support for PDF signatures as of 2026.

Java’s JCA provider model allows BouncyCastle to extend the JDK’s cryptographic capabilities, and BouncyCastle is essential for PDF signatures because it provides the CMS, ASN.1, and certificate generation capabilities that the JDK lacks.

In the next chapter, we examine hash functions and the mechanics of the signing operation itself — how the document’s bytes are reduced to a fixed-length digest, how that digest is signed, and what happens when the hash algorithm is compromised (as SHA-1 was, with devastating consequences for the PDF ecosystem).

partintro.simpara: This chapter covers the two primitives that every digital signature depends on: cryptographic hash functions and the sign/verify operation. We go deep on the SHA family, explain why MD5 and SHA-1 are still haunting PDF signatures decades after they were broken, and walk through the exact sequence of bytes that turns a document into a verifiable commitment.
If you already know what a hash function does, skip to for the forensic details of which algorithms you will actually encounter in the wild. If you want code, jump to .

chapter.title: What Hash Functions Do
chapter.simpara: A cryptographic hash function takes an input of arbitrary length and produces a fixed-size output — the digest or hash value. That is it. One operation, no keys involved, purely deterministic. Feed in the same bytes, get the same digest, every time, on every machine.
But the properties that make this useful for digital signatures are specific and non-negotiable:

table.title: Properties of a cryptographic hash function

entry.simpara: Same input always produces the same output. No randomness, no state. SHA-256("hello") is the same value on your laptop, on a server in Frankfurt, and on a Mars rover running Java 21. Always.

Regardless of whether the input is 1 byte or 1 terabyte, the output is always the same length. SHA-256 produces 32 bytes (256 bits). SHA-512 produces 64 bytes (512 bits). This fixed-size property is what makes hash functions useful as input to signing algorithms.

Change a single bit of the input, and on average 50% of the output bits change. The output looks completely unrelated to the original. There is no way to look at two digests and determine whether their inputs were similar.

Given a hash value h, it is computationally infeasible to find any input m such that hash(m) = h. You cannot reverse a hash function. This is not encryption — there is no key, no decryption, no way back.
Given an input m1, it is computationally infeasible to find a different input m2 such that hash(m1) = hash(m2). You cannot find a substitute document that produces the same hash.
It is computationally infeasible to find any two distinct inputs m1 and m2 such that hash(m1) = hash(m2). This is stronger than second pre-image resistance — the attacker gets to choose both inputs.

note.simpara: The distinction between second pre-image resistance and collision resistance matters enormously for digital signatures. An attacker who breaks collision resistance can prepare two documents with the same hash before either is signed — get one signed, then substitute the other. An attacker who breaks second pre-image resistance can find a substitute for an already signed document. Collision attacks are easier (birthday paradox — they require roughly 2^(n/2) work for an n-bit hash), which is why we retire algorithms at the collision resistance boundary, not the pre-image boundary.

chapter.title: Why We Hash Before Signing
chapter.simpara: You might wonder: why not just sign the document directly?
Three reasons, and they all matter:

listitem.simpara: Performance. RSA signing is slow. ECDSA signing is faster but still orders of magnitude slower than hashing. A 10 MB PDF would take an unacceptable time to sign directly with RSA-2048. Hashing reduces the input to 32 bytes (for SHA-256), and signing 32 bytes is fast regardless of the original document size.

Fixed-size input. RSA can only operate on data smaller than its modulus (typically 256 bytes for RSA-2048). You cannot feed a 10 MB document directly into RSA. Hashing solves this by producing a fixed-size value that fits within the algorithm’s constraints.

Security proof structure. The security of digital signature schemes is typically proven in two parts: the hash function provides collision resistance, and the signing algorithm provides unforgeability. Separating the two allows each component to be analyzed (and replaced) independently. When SHA-1 was broken, we could switch to SHA-256 without changing the RSA or ECDSA implementation.

chapter.simpara: The process is always the same:
chapter.screen: Sign: digest = hash(data) --> signature = sign(digest, privateKey) Verify: digest = hash(data) --> result = verify(digest, signature, publicKey)
chapter.simpara: The signer computes the hash of the data, then signs the hash with their private key. The verifier computes the hash of the same data independently, then checks the signature against that hash using the signer’s public key. If the hashes match and the signature is valid, the data has not been modified since signing.

important.simpara: The verifier computes the hash independently. The hash is not sent alongside the signature — it is recomputed from the original data. If an attacker modifies even one byte of the data, the verifier’s independently computed hash will differ from the hash that was signed, and verification will fail. This is the fundamental integrity guarantee of digital signatures.

chapter.title: The SHA Family
chapter.simpara: The Secure Hash Algorithm family, designed by the NSA and published by NIST, is what you will encounter in virtually every PDF signature created after 2010.

section.title: SHA-1: The Algorithm That Refused to Die
section.simpara: SHA-1 produces a 160-bit (20-byte) digest. It was published in 1995 as FIPS PUB 180-1 and became the default hash algorithm for essentially everything: TLS certificates, code signing, Git commits, and yes, PDF signatures.

The theoretical weaknesses appeared early. In 2005, Xiaoyun Wang, Yiqun Lisa Yin, and Hongbo Yu published an attack reducing the collision resistance of SHA-1 from the expected 2^80 operations to approximately 2^69 — still impractical, but a clear signal that the algorithm was weakening. NIST recommended transitioning away from SHA-1 by 2010 for digital signatures.
The practical break came on February 23, 2017.

section.title: The SHAttered Attack
section.simpara: A team from CWI Amsterdam (Marc Stevens) and Google (Elie Bursztein, Pierre Karpman) published the first practical SHA-1 collision. They produced two distinct PDF files — shattered-1.pdf and shattered-2.pdf — that had identical SHA-1 hashes but displayed completely different content. One showed a blue background with the text "SHAttered"; the other showed a red background with different text.
The numbers:

listitem.simpara: The attack required approximately 2^63.1 SHA-1 computations
That translated to roughly 6,500 CPU-years and 110 GPU-years of computation
Google estimated the cost at approximately $110,000 using cloud computing resources

The collision was found using a technique called an identical-prefix collision attack, where both files share a common prefix and the collision is achieved by appending carefully crafted "near-collision blocks"

table.title: How the SHAttered collision was constructed

entry.simpara: Both PDFs share an identical header and initial structure — valid PDF objects, page definitions, and the beginning of a JPEG image stream.

128 bytes of carefully computed data inserted into each file at the same offset. These blocks differ between the two files but are constructed so that the SHA-1 internal state converges to the same value after processing them.

After the collision blocks, the remainder of each file can be anything. Since the SHA-1 state is identical at that point, any shared suffix produces the same final hash. The attackers used this to embed different JPEG image data, producing visually distinct documents.

warning.simpara: The SHAttered attack has direct consequences for PDF signatures. If a PDF signature uses SHA-1 as its digest algorithm (SubFilter adbe.pkcs7.sha1 or a CMS SignedData with SHA-1 as the digest algorithm), an attacker who controls the document content before signing can prepare two versions with the same SHA-1 hash. Get one version signed, and the other is also "signed" — the signature validates on both. This is not a theoretical concern: the SHAttered team demonstrated it with actual PDF files.

section.title: The Cost of $110,000
section.simpara: It is worth dwelling on the attack cost because it illustrates how cryptographic security erodes over time. When SHAttered was published in 2017, $110,000 in cloud compute seemed significant. By 2020, the cost had dropped to an estimated $45,000 due to GPU price improvements. A 2020 paper by Ga{eacute}tan Leurent and Thomas Peyrin ("SHA-1 is a Shambles") demonstrated a chosen-prefix collision attack (more powerful than identical-prefix) for an estimated $45,000.

By 2026, a SHA-1 collision can likely be generated for under $20,000. This is well within the budget of a motivated individual, let alone a criminal organization or nation-state. The cost trajectory is clear: it only gets cheaper.

section.title: Consequences for PDF and the Deprecation Timeline
section.simpara: The SHAttered attack triggered a cascade of changes across the industry:

listitem.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 deprecated the adbe.pkcs7.sha1 SubFilter
Adobe Acrobat began showing warnings for SHA-1 signatures (initially a yellow warning, later escalated to an error on newer Acrobat versions)
The PAdES standards (ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04)) require SHA-256 or stronger for all profiles
The CA/Browser Forum prohibited SHA-1 in TLS certificates effective January 1, 2017 — a deadline that was already set before SHAttered, but the attack validated the urgency
Git, which used SHA-1 for commit hashes, began its multi-year migration to SHA-256 (still ongoing as of 2026)
Major browsers dropped SHA-1 certificate support: Chrome (January 2017), Firefox (January 2017), Edge (May 2017)

tip.simpara: If you encounter a PDF with a SHA-1 based signature, do not immediately panic. A SHA-1 signature is not forged — it was created legitimately at a time when SHA-1 was considered acceptable. The risk is that someone could have prepared a collision before signing. For documents signed before 2017 by trusted parties, the practical risk is low. For documents signed after 2017 with SHA-1, ask pointed questions about why.

section.title: SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512: The Modern Workhorses
section.simpara: These are the algorithms you should be using. All three are members of the SHA-2 family, published by NIST in 2001 as FIPS PUB 180-2.

table.title: SHA-2 family members used in PDF signatures

entry.simpara: The default for virtually all modern PDF signatures. PAdES-B-B requires SHA-256 as the minimum. If you are signing a PDF today, use SHA-256 unless you have a specific reason for a stronger algorithm.

Used when organizational policy requires stronger-than-256-bit hashing. Common in government and defense contexts. SHA-384 is actually a truncated SHA-512 with different initial values — it uses the 64-bit word size of SHA-512, which makes it faster than SHA-256 on 64-bit platforms.

Maximum strength in the SHA-2 family. Rare in PDF signatures because SHA-256 is considered sufficient, and SHA-512 produces larger digests that increase CMS signature size. Used in high-security environments.

note.simpara: SHA-384’s performance characteristic is surprising and worth knowing. Because it operates on 64-bit words (like SHA-512) rather than 32-bit words (like SHA-256), SHA-384 is actually faster than SHA-256 on modern 64-bit processors. If you need to hash large documents and your platform is 64-bit (it is), SHA-384 gives you more security bits at equal or better performance. This is one of those facts that sounds wrong but is measurably true.

section.title: SHA-3: The Insurance Policy
section.simpara: SHA-3 (Keccak), published as FIPS 202 in 2015, is a completely different design from SHA-2 — it uses a sponge construction rather than the Merkle-Damgard construction of SHA-1 and SHA-2. SHA-3 was selected by NIST specifically because it has no structural similarity to SHA-2, meaning any attack on SHA-2 would not apply.

You will not encounter SHA-3 in PDF signatures today. No PDF specification references it, no PAdES profile requires it, and no mainstream PDF library defaults to it. It exists as insurance: if SHA-2 is ever broken, SHA-3 is ready to replace it without a multi-year standardization process.
BouncyCastle 1.80 supports SHA-3 for signing, so the migration path exists when needed.

chapter.title: MD5: The Ghost in the Machine
chapter.simpara: MD5 produces a 128-bit (16-byte) digest. It was designed by Ronald Rivest (the "R" in RSA) in 1991 and published as RFC 1321. It was once the standard hash function for everything.
MD5 is broken. Not theoretically, not in some academic sense — practically, completely, irrecoverably broken for collision resistance.

section.title: The Wang-Yu Attacks (2004)
section.simpara: In August 2004, Xiaoyun Wang and Hongbo Yu (the same Wang who later weakened SHA-1) presented the first practical MD5 collision at CRYPTO 2004. They could find two distinct inputs with the same MD5 hash in less than an hour on a standard PC. The collision required approximately 2^39 MD5 operations — billions of times easier than the expected 2^64.

By 2006, Vlastimil Klima had reduced the attack to one minute on a single notebook computer. By 2008, Alexander Sotirov and Marc Stevens demonstrated a rogue CA attack using MD5 collisions, creating a fraudulent intermediate CA certificate that chained to a legitimate root. This was not an academic exercise — they obtained a real certificate from a real CA (RapidSSL, which used MD5 for certificate signing) and used MD5 collisions to create a rogue CA certificate that any browser would trust.
Today, MD5 collisions can be generated in seconds on commodity hardware. The algorithm is fully broken for any security-sensitive purpose.

section.title: Why MD5 Still Appears in Legacy PDFs
section.simpara: Despite being broken for collision resistance since 2004, you will encounter MD5 in:

listitem.simpara: Old PDF signatures created before 2005 using adbe.pkcs7.detached with MD5 as the digest algorithm
Legacy government and enterprise PDFs that were signed by systems deployed in the early 2000s

Some PKCS#12 keystores that use MD5-based key derivation for the encryption of the private key (this is a different use of MD5 — key derivation, not signature hashing — and while not ideal, the risk profile is different from using MD5 for signing)
The /ID array in PDF trailers, which traditionally contained MD5 hashes of file metadata (this is not security-sensitive)

caution.simpara: If you encounter a PDF signed with MD5 as the digest algorithm, the signature provides no collision resistance. An attacker who controlled the document before signing could have prepared a collision. For documents signed by trusted parties before 2004, the practical risk is limited (the attack was not known yet). For anything signed after 2004, the signature’s integrity guarantee is meaningless against a determined attacker.

chapter.title: The Signing Process Step by Step
chapter.simpara: Now we connect the hash function to the asymmetric cryptography from Chapter 1 and trace the exact sequence of operations that produces a PDF digital signature.

section.title: Step 1: Hash the Document
section.simpara: The signing library reads the bytes of the PDF specified by the /ByteRange array. This is not the entire file — it is everything except the /Contents hex string, which is where the signature will be placed. The gap exists because you cannot include the signature value in the data being signed (you would need to know the signature before computing it — a circular dependency).
The library feeds these bytes through the chosen hash algorithm:
section.screen: SHA-256( byteRange[0..offset1] || byteRange[offset2..end] ) = digest (32 bytes)
section.simpara: This 32-byte digest is the fingerprint of the document at the moment of signing.

section.title: Step 2: Pad the Hash
section.simpara: Before the hash can be signed with the private key, it must be padded to the correct size for the signing algorithm. For RSA, there are two padding schemes:

PKCS#1 v1.5 padding (the traditional scheme): The hash is prefixed with a DER-encoded DigestInfo structure that identifies the hash algorithm, then padded with 0xFF bytes to fill the modulus size. The structure looks like:
The padded message is: 0x00 0x01 [0xFF padding] 0x00 [DigestInfo DER bytes].

RSASSA-PSS (Probabilistic Signature Scheme): A more modern padding scheme that includes random salt, making signatures non-deterministic. PSS provides a tighter security proof than PKCS#1 v1.5, but it is less commonly used in PDF signatures because the entire PDF ecosystem was built around PKCS#1 v1.5. Some CAs issue PSS certificates, and you may encounter the OID 1.2.840.113549.1.1.10 (RSASSA-PSS) in the SignerInfo.signatureAlgorithm field.
For ECDSA, there is no padding step — the hash is used directly as input to the ECDSA signing equation.

warning.simpara: The choice of padding scheme is not just a technical detail. PKCS#1 v1.5 has known theoretical weaknesses (Bleichenbacher’s 1998 attack on encryption; related issues apply to signatures in certain implementation scenarios). RSASSA-PSS is provably secure under standard assumptions. In practice, PKCS#1 v1.5 remains safe for signatures (as opposed to encryption, where the Bleichenbacher attack is devastating), but security-conscious deployments should prefer PSS. The ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04) standards allow both.

section.title: Step 3: Encrypt with Private Key
section.simpara: The padded hash (for RSA) or raw hash (for ECDSA) is processed with the private key:

For RSA: s = paddedHashd mod n, where d is the private exponent and n is the modulus. The result s is the signature value — a number the same size as the modulus (256 bytes for RSA-2048, 384 bytes for RSA-3072).

For ECDSA: The algorithm generates a nonce k (either random or deterministic per RFC 6979), computes a curve point (x1, y1) = k * G, and derives the signature pair (r, s) from x1, the hash, and the private key d. The signature is the pair (r, s), each approximately the same size as the curve order (32 bytes each for P-256, totaling 64 bytes).

section.title: Step 4: Package as CMS SignedData
section.simpara: The raw signature value is not written directly into the PDF. It is wrapped in a CMS SignedData structure (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5) that includes:

listitem.simpara: The signature value itself
The signer’s certificate (and often the full certificate chain)
The SignerInfo containing the digest algorithm, signature algorithm, and signed attributes
The messageDigest signed attribute — the hash from Step 1
The signingTime attribute (optional but common)
The contentType attribute (always id-data for PDF)

section.simpara: This CMS blob is DER-encoded, then hex-encoded (each byte becomes two hex characters), and written into the /Contents entry of the signature dictionary.

chapter.title: The Verification Process Step by Step
chapter.simpara: Verification is the mirror image of signing, with one crucial addition: the verifier must independently recompute the hash and compare it against what the signer claims to have signed.

section.title: Step 1: Extract the CMS SignedData
section.simpara: The validator reads the /Contents hex string from the signature dictionary and decodes it from hex to DER to obtain the CMS SignedData structure.

section.title: Step 2: Re-Hash the Byte Range
section.simpara: The validator reads the /ByteRange array, extracts the specified byte ranges from the PDF file, and computes the hash using the algorithm specified in the SignerInfo.digestAlgorithm field.
This is the critical integrity check: if anyone has modified the PDF bytes after signing, this hash will differ from the original.

section.title: Step 3: Compare Hashes
section.simpara: The validator extracts the messageDigest attribute from the CMS SignerInfo signed attributes. This attribute contains the hash that the signer computed over the byte range content. The validator compares this with the independently computed hash from Step 2.
If they do not match, the document has been modified and validation fails immediately. There is no need to check the cryptographic signature.

section.title: Step 4: Verify the Cryptographic Signature
section.simpara: If the hashes match, the validator checks that the signature over the signed attributes (which include the messageDigest attribute) is cryptographically valid. For RSA: compute m' = se mod n and verify that the result matches the padded hash of the signed attributes. For ECDSA: verify the (r, s) pair against the hash using the public key point Q.
If this check passes, the signature is cryptographically valid — it was produced by someone holding the private key that corresponds to the public key in the certificate.

important.simpara: Cryptographic validity is necessary but not sufficient. A signature can be cryptographically valid and still be untrustworthy:

listitem.simpara: The certificate might be expired.
The certificate might have been revoked.
The certificate might not chain to a trusted root.
The signed byte range might not cover the entire document (the Bochum attacks exploited exactly this — see Chapter 24).
The certificate might lack the digitalSignature or nonRepudiation key usage extension (see Chapter 3).

important.simpara: Signature validation — as opposed to mere cryptographic verification — requires checking all of these conditions. Chapter 16 covers the full validation process.

chapter.title: DigestMethod in PDF Signatures
chapter.simpara: The hash algorithm used for a PDF signature is determined by the /SubFilter value and the CMS SignerInfo.digestAlgorithm field.

table.title: How the hash algorithm is specified

entry.simpara: The hash algorithm is specified inside the CMS structure. Can be any algorithm the signer chooses (SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512, or legacy SHA-1/MD5).
Deprecated by ISO 32000-2:2020. The SHA-1 hash of the entire byte range is embedded as the CMS content. Do not create new signatures with this SubFilter.
PAdES-compliant signatures. Must use SHA-256 or stronger per ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04). This is the recommended SubFilter for new signatures.
Document-level timestamp tokens (RFC 3161 — Internet X.509 PKI Time-Stamp Protocol (TSP)). The hash algorithm is specified in the timestamp request.

chapter.title: Hash Algorithm OIDs
chapter.simpara: When a hash algorithm appears inside a CMS SignedData structure, it is identified by an ASN.1 Object Identifier (OID). You need to know these OIDs because they appear in the raw bytes of every PDF signature, and when something goes wrong, you will be reading hex dumps.

table.title: Hash algorithm OIDs used in CMS (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) and NIST)

entry.simpara: Legacy only. Do not use for new signatures.
Deprecated. Still accepted by some validators for backward compatibility.
The default for modern PDF signatures. PAdES minimum.
Future use. Not currently seen in PDF signatures.

tip.simpara: The OID 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1 (SHA-256) is the one you will see most often. The prefix 2.16.840.1.101.3.4 is the NIST algorithm arc. The .2 subtree is hash algorithms, and .1 is SHA-256 specifically. Memorize this OID — you will recognize it instantly in ASN.1 dumps and hex views.

chapter.simpara: The OID structure 1.2.840.113549 is the RSA Security arc (the company, now part of EMC/Dell). MD5 (…​2.5) and various PKCS algorithms live under this prefix. The OID 1.3.14.3.2.26 for SHA-1 is under the OIW (Open Information Warehouse) arc — a historical artifact from the 1990s standardization process.

chapter.title: Signature Algorithm OIDs
chapter.simpara: The signing operation itself also has OIDs, which combine the hash algorithm with the public-key algorithm:
