table.title: Common signature algorithm OIDs in PDF signatures

entry.simpara: Most common combination in current PDF signatures.
Growing in usage. Smaller signatures than RSA.
Probabilistic RSA. The hash algorithm is specified in the parameters, not in the OID itself.

warning.simpara: When parsing CMS SignedData from a PDF signature, you may encounter a mismatch between the digestAlgorithm in the SignerInfo (which specifies the hash algorithm) and the hash algorithm implied by the signatureAlgorithm OID. For example, the digestAlgorithm might say SHA-256, but the signatureAlgorithm OID might be 1.2.840.113549.1.1.1 (RSA encryption, with no hash specified). This is technically allowed by RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) (the signature algorithm can be "RSA" and the hash is determined by the digestAlgorithm field), but it confuses many parsers. Always use the digestAlgorithm field as the authoritative source for which hash was used.

chapter.title: When Hash and Signature Algorithms Collide
chapter.simpara: An interesting question arises: what happens when you pair a hash algorithm with a signature algorithm designed for a different security level? For example, SHA-256 (128-bit security) with ECDSA P-384 (192-bit security)?
The answer is straightforward: the overall security level is the minimum of the two components. SHA-256 with P-384 provides 128-bit security, not 192-bit. The hash function is the bottleneck.
Standard pairings:

listitem.simpara: SHA-256 with RSA-3072 or ECDSA P-256: 128-bit security across the board. The default for most use cases.
SHA-384 with RSA-7680 or ECDSA P-384: 192-bit security. Government/defense contexts.
SHA-512 with ECDSA P-521: 256-bit security. Rarely needed.

chapter.simpara: Mismatched pairings are not wrong — they work correctly — but they waste resources. Using SHA-512 with RSA-2048 gives you 112-bit security (limited by RSA) while computing an unnecessarily expensive hash. Using SHA-256 with P-384 gives you 128-bit security (limited by SHA-256) while using a curve that provides more security than the hash can deliver.

tip.simpara: In practice, almost everyone uses SHA-256 with either RSA-2048/3072 or ECDSA P-256. The matched security levels make the overall system coherent, and SHA-256 is fast, universally supported, and has no known weaknesses. Do not overthink this unless you have specific compliance requirements that mandate higher security levels.

chapter.title: Java: Computing Hashes
chapter.simpara: The java.security.MessageDigest class is Java’s built-in hash computation API. For basic hashing, you do not need BouncyCastle — the JDK provides SHA-256 and SHA-512 out of the box.

callout.para: The only difference is a trailing period. The SHA-256 outputs differ in approximately 50% of their bits.

chapter.simpara: For PDF signing, you typically do not call MessageDigest directly. Instead, the signing library (PDFBox + BouncyCastle) computes the hash as part of the CMS signing operation. But knowing how to compute hashes independently is essential for debugging — when a signature fails validation, one of the first things to check is whether the hash of the byte range matches the messageDigest attribute in the CMS SignerInfo.

chapter.title: Java: Signing with BouncyCastle
chapter.simpara: Here is the complete signing flow using BouncyCastle 1.80. This code signs a byte array and produces a CMS SignedData structure — exactly what ends up in a PDF signature’s /Contents value.

callout.para: Extract the private key from the key store. In production, use an HSM (see Chapter 5).
Include the full certificate chain so validators can build the path without fetching intermediates.
For ECDSA keys, use hashAlgorithm + "withECDSA" instead. The algorithm name must match the key type.
Embedding certificates in the CMS is critical — without them, the validator has no chain to verify.
The false parameter means detached signature: the content is not included in the CMS structure.
The returned bytes are DER-encoded and will be hex-encoded to fill /Contents.

important.simpara: The false parameter in generator.generate(content, false) is the single most important boolean in PDF signing. It means "detached signature" — the signed content is not embedded in the CMS structure. PDF requires detached signatures because the content (the byte range) lives in the PDF file itself. If you pass true, the CMS will include the content, and some validators will reject it because the /SubFilter says adbe.pkcs7.detached but the CMS contains encapsulated content.

chapter.title: Debugging Hash Mismatches
chapter.simpara: When a PDF signature fails validation, the most common root cause is a hash mismatch: the hash of the byte range content does not match the messageDigest signed attribute in the CMS SignerInfo.
Here is how to debug this systematically:

tip.simpara: When debugging a hash mismatch, the most common causes are: (1) the byte range was extracted incorrectly — off by one byte, or not accounting for the gap correctly; (2) the PDF was modified after signing (perhaps by a tool that did a full save instead of incremental save); (3) the byte range array values do not add up to cover the entire file (there are bytes before or after the byte range that are not part of the signed content but should be — see Chapter 13 for the gory details).

chapter.title: Summary
chapter.simpara: Digital signatures are built on two primitives: hash functions provide integrity (any modification is detectable), and asymmetric signing provides authentication (only the private key holder could have created the signature) and non-repudiation (the signer cannot deny having signed).
For PDF signatures:

listitem.simpara: Use SHA-256 as the digest algorithm. It is the PAdES minimum, universally supported, and has no known weaknesses.
SHA-1 is deprecated. The SHAttered attack proved practical collision attacks are feasible. Do not create new SHA-1 signatures.
MD5 is broken. Collisions can be generated in seconds. Legacy only.
The hash is always computed over the byte range, not the entire PDF file. The byte range excludes the /Contents value (which contains the signature itself — you cannot sign your own signature).
CMS detached signatures are the standard: the content is not embedded in the CMS structure.
PKCS#1 v1.5 padding is dominant in PDF signatures, but RSASSA-PSS is gaining adoption for new deployments.

chapter.simpara: The next chapter introduces X.509 certificates — the mechanism that binds a public key to an identity, turning a raw cryptographic operation into a trust relationship.

abstract.simpara: A digital signature proves that someone with a specific private key signed some data. But who holds that private key? A public key is just a number — a very large number, but a number with no name attached.

X.509 certificates solve this problem. A certificate is a signed statement that binds a public key to an identity: "This public key belongs to Alice Smith at Acme Corp, and I, DigiCert, vouch for it." This chapter takes apart the X.509 certificate format, field by field, extension by extension, with the level of detail you need to debug real-world certificate problems in PDF signatures.

section.title: What a Certificate Is
section.simpara: At its core, an X.509 certificate is a data structure containing three things:

listitem.simpara: A subject — the identity (person, organization, or device) that the certificate represents
A public key — the subject’s public key, which can be used to verify signatures created with the corresponding private key
A digital signature from a Certificate Authority (CA) — a trusted third party that vouches for the binding between the subject and the public key

section.simpara: The CA’s signature is what gives the certificate its authority. Without it, you have a self-signed statement: "I am Alice, and here is my public key, trust me." With it, you have a verifiable claim backed by an entity that (presumably) verified Alice’s identity before signing.

The X.509 standard is defined in ITU-T Recommendation X.509 (originally from 1988, extensively revised since) and profiled for Internet use in RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile. When we talk about X.509 certificates in the context of PDF signatures, we mean the RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile profile — the Internet PKI profile that every browser, every operating system, and every PDF viewer actually implements.

section.title: ASN.1 and DER Encoding
section.simpara: Before we look at the certificate structure, we need to talk about how certificates are encoded, because you will encounter raw certificate bytes when debugging PDF signatures, and you need to understand what you’re looking at.

Certificates are defined using ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation One), a schema language for describing data structures. ASN.1 is to certificates what JSON Schema is to JSON — it defines the structure, types, and constraints. But unlike JSON, ASN.1 data is encoded in a compact binary format.

The encoding used for certificates is DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules), a subset of BER (Basic Encoding Rules) that produces exactly one encoding for any given value. This uniqueness property is critical: because a certificate’s signature covers its DER encoding, there can be only one valid encoding — otherwise the signature wouldn’t verify.

section.title: Tag-Length-Value
section.simpara: DER uses a Tag-Length-Value (TLV) structure. Every data element consists of:

listitem.simpara: Tag (1 or more bytes): identifies the type (SEQUENCE, INTEGER, BIT STRING, OID, etc.)
Length (1 or more bytes): the number of bytes in the value
Value (variable): the actual data

entry.simpara: The outermost wrapper of the certificate, and of most sub-structures (tbsCertificate, extensions, etc.)
Relative Distinguished Name components in the issuer and subject fields
Serial number, RSA modulus and exponent, version number
The signature value, and the subject public key value
Algorithm parameters (RSA with SHA-256 has NULL parameters)
Algorithm OIDs, extension OIDs, policy OIDs
Country codes and other restricted-character fields
Validity dates (for dates from 2050 onward, per RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.1.2.5)

tip.simpara: When you see 30 82 03 A5 at the start of a binary blob, you’re looking at a SEQUENCE with a two-byte length (the 82 means "the next 2 bytes are the length"), specifically 03 A5 = 933 bytes. This is almost certainly a certificate or a CMS structure. The 82 prefix on the length byte is the tell: 81 means one-byte length follows, 82 means two-byte length follows. Certificate structures are almost always in the 82 range (258-65535 bytes).

section.title: PEM vs. DER
section.simpara: You will encounter certificates in two physical formats:

listitem.simpara: DER: raw binary. File extensions: .der, .cer, .crt (ambiguous)
PEM: Base64-encoded DER wrapped in -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- / -----END CERTIFICATE----- headers. File extensions: .pem, .crt (ambiguous)

section.screen: -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- MIIFYjCCBEqgAwIBAgIQd70NbNs2+RrqIQ/E8FjTDTANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQsFAMDQ ... (Base64-encoded DER bytes) ... -----END CERTIFICATE-----
section.simpara: The content between the headers is the DER encoding, Base64-encoded with line breaks every 64 characters. Nothing more, nothing less. To get the DER bytes, strip the headers and Base64-decode.

warning.simpara: The .crt extension is ambiguous — it can be either DER or PEM. If you’re loading a certificate and get a parsing error, try the other format. Java’s CertificateFactory handles both, but BouncyCastle’s PEMParser expects PEM, and raw X509CertificateHolder(byte[]) expects DER.

section.title: Certificate Structure
section.simpara: An X.509v3 certificate has three top-level fields, defined in RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile Section 4.1:

callout.para: The "to be signed" certificate — all the data that the CA signs
The algorithm the CA used to create the signature (must match the algorithm in tbsCertificate)
The CA’s digital signature over the DER encoding of tbsCertificate

section.simpara: The CA signs tbsCertificate — the entire inner structure, DER-encoded. The signatureAlgorithm and signatureValue are outside the signed region (they can’t be inside it — the signature can’t sign itself, just as a PDF’s /Contents can’t be part of its own byte range).

section.title: tbsCertificate
section.simpara: This is where all the interesting data lives:
section.screen: TBSCertificate ::= SEQUENCE { version [0] EXPLICIT INTEGER DEFAULT v1, serialNumber INTEGER, signature AlgorithmIdentifier, issuer Name, validity Validity, subject Name, subjectPublicKeyInfo SubjectPublicKeyInfo, issuerUniqueID [1] IMPLICIT BIT STRING OPTIONAL, subjectUniqueID [2] IMPLICIT BIT STRING OPTIONAL, extensions [3] EXPLICIT Extensions OPTIONAL }

callout.para: Version: 0 = v1, 1 = v2, 2 = v3. All modern certificates are v3. If you see v1, it’s either very old or self-signed for testing.
Serial number: unique within the issuing CA. Must be a positive integer, at most 20 bytes. CAs use random serial numbers to prevent hash-based attacks.

Signature algorithm: must match the outer signatureAlgorithm field. This redundancy is specified by RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile to prevent algorithm substitution attacks.
Issuer: the Distinguished Name (DN) of the CA that signed this certificate.
Validity: notBefore and notAfter timestamps defining the certificate’s validity period.
Subject: the Distinguished Name of the entity this certificate represents.
Subject public key: the algorithm and the actual public key bytes.
Issuer/subject unique IDs: deprecated, never used in practice. Ignore them.
Extensions: the v3 extensions that make certificates actually useful. See below.

section.title: Distinguished Names
section.simpara: The issuer and subject fields use X.500 Distinguished Names — a hierarchical naming scheme from the 1980s that looks like this:
section.screen: CN=Alice Smith, O=Acme Corporation, L=San Francisco, ST=California, C=US
section.simpara: Each component is a Relative Distinguished Name (RDN) identified by an OID:

entry.simpara: Common Name — the certificate holder’s name
Organizational Unit (deprecated in TLS certificates since 2022, but still used in document signing certificates)
Subject serial number (not the certificate serial number — this is a personal or organizational identifier, like a tax ID or VAT number)
Email address (legacy; modern certificates use the Subject Alternative Name extension instead)

note.simpara: The DN ordering in X.509 certificates is most specific first (CN before O before C), which is the opposite of LDAP directory convention (C before O before CN). This catches people off guard when comparing DNs between different tools. Java’s X509Certificate.getSubjectX500Principal().getName() returns RFC 2253 format (CN first), while some tools show LDAP order (C first). When comparing issuer DNs for chain building, always compare the DER encoding, not the string representation.

section.title: Validity Period
section.simpara: RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.1.2.5 specifies that dates through 2049 use UTCTime (YYMMDDHHMMSSZ) and dates in 2050 and beyond use GeneralizedTime (YYYYMMDDHHMMSSZ). This means every certificate issued today uses UTCTime for both notBefore and notAfter — unless the certificate is valid until 2050 or later (some root CA certificates are).

important.simpara: For PDF signatures, the validity period has a nuanced interpretation. A signature made while the certificate was valid remains meaningful after the certificate expires — the certificate was valid at the time of signing. This is why timestamps are critical: a timestamp proves that the signature existed at a specific point in time, allowing validation even after certificate expiration. Without a timestamp, a validator must decide whether to check the certificate against the current time (strict) or the claimed signing time (lenient). Different validators make different choices, which is one of the many reasons the same signature shows "valid" in one viewer and "invalid" in another.

section.title: Certificate Extensions
section.simpara: Extensions are what make X.509v3 certificates useful for real-world applications. Without extensions, a certificate is just a name bound to a public key — no information about what the key can be used for, whether the certificate can issue other certificates, or how to check if it’s been revoked.
Each extension has three components:

listitem.simpara: OID: identifies the extension type
Critical flag: if true, a system that doesn’t understand this extension must reject the certificate
Value: the extension-specific data (an OCTET STRING containing DER-encoded content)

section.title: KeyUsage
section.simpara: OID: 2.5.29.15 | Defined in: RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.2.1.3 | Typically: Critical
The KeyUsage extension is a bit string that specifies what the public key in this certificate may be used for.

table.title: KeyUsage bits relevant to PDF signing

entry.simpara: Required for PDF signing. Indicates the key can be used to verify digital signatures (other than certificate and CRL signatures). This is the bit you need for signing documents.

Indicates the key is used for signatures where the signer cannot later deny having signed. Some certificate profiles for document signing set this bit instead of or in addition to digitalSignature. The RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile renamed this to contentCommitment but the old name persists in most tools.
Indicates the key is used to verify signatures on certificates. Set on CA certificates. If this bit is set on an end-entity certificate, something is wrong.
Indicates the key is used to verify signatures on CRLs. Set on CA certificates that issue CRLs.

warning.simpara: Some document signing certificates have only nonRepudiation (bit 1) set, without digitalSignature (bit 0). This is technically valid per RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile, but it causes problems: some validators check for digitalSignature and reject certificates that only have nonRepudiation. The safest approach for CAs issuing document signing certificates is to set both bits. If you’re validating signatures, check for either bit 0 or bit 1.

section.title: ExtendedKeyUsage
section.simpara: OID: 2.5.29.37 | Defined in: RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.2.1.12 | Typically: Non-critical
While KeyUsage is a coarse-grained bitmask, ExtendedKeyUsage specifies more precise purposes using OIDs.

table.title: ExtendedKeyUsage OIDs relevant to PDF signing

entry.simpara: Historically used for document signing certificates (S/MIME). Many older PDF signing certificates have this EKU.
Microsoft’s document signing EKU. Common on certificates from CAs that target Windows environments.
Adobe-specific EKU for AATL certificates. If a certificate has this EKU and chains to an AATL root, Acrobat shows maximum trust.
For OCSP responder certificates only. Not for document signing.

tip.simpara: When a PDF signature shows as "untrusted" in Adobe Acrobat but validates fine in other viewers, the first thing to check is the ExtendedKeyUsage. Acrobat is stricter about EKU than most other validators. A certificate without an explicit document signing EKU may be rejected by Acrobat even if it has digitalSignature in KeyUsage.

section.title: BasicConstraints
section.simpara: OID: 2.5.29.19 | Defined in: RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.2.1.9 | Typically: Critical
This extension distinguishes CA certificates from end-entity certificates:

listitem.simpara: cA: TRUE — this certificate can issue other certificates
cA: FALSE (or absent) — this is an end-entity certificate, it cannot issue other certificates
pathLenConstraint — if present, limits how many intermediate CAs can follow this one in a chain

caution.simpara: If you encounter a PDF signed by a certificate with BasicConstraints: cA=TRUE, something is deeply wrong. CA certificates should never be used for document signing — their private keys should be locked in HSMs and used only to sign other certificates. A document signed by a CA certificate suggests either a misconfiguration or a test setup that accidentally used the CA key for signing.

section.title: SubjectKeyIdentifier and AuthorityKeyIdentifier
section.simpara: SubjectKeyIdentifier (OID: 2.5.29.14): a hash of the certificate’s public key, used as a compact identifier. Typically the SHA-1 hash of the BIT STRING value of the subjectPublicKey (not the entire SubjectPublicKeyInfo structure — just the key bytes).

AuthorityKeyIdentifier (OID: 2.5.29.35): identifies the CA certificate that signed this one. Contains the CA’s SubjectKeyIdentifier (keyIdentifier), and optionally the CA’s issuer name and serial number.
These two extensions are the primary mechanism for chain building: follow the AuthorityKeyIdentifier from a certificate to find the CA certificate whose SubjectKeyIdentifier matches.

note.simpara: The SubjectKeyIdentifier is typically a 20-byte SHA-1 hash. Yes, SHA-1 — even in 2026. This is not a security concern because the SKI is used as an identifier, not for integrity verification. There is no attack value in finding a collision for a SubjectKeyIdentifier.

section.title: CRLDistributionPoints
section.simpara: OID: 2.5.29.31 | Defined in: RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.2.1.13
Contains one or more URLs where CRLs (Certificate Revocation Lists) can be downloaded to check if this certificate has been revoked.
In practice, this is almost always a single HTTP URL:
section.screen: http://crl.example.com/issuing-ca.crl

section.title: AuthorityInfoAccess
section.simpara: OID: 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.1 | Defined in: RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile §4.2.2.1
This extension is critical for chain building and revocation checking. It contains two types of access descriptions:

entry.simpara: URL of the OCSP responder for checking certificate revocation in real time. The validator sends a request with the certificate’s serial number and gets back a signed response saying "good," "revoked," or "unknown."

URL where the issuing CA’s certificate can be downloaded. This is how validators fetch missing intermediate certificates during chain building. If a PDF signature doesn’t embed the full chain, the validator can use this URL to find the intermediates.

important.simpara: The "CA Issuers" access method is the safety net for incomplete certificate chains. When a signing tool embeds only the end-entity certificate in the CMS (not the full chain), the validator must fetch the intermediates. It uses the CA Issuers URL to download the next certificate up the chain, then follows that certificate’s CA Issuers URL, and so on until it reaches a trusted root. This works — when you have network access. In offline validation scenarios, the chain must be complete. Always embed the full chain in your CMS signatures.

section.title: Certificate Profiles
section.simpara: Not all certificates are created equal. The purpose of a certificate is determined by its extensions, particularly KeyUsage and ExtendedKeyUsage.

entry.simpara: Signs documents, authenticates TLS sessions, signs email
10-30 years for roots, 5-10 years for intermediates

section.title: Document Signing Certificates
section.simpara: A certificate profile specifically for document signing typically has:

listitem.simpara: KeyUsage: digitalSignature and/or nonRepudiation
ExtendedKeyUsage: document signing OID (varies by CA)
Subject: identifies the signer (person or organization)
Validity: 1-3 years
CRL and OCSP: both present for revocation checking

section.title: How Different CAs Structure Their Certificates
section.simpara: Different CAs make different choices, and these choices affect interoperability:

entry.simpara: CN = person name, serialNumber = national ID, C = country code
Adobe document signing OID + email protection
Varies widely — some use EU-specific OIDs for qualified certificates
Varies — many EU national CAs are on AATL
Typically yes (this is their primary purpose)

tip.simpara: When debugging why a PDF signature is trusted in one viewer but not another, the CA’s AATL status is usually the answer. Adobe Acrobat trusts AATL certificates. Other viewers (macOS Preview, Okular, Firefox’s built-in PDF viewer) use the OS trust store, which is different. A DigiCert document signing certificate will be trusted in Acrobat (AATL member), probably trusted in Windows (DigiCert roots are in the Windows trust store), and possibly untrusted in Firefox (which uses its own trust store). Same certificate, same signature, three different trust decisions.

section.title: Common Certificate Problems in the Wild
section.simpara: Years of debugging PDF signatures have revealed a pattern of recurring certificate problems. Here are the ones you will encounter most often:

section.title: Missing Extensions
section.simpara: Symptom: Signature validates cryptographically but shows "untrusted" or "cannot determine if the signer is who they claim to be."

Cause: The signing certificate is missing critical extensions — typically ExtendedKeyUsage or SubjectKeyIdentifier. Some legacy CAs (particularly older European government CAs) issued certificates with minimal extensions that predate the current RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile profile requirements.
Fix: The certificate needs to be reissued with proper extensions. There is no workaround — you cannot add extensions to an existing certificate without re-signing it (which means the CA must do it).

section.title: Wrong Key Usage
section.simpara: Symptom: Signature fails validation with "key usage does not permit digital signature" or similar.

Cause: The certificate has KeyUsage set to keyEncipherment or dataEncipherment but not digitalSignature or nonRepudiation. This happens when someone uses a TLS server certificate (designed for key exchange) to sign a PDF document.
Fix: Use a certificate with the correct KeyUsage for document signing. A TLS certificate is not a signing certificate.

section.title: Expired Certificates
section.simpara: Symptom: "Certificate has expired" warning, even though the document was signed years ago when the certificate was valid.

Cause: The validator is checking the certificate against the current time instead of the signing time. Without a trusted timestamp, the validator has no proof of when the signature was actually created.

Fix: Include a timestamp in the signature (see Chapter 18 on LTV). A timestamp from a trusted TSA proves the signature existed at a specific time, allowing validators to check the certificate’s validity against that time rather than the current time.

section.title: Self-Signed Certificates
section.simpara: Symptom: "The signer’s identity is unknown" or "certificate is not issued by a trusted CA."
Cause: The signing certificate is self-signed (the issuer and subject are the same, and it signed itself). Self-signed certificates have no external trust anchor.

Fix: For production use, obtain a certificate from a recognized CA. For internal/testing use, you can add the self-signed certificate to the viewer’s trust store manually — but this defeats the purpose of the CA trust model.

note.simpara: Self-signed certificates are perfectly valid for testing and development. PDFalyzer’s self-signed certificate generation feature is designed exactly for this purpose. The cryptographic integrity of the signature is identical regardless of whether the certificate is self-signed or CA-issued — the difference is purely about trust.

section.title: Incomplete Certificate Chain
section.simpara: Symptom: "Unable to build certificate chain" or "issuer certificate not found."
Cause: The CMS signature embeds only the end-entity certificate, not the intermediate CA certificates. The validator cannot build a path from the end-entity to a trusted root.
Fix: Include the full certificate chain (end-entity + all intermediates) in the CMS certificates field. Do NOT include the root CA — the root must already be in the validator’s trust store.

important.simpara: This is one of the most common PDF signature problems. Always embed the full chain (minus the root) in your CMS signatures. The signing code in Chapter 2 already does this — note how it includes chain (from the PKCS#12 key store) in the CMSSignedDataGenerator. If your PKCS#12 file doesn’t contain the intermediates, you need to add them before signing.

section.title: Java: Loading and Inspecting Certificates
section.simpara: Here is how to load certificates and extract their fields using BouncyCastle 1.80.

callout.para: Returns the subject DN in RFC 2253 format (CN first). For display, you might want to parse this into individual components.
Serial numbers are typically displayed in hexadecimal. Some CAs use very large random serial numbers (up to 20 bytes per RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile).
getKeyUsage() returns a boolean array where index 0 = digitalSignature, 1 = nonRepudiation, …​, 5 = keyCertSign, 6 = cRLSign. Returns null if the extension is absent.
Java’s getExtendedKeyUsage() returns OIDs as strings. You need to map them to human-readable names yourself.
The OCSP URL is what you send revocation check requests to. This URL must be accessible from the validator’s network.

section.title: Dissecting a Real Signing Certificate
section.simpara: Let’s walk through a real document signing certificate, field by field, to see how the theory maps to practice.
section.screen: Certificate: Data: Version: 3 (0x2) Serial Number: 0a:55:14:f1:28:1d:45:87:21:f1:27:3c:e5:b6:4f:2a Signature Algorithm: sha256WithRSAEncryption Issuer: C=US, O=DigiCert Inc, CN=DigiCert SHA2 Assured ID CA Validity Not Before: Jan 15 00:00:00 2024 GMT Not After : Jan 14 23:59:59 2027 GMT Subject: C=US, ST=California, L=San Francisco, O=Acme Corporation, CN=Acme Document Signing Subject Public Key Info: Public Key Algorithm: rsaEncryption RSA Public-Key: (2048 bit)
section.simpara: Let’s annotate each field:

entry.simpara: Expected. All modern certificates are v3 to support extensions.
16 bytes (128 bits) of random data. DigiCert uses large random serials to prevent hash-based attacks on the serial number.
sha256WithRSAEncryption — SHA-256 hash with RSA signature. This is the algorithm the CA (DigiCert) used to sign this certificate.
DigiCert SHA2 Assured ID CA — this is an intermediate CA certificate. The "SHA2" in the name indicates it’s a post-SHA-1 intermediate. You’ll need to find this certificate to build the chain.
3-year validity (Jan 2024 to Jan 2027). Typical for document signing certificates. The signer needs to renew before Jan 14, 2027, or new signatures will be created with an expired certificate.
Identifies the organization (Acme Corporation) and the purpose (Document Signing). The CN often contains the signer’s name or the certificate’s purpose rather than a hostname.
RSA 2048-bit. The minimum for document signing. Some organizations require 4096-bit, but 2048 is widely accepted and faster.

section.simpara: The extensions would look like:
section.screen: X509v3 extensions: X509v3 Key Usage: critical Digital Signature X509v3 Extended Key Usage: 1.2.840.113583.1.1.5 X509v3 Basic Constraints: critical CA:FALSE X509v3 Subject Key Identifier: A1:B2:C3:D4:E5:F6:... X509v3 Authority Key Identifier: keyid:F8:7E:34:22:... Authority Information Access: OCSP - URI:http://ocsp.digicert.com CA Issuers - URI:http://cacerts.digicert.com/DigiCertSHA2AssuredIDCA.crt X509v3 CRL Distribution Points: Full Name: URI:http://crl3.digicert.com/sha2-assured-id-ca.crl URI:http://crl4.digicert.com/sha2-assured-id-ca.crl

entry.simpara: Correct for document signing. This certificate can create digital signatures. The critical flag means validators must enforce this.
This is the Adobe Authentic Documents Trust OID. It means this certificate is intended for PDF document signing and is likely on the AATL.
Correct. This is an end-entity certificate. It cannot issue other certificates.
A hash of the public key. Used for chain building (other certificates reference this to identify this certificate as their issuer).
Points to the issuing CA’s SubjectKeyIdentifier. Follow this to find DigiCert SHA2 Assured ID CA.
The OCSP responder URL. Send a request here to check if this certificate has been revoked.
The URL to download the issuing CA’s certificate. If the CMS signature doesn’t include the intermediate, a validator can fetch it here.
Two CRL URLs (redundancy). Download the CRL to check for revocation without OCSP.

tip.simpara: DigiCert provides two CRL URLs (crl3 and crl4) for redundancy. If one CDN is down, the other should still work. This is a best practice — if your CA provides only one CRL URL and it becomes unreachable, validators that use CRL checking will fail to validate signatures. OCSP is the preferred revocation checking method (real-time, smaller responses), but CRL is the fallback.

section.title: Summary
section.simpara: X.509 certificates are the bridge between raw cryptography and trust. They bind a public key to an identity, and the CA’s signature on that binding is what makes the trust model work — or fail, when CAs are compromised (DigiNotar), when extensions are missing, or when the chain is incomplete.
For PDF signatures:

listitem.simpara: Every signed PDF embeds one or more X.509 certificates in the CMS SignedData structure.
The signing certificate must have digitalSignature or nonRepudiation in KeyUsage.
The full chain (end-entity + intermediates, not the root) should be embedded in the CMS.
AIA extensions provide fallback URLs for chain building and OCSP revocation checking.
Trust is viewer-dependent: Acrobat uses AATL, browsers use their own trust stores, OS-level trust stores vary. The same certificate can be trusted in one context and untrusted in another.

section.simpara: The next chapter follows these certificates up the chain — from the end-entity signing certificate, through intermediates, to the root CA that anchors the entire trust hierarchy.

abstract.simpara: This chapter explains why a single certificate is never enough. We trace the trust delegation model from root CAs through intermediate certificates to the end-entity certificate that actually signs your PDF, then walk through the chain-building and path-validation algorithms that every validator must implement. We cover revocation checking — both CRL and OCSP — in exhaustive detail, because revocation failures are among the most common and most confusing reasons a PDF signature is rejected. Finally, we survey the trust stores that anchor the entire system: Adobe’s AATL, the EU Trusted List, OS-level stores, Java’s cacerts, and custom trust stores — explaining what each one is, who controls it, and when you need which.

If you already know how AuthorityKeyIdentifier links to SubjectKeyIdentifier and you can explain the difference between AATL and EUTL without hesitation, skip to Chapter 5. Everyone else: this is where we connect cryptographic identity to real-world trust.

chapter.title: Why Chains Exist
chapter.simpara: In Chapter 3 we established that an X.509 certificate binds a public key to an identity. A certificate authority (CA) vouches for that binding by signing the certificate with its own private key. But who vouches for the CA?

This is the trust delegation problem. A root CA cannot practically issue certificates to every person and organization on the planet. The operational burden would be staggering: vetting applicants, maintaining infrastructure, handling revocations, passing audits — all for millions of subscribers. Even if it could, concentrating all certificate issuance in a single entity would create a catastrophic single point of failure. If that one root CA were compromised, every certificate it had ever issued would become suspect.

The solution is hierarchical delegation. A root CA issues certificates not (primarily) to end users, but to intermediate CAs. Those intermediate CAs, in turn, issue certificates to other intermediates or to end-entity subscribers. The root CA’s private key stays in a vault — literally, in most cases, inside an HSM in a physically secured facility that is powered on only for scheduled signing ceremonies.
This creates a chain:

listitem.simpara: The root CA self-signs its own certificate. It is the trust anchor — the starting point that must be accepted on faith (or, more precisely, accepted because it appears in a trust store you have chosen to rely on).
One or more intermediate CAs hold certificates signed by the root CA (or by another intermediate above them). They perform the day-to-day work of issuing certificates.
The end-entity (sometimes called the "leaf") certificate is the one that belongs to the actual signer — the person or service that signs PDFs. This certificate is signed by an intermediate CA.

note.simpara: The term "chain" is somewhat misleading. In practice, a certificate’s trust path can be a directed acyclic graph rather than a simple linear chain. An intermediate CA might hold multiple certificates — one signed by Root A, another signed by Root B — enabling cross-certification between different PKI hierarchies. However, for PDF signatures, you almost always deal with simple linear chains of depth two or three: root → intermediate → end-entity, or root → intermediate → intermediate → end-entity. The rare cases where cross-certification matters are in government PKIs and the EU Trusted List hierarchy, which we cover later in this chapter.

chapter.title: Chain Structure in Detail
chapter.simpara: A typical PDF signing chain has three certificates:

table.title: Certificate chain for a PDF signing certificate

entry.simpara: Contains the signer’s public key. Has keyUsage with digitalSignature or nonRepudiation set. Has extendedKeyUsage with document signing or email protection OIDs. Issued by the intermediate CA. Short validity period (1-3 years).

Contains the intermediate CA’s public key. Has basicConstraints with cA=TRUE and a pathLenConstraint. Has keyUsage with keyCertSign and cRLSign. Issued by the root CA. Medium validity period (5-10 years).

Self-signed: the issuer and subject are identical. Has basicConstraints with cA=TRUE. Present in trust stores. Long validity period (20-30 years). Never transmitted in the CMS structure of a PDF signature (validators must already have it in their trust store).

chapter.simpara: The CMS SignedData structure in a PDF signature’s /Contents field typically includes certificates at positions 1 and 2 — the end-entity and intermediate(s). The root CA certificate is not included. This is by design: the validator must already trust the root, and including it in the signature would just waste space. The validator checks the chain by building a path from the end-entity up through the intermediates until it reaches a certificate that matches one in its trust store.

important.simpara: Some signing implementations include the root certificate in the CMS certificates field. This is not wrong per se — RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) allows it — but it serves no purpose. A validator will not trust a root certificate just because it appears inside a signature. Trust comes from the trust store, not from the document. If anything, including the root wastes space in the /Contents hex string, which matters when you are estimating the size of the content gap (Chapter 13). PDFBox does not include the root by default. iText does. Adobe Acrobat tolerates both approaches.

warning.simpara: A chain with a missing intermediate is the single most common cause of "certificate chain not trusted" errors in PDF signatures. The end-entity certificate is present, the root is in the trust store, but the intermediate that links them is nowhere to be found. The CMS structure does not contain it, and the validator cannot download it (or is configured not to). Always include the full chain (minus the root) in your CMS certificates field.

chapter.title: Building the Chain
chapter.simpara: Given a bag of certificates — perhaps from a CMS SignedData structure, perhaps from a PKCS#12 file, perhaps assembled from multiple sources — how do you construct the actual chain?
The algorithm relies on two X.509 extensions that exist specifically for this purpose:

section.title: AuthorityKeyIdentifier and SubjectKeyIdentifier
section.simpara: Every well-formed certificate contains:

listitem.simpara: SubjectKeyIdentifier (SKI): an extension on the certificate itself, identifying the public key it contains. Typically a SHA-1 hash of the public key’s bit string. Defined in RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 4.2.1.2.

AuthorityKeyIdentifier (AKI): an extension identifying the certificate that signed this one. Contains the SKI of the issuer’s certificate. Defined in RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 4.2.1.1.

section.simpara: The chain-building algorithm is conceptually simple:

listitem.simpara: Start with the end-entity certificate (the signer’s cert from the CMS SignerInfo).
Read its AKI extension. This gives you the SKI of the issuer.
Search the available certificates for one whose SKI matches that AKI.
When you find it, that is the next link in the chain. Read its AKI.
Repeat until you find a certificate where the subject equals the issuer (self-signed) or one that matches a trust anchor.

tip.simpara: In practice, chain building can fail for several reasons. The AKI extension might be absent (legal but rare in modern certificates). The SKI might not match because the issuing CA generated the SKI using a non-standard method (SHA-1 of the public key is the norm, but RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile does not mandate it). Or the intermediate certificate might simply not be available. When debugging chain failures, always dump both the AKI of the child and the SKI of the expected parent, and compare them byte by byte.

section.title: Fallback: Issuer Distinguished Name Matching
section.simpara: If the AKI/SKI matching fails — or if the certificates lack these extensions (which happens with very old CAs) — the fallback is matching the certificate’s Issuer DN against candidate certificates' Subject DN. This is less reliable because distinguished name comparison is notoriously tricky: leading/trailing whitespace, different string encodings (PrintableString vs UTF8String), case sensitivity of certain components — all of these can cause a mismatch even when the certificates are part of the same chain.

RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 7.1 defines the comparison rules for distinguished names, and they are considerably more complex than string equality. BouncyCastle’s X500Name.equals() handles this correctly; Java’s X500Principal.equals() usually does too, but edge cases exist with certain encoding combinations.

chapter.title: Path Validation: RFC 5280 Section 6
chapter.simpara: Building the chain gives you an ordered list of certificates. Path validation verifies that this chain is actually valid — that every link is cryptographically sound and that the certificates meet all the required constraints.

RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 6 defines the algorithm in six phases. The full specification runs to several pages of pseudocode; here we distill the essential checks that matter for PDF signature validation.

section.title: The Six Steps

table.title: Certificate path validation (RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 6)

entry.simpara: Set the initial trust anchor (root CA certificate), the expected issuer name, the expected public key, and policy constraints. These are the "inputs" to the validation algorithm.

For each certificate in the chain (from root to leaf): verify the signature on the certificate using the issuer’s public key, check that the certificate is within its validity period (notBefore ⇐ current time ⇐ notAfter), check that the certificate has not been revoked.

Check that the subject name and any subject alternative names comply with any name constraints imposed by certificates higher in the chain. A CA can restrict its subordinates to only issue certificates for certain domains or email addresses.

Process the certificate policies extension and any policy constraints. This determines which policies are valid for the entire chain. Policy processing is the most complex part of path validation and is rarely used in PDF signature contexts — but some government PKIs rely on it.

Verify that every CA certificate in the chain has basicConstraints with cA=TRUE, and that the pathLenConstraint is not exceeded. A pathLenConstraint of 0 means the CA can only issue end-entity certificates, not sub-CAs.

After processing all certificates, check the final certificate (the end-entity) against the expected usage. For PDF signing, this means checking keyUsage for digitalSignature or nonRepudiation, and optionally checking extendedKeyUsage.

warning.simpara: The JCA CertPathValidator expects the chain in a specific order: end-entity first, root last (or root omitted). If you pass the chain in the wrong order, validation will fail with a confusing error message about "unable to find valid certification path to requested target." This is the single most common mistake when using the JCA path validation API.

section.title: Validity Period Checking and Signing Time
section.simpara: There is a subtlety in validity period checking that matters enormously for PDF signatures. A certificate might be expired now but was valid at the time the document was signed. Should the signature be considered valid?

The answer depends on whether the signature includes a trusted timestamp. If the CMS SignedData contains a timestamp token in the unsigned attributes (see Chapter 18), and that timestamp was produced while the certificate was still valid, then the signature remains valid even after the certificate expires. This is the foundation of long-term validation (LTV) — the mechanism that allows a signed PDF to remain verifiable for decades.

Without a timestamp, most validators check the certificate against the current time. A signature made with a certificate that has since expired will show warnings or errors in Adobe Acrobat, even if the signature was perfectly valid when it was created.

important.simpara: This is why timestamps matter so much for PDF signatures. A timestamp cryptographically proves when the signature was created, allowing validators to check the certificate against the signing time rather than the current time. If you are building a signing service, always include a timestamp. Chapter 18 covers timestamps and LTV in exhaustive detail.

chapter.title: Revocation Checking
chapter.simpara: A certificate may be revoked before it expires. The private key might have been compromised, the subscriber might have left the organization, or the CA might have discovered that the certificate was issued incorrectly. Revocation checking is the mechanism by which validators discover whether a certificate has been revoked.
There are two mechanisms: Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs) and the Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP). Both have strengths and weaknesses, and a robust validator should support both.

section.title: CRL Deep Dive
section.simpara: A Certificate Revocation List is a signed data structure published by a CA that lists all certificates the CA has revoked. CRLs are defined in RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 5.

section.title: CRL Structure
section.simpara: A CRL contains:

table.title: CRL structure (simplified from RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 5.1)

entry.simpara: The "to-be-signed" portion, containing the issuer name, the thisUpdate timestamp (when the CRL was issued), the nextUpdate timestamp (when the next CRL will be issued), and the list of revoked certificates.
The algorithm used to sign the CRL (e.g., SHA-256 with RSA).
The CA’s signature over the tbsCertList, proving the CRL is authentic and unmodified.

section.simpara: Each entry in the revokedCertificates list contains:

listitem.simpara: The serial number of the revoked certificate
The revocation date

Optional CRL entry extensions, including the reasonCode (keyCompromise, cACompromise, affiliationChanged, superseded, cessationOfOperation, certificateHold, removeFromCRL, privilegeWithdrawn, aACompromise)

section.title: CRL Distribution Points
section.simpara: How does a validator find the CRL? The certificate itself contains a CRL Distribution Points extension (RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 4.2.1.13) with one or more URLs where the CRL can be downloaded. Typically these are HTTP URLs, though LDAP URLs appear in older enterprise PKIs.

section.title: The Size Problem
section.simpara: CRLs have a fundamental scalability problem: they grow monotonically. Every revoked certificate is listed until the certificate’s natural expiration date passes. A busy CA that revokes hundreds or thousands of certificates per year will produce CRLs that are megabytes in size.

Let’s Encrypt, for example, issues millions of certificates. A full CRL from Let’s Encrypt would be enormous. This is why Let’s Encrypt does not publish CRLs for individual certificates (they rely on OCSP and, more recently, CRL sharding).

For PDF signatures, the CRL size matters because long-term validation (LTV) requires embedding revocation data inside the PDF. A 2 MB CRL embedded in the Document Security Store (DSS) inflates the PDF by 2 MB. If the chain has three certificates and each CRL is large, the overhead becomes significant.

section.title: Delta CRLs
section.simpara: Delta CRLs (RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 5.2.4) were designed to mitigate the size problem. A delta CRL contains only the changes since the last "base" CRL was published. In theory, this allows validators to download a small delta rather than a large full CRL.

In practice, delta CRLs are rarely used. Most CAs do not publish them. Most validators do not request them. The overhead of tracking base/delta CRL relationships adds complexity that the industry has largely chosen to avoid, preferring OCSP for real-time status and full CRLs for archival purposes.

section.title: OCSP Deep Dive
section.simpara: The Online Certificate Status Protocol (RFC 6960 — Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP)) provides real-time, per-certificate revocation status. Instead of downloading a list of all revoked certificates, the validator sends a request asking "is this specific certificate revoked?" and gets back a signed response.

section.title: OCSP Request Structure
section.simpara: An OCSP request identifies the certificate by:

listitem.simpara: The hash of the issuer’s distinguished name
The hash of the issuer’s public key
The certificate’s serial number
An optional nonce to prevent replay attacks

section.title: OCSP Response Structure
section.simpara: The responder returns one of: good (not revoked), revoked (with the revocation time and optional reason), or unknown (the responder does not know about this certificate).

The response is signed, either by the CA itself or by a specially designated OCSP responder certificate — a certificate issued by the CA with the id-pkix-ocsp-nocheck extension, indicating it is authorized to sign OCSP responses on behalf of the CA.

section.title: The Nonce Problem
section.simpara: OCSP supports a nonce extension: the requester includes a random value, and the responder echoes it back. This prevents an attacker from replaying an old "good" response for a certificate that has since been revoked.

In practice, many OCSP responders ignore the nonce. They pre-compute responses for all active certificates and serve them from cache, which is incompatible with per-request nonces. Adobe’s OCSP responder, for example, does not support nonces. The security trade-off is real: without nonces, a replay window exists equal to the response’s nextUpdate time. For most PDF signature scenarios, this is acceptable — the replay window is typically hours, not days.

caution.simpara: When embedding OCSP responses in a PDF for LTV, nonce-less responses are actually preferable. A response with a nonce is bound to a specific request and cannot be reused. A cached, nonce-less response can be embedded in the PDF and validated later by anyone, which is exactly what LTV requires. This is a case where the "less secure" option (no nonce) is the correct choice for the specific use case.

section.title: OCSP Stapling
section.simpara: OCSP stapling is a mechanism where the entity presenting a certificate also presents a recent OCSP response for that certificate, pre-fetched from the OCSP responder. In TLS, the server staples the OCSP response to the TLS handshake.

For PDF signatures, the equivalent is embedding the OCSP response in the CMS SignedData structure or in the PDF’s Document Security Store (DSS). This eliminates the need for the validator to contact the OCSP responder at all — the revocation proof travels with the document, just like the certificate chain.

section.title: CRL vs OCSP: When to Use Which

table.title: CRL vs OCSP comparison for PDF signatures

entry.simpara: Can be cached locally; works offline once downloaded
Good: can be embedded in DSS
Good: can be embedded in DSS (prefer nonce-less)
Good: the CA does not learn which certificates you check
Poor: the OCSP responder learns exactly which certificates you are validating (which documents you are opening)

tip.simpara: For PDF signing services, use OCSP for real-time validation during signing, and embed both the OCSP response and relevant CRLs in the DSS for long-term validation. Belt and suspenders. Adobe Acrobat’s LTV process follows exactly this approach.

chapter.title: Trust Stores
chapter.simpara: A trust store is a collection of root CA certificates that a system has decided to trust. Without a trust store, there is no anchor for chain validation — every chain ends in a self-signed certificate, and if that certificate is not in a trust store, the chain is untrusted regardless of how cryptographically correct it is.
Different ecosystems maintain different trust stores, and understanding which one applies to your PDF signatures is critical.

section.title: Adobe Approved Trust List (AATL)
section.simpara: The Adobe Approved Trust List is a curated set of root certificates that Adobe Acrobat and Adobe Reader trust for signature validation. If your signing certificate chains to an AATL root, Acrobat shows a green checkmark with "Signed and all signatures are valid." If it does not — even if the certificate is perfectly valid, issued by a reputable CA, and trusted by your operating system — Acrobat shows a yellow warning triangle.
This is the single most important trust store for PDF signatures in practice, because Adobe Acrobat has approximately 75% of the worldwide PDF viewer market share.

section.title: How AATL Works
section.simpara: Adobe publishes the AATL as a signed file that Acrobat downloads automatically (the URL is hardcoded). The list is refreshed every 30 days. It contains approximately 250 root certificates from CAs around the world.
To get on the AATL, a CA must:

listitem.simpara: Pass a WebTrust or ETSI audit for PDF signing
Meet Adobe’s technical requirements (minimum RSA-2048, SHA-256 or stronger)
Sign an agreement with Adobe
Pay an annual fee (reportedly in the range of $10,000-$50,000 USD, though Adobe does not publish the exact figure)

section.title: The Monopoly Problem
section.simpara: The AATL creates a de facto monopoly on "valid" PDF signatures in the world’s dominant viewer. If you want your signed PDFs to show a green checkmark in Acrobat, you need a certificate from an AATL-member CA. Those certificates cost significantly more than standard TLS or code-signing certificates — typically $200-$500 per year for a basic document signing certificate, and considerably more for organizational certificates.

This pricing creates a barrier to entry. An individual developer who wants to sign PDFs "properly" (by Acrobat’s standards) must pay for an AATL-rooted certificate, even though the cryptographic security of a self-signed certificate is identical. The trust model imposes a financial cost on trust itself.

note.simpara: This is not unique to Adobe — the TLS ecosystem has a similar dynamic with browser trust stores. But in TLS, Let’s Encrypt disrupted the pricing model by offering free certificates. There is no Let’s Encrypt for PDF signing certificates. The AATL’s requirements (particularly the audit requirements) make a free document signing CA impractical.

section.title: EU Trusted List (EUTL)
section.simpara: The European Union Trusted List is a fundamentally different approach to trust. Instead of a single vendor curating a list, the EUTL is a hierarchy of machine-readable, cryptographically signed XML documents maintained by the 27 EU member states under the authority of the eIDAS regulation (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS)).

section.title: Structure: The LOT Hierarchy
section.simpara: The EUTL follows a List of Trusted Lists (LOT) architecture:

listitem.simpara: The European Commission publishes the LOT — a signed XML document listing the URLs of every member state’s national trusted list.

Each member state publishes its own trusted list — a signed XML document listing every Qualified Trust Service Provider (QTSP) in that country, along with the services they offer and the certificates associated with those services.
Each QTSP entry includes the service type (e.g., issuing qualified certificates for electronic signatures), the service status (granted, withdrawn, etc.), and the relevant certificates.

section.simpara: The LOT is defined by ETSI TS 119 612 V2.1.1 (2015-07) and is publicly accessible at a well-known URL maintained by the European Commission.

section.title: How EUTL Differs from AATL

entry.simpara: eIDAS regulation (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS)), legally binding across 27 member states
Machine-readable XML, signed per ETSI TS 119 612 V2.1.1 (2015-07)
Any CA willing to pay and pass audit
Only Qualified Trust Service Providers recognized under eIDAS
No fee to EU; the QTSP status is granted by the national supervisory body
Adobe Acrobat (imports EUTL), EU DSS validation framework, national validators
None (Acrobat showing "valid" has no legal meaning)
Qualified electronic signatures have the legal equivalent of handwritten signatures across the EU

section.simpara: Adobe Acrobat can import the EUTL in addition to the AATL. When configured to use both, Acrobat validates against the union of AATL and EUTL trust anchors. In European deployments, this is the standard configuration.

important.simpara: A signature that validates against the EUTL as a qualified electronic signature has legal standing that an AATL-only signature does not. The EUTL is not just a technical trust store — it is a legal instrument. If you are operating in the EU, the EUTL is the authoritative trust store for determining whether a signature meets the highest legal tier. Chapter 19 covers the eIDAS legal framework in detail.

section.title: Operating System Trust Stores
section.simpara: Every major operating system maintains its own root CA trust store:

listitem.simpara: Windows: The Microsoft Root Certificate Program. Certificates are distributed via Windows Update. The store is managed through the certmgr.msc snap-in or via certutil. Programs using the Windows CryptoAPI (Schannel, .NET) use this store automatically.

macOS: The Apple Root Certificate Program. Certificates are in the System Keychain. Managed through Keychain Access or security command-line tool. Programs using Security.framework (Safari, macOS apps) use this store.
Linux: Varies by distribution. Debian/Ubuntu use /etc/ssl/certs/ and the ca-certificates package. Red Hat/Fedora use /etc/pki/tls/certs/ and the ca-trust system. Updated via package manager.

section.simpara: For PDF signatures, OS trust stores are relevant primarily when using system-level PDF viewers (Preview on macOS, Evince on Linux) or when a Java application is configured to use the OS trust store instead of Java’s cacerts. Adobe Acrobat on Windows and macOS does not use the OS trust store for signature validation by default — it uses the AATL (and optionally the EUTL).

section.title: Java cacerts
section.simpara: Java ships with its own trust store: a PKCS#12 file (or JKS in older JDKs) located at $JAVA_HOME/lib/security/cacerts. The default password is the string changeit (and almost nobody changes it).
The Java cacerts store is maintained by the JDK vendor (Oracle, Eclipse Adoptium, Amazon Corretto, etc.) and typically mirrors the major browser/OS trust stores with some additions and removals.
To add a custom root CA to the Java trust store (for testing or internal PKIs):
section.programlisting: keytool -importcert \ -alias "my-internal-ca" \ -file my-root-ca.pem \ -keystore $JAVA_HOME/lib/security/cacerts \ -storepass changeit

warning.simpara: Modifying the system cacerts file affects all Java applications on that machine. For production deployments, prefer creating a separate trust store and configuring your application to use it via javax.net.ssl.trustStore system property or programmatic TrustManagerFactory configuration. Do not modify the global cacerts unless you are administering the entire machine.

section.title: Custom Trust Stores
section.simpara: Sometimes none of the standard trust stores are appropriate:

listitem.simpara: Internal PKIs: Your organization runs its own CA hierarchy for internal document signing. The root CA is not in AATL, EUTL, or any OS trust store, because it was never intended to be publicly trusted.
Restricted trust: You want to validate PDF signatures but only trust a specific subset of CAs — perhaps only EUTL-listed QTSPs, not the entire AATL.
Air-gapped environments: Systems with no network access cannot download CRL/OCSP responses or refresh trust stores. All trust material must be pre-loaded.

section.simpara: In Java, creating a custom trust store is straightforward:

table.title: Trust store comparison for PDF signature validation

entry.simpara: Varies by member state; ~100 QTSPs total
LOT refresh per ETSI TS 119 612 V2.1.1 (2015-07)

chapter.title: Debugging Chain Failures
chapter.simpara: When a PDF signature shows "certificate chain not trusted," here is the systematic approach:

section.title: Step 1: Extract the Certificates
section.simpara: Open the PDF in PDFalyzer or use PDFBox to extract the CMS SignedData from /Contents. List every certificate in the CMS certificates field. Note the subject DN, issuer DN, serial number, SKI, and AKI of each.

section.title: Step 2: Build the Chain Manually
section.simpara: Starting from the signing certificate, match AKI to SKI. Can you build a complete chain from the signer to a self-signed root? If a link is missing, that is your problem — the CMS is missing an intermediate.

section.title: Step 3: Check the Trust Anchor
section.simpara: Is the root certificate in the relevant trust store? If you are validating in Acrobat, check the AATL. If you are validating in your Java application, check cacerts or your custom trust store. The root being "well-known" is not enough — it must be in the specific trust store the validator is using.

section.title: Step 4: Check Validity Periods
section.simpara: Is every certificate in the chain currently within its validity period? Or, if the signature has a timestamp, was every certificate valid at the time the timestamp was created?

section.title: Step 5: Check Revocation
section.simpara: Is every certificate in the chain un-revoked? Can the validator reach the CRL distribution points or OCSP responders listed in the certificates? Firewall rules, proxy settings, and DNS resolution failures are common culprits in enterprise environments.

section.title: Step 6: Check Key Usage
section.simpara: Does the signing certificate have the correct keyUsage bits set? Some validators (including Adobe Acrobat) require nonRepudiation (also called contentCommitment) for document signatures. Others accept digitalSignature. If neither is set, the certificate is not suitable for signing regardless of chain validity.

tip.simpara: When you encounter a chain failure in production, resist the urge to immediately add the root certificate to your trust store. First, understand why the chain is failing. Is it a missing intermediate? A revoked certificate? A validator misconfiguration? Adding a root to a trust store should be a deliberate decision, not a debugging shortcut. Every root you add is a root you trust to issue certificates for any purpose — including, potentially, certificates that could impersonate other entities.

chapter.title: Cross-Certification and Bridge CAs
chapter.simpara: We mentioned earlier that trust paths can be graphs, not just chains. Cross-certification is the mechanism that creates these more complex topologies.

When two independent PKI hierarchies need to trust each other’s certificates, they can cross-certify: Root A issues a certificate for Root B’s public key (or an intermediate in B’s hierarchy), and vice versa. This creates a trust bridge between the two hierarchies without requiring end-entity certificates to be re-issued.

The US Federal PKI uses a Bridge CA architecture: the Federal Bridge CA cross-certifies with individual agency CAs, enabling certificates issued by the Department of Defense to be validated by systems that trust the Department of State’s root, and vice versa.

For PDF signatures, cross-certification is relevant when a signing certificate chains to a root that is not directly in the validator’s trust store, but is cross-certified with a root that is. The chain-building algorithm must consider cross-certificates as additional links, potentially finding a path through an unexpected route.

This adds complexity to chain building, and not all validators handle it correctly. Adobe Acrobat supports cross-certification for AATL and EUTL roots. Java’s CertPathValidator supports it if you provide the cross-certificates as additional material via PKIXBuilderParameters.addCertStore(). PDFBox relies on BouncyCastle for chain building and validation, and BouncyCastle handles cross-certification correctly in most cases.

note.simpara: If you are debugging a chain failure and the certificates seem correct but the path does not reach a trust anchor, check for cross-certificates. The missing link might not be a traditional intermediate CA but a cross-certificate that bridges two PKI hierarchies. Cross-certificates are sometimes stored in LDAP directories rather than embedded in the CMS structure, making them easy to overlook.

chapter.title: Summary
chapter.simpara: Certificate chains exist because no single CA can practically serve everyone. The hierarchical delegation model — root CAs issuing to intermediate CAs, intermediates issuing to end-entities — distributes the operational burden while maintaining a chain of trust back to a known anchor.

Chain building matches AuthorityKeyIdentifier to SubjectKeyIdentifier, walking from the end-entity up through intermediates to the root. Path validation (RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile section 6) verifies that every link in the chain is cryptographically sound, temporally valid, and compliant with the constraints imposed by certificates higher in the chain.

Revocation checking — via CRLs for bulk status and OCSP for real-time, per-certificate status — ensures that compromised or withdrawn certificates are detected. For PDF signatures, embedding revocation data in the document (via the DSS or CMS unsigned attributes) enables long-term validation without network access.

The trust store anchoring the chain determines whether a signature is considered "trusted." In the PDF world, the AATL dominates because Adobe Acrobat dominates. In the EU, the EUTL provides legally binding trust anchors for qualified electronic signatures. OS trust stores and Java’s cacerts serve broader ecosystems but are not the primary validation paths for most PDF viewers.

When chain validation fails, the debugging process is methodical: extract certificates, build the chain manually, check the trust anchor, verify validity periods, check revocation status, and verify key usage. Most chain failures in production are caused by missing intermediates, not by actual trust problems.

The next chapter examines the formats in which keys and certificates are stored and transported — PKCS#12, PEM, DER, JKS, and PKCS#8 — because loading the wrong format is the quickest way to turn a working certificate chain into an inscrutable exception stack trace.

abstract.simpara: This chapter maps the landscape of key and certificate storage formats — the containers that hold the private keys, public keys, and certificates you need to sign a PDF. We cover PKCS#12, PEM, DER, JKS, PKCS#8, and PKCS#1, explaining the history, internal structure, and practical quirks of each. We include a complete format-conversion matrix and address the HSM integration problem: what happens when the private key cannot be exported at all.

If you have never been bitten by a "wrong format" exception when loading a keystore, consider yourself lucky and read this chapter anyway. If you have, you will find the explanations and conversion commands you wish you had found the first time.

chapter.title: PKCS#12 / PFX: The Universal Container
chapter.simpara: PKCS#12 is the format you will use most often for PDF signing. It bundles a private key, the corresponding certificate, and (usually) the full certificate chain into a single, password-protected file. The standard file extensions are .p12 and .pfx.

section.title: History
section.simpara: The lineage is tangled, as most things in cryptography are.

Microsoft created PFX (Personal Information Exchange) in the mid-1990s as a container format for exporting keys and certificates from Internet Explorer and IIS. RSA Laboratories, working on their PKCS (Public-Key Cryptography Standards) series, developed PKCS#12 as a platform-neutral alternative. The two formats converged over time: PKCS#12 v1.0 (1999) incorporated Microsoft’s PFX design, and RFC 7292 (2014) formalized the standard.
Today, .p12 and .pfx are effectively interchangeable extensions for the same format. Some tools (notably Windows) prefer .pfx; others (Java, OpenSSL) prefer .p12. The binary content is identical.

note.simpara: You will occasionally encounter ancient .pfx files from the late 1990s that use a pre-standard PFX format incompatible with PKCS#12. These are rare in 2026 but not extinct. OpenSSL will refuse to parse them, producing an error about "unknown bag type." If you encounter one, the only reliable conversion path is through a legacy Windows system or Internet Explorer’s certificate export.

section.title: Internal Structure
section.simpara: A PKCS#12 file is a DER-encoded ASN.1 structure containing:

entry.simpara: The outermost wrapper. Contains a version number (always 3), the authenticated safe, and an optional MAC (Message Authentication Code) for integrity verification.

A SEQUENCE of ContentInfo values, each containing one or more SafeBag objects. The ContentInfo can be unencrypted (data), encrypted with a password-based cipher (encryptedData), or encrypted with a public key (envelopedData — rarely used).

The actual content container. Types include keyBag (unencrypted private key), pkcs8ShroudedKeyBag (encrypted private key — the most common), certBag (certificate), crlBag (CRL), and secretBag (arbitrary secret).

An HMAC computed over the AuthenticatedSafe using a key derived from the password. This verifies that the password is correct and that the file has not been tampered with. The MAC uses PBKDF2 or a similar key derivation function.

section.simpara: The typical PKCS#12 file for PDF signing contains:

listitem.simpara: One pkcs8ShroudedKeyBag — the private key, encrypted with a password-derived key
One or more certBag entries — the signer’s certificate and the intermediate CA certificate(s)

section.simpara: The private key encryption within the PKCS#12 uses a password-based encryption scheme. Historically, this was PBE-SHA1-3DES (also known as pbeWithSHAAnd3-KeyTripleDES-CBC), which is the default in many older tools and in Java’s PKCS#12 implementation prior to JDK 17.

caution.simpara: PBE-SHA1-3DES is weak by modern standards. SHA-1 for key derivation and Triple DES for encryption are both deprecated. OpenSSL 3.x defaults to AES-256-CBC with PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 for new PKCS#12 files, which is much stronger. However, some tools — including older versions of Adobe Acrobat — cannot read PKCS#12 files encrypted with the newer algorithms. If you generate a PKCS#12 with OpenSSL 3.x and Acrobat cannot open it, try re-exporting with the legacy flag: openssl pkcs12 -export -legacy -in cert.pem -inkey key.pem -out keystore.pfx.

section.title: Loading PKCS#12 in Java

callout.para: The password for the key entry is typically the same as the store password for PKCS#12 files, but the API allows them to be different. Some tools (notably keytool) set the key password equal to the store password when creating PKCS#12 files.

tip.simpara: When a PKCS#12 file fails to load with IOException: keystore password was incorrect, the error is misleading. It can mean (a) the password is actually wrong, (b) the file is corrupted, (c) the file uses an encryption algorithm not supported by your JDK, or (d) the file is not actually PKCS#12 format at all (perhaps it is PEM-encoded and was given a .p12 extension by mistake). Always check the file format first: openssl pkcs12 -info -in file.p12 -nokeys -passin pass:test 2>&1 | head -5 will quickly tell you whether the file is valid PKCS#12 and whether the password works.

chapter.title: PEM: The Text Format
chapter.simpara: PEM (Privacy-Enhanced Mail) is the most human-friendly key and certificate format — and, paradoxically, has nothing to do with email privacy anymore.

section.title: History
section.simpara: PEM was defined in RFCs 1421-1424 (1993) as a framework for email encryption. The framework itself was complex, poorly adopted, and quickly superseded by S/MIME and PGP. But the encoding format — Base64-encoded DER data bracketed by header and footer lines — became the de facto standard for exchanging cryptographic objects in text form.
The format is simple:
section.programlisting: -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- MIIDXTCCAkWgAwIBAgIJAJC1HiIAZAiUMA0GCSqGSIb3Qw0teleBAUAMEUxCzAJ BgNVBAYTAkFVMRMwEQYDVQQIDApTb21lLVN0YXRlMSEwHwYDVQQKDBhJbnRlcm5l ... (Base64-encoded DER data, typically 64 or 76 characters per line) ... dGhlIGNlcnRpZmljYXRl -----END CERTIFICATE-----
section.simpara: The header line identifies the content type. Common headers include:

entry.simpara: PKCS#1 RSA-specific private key (legacy OpenSSL format)

section.title: Multiple Objects in One File
section.simpara: A PEM file can contain multiple objects — this is one of its most useful features. A common pattern for PDF signing is to concatenate the certificate chain into a single file:
section.programlisting: -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- (end-entity certificate) -----END CERTIFICATE----- -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- (intermediate CA certificate) -----END CERTIFICATE----- -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- (root CA certificate -- optional) -----END CERTIFICATE-----
section.simpara: Java’s CertificateFactory.generateCertificates(InputStream) (note the plural) reads all certificates from such a file. BouncyCastle’s PEMParser reads any PEM objects sequentially.

section.title: Loading PEM with BouncyCastle

warning.simpara: The PEMParser class reads objects sequentially. If your PEM file contains a private key followed by certificates, you must call parser.readObject() once for the key and then again for each certificate. A common mistake is to read only the first object and close the parser, missing the certificates entirely.

chapter.title: DER: The Binary Encoding
chapter.simpara: DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) is the canonical binary encoding for ASN.1 data structures. Every cryptographic object you encounter — certificates, private keys, CMS structures, OCSP responses — has a DER encoding. PEM is just Base64-encoded DER with headers.

section.title: Relationship to PEM
section.simpara: The conversion between DER and PEM is trivial:

listitem.simpara: DER to PEM: Base64-encode the DER bytes, split into 64-character lines, wrap with the appropriate header/footer.
PEM to DER: Strip the header/footer lines, concatenate the remaining lines, Base64-decode.

section.simpara: DER files have no standard extension — you will see .der, .cer, .crt, and even .pem used for DER-encoded data (incorrectly, but it happens). The reliable way to distinguish DER from PEM is to check whether the file starts with -----BEGIN: if yes, PEM; if no, DER (or something else entirely).

section.title: When DER Matters
section.simpara: For PDF signatures, DER encoding is everywhere:

listitem.simpara: The CMS SignedData in the /Contents field is DER-encoded (then hex-encoded)
Each certificate in the CMS is DER-encoded
OCSP responses embedded in the DSS are DER-encoded
CRLs embedded in the DSS are DER-encoded

section.simpara: When you parse these structures with BouncyCastle, the library handles the DER encoding/decoding transparently. You only deal with DER directly when debugging at the byte level — dumping the hex content of /Contents and feeding it to openssl asn1parse, for example.
section.programlisting:
# Dump the ASN.1 structure of a DER-encoded CMS blob openssl asn1parse -inform DER -in signature.der
# Convert DER certificate to PEM openssl x509 -inform DER -in cert.der -outform PEM -out cert.pem
# Convert PEM certificate to DER openssl x509 -inform PEM -in cert.pem -outform DER -out cert.der

chapter.title: JKS: Java KeyStore (Legacy)
chapter.simpara: JKS (Java KeyStore) was Java’s original proprietary keystore format, introduced in JDK 1.2 (1998). It served the Java ecosystem for nearly two decades and is still encountered in legacy enterprise systems.

section.title: Why JKS Is Being Replaced
section.simpara: JKS has several problems:

listitem.simpara: Proprietary format: Only Java tools can read it. OpenSSL cannot parse JKS. Non-Java applications cannot use JKS keystores.

Weak encryption: The private key protection in JKS uses a custom, non-standard algorithm based on password-derived key material and a proprietary obfuscation scheme. It is not PBE by any recognized standard.
Case-insensitive aliases: JKS converts all aliases to lowercase, which can cause subtle bugs when migrating to PKCS#12 (which is case-sensitive).
No standard for interoperability: Migrating keys between JKS and non-Java systems always requires format conversion.

section.simpara: Since JDK 9, the default keystore type in Java is PKCS12, not JKS. The keytool utility creates PKCS#12 keystores by default. If you are starting a new project, always use PKCS#12.

tip.simpara: To check which keystore type you are dealing with: keytool -list -keystore mystore.jks will prompt for a password and display entries.
