If you see "Keystore type: jks" in the output, it is a JKS file. If you see "Keystore type: PKCS12", it is PKCS#12. To convert JKS to PKCS#12: keytool -importkeystore -srckeystore old.jks -destkeystore new.p12 -deststoretype PKCS12.

chapter.title: PKCS#8: Private Key Format
chapter.simpara: PKCS#8 (RFC 5958, originally RSA PKCS#8) is the standard format for private keys, independent of the key algorithm. When you extract a private key from a PKCS#12 file in Java (keyStore.getKey()), the key’s getFormat() method returns "PKCS#8" — that is the encoding format of the key bytes.

section.title: Unencrypted vs Encrypted
section.simpara: PKCS#8 defines two structures:

listitem.simpara: PrivateKeyInfo (unencrypted): Contains the algorithm identifier and the raw private key data. Stored in PEM files with the header -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----.
EncryptedPrivateKeyInfo: The PrivateKeyInfo wrapped in a password-based encryption layer. Stored in PEM files with the header -----BEGIN ENCRYPTED PRIVATE KEY-----.

section.simpara: The privateKey octet string contains the algorithm-specific key data — an RSA private key (PKCS#1 structure) for RSA, or an EC private key structure for ECDSA. PKCS#8 wraps these algorithm-specific formats in a uniform container.

note.simpara: PKCS#8 is the format that the JCA uses internally. When you call privateKey.getEncoded(), the returned bytes are PKCS#8 PrivateKeyInfo. When you call publicKey.getEncoded(), the returned bytes are SubjectPublicKeyInfo (the X.509 public key format, defined in RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile). These format names — PKCS#8 for private keys, X.509 for public keys — appear in Java’s Key.getFormat() and in PEM headers.

chapter.title: PKCS#1: RSA-Specific Key Format
chapter.simpara: PKCS#1 (RFC 8017) defines the RSA-specific private key structure:
chapter.programlisting: RSAPrivateKey ::= SEQUENCE { version Version, modulus INTEGER, -- n publicExponent INTEGER, -- e privateExponent INTEGER, -- d prime1 INTEGER, -- p prime2 INTEGER, -- q exponent1 INTEGER, -- d mod (p-1) exponent2 INTEGER, -- d mod (q-1) coefficient INTEGER -- (inverse of q) mod p }
chapter.simpara: This is the format stored inside a PKCS#8 PrivateKeyInfo when the algorithm is RSA. It is also the format used in PEM files with the header -----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY----- — OpenSSL’s "traditional" format.
The distinction between PKCS#1 and PKCS#8 confuses many developers:

listitem.simpara: -----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY----- is PKCS#1 (RSA-specific, no algorithm identifier wrapper)
-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY----- is PKCS#8 (algorithm-agnostic, contains an algorithm identifier)

chapter.simpara: Both contain the same RSA key data. PKCS#8 simply wraps the PKCS#1 structure with an additional layer that identifies the algorithm. If you try to load a PKCS#1 PEM file using code that expects PKCS#8, you will get a parsing error — and vice versa.

important.simpara: OpenSSL’s default output format changed between versions. OpenSSL 1.x used PKCS#1 (-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----) by default for RSA keys. OpenSSL 3.x uses PKCS#8 (-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----) by default. If you are following a tutorial written for an older OpenSSL version, the key files it produces may be in a different format than your current OpenSSL generates. Always check the PEM header.

chapter.title: Format Conversion Reference
chapter.simpara: This matrix shows how to convert between formats using openssl and keytool. Every PDF signing project eventually needs at least one of these conversions.

table.title: Key and certificate format conversion commands

entry.simpara: openssl pkcs12 -in store.p12 -nocerts -out key.pem (key) then openssl pkcs12 -in store.p12 -nokeys -out cert.pem (cert)

simpara.literal: openssl x509 -inform DER -in cert.der -outform PEM -out cert.pem

tip.simpara: The -nodes flag in the OpenSSL PKCS#12 export command means "no DES" — the private key will not be encrypted in the output PEM file. This is convenient for testing but dangerous in production. Never leave unencrypted private keys on disk in production environments. Use -aes256 instead of -nodes to encrypt the output private key with AES-256.

chapter.title: HSM Integration: When Keys Cannot Leave
chapter.simpara: A Hardware Security Module (HSM) is a dedicated hardware device that generates, stores, and uses cryptographic keys internally. The private key never leaves the HSM — all signing operations happen inside the device. This provides the strongest possible key protection: even if the server is completely compromised, the attacker cannot extract the private key.

section.title: PKCS#11
section.simpara: The standard interface for HSM access is PKCS#11 (also called Cryptoki), defined by OASIS (originally by RSA Laboratories). PKCS#11 defines a C API for cryptographic operations: key generation, signing, encryption, etc. The HSM vendor provides a shared library (.so on Linux, .dll on Windows) that implements the PKCS#11 interface.
Java accesses PKCS#11 via the SunPKCS11 provider — a JCA provider that bridges the Java cryptographic API to a PKCS#11 library.

callout.para: The config file specifies the PKCS#11 library path, slot number, and other HSM-specific parameters.
The PIN authenticates to the HSM token. Unlike PKCS#12 passwords, HSM PINs are typically short numeric codes enforced by the hardware.

section.title: Why This Matters for PDF Signing
section.simpara: When you use PDFBox’s SignatureInterface.sign() with an HSM-backed key, the signing operation is transparently delegated to the HSM. The PrivateKey object returned by ks.getKey() is a handle, not the actual key material. The JCA provider intercepts the signing call, forwards it to the HSM via PKCS#11, and returns the result. Your signing code does not change — the same CMSSignedDataGenerator code from Chapter 2 works with HSM keys and software keys identically.
This is the elegance of the JCA provider model: the signing code is agnostic to key storage. Whether the key lives in a PKCS#12 file, a JKS keystore, or an HSM, the API is the same.

important.simpara: For eIDAS qualified electronic signatures, the private key must be stored in a Qualified Signature Creation Device (QSCD) — an HSM or smart card that has been certified to Common Criteria EAL 4+ or equivalent. Software keystores (PKCS#12, JKS) are not QSCDs. If you are building a signing service that needs to produce qualified electronic signatures, HSM integration is not optional — it is a legal requirement under Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article 29.

chapter.title: Why Format Conversion Goes Wrong
chapter.simpara: Format conversions are a perennial source of frustration. Here are the most common failure modes:

section.title: Password Encoding Mismatch
section.simpara: PKCS#12 passwords can be encoded as either UTF-8 or UTF-16BE (BMPString in ASN.1 terminology) for the key derivation function. The PKCS#12 standard specifies BMPString, but some implementations use UTF-8.

If you create a PKCS#12 with a password containing non-ASCII characters (umlauts, accented characters, CJK characters) using OpenSSL, and then try to open it in Java, the key derivation may produce a different key because the two implementations encode the password differently.
The fix: use only ASCII characters in PKCS#12 passwords. This is not a theoretical recommendation — it is a practical necessity in cross-platform environments.

section.title: OpenSSL Legacy Format vs PKCS#8
section.simpara: As mentioned above, OpenSSL’s default private key format changed between major versions. A script that worked with OpenSSL 1.1.1 may produce keys that your PKCS#8-expecting Java code cannot parse when run with OpenSSL 3.x, or vice versa.
Always check the PEM header and convert as needed.

section.title: Certificate Chain Order in PKCS#12
section.simpara: The PKCS#12 specification does not mandate a specific order for certificates in the file. Some tools store the end-entity certificate first, followed by intermediates. Others store them in reverse order. Java’s KeyStore.getCertificateChain() returns them in order (end-entity first), regardless of how they are stored in the file — but only if the certificates are associated with the correct key alias.

If the chain is missing intermediates (because they were not included when the PKCS#12 was created), getCertificateChain() returns only the end-entity certificate. This produces the classic "chain not trusted" error at signing or validation time.

tip.simpara: When creating a PKCS#12 for PDF signing, always include the full chain (minus the root). With OpenSSL: openssl pkcs12 -export -in signer.pem -inkey signer-key.pem -certfile intermediate-chain.pem -out signing.p12. The -certfile flag adds the intermediate certificates. Without it, only the end-entity certificate is included.

section.title: DER vs PEM Confusion
section.simpara: Renaming a file’s extension does not change its format. A file named cert.pem that is actually DER-encoded will cause a Base64 decode error. A file named cert.der that is actually PEM-encoded will cause an ASN.1 parse error.
To reliably detect the format:
section.programlisting:
# If the file starts with "-----BEGIN", it is PEM head -1 cert.pem
# Try parsing as DER; if it fails, it is probably PEM (or corrupted) openssl x509 -inform DER -in cert.der -noout 2>&1
# The file command on Linux/macOS can often identify the format file cert.pem

chapter.title: Summary
chapter.simpara: Key material comes in several formats, each with its own history, strengths, and failure modes:

listitem.simpara: PKCS#12 is the universal container for private keys and certificate chains. Use it for PDF signing keystores.

PEM is the human-readable, Base64-encoded format for individual certificates, keys, and other cryptographic objects. Use it for interchange with OpenSSL and when you need to inspect contents visually.
DER is the binary ASN.1 encoding underlying everything. You encounter it inside PDF signatures (the CMS blob in /Contents), in OCSP responses, and in CRLs.
JKS is Java’s legacy keystore format. Migrate to PKCS#12.
PKCS#8 is the standard private key format (algorithm-agnostic). Java uses it internally.
PKCS#1 is the RSA-specific key format. Watch for the PEM header to distinguish it from PKCS#8.

chapter.simpara: For HSM-backed signing (mandatory for eIDAS qualified signatures), keys are accessed via PKCS#11 and never leave the hardware. The JCA provider model makes this transparent to your signing code.

Format conversion problems — password encoding, legacy vs modern defaults, missing chain certificates, DER/PEM confusion — are among the most common issues in PDF signing deployments. The conversion reference table in this chapter and a systematic approach to checking file formats will save you hours of debugging.

In the next chapter, we examine the CMS SignedData structure — the envelope that carries the cryptographic signature, the certificate chain, and the signed attributes from the signer to the validator, all encoded inside the PDF’s /Contents hex string.

partintro.simpara: Every PDF digital signature carries its cryptographic payload inside a CMS SignedData structure — a binary envelope defined by RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) that bundles the signature value, the signer’s certificate chain, the digest algorithm, and a set of signed metadata attributes into a single DER-encoded blob. This chapter dissects that envelope layer by layer, from the outermost ContentInfo wrapper down to the individual signed attributes, with annotated ASN.1 dumps and BouncyCastle 1.80 code on Java 21. If you understand CMS SignedData, you understand the heart of what lives inside the /Contents hex string of every signed PDF.
If you have parsed CMS structures before, skip to for the signed attributes encoding trap that has caused more validation failures than any other single issue. If you want code, jump to .

chapter.title: From PKCS#7 to CMS: A History
chapter.simpara: The Cryptographic Message Syntax did not spring into existence fully formed. It evolved over sixteen years through three distinct phases, each driven by different organizations and different needs. Understanding this lineage matters because the older formats are still embedded in millions of signed PDFs, and the naming confusion between "PKCS#7" and "CMS" persists in specifications, code comments, and error messages to this day.

section.title: PKCS#7: RSA Laboratories, 1993
section.simpara: RSA Laboratories published PKCS#7 (Public-Key Cryptography Standard #7) in November 1993 as a general-purpose format for signed and encrypted data. The full title was "Cryptographic Message Syntax Standard," which is ironic because it was not yet the CMS we know today — it was a proprietary RSA specification, not an IETF standard.
PKCS#7 version 1.5 defined the core structure that survives almost unchanged:

listitem.simpara: A ContentInfo wrapper with an OID identifying the content type
A SignedData type containing digest algorithms, signer information, certificates, and CRLs
A SignerInfo type containing the actual signature and per-signer metadata
Support for both attached (encapsulated) and detached signatures

section.simpara: The design was influenced by the PEM (Privacy-Enhanced Mail) framework from RFCs 1421-1424 and by the emerging S/MIME work at RSA. The goal was a format that could carry signed data for email, file signing, code signing, and any other context where a digital signature needed to travel alongside or separately from its content.

note.simpara: The "PKCS#7" name persists everywhere. The PDF specification uses adbe.pkcs7.detached and adbe.pkcs7.sha1 as SubFilter values. Java’s KeyStore.getInstance("PKCS7") exists. OpenSSL has pkcs7 commands. In all of these contexts, the actual format in use is CMS as defined by RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS), not the original 1993 PKCS#7. The names are legacy; the structures have evolved.

section.title: S/MIME and RFC 2315, 1998
section.simpara: The S/MIME (Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions) working group at the IETF took PKCS#7 and brought it into the standards process. RFC 2315 (March 1998) republished PKCS#7 v1.5 essentially verbatim as an informational RFC, giving it a stable, citable reference in the IETF document series.

But the S/MIME group needed enhancements that RSA’s original design did not provide: better algorithm agility, support for attribute certificates, and a cleaner separation between the content being signed and the signature metadata. This work produced the first true CMS specification.

section.title: CMS: RFC 2630 (1999) and RFC 3852 (2004)
section.simpara: RFC 2630 (June 1999) was the first IETF-standards-track CMS specification. It renamed the format from "PKCS#7" to "Cryptographic Message Syntax" and introduced several changes:

listitem.simpara: The SignedData version number advanced from 1 to 3 (with version selection rules based on which optional fields are present)
EncapsulatedContentInfo replaced the old ContentInfo for the inner content, allowing non-data content types
The SignerIdentifier was formalized with two choices: issuerAndSerialNumber (the classic) and subjectKeyIdentifier (new)
Attribute certificates were added to the certificates field as an option alongside X.509 certificates
The digestAlgorithms SET was required to be consistent with the algorithms actually used in SignerInfo entries

section.simpara: RFC 3852 (July 2004) was a revision that clarified ambiguities and tightened requirements, particularly around the encoding of signed attributes.

section.title: RFC 5652: The Current Standard (2009)
section.simpara: RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) (September 2009) is the current and authoritative CMS specification. It obsoletes RFC 3852 and is the normative reference used by ISO 32000-2:2020, the PAdES standards (ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04)), and BouncyCastle.

The changes from RFC 3852 were modest — mostly editorial clarifications and alignment with updated ASN.1 modules. The core structures are stable and have not changed since 1999. When we refer to "CMS" in this book, we mean RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) unless otherwise noted.

entry.simpara: RSA Laboratories' original specification. Defined SignedData, EnvelopedData, and the ContentInfo wrapper. Still referenced by name in PDF SubFilter values.
PKCS#7 republished as an informational RFC. No technical changes, but gave the format an IETF-citable reference.
First standards-track CMS. Introduced version 3 SignedData, SubjectKeyIdentifier as an alternative SignerIdentifier, and formalized attribute handling.
Clarified signed attributes encoding requirements. Tightened consistency rules for digestAlgorithms SET.
Current standard. Obsoletes RFC 3852. The normative reference for all modern PDF signature work.

chapter.title: Why PDF Uses CMS
chapter.simpara: When Adobe added digital signatures to PDF 1.3 in 1999, they needed a container format for the cryptographic signature. They chose PKCS#7 (which was becoming CMS at exactly the same time) for pragmatic reasons:

listitem.simpara: It was the only real option. In 1999, the choices for cryptographic message formats were PKCS#7/CMS and PGP. PKCS#7/CMS was the enterprise and government standard; PGP was associated with personal email encryption. For a document format targeting business use, PKCS#7/CMS was the obvious choice.

It bundles everything needed for validation. A CMS SignedData structure can carry the signature value, the signer’s certificate, intermediate CA certificates, CRLs, and timestamp tokens — everything a validator needs without making network requests. This self-contained property is critical for PDF, where a signed document might be validated years after signing, on an air-gapped system with no network access.

It supports detached signatures natively. PDF needs the signed content (the byte range) to live in the PDF file, not inside the CMS structure. CMS’s detached signature mode was designed for exactly this use case.

chapter.simpara: The practical consequence is that the /Contents value in every PDF signature dictionary is a hex-encoded, DER-encoded CMS SignedData structure. When ISO 32000-2:2020 says the /Contents field contains "the DER-encoded PKCS#7 binary data object," it means a CMS SignedData as defined by RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS). The terms are used interchangeably in the spec, which is historically understandable but terminologically sloppy.

chapter.title: The ContentInfo Wrapper
chapter.simpara: Every CMS structure is wrapped in a ContentInfo — the outermost ASN.1 envelope that identifies what type of CMS object is inside.
For a PDF signature, the contentType is always:

This OID lives under the RSA Security arc (1.2.840.113549) in the PKCS#7 namespace (.1.7), with .2 identifying SignedData specifically. The content field contains the SignedData structure itself, tagged with EXPLICIT [0].

entry.simpara: Used as the eContentType inside SignedData to indicate that the signed content is arbitrary data (the PDF byte range)
The outermost ContentInfo type for all PDF signatures
Not used in PDF signatures (used for encryption)
Used as the eContentType for RFC 3161 timestamp tokens embedded in PDF signatures

important.simpara: The OID 1.2.840.113549.1.7.2 is the first thing a CMS parser encounters when reading a PDF signature. If this OID is missing or wrong, the entire structure is unparsable. When you are debugging a broken signature by examining raw hex, look for the DER encoding of this OID near the beginning of the /Contents value: 06 09 2A 86 48 86 F7 0D 01 07 02. Those eleven bytes (tag 06, length 09, then the nine OID bytes) are the signature of a CMS SignedData structure.

chapter.title: The SignedData Structure
chapter.simpara: Inside the ContentInfo wrapper, the SignedData structure contains everything related to the signature:
chapter.programlisting: SignedData ::= SEQUENCE { version CMSVersion, digestAlgorithms DigestAlgorithmIdentifiers, encapContentInfo EncapsulatedContentInfo, certificates [0] IMPLICIT CertificateSet OPTIONAL, crls [1] IMPLICIT RevocationInfoChoices OPTIONAL, signerInfos SignerInfos }

callout.para: Version number: 1, 3, 4, or 5, depending on which optional fields are present. For PDF signatures with X.509 certificates and issuerAndSerialNumber in SignerInfo, this is typically 1.
SET OF digest algorithm identifiers. Must include every algorithm used by any SignerInfo in this structure. For a single-signer PDF, this is a set with one element (e.g., SHA-256).
The content that was signed. For PDF detached signatures, the eContent field is absent — the content lives in the PDF file itself.
The certificate collection. Typically contains the signer’s end-entity certificate plus all intermediate CA certificates needed to build the chain to a trust anchor.

CRLs or OCSP responses for revocation checking. Rarely populated in basic PDF signatures; more common in PAdES-B-LT and PAdES-B-LTA profiles where revocation data is embedded for long-term validation.
SET OF SignerInfo structures — one per signer. PDF signatures almost always have exactly one SignerInfo.

section.title: Version Number Rules
section.simpara: The version field is not arbitrary — RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5.1 specifies strict rules:

table.title: SignedData version selection (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5.1)

entry.simpara: No attribute certificates in certificates, no OtherRevocationInfoFormat in crls, no version-3 SignerInfo, and the encapContentInfo.eContentType is id-data
The encapContentInfo.eContentType is other than id-data (e.g., id-ct-TSTInfo for a timestamp token), OR the structure contains SignerInfo version 3
Attribute certificates with version 2 are present in certificates

section.simpara: For the vast majority of PDF signatures, the version is 1. Timestamp tokens embedded as unsigned attributes use version 3 because their inner eContentType is id-ct-TSTInfo rather than id-data.

warning.simpara: Some older signing libraries (notably early versions of iText before version 5.3) hardcoded the version to 1 regardless of the content type. This produced technically non-compliant CMS structures that happened to validate in most tools but failed strict validators like the EU’s DSS library. If you encounter a CMS blob with version 1 but a non-id-data content type, that is a bug in the signing library, not a feature.

section.title: The digestAlgorithms SET
section.simpara: This is a SET OF AlgorithmIdentifier, declared at the top level of SignedData to allow a parser to identify all digest algorithms in use before reading any SignerInfo entries. The intent is optimization: a validator reading a large detached signature can start computing digests in parallel before it reaches the SignerInfo structures.
For PDF signatures with a single signer using SHA-256:
section.programlisting: digestAlgorithms: SET (1 element) SEQUENCE OID: 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1 (SHA-256) NULL (parameters, absent for SHA-2)

tip.simpara: The NULL parameters field for SHA-2 algorithms is a persistent source of interoperability issues. RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) says the parameters "SHOULD be omitted" for SHA-256 and related algorithms, but some older implementations include an explicit NULL (tag 05 00), and some validators reject structures where it is absent. BouncyCastle 1.80 includes the explicit NULL by default, which is the safe choice for maximum compatibility.

section.title: EncapsulatedContentInfo: Detached vs Encapsulated
section.simpara: This is where detached and encapsulated signatures diverge:

listitem.simpara: Detached (PDF standard): eContent is absent. The signed content lives outside the CMS structure — in the PDF file, specified by the /ByteRange array. The eContentType is id-data (1.2.840.113549.1.7.1).
Encapsulated: eContent is present and contains the signed data. This mode is used in S/MIME email signatures where the message body is embedded in the CMS structure.

section.simpara: For the SubFilter adbe.pkcs7.sha1 (deprecated), the CMS uses a hybrid approach: the eContent field contains the SHA-1 hash of the byte range (not the byte range itself), making it technically an encapsulated signature over a hash rather than a detached signature over the content. This design was misguided and is one of the reasons adbe.pkcs7.sha1 was deprecated.

caution.simpara: If you are building a PDF signing implementation and you accidentally pass true for the encapsulate parameter in CMSSignedDataGenerator.generate(), the entire byte range content will be embedded inside the CMS structure, inside the /Contents hex string, inside the PDF. The result is a signature that is dramatically larger than necessary (potentially megabytes), wastes space, and will be rejected by validators that expect a detached signature when the SubFilter is adbe.pkcs7.detached or ETSI.CAdES.detached.

section.title: The certificates Field
section.simpara: The certificates field is an IMPLICIT SET that can contain:

listitem.simpara: X.509 certificates (Certificate type from RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile)
Attribute certificates (v1 or v2) — rarely used in PDF signatures
Other certificate formats — essentially never used

section.simpara: For PDF signatures, this field should contain at a minimum the signer’s end-entity certificate and all intermediate CA certificates needed to chain to a trust anchor. The root CA certificate is traditionally not included because the validator must already trust it — including it adds bytes without adding trust.

The ordering of certificates in this SET is undefined by RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS). A validator must be prepared to receive certificates in any order and build the chain by matching issuer/subject Distinguished Names and Authority/Subject Key Identifiers.

chapter.title: SignerInfo: The Core of the Signature
chapter.simpara: Each SignerInfo structure represents one signer’s contribution to the SignedData:
chapter.programlisting: SignerInfo ::= SEQUENCE { version CMSVersion, sid SignerIdentifier, digestAlgorithm DigestAlgorithmIdentifier, signedAttrs [0] IMPLICIT SignedAttributes OPTIONAL, signatureAlgorithm SignatureAlgorithmIdentifier, signature SignatureValue, unsignedAttrs [1] IMPLICIT UnsignedAttributes OPTIONAL }

callout.para: Version 1 if sid is issuerAndSerialNumber, version 3 if sid is subjectKeyIdentifier.
Identifies which certificate belongs to this signer.
The hash algorithm used to compute the message digest (e.g., SHA-256).
Attributes that are included in the signature computation — critical for PDF signatures.
The signature algorithm (e.g., SHA-256 with RSA, or ECDSA with SHA-256).
The raw signature bytes.
Attributes added after signing — used for timestamps, counter-signatures.

section.title: SignerIdentifier: Finding the Signer’s Certificate
section.simpara: The issuerAndSerialNumber form is dominant in PDF signatures. It contains the issuer DN and serial number of the signer’s certificate — enough to uniquely identify the certificate within the certificates SET.
The subjectKeyIdentifier form uses the Subject Key Identifier extension from the certificate. It is more compact but less commonly used because older validators do not always support it.

section.title: Signed Attributes: What Gets Signed
section.simpara: When signed attributes are present (and for PDF signatures, they must be present per ISO 32000-2:2020), the signature is computed over the DER encoding of the signed attributes — not over the content itself. The content’s digest is included as a signed attribute, creating an indirect binding:
section.screen: content --(hash)--> messageDigest attribute | signed attributes --(sign)--> signature value
section.simpara: The mandatory signed attributes for PDF signatures are:

table.title: Required signed attributes in PDF CMS signatures

entry.simpara: Must be id-data (1.2.840.113549.1.7.1). Identifies the type of content that was signed. Required by RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) whenever signed attributes are present.

The hash of the content (the PDF byte range). This is the attribute that binds the CMS signature to the PDF document bytes. The validator recomputes this hash and compares it against the value in this attribute.
The time the signer claims to have signed. This is self-asserted and not cryptographically verified — use a timestamp authority for trusted time (see unsignedAttrs).

Required by PAdES (ETSI EN 319 122-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04)). Contains a hash of the signer’s certificate, binding the signature to a specific certificate and preventing certificate substitution attacks. Without this attribute, an attacker could replace the signer’s certificate with a different certificate that has the same public key.

note.simpara: The signingTime attribute is optional per RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) but effectively mandatory in practice. Every PDF viewer displays the signing time, and a signature without one looks suspicious to users. However, the signing time is not cryptographically trustworthy — the signer controls the clock. For legally binding timestamps, use an RFC 3161 timestamp token in the unsigned attributes (Chapter 18).

section.title: The ESS-signing-certificate-v2 Attribute
section.simpara: This attribute deserves special attention because it is the dividing line between a basic CMS signature and a CAdES-compliant signature — and therefore between a signature that merely works and one that meets European regulatory requirements.
The structure contains:

The certHash is a hash of the signer’s DER-encoded certificate. A validator computes this hash independently and compares it against the attribute value, ensuring that the certificate in the CMS structure is the one the signer intended to use.

Without this attribute, a subtle attack is possible: if two certificates share the same public key (which can happen if a certificate is re-issued with different extensions or validity periods), an attacker could swap the certificate in the CMS structure without invalidating the signature. The ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute prevents this by binding the signature to the exact certificate, not just the public key.

important.simpara: PAdES baseline profiles (PAdES-B-B and above) require ESS-signing-certificate-v2. A PDF signature that uses the SubFilter ETSI.CAdES.detached but lacks this attribute is non-compliant with ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04). The EU’s DSS validation library will flag it as a format failure. If you are implementing PDF signing and need European compliance, this attribute is not optional.

chapter.title: The Signed Attributes DER Encoding Trap
chapter.simpara: This section describes the single most common cause of CMS signature validation failures. It has tripped up every signing library author at least once, and it will trip you up too if you are building or debugging a CMS implementation at the byte level.

section.title: The Problem
section.simpara: RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5.4 specifies how signed attributes are encoded for signature computation:

blockquote.simpara: The result of the message digest calculation process depends on whether the signedAttrs field is present. When the field is present, […​] the complete DER encoding of the SignedAttrs value contained in the signedAttrs field is [used]. […​] the IMPLICIT [0] tag in the signedAttrs is not used for the DER encoding, rather an EXPLICIT SET OF tag is used.

section.simpara: Here is what this means in practice:

listitem.simpara: In the SignerInfo structure, the signedAttrs field is tagged [0] IMPLICIT, meaning the ASN.1 tag byte is A0 (context class, constructed, tag 0).

But when computing the signature over the signed attributes, the tag must be replaced with 31 (UNIVERSAL SET OF) — the tag that would be used if the attributes were a standalone SET OF, not an implicitly tagged field.
The rest of the encoding (length bytes and content) remains identical.

section.simpara: This means the bytes that are signed are not the bytes that appear in the CMS structure. You must re-tag the signed attributes before computing or verifying the signature.

section.title: Why This Exists
section.simpara: The reason is rooted in ASN.1 encoding theory. The signedAttrs field uses IMPLICIT tagging to save one byte in the encoding (implicit tagging replaces the original tag; explicit tagging adds a wrapper). But the signature must be computed over the canonical encoding of the attributes as a SET OF, not over their implicitly tagged representation within the SignerInfo. The two encodings differ only in the first byte (A0 vs 31), but that single byte difference is enough to invalidate the signature.

warning.simpara: If you are computing a CMS signature manually (perhaps because you are building a remote signing integration where the hash is computed locally and the signature is applied remotely), you must re-tag the signed attributes before hashing them. Use tag 31 (SET OF), not A0 (IMPLICIT [0]). BouncyCastle handles this correctly in its CMSSignedDataGenerator, but if you are working at a lower level — constructing CMS structures from raw ASN.1 objects, or implementing a signature interface for an HSM — this is the byte that will break you.

tip.simpara: When debugging a signature that fails cryptographic verification but the messageDigest attribute matches the content hash, the re-tagging issue is the first thing to check. Extract the signed attributes bytes from the CMS structure, verify that the first byte is 31 (not A0) when fed into the signature verification, and compare the hash of those bytes against the decrypted signature value.

chapter.title: Unsigned Attributes: Post-Signature Additions
chapter.simpara: Unsigned attributes are added to the SignerInfo after the signature has been computed. They are not covered by the signature and can be added or modified without invalidating it. This is by design — the primary use case is adding information that is not available at signing time.
The most important unsigned attributes for PDF signatures:

table.title: Common unsigned attributes in PDF CMS signatures

entry.simpara: An RFC 3161 timestamp token over the signature value. Provides a trusted, third-party assertion of the time the signature existed. This is the mechanism for timestamping described in Chapter 18.
A counter-signature — another signer’s signature over the original signature value. Rarely used in PDF signatures directly; timestamps are the modern replacement.

chapter.simpara: The timestamp token in the signature-time-stamp attribute is itself a CMS SignedData structure, with its eContentType set to id-ct-TSTInfo (1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.1.4). This creates a nested CMS-within-CMS structure: the outer SignedData is the PDF signature, and the inner SignedData (inside an unsigned attribute of the outer) is the timestamp.

chapter.title: CAdES: CMS Advanced Electronic Signatures
chapter.simpara: The basic CMS SignedData structure defined by RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) is cryptographically complete but lacks features needed for European regulatory compliance. CAdES (CMS Advanced Electronic Signatures), defined in ETSI EN 319 122-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04), extends CMS with additional signed attributes and profile rules.
The key additions CAdES makes to CMS for PDF signing:

listitem.simpara: ESS-signing-certificate-v2 (discussed above) — mandatory in all CAdES profiles, binds the signature to a specific certificate.

Signature policy identifier — optional attribute that references a specific signature policy (a set of rules defining the meaning and validation requirements of the signature). Used in some government contexts.
Commitment type indication — optional attribute declaring what the signer intends by signing (proof of origin, proof of receipt, proof of approval, etc.).
Signer location — optional attribute with the signer’s claimed location at signing time.

chapter.simpara: The relationship between CMS, CAdES, and PAdES is hierarchical:
chapter.screen: CMS (RFC 5652) | +-- CAdES (ETSI EN 319 122) = CMS + mandatory signed attributes | +-- PAdES (ETSI EN 319 142) = CAdES embedded in PDF per ISO 32000-2
chapter.simpara: A PAdES signature is a CAdES signature is a CMS signature. Each layer adds constraints but does not remove any. A basic adbe.pkcs7.detached signature is CMS but not necessarily CAdES. An ETSI.CAdES.detached signature is CMS, CAdES, and PAdES.

chapter.title: Old PKCS#7 vs Modern CMS: What Changed
chapter.simpara: If you work with PDF signatures long enough, you will encounter CMS structures produced by older libraries that do not quite match what RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) specifies. The most common issues trace back to libraries that implemented the original PKCS#7 v1.5 rather than CMS, or that implemented an intermediate version with bugs that were never fixed because "it worked in Acrobat."

table.title: Differences between legacy PKCS#7 and modern CMS in the wild

entry.simpara: Version depends on which optional fields are present (1, 3, 4, or 5)
May list algorithms not actually used by any SignerInfo
Must be consistent with SignerInfo entries
Some libraries used BER (not DER) encoding for the SET OF, causing non-deterministic byte ordering
Must use DER, which enforces a specific ordering of SET elements
No ordering defined; validators must handle any order
SHA-2 algorithms SHOULD omit parameters, but explicit NULL is tolerated

warning.simpara: Early versions of iText (before 5.3.x) and some older .NET signing libraries produced CMS structures with BER-encoded signed attributes. BER allows multiple valid encodings for the same data — specifically, SET OF elements may appear in any order. DER requires a specific ordering (by encoded value). If the signing library used one ordering and the validator expects another, the hash of the signed attributes will not match and the signature will fail cryptographic verification. The signature is not actually broken — it was computed over BER-encoded bytes, and if you verify against the same bytes, it passes. But strict DER-only validators will reject it. This is the most common "it works in Acrobat but fails in DSS" scenario.

chapter.title: Java: Parsing CMS SignedData
chapter.simpara: Here is how to parse a CMS SignedData structure extracted from a PDF signature’s /Contents field, using BouncyCastle 1.80:

callout.para: The CMSSignedData constructor parses the DER bytes into an in-memory object graph.
PDF signatures have exactly one SignerInfo. Multiple signers use multiple signature dictionaries, not multiple SignerInfos in one CMS structure.
The getSID() method returns a selector that matches against the SignerIdentifier — either issuerAndSerialNumber or subjectKeyIdentifier.
The raw signature value. For RSA, this is the encrypted hash. For ECDSA, this is the DER-encoded (r, s) pair.

chapter.title: Java: Building CMS SignedData
chapter.simpara: Creating a CMS SignedData for PDF signing is the reverse of parsing. The following example uses CMSSignedDataGenerator with explicit control over the signed attributes:

callout.para: JcaSignerInfoGeneratorBuilder.build() automatically adds the standard signed attributes (contentType, messageDigest, signingTime). For CAdES compliance, you need to add ESS-signing-certificate-v2 explicitly using a CMSAttributeTableGenerator.
The false parameter is critical: detached signature. The byte range content is hashed but not embedded in the CMS structure.

chapter.title: Annotated ASN.1 Dump of a Real PDF Signature
chapter.simpara: The following is the output of openssl asn1parse run against the DER-decoded /Contents of an actual PDF signature. Each line is annotated with what it represents in the CMS structure.
chapter.programlisting: 0:d=0 hl=4 l=2417 cons: SEQUENCE -- ContentInfo 4:d=1 hl=2 l= 9 prim: OBJECT :pkcs7-signedData -- OID 1.2.840.113549.1.7.2 15:d=1 hl=4 l=2402 cons: cont [ 0 ] -- EXPLICIT [0] content 19:d=2 hl=4 l=2398 cons: SEQUENCE -- SignedData 23:d=3 hl=2 l= 1 prim: INTEGER :01 -- version = 1 26:d=3 hl=2 l= 15 cons: SET -- digestAlgorithms 28:d=4 hl=2 l= 13 cons: SEQUENCE 30:d=5 hl=2 l= 9 prim: OBJECT :sha256 -- OID 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1 41:d=5 hl=2 l= 0 prim: NULL -- parameters (explicit NULL) 43:d=3 hl=2 l= 11 cons: SEQUENCE -- encapContentInfo 45:d=4 hl=2 l= 9 prim: OBJECT :pkcs7-data -- eContentType = id-data -- OID 1.2.840.113549.1.7.1 -- (eContent ABSENT = detached) 56:d=3 hl=4 l=1735 cons: cont [ 0 ] -- certificates [0] IMPLICIT 60:d=4 hl=4 l= 872 cons: SEQUENCE -- Certificate #1 (end-entity) 936:d=4 hl=4 l= 855 cons: SEQUENCE -- Certificate #2 (intermediate CA) 1795:d=3 hl=4 l= 624 cons: SET -- signerInfos 1799:d=4 hl=4 l= 620 cons: SEQUENCE -- SignerInfo 1803:d=5 hl=2 l= 1 prim: INTEGER :01 -- version = 1 1806:d=5 hl=3 l= 141 cons: SEQUENCE -- sid = issuerAndSerialNumber 1809:d=6 hl=3 l= 118 cons: SEQUENCE -- issuer DN 1930:d=6 hl=2 l= 19 prim: INTEGER :... -- serialNumber 1951:d=5 hl=2 l= 13 cons: SEQUENCE -- digestAlgorithm 1953:d=6 hl=2 l= 9 prim: OBJECT :sha256 -- SHA-256 1964:d=6 hl=2 l= 0 prim: NULL 1966:d=5 hl=3 l= 189 cons: cont [ 0 ] -- signedAttrs [0] IMPLICIT 1969:d=6 hl=2 l= 24 cons: SEQUENCE -- contentType attribute 1995:d=6 hl=2 l= 47 cons: SEQUENCE -- messageDigest attribute 2044:d=6 hl=2 l= 28 cons: SEQUENCE -- signingTime attribute 2074:d=6 hl=2 l= 79 cons: SEQUENCE -- ESS-signing-certificate-v2 2155:d=5 hl=2 l= 13 cons: SEQUENCE -- signatureAlgorithm 2157:d=6 hl=2 l= 9 prim: OBJECT :sha256WithRSAEncryption -- OID 1.2.840.113549.1.1.11 2168:d=6 hl=2 l= 0 prim: NULL 2170:d=5 hl=4 l= 256 prim: OCTET STRING -- signature (256 bytes = RSA-2048) 2430:d=5 hl=2 l= 7 cons: cont [ 1 ] -- unsignedAttrs [1] IMPLICIT -- (timestamp token would be here)

note.simpara: The offsets in this dump are from the start of the DER-encoded CMS blob, not from the start of the PDF file. To get the absolute file offset, add the starting offset of the /Contents hex string (as specified by /ByteRange) plus the hex-to-binary decoding offset. When debugging with a hex editor, remember that the /Contents value is hex-encoded: each byte in the ASN.1 dump corresponds to two hex characters in the PDF file.

chapter.title: Summary
chapter.simpara: CMS SignedData is the envelope that carries every PDF digital signature. Its lineage stretches from RSA’s PKCS#7 in 1993 through the S/MIME working group to the current RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) standard. The core design has been stable for over twenty-five years, which is both its strength (mature, well-understood, universally supported) and its burden (legacy quirks from 1993 are still with us).
The key points for PDF signature work:

listitem.simpara: ContentInfo wraps everything. The OID 1.2.840.113549.1.7.2 identifies the CMS as SignedData. Learn to recognize its DER encoding (06 09 2A 86 48 86 F7 0D 01 07 02) in hex dumps.
PDF signatures are detached. The eContent in EncapsulatedContentInfo is absent. The signed content lives in the PDF file, addressed by /ByteRange.

Signed attributes are signed, not the content. The signature covers the DER-encoded signed attributes, which include the messageDigest (hash of the content). This two-level indirection is essential to understand.

The DER re-tagging trap is real. The signed attributes must be re-tagged from A0 to 31 before hashing for signature computation. BouncyCastle handles this, but any lower-level implementation must get this right.
ESS-signing-certificate-v2 is the CAdES requirement. Without it, the signature is CMS-valid but not CAdES-valid and therefore not PAdES-compliant.
Unsigned attributes carry timestamps. The signature-time-stamp attribute contains a nested CMS structure — a timestamp token from an RFC 3161 TSA.
Legacy PKCS#7 differences still cause interop failures. BER vs DER encoding of signed attributes is the most common culprit.

chapter.simpara: The next part of this book turns to PDF internals — the file structure, incremental updates, and form fields that the CMS SignedData is embedded within. Understanding the container is meaningless without understanding the document it lives in.

partintro.simpara: Every PDF file on earth — whether it is a single-page receipt from your corner bakery or a 4,000-page securities filing with twelve nested signatures — is built from the same four structural components, arranged in the same order, governed by the same rules that John Warnock and Chuck Geschke laid down at Adobe in 1993. If you want to understand why a signature’s /ByteRange array contains raw file offsets, why cross-reference tables use fixed-width 20-byte entries, or why some PDFs have three %%EOF markers, you have to understand this structure at the byte level.

This chapter dismantles a PDF file from the first byte to the last. We will trace headers, decode every object type the specification defines, read cross-reference tables in both the classic ASCII format and the newer compressed stream format, examine the trailer dictionary, walk through a minimal PDF byte by byte, and then connect all of it back to the one thing we care about most: digital signatures.

chapter.title: The Four Sections
chapter.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5 defines the layout. Every conforming PDF file consists of these four parts, in this order:

listitem.simpara: Header — a single line declaring the PDF version, plus an optional binary comment
Body — the collection of indirect objects that define everything in the document: pages, fonts, images, form fields, annotations, and signatures
Cross-reference table — an index that maps object numbers to byte offsets in the file, enabling random access without linear scanning

Trailer — a dictionary that tells the parser where to find the cross-reference table, which object is the document root, and (for incremental updates) where to find the previous cross-reference section

chapter.simpara: This four-part structure has been stable since PDF 1.0. PDF 1.5 introduced an alternative binary representation for the cross-reference data (cross-reference streams), and PDF 2.0 deprecated some legacy metadata keys, but the fundamental anatomy has not changed in over three decades.

section.title: The Header
section.simpara: A PDF file begins with a version identifier on the first line:

That is nine bytes: the five characters %PDF-, the version number, and a line ending. The % character is the PDF comment delimiter, which means the header line is technically a comment — a design choice that lets non-PDF-aware tools skip it without error.

The version in the header is the file version. ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.2 specifies that a /Version key in the document catalog may override this value. In practice, many generators write %PDF-1.7 in the header and then set /Version /2.0 in the catalog when they need PDF 2.0 features. This dual-version mechanism exists for backward compatibility: an older parser that does not understand the catalog /Version will still see a version number it can process (or at least attempt to process) in the header.

section.title: The Binary Comment Trick
section.simpara: Immediately after the header line, you will almost always find a second comment line containing four or more bytes with values above 127 (0x80):
section.screen: %âãÏÓ
section.simpara: This is not decoration. Its purpose is purely practical: it forces file-type detection heuristics — in web servers, email gateways, FTP clients, operating systems — to classify the file as binary rather than ASCII text. Without this line, a transfer in ASCII mode (remember the FTP ASCII vs BINARY setting?) could mangle the binary stream data inside the PDF. ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.2 recommends but does not require this binary hint.
You will occasionally encounter PDFs without the binary comment — usually from minimalist generators or test harnesses. The file is still valid, but it is more vulnerable to transfer corruption.

tip.simpara: When debugging PDF files at the byte level, the header is your first sanity check. If %PDF- is not the first five bytes, something has happened upstream: a Unicode BOM was prepended, an HTML wrapper was added, a proxy rewrote the response body, or the file was opened as text and line endings were converted. We have encountered every one of these in production systems.

section.title: The Body
section.simpara: The body contains all the PDF objects that constitute the document. Page trees, content streams, font dictionaries, image data, form fields, metadata, and — critically for us — signature dictionaries and their CMS payloads.
Objects in the body are written as indirect objects, each wrapped in a numbered envelope:
section.screen: 7 0 obj << /Type /Page /MediaBox [0 0 612 792] /Contents 12 0 R >> endobj
section.simpara: The prefix 7 0 obj means "object number 7, generation number 0." The keyword endobj marks the end of the object. Between them is the object’s value — here, a dictionary describing a page.

Objects may appear in any order. There is no requirement that object 1 precedes object 2, or that page objects come before font objects. The cross-reference table handles the mapping from logical object number to physical byte offset. This is a fundamental design principle: the physical ordering of objects in the file has no bearing on the logical structure of the document.

Different PDF generators produce wildly different orderings. Adobe Acrobat tends to cluster related objects together. Apache PDFBox writes objects in the order they were allocated in memory. Linearized PDFs (the "fast web view" format described in ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.6) deliberately reorder objects so that the first page can be displayed before the entire file is downloaded. None of this changes the logical content — but it changes the byte offsets, which in turn changes the byte range that a signature covers.

warning.simpara: Some tools assume objects appear in numerical order. They do not. If you are writing a PDF parser or debugging one, never assume object N appears before object N+1 in the file. Always consult the cross-reference table.

section.title: The Cross-Reference Table
section.simpara: After the body comes the cross-reference table (commonly called the "xref table") — an index that records the byte offset where each object starts in the file.

The keyword xref marks the beginning. The line 0 8 means "this section starts at object number 0 and contains 8 entries." Each subsequent line is exactly 20 bytes (including the line terminator) and contains three fields:

listitem.simpara: Byte offset (10 digits, zero-padded) — the position in the file where the object starts
Generation number (5 digits, zero-padded) — used for object versioning; almost always 00000
In-use flag — n means the object is in use; f means it is free (deleted)

section.simpara: The very first entry (object 0) is always free, with generation number 65535. It serves as the head of a linked list of free objects. Each free entry’s "byte offset" field actually contains the object number of the next free entry, forming a chain. This mechanism allows PDF writers to reuse deleted object numbers — a feature that mattered when PDFs were small and object numbers precious, and that is now mostly vestigial.

note.simpara: The 20-byte-per-entry format is non-negotiable. This rigidity is deliberate: it enables random access to any entry via arithmetic (entry_offset = xref_start + header_bytes + object_number * 20) without scanning the entire table. This random-access property is what allows a viewer to display a single page from a 10,000-page document without loading the whole file into memory.

section.simpara: The generation number increments when an object is deleted and its number is later reused, preventing stale references (7 0 R) from resolving to the wrong content. In practice, nearly every object you encounter in the wild has generation 0. The mechanism exists, the specification defines it carefully, and production PDFs almost never exercise it.

section.title: The Trailer
section.simpara: Immediately after the cross-reference table comes the trailer:
section.screen: trailer << /Size 8 /Root 1 0 R /Info 6 0 R /ID [<a9f2c74b...> <a9f2c74b...>] >> startxref 4892 %%EOF
section.simpara: The trailer dictionary is the most important dictionary in the file, because everything starts here. A PDF parser begins at the end of the file. It searches backward for %%EOF, then backward for startxref, reads the byte offset (4892), and jumps to that offset to find the cross-reference table. From the xref table, it reads the trailer dictionary. From the trailer dictionary’s /Root key, it finds the document catalog. From the catalog, it can navigate to every object in the document.

Parsing a PDF starts from the end, not the beginning. This backward-search strategy is fundamental to how incremental updates work (Chapter 8), and it is the reason a PDF can be extended by appending data without rewriting the beginning of the file.

table.title: Trailer Dictionary Keys — ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.5, Table 15

entry.simpara: Total number of entries in the cross-reference table (including object 0). One greater than the highest object number used.
The document catalog — root of the entire logical document tree.
Document information dictionary. Optional and deprecated in PDF 2.0 (replaced by XMP metadata), but still universally present in real-world files.
File identifiers. The first element is assigned at creation and never changes; the second is updated on each save. Signatures depend on /ID — PAdES profiles strongly recommend it.
Byte offset of the previous cross-reference section. This is how incremental updates chain together (Chapter 8).
Encryption dictionary. The signature value itself (/Contents) is never encrypted even in encrypted PDFs, but the surrounding content may be.

important.simpara: The /ID array is not cosmetic. Some signing tools use the first element as input to seed value dictionaries. Some validators check /ID consistency across incremental revisions. If a tool strips or regenerates /ID during processing, existing signatures may fail validation. We have seen this happen with document management systems that "normalize" PDFs on upload.

chapter.title: PDF Object Types
chapter.simpara: Every value in a PDF file is one of exactly ten types, defined in ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.3. Understanding them is necessary for reading raw PDF structure, debugging signatures at the COS level, and working with PDFBox’s object model.

section.title: Boolean, Integer, Real, Null
section.simpara: Boolean — the keywords true or false. Used in flags like /NeedAppearances true.

Integer — a signed whole number with no decimal point: 42, -7, 0. The four values in a /ByteRange array are integers. Integers in PDF have no fixed bit width; implementations typically use 64-bit storage.
Real — a signed number with a decimal point: 3.14, -0.001, 72.0. Used in coordinate values, transformation matrices, and font metrics.
Null — the keyword null. A dictionary key mapped to null is equivalent to the key being absent. Null references are resolved as "object not found."

section.title: String (Literal and Hexadecimal)
section.simpara: PDF has two string representations:

Literal string — parentheses-delimited: (Hello World). Backslash escapes handle special characters: \(, \), \\, \n, \r, \t, and octal escapes like \101 (the letter A). Unbalanced parentheses must be escaped; balanced ones need not be.

Hexadecimal string — angle-bracket-delimited hex pairs: <48656C6C6F>. Each pair of hex digits represents one byte. The /Contents value in a signature dictionary is always a hex string because it contains raw binary CMS data. The hex string is right-padded with zeros to fill the space reserved during signing.

section.title: Name
section.simpara: A slash-prefixed identifier: /Type, /Page, /Sig, /ByteRange. Names are atoms, not strings — they serve as dictionary keys and enumeration values. Names containing characters outside the printable ASCII range use a #xx hex escape: /F#23oo represents the name F#oo.

section.title: Array
section.simpara: An ordered collection enclosed in square brackets: [0 14000 30408 14592]. Arrays can contain any mix of types, including other arrays and dictionaries. The /ByteRange value is an array of four integers.

section.title: Dictionary
section.simpara: Key-value pairs enclosed in double angle brackets:
section.screen: << /Type /Sig /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite /SubFilter /adbe.pkcs7.detached >>
section.simpara: Keys are always names. Values can be any type. Dictionaries are the workhorse of PDF — every page, every font, every annotation, every signature is a dictionary or contains one.

section.title: Stream
section.simpara: A dictionary followed by raw binary data, delimited by stream and endstream:
section.screen: 8 0 obj << /Type /XObject /Subtype /Image /Length 12345 /Filter /FlateDecode >> stream ...binary data (12345 bytes, possibly compressed)... endstream endobj
section.simpara: Streams are the only mechanism for storing bulk data: page drawing instructions, images, embedded font programs, cross-reference data, and CMap tables. The /Length key must be exact — if it is wrong, the parser reads too few or too many bytes, corrupting everything that follows.

caution.simpara: A wrong /Length is one of the most common forms of PDF corruption from buggy generators. When debugging a corrupt PDF, always compare the /Length value against the actual byte count between stream and endstream. QPDF’s --check flag catches this automatically.

section.title: Indirect Object and Indirect Reference
section.simpara: An indirect object wraps any value with an object number and generation number: 12 0 obj …​ endobj. An indirect reference is a pointer to such an object: 12 0 R. The cross-reference table resolves these: given 12 0 R, the parser looks up object 12, generation 0, in the xref table, seeks to the recorded byte offset, and reads the object.

Indirect references are the glue that holds a PDF together. The catalog references the page tree, the page tree references individual pages, pages reference content streams and annotations, annotations reference appearance streams — all through indirect references.

chapter.title: Cross-Reference Streams
chapter.simpara: PDF 1.5 introduced cross-reference streams — an alternative to the classic ASCII xref table that stores the same data in a compressed binary stream object.

section.title: Why They Exist
section.simpara: The classic xref table has two problems. First, the 20-bytes-per-entry format is wasteful: a PDF with 100,000 objects needs a 2 MB xref table of pure ASCII text. Cross-reference streams compress this data 5-10x using standard stream filters (typically FlateDecode). Second, the classic format has no entry type for objects stored inside object streams (covered next); cross-reference streams introduce a Type 2 entry for exactly this purpose.

section.title: Structure
section.screen: 42 0 obj << /Type /XRef /Size 100 /W [1 3 1] /Index [0 100] /Root 1 0 R /Info 5 0 R /ID [<abc123> <abc123>] /Filter /FlateDecode /Length 487 >> stream ...compressed binary data... endstream endobj
section.simpara: The stream dictionary serves double duty: it contains both the stream parameters (/Filter, /Length) and the trailer parameters (/Root, /Info, /ID, /Prev, /Size). There is no separate trailer keyword when cross-reference streams are used.
The /W array specifies field widths in bytes for each entry. /W [1 3 1] means: 1 byte for the type field, 3 bytes for field 2, 1 byte for field 3 — 5 bytes total per entry.

table.title: Cross-Reference Stream Entry Types — ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.8, Table 18

entry.simpara: Generation number of the free entry
Byte offset of the object in the file
Object number of the containing object stream
Index of this object within that object stream

section.simpara: Type 2 is the key innovation. It says "this object is not a standalone indirect object in the file body; it is packed inside object stream N, at position M." Classic xref tables cannot express this.

The /Index array specifies which object numbers are covered. /Index [0 100] means entries for objects 0 through 99. A cross-reference stream can have multiple subsections: /Index [0 50 200 10] covers objects 0-49 and 200-209.

warning.simpara: Some PDF libraries produce files that start with a classic xref table but switch to cross-reference streams in incremental updates (or vice versa). This is legal per the specification, but it trips up parsers that expect consistency. PDFBox handles the transition transparently; not all tools do.

chapter.title: Object Streams
chapter.simpara: PDF 1.5 also introduced object streams: multiple indirect objects packed into a single compressed stream, eliminating the repeated N 0 obj…​endobj framing overhead.
chapter.screen: 10 0 obj << /Type /ObjStm /N 5 /First 32 /Filter /FlateDecode /Length 418 >> stream ...compressed data containing 5 objects... endstream endobj
chapter.simpara: The /N key gives the count of packed objects; /First gives the byte offset within the decompressed data where the first object’s value begins (the preceding bytes contain a table of object numbers and offsets).

Not everything can live in an object stream. ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.7 excludes: stream objects (they have their own stream/endstream framing), the encryption dictionary, objects with non-zero generation numbers, and the cross-reference stream itself. Signature dictionaries can technically live in object streams (they are dictionaries, not streams), but signing tools almost never place them there because it would complicate byte-range calculation.

tip.simpara: If you need to find a signature dictionary in a raw PDF, do not search for /Type /Sig in the file bytes — it might be compressed inside an object stream. Use qpdf --show-object=N where N is the object number. QPDF decompresses object streams transparently.

chapter.title: Hex Dump Walkthrough: A Minimal Valid PDF
chapter.simpara: Let us trace through a complete, minimal PDF file, annotating every section:
chapter.screen:
%PDF-1.7 %âãÏÓ
1 0 obj << /Type /Catalog /Pages 2 0 R >> endobj
2 0 obj << /Type /Pages /Kids [3 0 R] /Count 1 >> endobj
3 0 obj << /Type /Page /Parent 2 0 R /MediaBox [0 0 612 792] /Contents 4 0 R /Resources << /Font << /F1 5 0 R >> >> >> endobj
4 0 obj << /Length 44 >> stream BT /F1 24 Tf 100 700 Td (Hello) Tj ET endstream endobj
5 0 obj << /Type /Font /Subtype /Type1 /BaseFont /Helvetica >> endobj
xref 0 6 0000000000 65535 f 0000000015 00000 n 0000000066 00000 n 0000000121 00000 n 0000000286 00000 n 0000000384 00000 n
trailer << /Size 6 /Root 1 0 R >> startxref 453 %%EOF

callout.para: Header: PDF version 1.7
Binary hint: four bytes above 0x80, forcing binary classification
Catalog: root of the document tree; /Pages points to the page tree root
Pages node: page tree root with one child page
Page: defines the media size (US Letter), references a content stream and font resources
Content stream: drawing instructions — Begin Text, set font F1 at 24pt, move to (100,700), show "Hello", End Text
Font: a reference to standard Helvetica (not embedded — viewers provide a substitute)
Cross-reference table: six entries mapping object numbers to byte offsets
Trailer: /Size 6 (six xref entries), /Root points to the catalog
startxref: byte offset of the xref keyword
%%EOF: end-of-file marker

chapter.simpara: A parser reads this backward: find %%EOF, find startxref, jump to byte 453, parse the xref table, read the trailer, follow /Root to object 1, and navigate from there.

note.simpara: This minimal PDF omits a /Info dictionary, /ID array, and font embedding. Production PDFs have dozens to hundreds of objects, but the structural skeleton is identical.

chapter.title: How Different Generators Produce Different Structures
chapter.simpara: Not all PDFs are created equal at the byte level, and the differences matter when you are computing byte ranges for signatures.
Adobe Acrobat produces linearized files by default, with objects reordered for progressive display and a hint table at the beginning. It uses cross-reference streams in modern versions.

Apache PDFBox (3.0.0) writes objects in memory-allocation order, uses classic xref tables by default, and does not linearize. Its incremental save mode (saveIncremental()) produces clean append-only updates.
iText / OpenPDF frequently uses cross-reference streams even for PDF 1.7 files, and its object ordering varies by version.
LibreOffice produces classic xref tables with object ordering that appears semi-random (it reflects internal processing order).
Microsoft Word (Save as PDF) produces well-structured files with cross-reference streams and object streams, heavily compressed.

QPDF can transform between any of these representations: linearize, de-linearize, convert xref tables to streams or vice versa, unpack object streams, and normalize whitespace. It is the Swiss Army knife of PDF structural debugging.

tip.simpara: When comparing two versions of a signed PDF at the structural level:
tip.programlisting:
# Show all xref entries and their origins qpdf --show-xref document.pdf
# Convert to JSON for semantic comparison qpdf --json document-v1.pdf > /tmp/v1.json qpdf --json document-v2.pdf > /tmp/v2.json diff /tmp/v1.json /tmp/v2.json
# Decompress everything for human-readable byte-level comparison qpdf --qdf --object-streams=disable input.pdf readable.pdf

chapter.title: Why File Structure Matters for Signatures
chapter.simpara: We have spent this chapter on raw PDF anatomy for a specific reason: PDF digital signatures operate at the byte level, not the logical-object level.

The /ByteRange array in a signature dictionary specifies exactly which bytes in the physical file are hashed and signed. Verification reads those exact bytes, computes a cryptographic hash, and compares it against the hash embedded in the CMS SignedData structure. If a single byte differs — because an object was renumbered, a cross-reference entry was reformatted, whitespace was normalized, or a stream was recompressed — the hash will not match, and the signature is invalid.
This has concrete consequences:

Re-saving breaks signatures. Loading a signed PDF into memory and writing it back out — even without changing any logical content — rewrites the file from scratch: new object ordering, new byte offsets, new xref entries. The bytes change. The signature dies. Signed PDFs must be saved incrementally (Chapter 8).
Linearization affects byte layout. Linearizing a PDF after signing rearranges objects for progressive display. The bytes move. The signature breaks. Always linearize before signing, never after.

Cross-reference format affects layout. Whether a file uses classic xref tables or xref streams changes the physical bytes in the file. A signature computed against one format will not validate if the format changes, even when the logical content is identical.

Object streams affect findability. If a signature dictionary is packed inside an object stream, byte-level tools that search for /Type /Sig in the raw file will miss it. Always use proper PDF parsing, never raw byte scanning, when searching for signature-related objects.

chapter.title: PDFBox COS Layer: Mapping PDF Types to Java
chapter.simpara: PDFBox’s COS (Carousel Object System) layer provides a one-to-one mapping from PDF types to Java classes:

entry.simpara: Both literal and hex; setForceHexForm(true) for hex output
Interned: COSName.TYPE, COSName.SIG, custom via COSName.getPDFName("ByteRange")
getItem() returns raw value; getDictionaryObject() dereferences indirect refs
Extends COSDictionary; createInputStream() for decoded data
Wraps COSBase with object number and generation

important.simpara: The distinction between getItem() and getDictionaryObject() is critical when working with signatures. getItem() returns the raw value — if the entry is an indirect reference, you get the COSObject wrapper (which carries the object number). getDictionaryObject() follows the reference and returns the target dictionary. Use getDictionaryObject() when you need the content; use getItem() when you need the object number (e.g., to correlate with xref byte offsets).

chapter.simpara: Here is how to traverse the file structure from trailer to signature dictionaries using PDFBox 3.0.0:

section.title: Incorrect /Length Values
section.simpara: A stream’s /Length does not match its actual byte count. This corrupts parsing downstream and can shift xref offsets, making byte-range verification fail. QPDF catches this:
section.programlisting: qpdf --check suspect.pdf

section.title: Missing /ID Array
section.simpara: Some generators omit /ID entirely. While technically optional, its absence can trip up validators that check it as part of integrity verification. Use the PDFBox code above to detect missing /ID programmatically.

section.title: Multiple %%EOF Markers
section.simpara: A PDF with incremental updates (Chapter 8) will have multiple %%EOF markers. This is normal for signed documents. But some web-based PDF processors strip everything after the first %%EOF, destroying all incremental updates and all signatures.

section.title: Trailing Bytes After Final %%EOF
section.simpara: Bytes after the last %%EOF are not part of the PDF. For signed documents, the /ByteRange of the outermost signature must cover every byte up to the end of the file. Trailing bytes outside the byte range create a validation gap that the Bochum ISA attacks (Chapter 24) exploited.

section.title: Cross-Reference Table Corruption
section.simpara: Byte offsets in the xref table do not match actual object positions. This happens when a tool modifies the body without rebuilding the xref, or when binary transfer corrupts bytes.

warning.simpara: QPDF can repair broken xref tables, but repair involves a full rewrite, which invalidates all signatures. Use --check for diagnosis without modification. For signed documents, repair is not an option — you need the original uncorrupted file.

chapter.title: What Comes Next
chapter.simpara: We have now dissected the complete physical structure of a PDF file: header, body, cross-reference table (in both classic ASCII and compressed stream formats), object streams, trailer, and %%EOF. We have mapped every PDF object type to its PDFBox COS class, walked through a minimal file byte by byte, and connected the physical structure to the requirements of digital signatures.

Chapter 8 builds directly on this foundation with incremental updates — the append-only mechanism that makes PDF signatures possible in the first place. If this chapter was about the structure of a single snapshot, Chapter 8 is about how those snapshots stack on top of each other, forming the layered history that signatures depend on to survive.

abstract.simpara: If there is one mechanism in the PDF specification that you must understand before working with digital signatures, it is incremental updates. Not because they are complicated — the concept is straightforward — but because every aspect of PDF signing depends on them. The byte range that defines what is signed, the ability to add multiple signatures without invalidating earlier ones, the entire model of "append, never rewrite" — all of it rests on incremental updates.

This chapter explains how incremental updates work at the byte level, why they exist, what goes wrong when tools ignore them, and how to inspect and debug them in real PDF files. When you reach Chapter 12 and we start signing documents, you will be glad you spent time here first.

chapter.title: What Incremental Updates Are
chapter.simpara: A PDF file, at its simplest, has four parts: a header (%PDF-1.x or %PDF-2.0), a body of numbered objects, a cross-reference table (or cross-reference stream) that maps object numbers to byte offsets, and a trailer that points to the root object and the cross-reference table. The file ends with the %%EOF marker.
Chapter 7 covered this structure in detail. What Chapter 7 did not cover is what happens when you modify a PDF.
There are exactly two ways to save changes to a PDF file:

listitem.simpara: Full rewrite — rebuild the entire file from scratch, writing a new body, a new cross-reference table, and a new trailer. The old file is replaced entirely.

Incremental update (also called incremental save or append mode) — leave the original file untouched and append new data to the end. The new data contains only the objects that changed (or were added), a new cross-reference section mapping those objects, and a new trailer. The file now has two %%EOF markers: the original one (buried in the middle of the file) and the new one at the end.

chapter.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.6 defines incremental updates:

blockquote.simpara: The updated PDF file shall contain the original data of the PDF file plus appended update data. Updates may be appended to a file multiple times. The update data added each time a file is updated shall contain only those objects that have been added or modified, along with a cross-reference section and trailer for those objects.

chapter.simpara: The key phrase is "the original data of the PDF file" — the original bytes are preserved, byte for byte, untouched. This is not a diff or a delta. It is raw appending.

chapter.title: Why Incremental Updates Exist
chapter.simpara: Incremental updates solve three distinct problems, and understanding all three explains why the mechanism is designed the way it is.

section.title: Problem 1: Performance on Large Documents
section.simpara: A 500-page PDF with embedded fonts and high-resolution images might be 200 MB. If a user fills in a single form field — changing perhaps 50 bytes of actual content — a full rewrite would require reading, processing, and writing all 200 MB. An incremental update appends a few kilobytes.

This was especially important in the 1990s when the format was designed. Disk I/O was slow, memory was scarce, and rewriting a large file for a minor change was unacceptable. Even today, incremental saves are noticeably faster for large documents.

section.title: Problem 2: Preserving Previous State
section.simpara: When a PDF is incrementally updated, the previous revision is still there — it is the bytes before the appended data. This means you can "undo" an incremental update by truncating the file at the previous %%EOF boundary, recovering the exact document as it existed before the change.

This property is not a side effect. It is a design goal. ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.6 explicitly states that conforming readers should be able to retrieve previous revisions of a document by following the chain of /Prev pointers in the trailers.

For workflows where audit trails matter — legal documents, contracts, government forms — this built-in version history is valuable. You do not need an external version control system. The PDF file is the version history.

section.title: Problem 3: Digital Signatures
section.simpara: This is the reason we are here, and it deserves its own section (). The short version: a digital signature covers a specific byte range. If you rewrite the file, the bytes change, and the signature becomes invalid. Incremental updates allow modifications after signing without disturbing the signed bytes.

important.simpara: Problem 3 is not optional. If you are building any tool that edits PDFs which may contain signatures — even if your tool has nothing to do with signing — you must support incremental saves. A full rewrite of a signed PDF destroys every signature in it. There is no workaround, no clever trick, no way to reassemble the pieces. The signed bytes are gone.

chapter.title: Anatomy of an Incremental Update
chapter.simpara: Let us walk through exactly what gets appended during an incremental update. Suppose we start with a simple PDF containing three objects: a catalog (object 1), a page (object 2), and a page content stream (object 3).

section.title: The Original File
section.screen:
%PDF-1.7 1 0 obj << /Type /Catalog /Pages 2 0 R >> endobj
2 0 obj << /Type /Page /Contents 3 0 R /MediaBox [0 0 612 792] >> endobj
3 0 obj << /Length 44 >> stream BT /F1 12 Tf 100 700 Td (Hello) Tj ET endstream endobj
xref 0 4 0000000000 65535 f 0000000009 00000 n 0000000066 00000 n 0000000148 00000 n
trailer << /Size 4 /Root 1 0 R >>
startxref 252 %%EOF

callout.para: Three objects in the body
Cross-reference table mapping object numbers to byte offsets
Trailer with /Size (total object count) and /Root (catalog reference)
startxref gives the byte offset of the xref table
End-of-file marker

section.simpara: Nothing surprising here. This is the standard PDF structure from Chapter 7. Let us say this file is 350 bytes long.

section.title: Appending an Incremental Update
section.simpara: Now suppose we want to change the text on the page from "Hello" to "Hello World". We need a new version of object 3. Instead of rewriting the entire file, we append:
section.screen:
3 0 obj << /Length 50 >> stream BT /F1 12 Tf 100 700 Td (Hello World) Tj ET endstream endobj
xref 0 1 0000000000 65535 f 3 1 0000000351 00000 n
trailer << /Size 4 /Root 1 0 R /Prev 252 >>
startxref 460 %%EOF

callout.para: The new version of object 3, with updated content
A new cross-reference section — but only for changed objects
The xref entry for object 3 now points to byte offset 351 (the start of the new object 3)
The new trailer includes a /Prev key pointing to the previous xref table (at byte 252)
startxref points to the new xref section
A second %%EOF marker

section.simpara: The file now contains two copies of object 3: the original at byte offset 148 and the new one at byte offset 351. The original cross-reference table (at byte 252) still points to the old version. The new cross-reference section (at byte 460) points to the new version.

A conforming reader starts from the end of the file, finds the last startxref, reads the new xref section, and sees that object 3 is at byte 351. For objects 1 and 2, the new xref section has no entries — so the reader follows the /Prev pointer to the previous xref table and finds them there.
The result: the reader sees the updated document. But the old bytes are still present, untouched, in the first 350 bytes of the file.

section.title: The /Prev Chain
section.simpara: The /Prev key in each trailer forms a linked list of cross-reference sections, ordered from newest to oldest. A reader reconstructing the full object map starts with the newest xref section, then walks the /Prev chain, adding entries for any object numbers it has not yet seen. The first entry wins — newer revisions override older ones.
For a PDF that has been incrementally updated three times, the chain looks like this:
section.screen:
%%EOF (revision 3 — current) startxref -> xref_3 trailer_3: /Prev -> xref_2
%%EOF (revision 2) startxref -> xref_2 trailer_2: /Prev -> xref_1
%%EOF (revision 1 — original) startxref -> xref_1 trailer_1: (no /Prev — this is the first revision)
section.simpara: Each revision’s trailer may also update the /Root reference if the catalog object was modified. In practice, the /Root often stays the same — the catalog object number does not change, but the catalog object might be replaced with a new version that references new page trees or updated AcroForm dictionaries.

tip.simpara: Counting %%EOF markers tells you the number of revisions. A freshly created PDF has one %%EOF. After one incremental save, it has two.
