After n incremental saves, it has n + 1…​ unless some tool has done a full rewrite at some point, collapsing all revisions back to one. A signed PDF with two signatures typically has three %%EOF markers: the original document, the first signature, and the second signature.

chapter.title: How Objects Are Overridden
chapter.simpara: The override mechanism is simple: same object number, new xref entry.

When a conforming reader encounters object 3 0 in both the new xref section and a previous one, it uses the entry from the newest section. The old object still exists in the file — it occupies bytes, it can be found by parsing — but it is logically superseded.
This has important implications:

listitem.simpara: No object is ever deleted. You can add an xref entry marking an object as "free" (generation number incremented, next free object pointer updated), but the bytes of the old object remain in the file. Deleting content from a PDF does not remove it from the file — it merely hides it behind a newer xref section.

Object numbers are stable. Object 3 is always object 3, whether you are looking at revision 1 or revision 5. The content of object 3 may differ across revisions, but the number does not change. This is critical for signatures because the /ByteRange and cross-reference tables use byte offsets and object numbers as their addressing scheme.

Generation numbers exist but are rarely used. The spec allows incrementing the generation number when an object is freed and reused, but in practice, most tools reuse generation 0 for everything. Do not write code that assumes generation numbers will be non-zero — you will almost never see them in the wild.

warning.simpara: Some tools claim to "optimize" PDFs by removing unused objects and rebuilding the xref table. This is a full rewrite. If the PDF contains signatures, every signature is destroyed. Tools that do this include some versions of Ghostscript’s pdfwrite device, QPDF’s --linearize option (when not combined with --preserve-unreferenced-objects), and various online "PDF optimizer" services. If you are processing signed PDFs in an automated pipeline, test your tools carefully.

chapter.title: Cross-Reference Streams and Incremental Updates
chapter.simpara: PDF 1.5 introduced cross-reference streams as an alternative to the traditional ASCII cross-reference table. Instead of a plaintext table, the cross-reference data is stored as a compressed binary stream object. The trailer dictionary is merged into the stream dictionary — there is no separate trailer keyword.

Incremental updates work identically with cross-reference streams. The appended data contains a new cross-reference stream object with entries for modified objects, and the stream dictionary includes a /Prev key pointing to the previous cross-reference stream’s byte offset.
chapter.screen:
15 0 obj << /Type /XRef /Size 16 /W [1 3 1] /Root 1 0 R /Prev 8432 /Filter /FlateDecode /Length 87 >> stream ...compressed binary xref data... endstream endobj
startxref 9150 %%EOF

callout.para: The cross-reference stream is itself a PDF object (object 15 in this case)
/W defines the width of each field in the binary data: 1 byte for type, 3 bytes for offset, 1 byte for generation
/Prev points to the previous cross-reference stream, just as in the classic trailer
startxref points to this stream object

chapter.simpara: The /Prev chain works exactly the same way. A reader walks the chain, building the complete object map from newest to oldest, using the first entry encountered for each object number.

note.simpara: A PDF file can mix classic xref tables and cross-reference streams across revisions. The original file might use a classic table, and an incremental update might append a cross-reference stream (or vice versa). A conforming reader must handle both. PDFBox handles this transparently — Loader.loadPDF() follows the /Prev chain regardless of whether each link is a table or a stream.

chapter.title: Why Incremental Updates Are Critical for Signatures
chapter.simpara: This is the most important section in this chapter. Everything else — the performance benefits, the revision history, the object override mechanics — is useful context. But for digital signatures, incremental updates are not a convenience. They are a requirement.

section.title: The Fundamental Constraint
section.simpara: A PDF digital signature covers a specific byte range (see Chapter 13 for the complete deep dive). The /ByteRange array in the signature dictionary specifies exactly which bytes in the file are hashed and signed. For the signature to remain valid, those bytes must not change. Not a single one.

If you do a full rewrite of a signed PDF — reorganizing objects, recomputing byte offsets, rebuilding the cross-reference table — the bytes at the signed offsets will be different. The hash will not match. The signature is dead.

An incremental update avoids this problem by not touching the original bytes. The signed region (which covers everything from the start of the file through the /Contents gap and out to the %%EOF of the revision that was signed) remains intact. New content is appended after the signed region.

section.title: Signing Is an Incremental Update
section.simpara: When PDFBox (or any compliant library) signs a PDF, it performs an incremental update. The signing operation appends:

listitem.simpara: A new or updated signature field object (with the /V reference pointing to the signature dictionary)
The signature dictionary object itself (containing /ByteRange, /Contents, /Filter, /SubFilter, and the rest)
An updated AcroForm dictionary (with the new field added to /Fields and /SigFlags set)
A new cross-reference section mapping these new/updated objects
A new trailer with /Prev pointing to the previous revision
A new %%EOF

section.simpara: The signature’s byte range covers everything from byte 0 to the end of this new %%EOF — including the bytes of all previous revisions. The signature literally covers the entire file, minus the gap where the CMS blob sits.

section.title: Multiple Signatures
section.simpara: Now consider what happens when a second person signs the same PDF. The second signature is another incremental update, appended after the first. The second signature’s byte range starts at byte 0 and extends to the end of its %%EOF — which means it covers the first signature’s bytes too.

The first signature’s byte range covers bytes up to the first signature’s %%EOF. The second signature’s appended data falls after that range. As far as the first signature is concerned, those bytes do not exist — they are outside the range it covers, and that is fine.
This is how multiple signatures coexist:
section.screen:
| Original document | Revision 1 (%%EOF) | + First signature append | Revision 2 (%%EOF) | + Second signature append | Revision 3 (%%EOF)
First signature's byte range: [0 .. end of revision 2] (minus its /Contents gap) Second signature's byte range: [0 .. end of revision 3] (minus its /Contents gap)
section.simpara: The first signature validates against revisions 1 and 2. The second signature validates against revisions 1, 2, and 3. Neither signature invalidates the other.

important.simpara: This only works because each signature is appended incrementally. If the second signer’s tool did a full rewrite instead of an incremental save, the first signature’s bytes would be destroyed, and both signatures would be invalid. This is why /SigFlags bit 2 (AppendOnly) exists — it tells editors "you must use incremental save, or you will break things."

section.title: Form Filling After Signing
section.simpara: The same principle applies to form filling after a certification signature with /DocMDP permission level 2 (which allows form filling and signing but no other changes). When a user fills in a form field, the PDF viewer should save the change as an incremental update. The filled field value is a new object appended to the file, and the certification signature remains valid because its byte range is untouched.

If the viewer does a full save instead, the certification signature breaks. The viewer has just violated the signer’s explicit instruction (via /DocMDP) that the document should only be modified incrementally.

chapter.title: What Goes Wrong: Flattening and Full Rewrites
chapter.simpara: Now that we understand why incremental updates matter, let us catalog the ways they get destroyed.

section.title: The Flattening Problem
section.simpara: "Flattening" a PDF typically means merging all incremental updates into a single revision — rewriting the file with one body, one cross-reference table, one trailer, and one %%EOF. Some tools call this "optimizing," "linearizing," "compressing," or just "saving."
The result is a smaller, cleaner file. It is also a file in which every digital signature is invalid.
Common tools that flatten by default or by option:

listitem.simpara: Ghostscript (gs -sDEVICE=pdfwrite) — always does a full rewrite; there is no incremental mode
QPDF with --linearize — reorganizes the file for progressive loading, destroying incremental history
Some online PDF tools — "compress PDF" or "optimize PDF" services typically do a full rewrite
LibreOffice (depending on version and configuration) — older versions did not always use incremental save, even when /SigFlags bit 2 was set
Adobe Acrobat "Save As" vs "Save" — "Save" uses incremental update; "Save As" does a full rewrite (destroying signatures unless the user re-signs)

warning.simpara: If you are building an automated PDF processing pipeline and signed PDFs will pass through it, you must audit every tool in the pipeline to ensure it uses incremental saves (or does not modify the file at all). A single non-incremental save anywhere in the pipeline will invalidate every signature. This is the number one cause of "signatures were valid when I sent the PDF but invalid when the recipient opened it."

section.title: The "Save" vs "Save As" Trap
section.simpara: Adobe Acrobat’s behavior is the one most users encounter:

listitem.simpara: File > Save performs an incremental update. Signatures are preserved.
File > Save As writes a new file from scratch. Every signature in the document is destroyed.

section.simpara: Acrobat shows a warning dialog when you attempt Save As on a signed document, but many users click through warnings without reading them. Other PDF editors may not even warn.

tip.simpara: When training users who work with signed PDFs, the single most important instruction is: "Never use Save As. Always use Save." This one piece of advice prevents more signature breakage than any amount of technical configuration.

chapter.title: Extracting a Specific Revision
chapter.simpara: Because incremental updates are purely additive — new bytes appended after the previous %%EOF — extracting a previous revision is trivial: truncate the file at the appropriate %%EOF boundary.
If a PDF has three %%EOF markers (three revisions), you can extract:

listitem.simpara: Revision 1: all bytes from the start of the file up to and including the first %%EOF
Revision 2: all bytes from the start up to and including the second %%EOF
Revision 3: the entire file (up to and including the third %%EOF)

chapter.simpara: This is not a diff extraction — each "revision" is a complete, self-contained PDF file that a reader can open and render. Revision 1 is the original document. Revision 2 is the original plus the first set of changes. And so on.

section.title: Extracting with PDFBox
section.simpara: PDFBox does not provide a built-in "extract revision N" method, but the approach is straightforward: find the N-th %%EOF marker and copy bytes from 0 to that position.

note.simpara: This approach works for classic incremental updates. It does not handle linearized PDFs correctly (see ), because linearized files have a %%EOF at the end of the first part that is not a revision boundary. For production code, you should also handle the case where %%EOF appears inside a stream object (rare but technically possible in pathological files).

section.title: Extracting with QPDF
section.simpara: QPDF provides a more robust approach from the command line:
section.programlisting:
# Show all cross-reference sections and their offsets qpdf --show-xref input.pdf
# Extract a specific page range (not exactly revision extraction, # but useful for isolating content) qpdf input.pdf --pages . 1-5 -- output.pdf
section.simpara: QPDF does not have a built-in "extract revision N" command, but --show-xref gives you all the information you need to determine the %%EOF boundaries manually.

chapter.title: How PDFBox Handles Incremental Saves
chapter.simpara: PDFBox 3.0.0 provides two distinct save methods, and choosing the wrong one is a common source of signature breakage.

section.title: doc.save() — Full Rewrite
section.simpara: The standard save() method writes the entire document from scratch:
This produces a clean, single-revision PDF. All incremental update history is lost. All signatures are invalid.
Use save() only when:

listitem.simpara: The document has no signatures and you do not need to preserve revision history
You are creating a new document from scratch
You explicitly want to flatten the file (and accept the consequences)

section.title: doc.saveIncremental() — Append Mode
section.simpara: The saveIncremental() method appends only the changed objects:

This is what you must use when the document contains signatures. PDFBox tracks which objects have been modified (via the COSUpdateInfo mechanism) and writes only those objects, plus a new cross-reference section and trailer, to the output.

important.simpara: saveIncremental() requires that the document was loaded from a stream or byte array — PDFBox needs the original bytes to copy them to the output before appending. If you loaded the document from a File and then try to write incrementally to a different output stream, the original bytes are read from the file. If you try to overwrite the same file, you will corrupt it. Always load from bytes and write to a new output.

section.title: The saveIncremental() and Signing Workflow
section.simpara: When signing a PDF, you do not call saveIncremental() directly. Instead, you use the signing API:

callout.para: saveIncremental() here does three things: writes the original bytes, appends the new objects (including the signature dictionary with a placeholder /Contents), computes the hash over the byte ranges, calls your SignatureInterface.sign() method to get the CMS bytes, and writes them into the placeholder.

section.simpara: The signing operation is always an incremental save. PDFBox enforces this — there is no doc.save() variant that supports signing, because signing with a full rewrite is logically impossible (the signed byte range would cover a file that no longer exists).

chapter.title: Multiple %%EOF Markers: What They Mean
chapter.simpara: Every %%EOF marker in a PDF represents the end of a revision. Counting them tells you the document’s history:

listitem.simpara: 1 %%EOF: A single-revision PDF. Never been incrementally updated. Either freshly created, or previously incrementally updated but later flattened by a full rewrite.
2 %%EOF: One incremental update after the original. Common for a single signature, a single form fill, or a single annotation addition.
3 %%EOF: Two incremental updates. Common for a document with two signatures, or one signature plus a subsequent form fill.
N %%EOF: N-1 incremental updates after the original. Documents that have been through complex workflows (multiple signers, multiple form fill rounds, annotation rounds) can have many revisions.

tip.simpara: A quick way to count revisions in a PDF is: grep -c '%%EOF' document.pdf. This works for most files, but be aware that %%EOF could theoretically appear inside a stream object (unlikely in practice). For reliable counting, parse the /Prev chain instead.

section.title: What Revision Counts Tell You About a Signed PDF
section.simpara: For signature validation, the revision count carries diagnostic information:

listitem.simpara: A signed PDF with exactly 2 %%EOF markers is the simplest case: original document + one signature. The signature covers everything in revision 1 and 2.

A signed PDF with 3 %%EOF markers might mean: original + signature + post-signature modification (form fill, annotation, second signature). The first signature covers revisions 1-2. Whatever is in revision 3 was added after signing — whether it is permissible depends on the /TransformMethod and /DocMDP settings.

A signed PDF with only 1 %%EOF is suspicious. Either the signature was created during the initial save (some tools do this), or the file was flattened after signing (which means the signature is almost certainly invalid, even if the bytes were carefully reconstructed — see the discussion in Chapter 24 about the Incremental Saving Attack).

chapter.title: Tracing the Revision History
chapter.simpara: To reconstruct the complete revision history of a PDF, you walk the /Prev chain from the newest trailer back to the oldest:

note.simpara: PDFBox’s public API does not expose a convenient "iterate over all revisions" method. The internal COSParser follows the /Prev chain during loading, but the individual revision trailers are not directly accessible after parsing. For production revision analysis, consider using QPDF’s --show-xref output (see ) or writing a custom parser that reads the raw bytes and follows the /Prev pointers manually.

chapter.title: Linearized PDFs and Incremental Updates
chapter.simpara: Linearized PDFs (sometimes called "optimized for fast web view") have a special structure designed for progressive loading over HTTP. A linearized PDF reorganizes its objects so that the first page can be displayed before the entire file has been downloaded.

The linearization adds a linearization dictionary (object 1, typically) and restructures the file into two parts separated by an internal %%EOF — but this internal %%EOF is not a revision boundary. It is part of the linearization structure.
This creates a compatibility problem with incremental updates:

listitem.simpara: A linearized PDF already has an unusual structure with cross-reference data at both the beginning and end of the file.

An incremental update appends data after the final %%EOF, which is fine for non-linearized PDFs but technically invalidates the linearization (because the linearization dictionary’s /L key records the total file length, which is now wrong).
Some viewers re-linearize on save, which is a full rewrite, which destroys signatures.

chapter.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.6 states:

blockquote.simpara: If an incremental update is made to a linearized PDF file, the result is a valid non-linearized PDF file.

chapter.simpara: In other words, an incremental update on a linearized PDF destroys the linearization but produces a valid PDF. This is acceptable — the file simply loses its "fast web view" optimization.

caution.simpara: If your workflow requires both linearization and digital signatures, you must linearize before signing, not after. Linearizing after signing is a full rewrite that destroys signatures. Signing after linearizing produces a valid signed PDF that is no longer linearized. There is no way to have a linearized, signed PDF that remains linearized after signing — the incremental update required by signing inherently breaks the linearization structure.

chapter.title: The Incremental Saving Attack
chapter.simpara: The incremental update mechanism that makes PDF signatures possible is also the mechanism that attackers have exploited to bypass those signatures.
The Incremental Saving Attack (ISA), described by the Ruhr University Bochum team in their CCS 2019 paper, works as follows:

listitem.simpara: The attacker starts with a legitimately signed PDF
The attacker appends a new incremental update to the file — adding, modifying, or replacing objects (pages, text, images, form field values)
The attacker does not touch the signed bytes; the incremental update is appended after the signed region
The attacker presents the modified file to a PDF viewer
The viewer reads the file from the end, uses the newest cross-reference section, and renders the attacker’s content
The viewer validates the signature by checking the original byte range — which is untouched — and reports "Signature is valid"

chapter.simpara: The signature is valid — over the bytes it covers. But the viewer is displaying content from outside the signed byte range. The user sees a green checkmark and attacker-controlled content.

caution.simpara: The defense against ISA is conceptually simple but difficult to implement correctly: a viewer must detect that content has been appended after the signed revision and warn the user. Specifically, the viewer must check whether the byte range of the last signature covers the entire file. If the file extends beyond the end of the signed byte range, there are unsigned changes.

Whether those changes are permissible (e.g., a subsequent signature, an allowed form fill under DocMDP level 2) or malicious requires deeper analysis of what the appended objects actually do — which is where the complexity lies.

Chapter 24 covers the ISA and its variants in complete technical detail, including the specific techniques attackers used to bypass even the "unsigned content detected" warnings that some viewers implemented as an initial defense.

chapter.title: Debugging Incremental Updates
chapter.simpara: When something goes wrong with a signed PDF — the signature shows as invalid, a form field value disappeared, a viewer crashes — the first thing to check is the incremental update structure.

section.title: QPDF: Your Best Friend
section.simpara: QPDF is an indispensable tool for PDF debugging. For incremental update analysis, the key commands are:
section.programlisting:
# Show all cross-reference entries with their types and offsets qpdf --show-xref signed-document.pdf
# Show the structure of the file (objects, streams, references) qpdf --show-object=trailer signed-document.pdf
# Check the file for structural errors qpdf --check signed-document.pdf
# Show a specific object qpdf --show-object=42,0 signed-document.pdf
section.simpara: The --show-xref output is particularly valuable. It shows every object number, its byte offset, and which cross-reference section it belongs to. From this output, you can determine:

listitem.simpara: How many cross-reference sections exist (i.e., how many revisions)
Which objects were added or modified in each revision
Whether the xref entries are consistent (offsets point to actual objects)

section.title: Comparing Revisions
section.simpara: To understand what changed between revisions, extract each revision (by truncating at %%EOF boundaries as described earlier) and compare them:
section.programlisting:
# Extract revision 1 (original document) # Suppose the first %%EOF is at byte 45230 head -c 45230 signed-document.pdf > revision1.pdf
# Extract revision 2 (after first signature) # Suppose the second %%EOF is at byte 67890 head -c 67890 signed-document.pdf > revision2.pdf
# Compare the objects in each revision qpdf --show-xref revision1.pdf > xref1.txt qpdf --show-xref revision2.pdf > xref2.txt diff xref1.txt xref2.txt
section.simpara: The diff will show you exactly which objects were added or modified in the incremental update. For a signature, you should see the signature dictionary object, the updated AcroForm, and possibly an updated catalog or page annotation list.

section.title: Hex-Level Inspection
section.simpara: For the most stubborn problems, there is no substitute for looking at the raw bytes. Open the PDF in a hex editor and search for %%EOF. Each occurrence marks a revision boundary. The bytes between two consecutive %%EOF markers (plus the preceding startxref and trailer) constitute one incremental update.

simpara.emphasis: A debugging checklist for incremental update problems:

listitem.simpara: Count %%EOF markers — does the number match the expected number of revisions?
Check each trailer’s /Prev value — does the chain form a consistent linked list?
Verify that startxref in each revision points to a valid xref section
For signed PDFs: does the byte range of each signature end at a %%EOF boundary?
Are there any bytes after the last %%EOF? (There should not be — trailing garbage can confuse some parsers.)
Open each extracted revision independently — does it render correctly?
Compare the object sets across revisions — do the changes make sense for the expected workflow?

chapter.title: Real-World Scenarios
chapter.simpara: The theory of incremental updates is clean. The practice is messy. Here are the scenarios you will encounter most often.

section.title: Scenario 1: Single Signature
section.simpara: The simplest case. A document is created, then signed. The file has two revisions (two %%EOF markers). The signature’s byte range covers the entire file. No complications.

section.title: Scenario 2: Two Approval Signatures
section.simpara: Alice signs first, then Bob. The file has three revisions: original, Alice’s signature, Bob’s signature. Alice’s byte range covers revisions 1-2. Bob’s byte range covers revisions 1-3. When validating Alice’s signature, a viewer should check the byte range of Alice’s signature and warn if changes between revision 2 and 3 go beyond what Alice’s /TransformMethod permits (typically, adding more signatures is allowed).

section.title: Scenario 3: Certification + Approval
section.simpara: The document author applies a certification signature with /DocMDP permission level 2 (form fill and sign allowed). Later, a user fills form fields and applies an approval signature. The file has three or four revisions (original, certification, form fill, approval — or the form fill and approval might be in the same incremental update). The certification signature permits the form fill and the additional signature. A viewer must verify that the changes in the later revisions are within the scope of the DocMDP permissions.

section.title: Scenario 4: Annotations After Signing
section.simpara: A user adds a sticky note annotation to a signed PDF. If the editor saves incrementally, a new revision is appended with the annotation object and an updated page annotations array. The original signature’s byte range is untouched. Whether the annotation is allowed depends on the signature’s /TransformMethod:

listitem.simpara: DocMDP level 1 (no changes allowed): the annotation violates the permissions. A conforming viewer should warn that the document has been modified in a way that exceeds the signer’s permissions.
DocMDP level 2 (form fill and sign only): annotations are not form filling, so this is a violation.
DocMDP level 3 (annotations, form filling, and signing allowed): the annotation is permitted.

section.simpara: The incremental update itself is structurally fine in all three cases. The question is whether the content of the update is semantically permissible. This distinction — structural validity vs. semantic permissibility — is at the heart of the Incremental Saving Attack (Chapter 24).

section.title: Scenario 5: The Flattened Disaster
section.simpara: A signed PDF passes through an automated document management system that "optimizes" PDFs for storage. The optimization tool does a full rewrite. The output PDF has one %%EOF, no /Prev chain, and the signature dictionary’s byte range references offsets that no longer correspond to the correct content. The signature is permanently invalid. The signed bytes are gone.
There is no recovery. The original file must be retrieved from backup (if it exists) or the document must be re-signed.

important.simpara: This scenario is extremely common in enterprise environments. Document management systems, email gateways, PDF/A conversion tools, and print spoolers all potentially rewrite PDFs. If your organization processes signed PDFs, audit your entire document pipeline for non-incremental saves. Every tool that touches a signed PDF must either leave it completely unmodified or use incremental saves exclusively.

section.title: Scenario 6: The Double-Linearization Trap
section.simpara: A document is created with linearization enabled (for fast web view), signed, and then processed by a tool that re-linearizes it. The re-linearization is a full rewrite. The signature is destroyed.
The fix: disable linearization in any tool that processes signed PDFs, or ensure that no post-signing processing step involves linearization.

chapter.title: Summary
chapter.simpara: Incremental updates are the mechanism that makes PDF digital signatures possible. Without them, signing a PDF would require that the document never be modified again — no additional signatures, no form filling, no annotations, nothing. With them, a signed PDF can evolve through a controlled sequence of revisions, each one adding new content without disturbing the bytes that previous signatures cover.
The rules are simple:

listitem.simpara: A signed PDF must be saved incrementally. Always.
The byte range of each signature covers a specific set of revisions.
New content is appended after the signed byte range.
The /Prev chain in the trailers links all revisions together.
Extracting a specific revision means truncating at the appropriate %%EOF boundary.
A full rewrite destroys all incremental history and all signatures.
Linearization and incremental updates are incompatible after the first incremental save.

chapter.simpara: The next chapter (Chapter 9) examines AcroForm fields — the form infrastructure that signature fields are built on. Chapter 12 will bring incremental updates and signature architecture together, and Chapter 13 will dissect the byte range in complete detail. If the byte range is where signatures live or die, incremental updates are the ground they stand on.

abstract.simpara: Signature fields are form fields. This is not a metaphor. A /Sig field lives in exactly the same data structure as a text box, a checkbox, or a dropdown — the AcroForm field tree defined in ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.7. If you do not understand how the AcroForm dictionary, the field hierarchy, widget annotations, and appearance streams work, you will not be able to create, debug, or validate PDF signatures reliably.
This chapter builds that understanding from the ground up.

chapter.title: The AcroForm Dictionary
chapter.simpara: Every PDF that contains interactive form fields has an /AcroForm entry in the document catalog. This is the root of all form infrastructure in the document: the field list, the signature flags, the default appearance string, the default resources. If /AcroForm is missing from the catalog, the document has no interactive forms — period. Some tools create signature fields and forget to create the AcroForm entry, which leads to signatures that exist as orphaned objects in the file but are invisible to every conforming viewer.
The AcroForm dictionary lives at:
Here is a representative AcroForm dictionary with all keys that matter for signature work:
chapter.screen: << /Fields [10 0 R 11 0 R 12 0 R] % /SigFlags 3 % /NeedAppearances false % /DA (/Helv 0 Tf 0 g) % /DR << % /Font << /Helv 8 0 R /ZaDb 9 0 R >> >> >>

callout.para: /Fields — array of references to the top-level field dictionaries. Every field in the document must be reachable from this array (directly or through parent-child relationships).
/SigFlags — a bit field that controls signature-related behavior. Value 3 means both bit 1 (SignaturesExist) and bit 2 (AppendOnly) are set.
/NeedAppearances — tells the viewer whether to regenerate appearance streams before rendering.
/DA — default appearance string, used for fields that do not have their own /DA.
/DR — default resources dictionary, providing fonts and other resources for appearance generation.

chapter.simpara: Let’s examine each of these in the depth they deserve.

section.title: /Fields — The Root of the Field Tree
section.simpara: The /Fields array contains indirect references to the document’s top-level field dictionaries. "Top-level" means fields that have no parent — they are the roots of potentially nested field trees. A flat form with ten independent fields will have ten entries in /Fields. A form with a hierarchical namespace (like address.city, address.zip) will have a single address parent in /Fields, with city and zip as children.

important.simpara: A field that is not reachable from /Fields does not exist as far as a conforming reader is concerned. We have seen PDFs where a signing tool created a signature field object, set its /V to point at a signature dictionary, added a widget annotation to a page — and forgot to add the field to /Fields. The signature data is physically present in the file. The CMS bytes are valid. But no viewer will find it, because the AcroForm field tree does not reference it.
If you are debugging a "missing signature" problem, check /Fields first.

section.title: /SigFlags — The Two Bits That Protect Everything
section.simpara: We cover /SigFlags in depth here because getting it wrong destroys signatures — silently, irreversibly, and usually long after the signing ceremony is over.

table.title: SigFlags bit definitions — ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.7.2, Table 225

entry.simpara: Declares that the document contains at least one signature field with a value. Conforming readers use this as a signal to check for signatures and display validation status. Without it, some viewers will not even look for signatures.

Mandates that any modifications to the document shall use incremental updates only. This is the single most important protection for existing signatures. If a viewer or editor does a full save (rewriting the entire file from scratch), every signature in the document becomes invalid — the bytes have changed, the byte ranges no longer match, and the cryptographic verification will fail.

section.simpara: The only correct value for /SigFlags in a signed document is 3 (both bits set). A value of 1 (SignaturesExist without AppendOnly) is technically allowed by the spec but is a bug in practice — it tells viewers that signatures exist but does not protect them from being destroyed by a full save.

warning.simpara: Many PDF generation tools — including some versions of LibreOffice, older versions of iText, and various government form generators — set /SigFlags to 1 or omit it entirely. If you receive a signed PDF with /SigFlags missing or set to 1, the signatures are at risk. Any editor that performs a full save will destroy them.

section.simpara: AppendOnly matters because incremental updates are what make multi-signature workflows possible — each signer appends their changes without moving the bytes that previous signers' byte ranges reference. Without AppendOnly, an editor may do a full rewrite, destroying every signature.
We have also seen the converse: /SigFlags is 0 but signed fields exist with valid CMS data. Acrobat shows "No Signatures" because bit 1 was never set, so it never looked.

important.simpara: The AppendOnly flag is advisory, not enforced by the file format. A malicious or poorly written tool can ignore it. The flag protects against accidental destruction by well-behaved editors. Intentional tampering is what the cryptographic signature itself guards against.

section.title: /NeedAppearances — The Flag That Breaks Signatures
section.simpara: The /NeedAppearances flag (boolean, default false) tells the viewer to regenerate appearance streams before rendering. For ordinary fields this is a convenience — skip generating appearances and let the viewer handle it. For signature fields, it is a disaster.

When a viewer regenerates appearances, it modifies the document bytes. If the document is signed, the signature becomes invalid. It does not matter that the viewer was "just" fixing up visuals — the bytes changed.

warning.simpara: If /NeedAppearances is true in a signed document, either: (1) the viewer regenerates appearances and the signature fails, or (2) the viewer skips regeneration and some fields show blank rectangles. Never set /NeedAppearances to true in a document that will be signed. Always generate complete appearance streams for every field before signing.

section.title: /DA — Default Appearance String
section.simpara: The /DA (Default Appearance) string is a PDF content stream fragment that specifies the default font, size, and color for variable text in form fields. It typically looks like:
section.screen: /Helv 0 Tf 0 g
section.simpara: This means: use the font aliased as /Helv, at size 0 (auto-size), in black (0 g). The font name /Helv must be defined in the /DR (Default Resources) dictionary.

Why does this matter for signatures? Because if a signing tool creates a visible signature appearance that contains text — the signer’s name, the date, the reason — and the font referenced in the appearance stream is not available in /DR and not embedded in the appearance’s own /Resources, some viewers will fall back to /DA and render the text in Helvetica or not at all.

tip.simpara: Always embed the font resources directly in the signature field’s appearance stream /Resources dictionary. Do not rely on /DR. The appearance stream is a self-contained Form XObject and should carry everything it needs to render correctly, regardless of what the parent AcroForm provides.

section.title: /DR — Default Resources
section.simpara: The /DR dictionary provides fonts, color spaces, and other resources available to all form fields. The two fonts you will encounter in virtually every AcroForm document are /Helv (Helvetica, for text) and /ZaDb (ZapfDingbats, for checkbox ticks and radio button bullets).

note.simpara: The /DR fonts are not the same as page-level fonts. A page’s /Resources dictionary is independent. If a signature appearance stream references a font from the page’s resources but not from /DR or the appearance’s own resources, rendering may fail in viewers that strictly follow the spec.

section.title: Parent-Child Relationships and Fully Qualified Names
section.simpara: The AcroForm field tree is not flat. Fields can be nested using /Parent and /Kids keys. A parent field << /T (address) /Kids [20 0 R 21 0 R] >> with a child field << /T (city) /Parent 19 0 R /FT /Tx >> produces the fully qualified name address.city.

The fully qualified name is constructed by concatenating /T (partial name) values from root to leaf, separated by periods. This is the name you use in PDFBox’s acroForm.getField("address.city"), in JavaScript, and in FDF data files.

tip.simpara: Debugging field-not-found errors: If acroForm.getField("Signature1") returns null, check whether the field is nested. It might be form.Signature1 or page1.Signature1. Use acroForm.getFieldTree() to iterate every field in the hierarchy and inspect the fully qualified names.

section.title: Terminal vs. Non-Terminal Fields
section.simpara: A terminal field is a field with no /Kids, or whose /Kids are all widget annotations (not other fields). Terminal fields have a /FT (field type) key — /Tx, /Btn, /Ch, or /Sig.
A non-terminal field is a pure grouping node. It has /Kids that are other fields, and it does not have /FT. Its only purpose is to provide a namespace prefix.

warning.simpara: Some PDF generators create non-terminal fields that also carry /FT. This is technically a spec violation (ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.7.4.1), but it happens. PDFBox handles it gracefully in most cases, but other libraries may choke. If you are building a field tree, keep non-terminal nodes clean — no /FT, no /V, no /Rect.

chapter.title: Field Types
chapter.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.7.4 defines four field types. All of them can appear in the same AcroForm, side by side, and a single document frequently contains all four — especially government and legal forms that combine text input, checkboxes, dropdown selections, and signature fields.

section.title: /Tx — Text Fields
section.simpara: Text fields hold user-entered text (value in /V as a text string). Key properties: /MaxLen (maximum character count), /Q (text alignment: 0=left, 1=center, 2=right). Text fields interact with signatures in one important way: if a text field is filled after a document is signed, the fill operation is an incremental update. Whether this invalidates the signature depends on the /Lock and /FieldMDP settings (covered in Chapter 14).

section.title: /Btn — Button Fields
section.simpara: Button fields come in three flavors, distinguished by the /Ff (field flags) bits: pushbuttons (bit 17 set, no persistent value), checkboxes (bit 17 clear, bit 16 clear, two-state toggles with values like /Yes or /Off), and radio buttons (bit 17 clear, bit 16 set, mutually exclusive options). For signatures, button fields matter primarily in the context of form flattening and field locking.

section.title: /Ch — Choice Fields
section.simpara: Choice fields present a list of options: combo boxes (bit 18 set, a dropdown) and list boxes (bit 18 clear, a scrollable list). Options are defined in the /Opt array, where each entry can be a simple text string or a two-element array [export_value, display_text].

section.title: /Sig — Signature Fields
section.simpara: Signature fields are where form infrastructure meets cryptographic infrastructure. A /Sig field is, structurally, just another form field in the AcroForm tree. But its /V (value) is not a text string or a name — it is a signature dictionary (/Type /Sig), which is a complex structure containing the byte range, the CMS data, the signing filter, timestamps, and more.
A signature field before signing:
section.screen: << /FT /Sig /T (Signature1) /V null % or /V absent entirely /Rect [100 600 300 650] /Subtype /Widget /P 5 0 R >>
section.simpara: The same field after signing:
section.screen: << /FT /Sig /T (Signature1) /V 42 0 R % reference to the /Sig dictionary /Rect [100 600 300 650] /Subtype /Widget /P 5 0 R /AP << /N 43 0 R >> % appearance stream added by the signing tool >>
section.simpara: Object 42 is the signature dictionary (covered in depth in Chapter 12). Object 43 is the visual appearance of the signature.

important.simpara: The signature field is not the signature. The field is the container. The signature dictionary (the /V value) is the content. A document can have signature fields with no value — these are placeholder fields, pre-positioned by a template author, waiting for a signer to fill them in.
This distinction matters because:

listitem.simpara: A document with an empty signature field still has an entry in /Fields.
The /SigFlags should not have bit 1 set until at least one field actually has a value.
Validation logic should check /V, not just the presence of /FT /Sig fields.

section.simpara: What makes /Sig fields unique: (1) the value is a complex dictionary, not a simple string; (2) signing computes a hash over the entire file, not just the field; (3) the /SV (Seed Value) and /Lock dictionaries allow the field to constrain which algorithms, certificates, and post-signing modifications are acceptable; and (4) the widget’s /Rect determines visibility — a zero-size rect means an invisible signature.

section.title: The Connection Between Fields and Pages
section.simpara: A form field is an abstract data object (name, type, value). A widget annotation is its visual representation on a specific page (coordinates, size, appearance).

In the most common case, the field and widget are merged into a single PDF object — the same dictionary carries both /FT (field type) and /Subtype /Widget, both /T (field name) and /Rect (position). The spec (ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.5.6.19) also allows separating them: a field’s /Kids array can contain widget annotations, each with /Parent pointing back to the field. This allows a single field to have widgets on multiple pages — unusual, but legal.

tip.simpara: When debugging, check whether the field and widget are merged or separate. PDSignatureField.getWidgets() returns the list of widgets. An empty list means no visual representation — fine for invisible signatures, a bug if you expected a visible one.

section.title: Page Association
section.simpara: A widget must be associated with a page via two links: the widget’s /P key referencing the page dictionary, and the page’s /Annots array containing a reference to the widget. Both should be present. If the widget is missing from /Annots, it will not render — even if /P points to the correct page.

warning.simpara: Some signing tools add the widget to the field tree but forget to add it to the page’s /Annots array. The signature is valid cryptographically but invisible on every page. This is one of the most common "invisible signature" bugs that is not actually an invisible signature — it is a missing annotation reference.

section.title: Normal, Rollover, and Down
section.simpara: The /AP dictionary can define three appearance streams: /N (Normal — the only one that matters for signatures), /R (Rollover — mouse hover, rarely used), and /D (Down — click state, irrelevant for signatures).

Each can be a single Form XObject stream or a subdictionary mapping state names to streams. Checkboxes use a subdictionary (<< /Yes 60 0 R /Off 61 0 R >>). Signature fields always use a single stream — signatures do not have states.

section.title: Why Appearance Streams Matter for Signatures
section.simpara: If the appearance stream is missing, the viewer either renders a blank rectangle, tries to generate one on the fly (dangerous if the document is signed — see /NeedAppearances above), or shows a generic placeholder. For signed fields, the only correct approach is to have the signing tool generate a complete appearance stream before signing.

note.simpara: The appearance stream is included in the signed byte range. Modifying it after signing invalidates the signature. However, the appearance is not semantically bound to the CMS content — the appearance could say "Signed by Alice" while the certificate belongs to Bob. The viewer must verify both independently.

section.title: Generating Appearances with PDFBox
section.simpara: PDFBox provides the PDAcroForm.refreshAppearances() method (and its per-field equivalent) to generate appearance streams for all fields. This is the equivalent of setting /NeedAppearances to true and having the viewer regenerate — except you do it programmatically, before signing, so the appearances are part of the signed bytes.

caution.simpara: refreshAppearances() will overwrite existing appearance streams. If you have custom signature appearances (hand-drawn images, company logos), call this before setting up your custom appearances, or call it only on non-signature fields. For signature appearances, most signing workflows build the appearance stream manually using a PDFormXObject — Chapter 15 covers this in detail.

section.title: What XFA Was and Why It Was Deprecated
section.simpara: XFA (XML Forms Architecture) was Adobe’s attempt to replace the limited AcroForm system with a powerful XML-based form description language. An XFA form embeds XML data inside the PDF file (in the /XFA key of the AcroForm dictionary), and a viewer that supports XFA renders the form from XML rather than from the AcroForm field tree. XFA could do things AcroForm could not: dynamic layouts, complex validation rules, repeating sections, and data binding to external schemas.

ISO 32000-2:2020 deprecated XFA for three reasons: (1) only Adobe implemented it fully — interoperability was abysmal; (2) XFA and AcroForm coexist awkwardly in the same file, with the two representations frequently disagreeing about field values; and (3) XFA’s scripting capabilities have been exploited in multiple CVEs.

section.title: Why XFA Still Causes Problems
section.simpara: Deprecation does not mean disappearance. Millions of XFA-based PDF forms are still in circulation — government tax forms, insurance applications, healthcare records. When these documents are signed, the signature covers the entire file, including the XFA data.
The problems:

listitem.simpara: PDFBox does not support XFA rendering. If you load an XFA document in PDFBox, you get the AcroForm fallback. The field values you see may differ from what an XFA-aware viewer shows. If you sign based on the AcroForm representation, the signature covers bytes that an XFA viewer interprets differently.

Field names may not match. XFA and AcroForm can use different naming conventions. A signature field named form1[0].#subform[0].SignatureField1[0] in XFA might be SignatureField1 in the AcroForm fallback.
Validation ambiguity. If the XFA representation says one thing and the signed AcroForm says another, which one did the signer actually see and intend to sign?

tip.simpara: If you encounter an XFA document in a signing workflow, the safest approach is to flatten the XFA to pure AcroForm (removing the /XFA key and ensuring all field values are represented in the AcroForm structure) before any signatures are applied. PDFBox’s PDAcroForm.flatten() can help, but test thoroughly — XFA flattening is lossy by nature.

chapter.title: Form Flattening and Signatures
chapter.simpara: Form flattening converts interactive fields into static page content: field values are "burned in" to page content streams, widget annotations are removed, and the AcroForm structure is deleted. The result is a static document that looks like the filled-in form but cannot be edited.

Flattening destroys every signature in the document. The signature fields themselves are removed (they are form fields), the byte ranges no longer point at the original bytes, and the AcroForm structure referencing the signatures is gone.

warning.simpara: Never flatten a signed document. This sounds obvious, but it happens constantly in automated workflows — sign, then flatten "for archival" or "to prevent editing." The result is a static PDF with no valid signatures. If you need to prevent editing after signing, use field locking (/Lock) and certification signatures (/DocMDP) — not flattening.

section.title: PDAcroForm: The Entry Point
section.simpara: In PDFBox 3.0.0, the AcroForm is accessed through the document catalog:

note.simpara: acroForm.getFields() returns only top-level fields. To traverse the full tree including children, use acroForm.getFieldTree(), which returns a flat iterable over every field in the hierarchy.

section.title: Field Tree Traversal
section.simpara: acroForm.getFieldTree() returns a flat iterable over every field in the hierarchy — almost always what you want. For finding signature fields specifically:
If you need tree depth or explicit parent-child relationships, use PDNonTerminalField.getChildren() and recurse manually.

section.title: PDSignatureField and PDSignature
section.simpara: PDSignatureField is the PDFBox class representing a /Sig field. It provides access to the signature value (the /V dictionary) through getSignature(), which returns a PDSignature or null if the field is unsigned.

important.simpara: PDAcroForm.getField(String name) uses the fully qualified name. If the field is nested (e.g., signatures.approval), you must pass the full path. Passing just approval will return null. This is one of the most common sources of "field not found" bugs.

section.title: Creating Signature Fields Programmatically
section.simpara: Creating a signature field involves three steps: create the field, create the widget annotation, and wire them into the AcroForm and page structure.

simpara.emphasis: Common mistakes when creating signature fields:

listitem.simpara: Forgetting to add the widget to the page’s /Annots array. The field exists in the AcroForm but is invisible.
Forgetting to add the field to /Fields. The widget is on the page but no viewer considers it a form field.
Not setting /SigFlags. The field is there, it gets signed, but editors may do a full save and destroy it.

Using new PDRectangle(x, y, w, h) vs. raw array. PDFBox’s PDRectangle constructor takes (x, y, width, height), but the raw PDF /Rect array is [llx lly urx ury]. PDFBox handles the conversion, but if you’re debugging raw COS dictionaries, remember the coordinate difference.

chapter.title: Common Form/Signature Interaction Bugs
chapter.simpara: Years of debugging have revealed a predictable set of bugs that occur at the intersection of AcroForm infrastructure and signature functionality. These are not edge cases — they are the everyday reality of working with signed PDF forms.

section.title: Missing Appearance Streams
section.simpara: Symptom: Signature validates, but the rectangle on the page is blank. Cause: No /AP entry on the widget, or /NeedAppearances is true and the viewer destroyed the original appearance during regeneration. Fix: Always generate a complete PDAppearanceStream during signing and set it as the normal appearance on the widget (see Chapter 15 for details).

section.title: Wrong Field Hierarchy
section.simpara: Symptom: PDFBox finds the field but Acrobat does not, or vice versa. Cause: Inconsistent parent-child chain — a child references a parent that does not list it in /Kids, or the fully qualified name computed by concatenating /T values does not match expectations. Fix: Validate the tree bidirectionally: every child’s /Parent must list it in /Kids, and vice versa.

section.title: SigFlags Not Set or Wrong
section.simpara: Symptom: Signatures are valid, but a subsequent edit does a full save and destroys them all. Cause: /SigFlags is 0, 1, or absent. Fix: Set /SigFlags to 3 when creating the first signature field — before signing, not after.

section.title: Orphaned Signature Fields
section.simpara: Symptom: Signature panel shows a signature with no visual element on any page; or shows nothing despite signature data existing in the file. Cause: Widget is missing from a page’s /Annots (variant 1), or the field is not referenced from /Fields (variant 2). Fix: Verify both paths: field → AcroForm /Fields, and widget → page /Annots.

section.title: Acrobat vs. PDFBox Rendering Differences
section.simpara: Symptom: A form that looks perfect in Acrobat renders differently in PDFBox (or vice versa).

Common causes: Acrobat aggressively substitutes missing fonts (PDFBox does not — it uses only what is embedded or available on the system). Acrobat regenerates appearances when /NeedAppearances is true (PDFBox requires an explicit refreshAppearances() call). Acrobat is lenient about malformed COS dictionaries (a field with /FT on a non-terminal node may work in Acrobat but cause unexpected behavior in PDFBox). And Acrobat interprets /DA strings flexibly — font size 0 means "auto-size," missing color operators are silently defaulted — while PDFBox parses more strictly.

tip.simpara: When debugging cross-viewer differences, compare the raw COS structure. In PDFBox: field.getCOSObject().toString(). In Acrobat: Preflight tool or JavaScript console (this.getField("name").valueAsString). The differences are almost always at the COS level.

section.title: Real-World Scenario: The Government Tax Form
section.simpara: A government tax authority distributes a PDF form designed in Adobe LiveCycle Designer. The form uses XFA internally with an AcroForm fallback. A citizen fills it in using Chrome’s built-in PDF viewer (which uses the AcroForm fallback because it does not support XFA). The citizen’s tax advisor opens the filled form in Acrobat (which does support XFA), sees different field values because the XFA data was never updated, and signs the document.

The signature is technically valid — it covers the bytes that were present at signing time. But the signed content includes XFA data that disagrees with the AcroForm data. There is no clean fix short of removing XFA from the document entirely. This is precisely why ISO 32000-2:2020 deprecated XFA, and why the deprecation matters for real-world signature workflows.

partintro.simpara: The PDF coordinate system is the single most common source of signature placement bugs. It is conceptually simple — an origin at the bottom-left corner, units in points — but it collides head-on with how every screen coordinate system works, how page rotation transforms visual layout without changing coordinates, and how annotation rectangles encode position in a way that rewards precision and punishes guessing.

If you have ever placed a signature at what you thought was the bottom of a page and found it at the top, or created a perfectly valid signature that appeared as a mirror image on a rotated page, or spent an afternoon trying to figure out why your annotation rectangle produced a zero-height invisible box — this chapter is for you.

We will cover the coordinate system from first principles, work through every page box that ISO 32000-2:2020 defines, explain how page rotation interacts with coordinates, show how the Current Transformation Matrix works, and then tie it all back to signature placement with practical PDFBox code and debugging techniques.

chapter.title: User Space: The Default Coordinate System
chapter.simpara: Every PDF page has a user space coordinate system defined in ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 8.3.2. The rules are few but non-negotiable:

listitem.simpara: The origin is at the bottom-left corner of the page.
The x-axis increases to the right.
The y-axis increases upward.
One unit equals 1/72 of an inch — which is exactly one typographic point.

chapter.simpara: That last property is not a coincidence. PDF was born as a page description language for printing, and the typographic point has been the standard unit of measure in the printing industry since the 18th century. When the specification says a page is 612 units wide and 792 units tall, that’s 8.5 by 11 inches — US Letter size. A4 paper (the ISO 216 standard used nearly everywhere outside the US) is 595.28 by 841.89 points (210 by 297 millimetres).

note.simpara: The 1/72-inch definition is a convenient approximation. The traditional printer’s point (the Didot point, the Pica point) varies slightly depending on which historical tradition you follow. PDF sidesteps this ambiguity by defining one user space unit as exactly 1/72 inch, period. ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 8.3.2.3 makes this explicit.

chapter.simpara: The bottom-left origin is where most coordinate confusion begins. Every windowing system you have used — HTML, Java Swing, JavaFX, Android, iOS, Win32 — places the origin at the top-left corner with the y-axis increasing downward. We are trained to think of y=0 as the top. In PDF, y=0 is the bottom.

This means that when you want to place a signature 50 points from the top of a US Letter page, the y-coordinate is not 50 — it is 792 minus 50 minus the height of the signature rectangle. We will return to this conversion formula repeatedly throughout this chapter because it is the root cause of the most common signature positioning bugs.

chapter.title: Page Boxes: The Five Rectangles
chapter.simpara: A PDF page does not have a single "size." It has up to five nested rectangles, each serving a different purpose. ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 14.11.2 defines them all. Understanding which box matters for signature placement — and which boxes most documents actually define — prevents an entire category of positioning errors.

section.title: MediaBox (Required)
section.simpara: The MediaBox defines the physical medium — the full extent of the page as it would be printed or rendered. It is the only required page box; every other box defaults to the MediaBox if absent.
For a US Letter page:
section.screen: /MediaBox [0 0 612 792]
section.simpara: The array is [llx lly urx ury] — lower-left x, lower-left y, upper-right x, upper-right y. The MediaBox almost always starts at the origin [0 0 …​], but it does not have to. A MediaBox of [100 100 712 892] is perfectly legal and defines the same 612-by-792 point area, just shifted. Most tools normalize to zero, but when debugging coordinates, never assume.

section.title: CropBox
section.simpara: The CropBox defines the region to which the page contents are clipped when displayed or printed. Think of it as the visible window into the MediaBox. If absent, the CropBox defaults to the MediaBox.
A common use case: a document designed for A4 paper with crop marks. The MediaBox includes the crop marks (larger than the final page), and the CropBox clips to the actual content area.
For signature placement, the CropBox is the relevant boundary in most viewers. Adobe Acrobat positions annotations relative to the CropBox, not the MediaBox, when they differ.

warning.simpara: If you compute signature coordinates against the MediaBox but the document has a smaller CropBox, your signature may appear to be shifted or partially off-screen. Always check page.getCropBox() in PDFBox and use its dimensions as the reference frame.

section.title: BleedBox
section.simpara: The BleedBox defines the region to which the page contents are clipped when output in a production (printing) environment. It is slightly larger than the TrimBox to allow for bleed — the area where ink extends beyond the final cut edge to avoid white margins from imprecise cutting.
The BleedBox is relevant for professional printing workflows and has no meaningful impact on digital signature placement. We mention it for completeness.

section.title: TrimBox
section.simpara: The TrimBox defines the intended final dimensions of the page after trimming. In a print-production workflow, the TrimBox is the "real" page boundary — the finished product.

Like the BleedBox, the TrimBox is irrelevant for signature placement in digital workflows. However, if you are building a signing service that handles print-ready PDFs, be aware that the TrimBox may be significantly smaller than the MediaBox, and your users' mental model of "the page" aligns with the TrimBox, not the MediaBox.

section.title: ArtBox
section.simpara: The ArtBox defines the extent of the page’s "meaningful content" as intended by the author. It is rarely used in practice and has no impact on signature placement.

section.title: Which Box Matters for Signatures?
section.simpara: For digital signature placement, the decision tree is simple:

listitem.simpara: Use the CropBox as your reference frame. In PDFBox, page.getCropBox() returns the CropBox if defined, otherwise it falls back to the MediaBox. This is the correct default.
If you need to verify that your annotation rectangle falls within the visible area, check against the CropBox.
Never assume the CropBox starts at [0 0 …​]. A CropBox of [50 50 562 742] shifts the visible origin.

tip.simpara: PDFBox’s PDPage.getCropBox() handles the fallback-to-MediaBox logic automatically. You almost never need to call getMediaBox() directly for positioning purposes. The one exception is when you need to know the physical page dimensions for coordinate translation on pages with a non-zero CropBox origin.

chapter.title: Annotation Rectangles
chapter.simpara: Every annotation — including signature widget annotations — has a /Rect entry that specifies its position on the page. The /Rect is an array of four numbers:
Where [x1 y1] is the lower-left corner and [x2 y2] is the upper-right corner, both in user space coordinates relative to the page’s coordinate system.
For a signature that should appear 100 points from the left edge, 50 points from the bottom edge, and is 200 points wide by 60 points tall:
That gives us: lower-left at (100, 50), upper-right at (100+200, 50+60) = (300, 110).

important.simpara: The convention is lower-left to upper-right. If you accidentally swap these — providing upper-left to lower-right, for example — some viewers will silently normalize the rectangle, while others will render a zero-area or inverted annotation. PDFBox’s PDRectangle constructor takes (x, y, width, height) where (x, y) is the lower-left corner, so it handles this correctly if you feed it the right values.

chapter.title: Why Signature Positioning Goes Wrong
chapter.simpara: Here is the bug that every developer writes exactly once. We want a signature at the visual top-right corner of a US Letter page. In screen coordinates (top-left origin), that would be approximately (400, 30) for a rectangle starting near the top-right. So we write:
chapter.programlisting: PDRectangle rect = new PDRectangle(400, 30, 200, 50);
chapter.simpara: And the signature appears at the bottom-right of the page. Because y=30 in PDF user space is 30 points from the bottom, not 30 points from the top.
The correct conversion is:
The formula is: pdfY = pageHeight - topOffset - rectangleHeight.
This is the single most important formula in this chapter. Memorize it, put it in a utility method, and never compute it inline.

warning.simpara: If your signing UI lets users click a position on a rendered page, the click coordinates are in screen space (origin top-left). You must invert the y-axis before creating the PDF annotation rectangle. Failing to do so produces signatures that appear to be vertically "mirrored" — a signature placed at the top appears at the bottom, and vice versa.

chapter.title: Page Rotation
chapter.simpara: The /Rotate key in a page dictionary specifies a rotation angle that viewers should apply when displaying the page. Legal values are 0, 90, 180, and 270 (degrees, clockwise).

Here is the critical insight that trips up even experienced developers: page rotation does not change the coordinate system. It changes how the viewer displays the coordinates, but the coordinates themselves — and the annotation rectangles — remain in the original, unrotated space.

Consider a US Letter page with /Rotate 90. The viewer displays it in landscape orientation: 792 points wide and 612 points tall. But the MediaBox is still [0 0 612 792] and the coordinate system still has its origin at the bottom-left corner of the unrotated page.

This means that if you compute an annotation rectangle for a 90-degree-rotated page using the visual (rotated) dimensions, your coordinates will be wrong. The annotation will appear in a completely unexpected location because the viewer applies the rotation after positioning the annotation in the unrotated coordinate space.

section.title: Rotation and Signature Placement
section.simpara: To place a signature at a specific visual position on a rotated page, we must think in the unrotated coordinate space and account for the rotation.
For a page rotated 90 degrees clockwise:

listitem.simpara: The visual width (what the user sees) corresponds to the unrotated height (792 for US Letter).
The visual height corresponds to the unrotated width (612 for US Letter).
A point at visual position (vx, vy) — where vy is measured from the visual top — maps to unrotated coordinates (vy_from_bottom, vx) approximately, depending on the rotation direction.

section.simpara: The general approach in PDFBox:

tip.simpara: Wrap this coordinate transformation in a dedicated utility method and unit-test it for all four rotation values. This is the kind of code where an off-by-one error in a single case produces a bug that only manifests for rotated pages — which may be 5% of your input documents, ensuring the bug survives initial testing and surfaces in production.

chapter.title: Transformation Matrices and the CTM
chapter.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 8.3.3 defines the Current Transformation Matrix (CTM) — a 3x3 affine transformation matrix that maps user space coordinates to device space coordinates. Every content stream operates under a CTM that controls translation, scaling, rotation, and skewing.
The CTM is represented as six numbers [a b c d e f], corresponding to the matrix:
chapter.screen: | a b 0 | | c d 0 | | e f 1 |
chapter.simpara: For signature appearances, the CTM matters because the appearance stream (the Form XObject that draws the visual signature) has its own coordinate system. The /BBox of the appearance stream defines the drawing area, and the viewer maps this BBox into the annotation rectangle using a transformation derived from the rectangle dimensions and the BBox dimensions.

When the appearance stream’s BBox does not match the annotation rectangle’s aspect ratio, the viewer scales the appearance to fit — potentially distorting the signature image. If the BBox is [0 0 200 50] but the annotation rectangle is 200 by 100 points, the appearance will be stretched vertically by a factor of 2.

important.simpara: When creating signature appearances in PDFBox, always ensure the appearance stream’s BBox matches the dimensions of the annotation rectangle. If you use PDRectangle for both and create them from the same width and height values, this happens automatically. The bug occurs when one is constructed independently of the other, or when the annotation rectangle is modified after the appearance is created.

chapter.simpara: Content streams use operators like cm (concatenate matrix) to modify the CTM:

For signature placement, you rarely need to manipulate the CTM directly. PDFBox handles the coordinate mapping between the appearance stream and the annotation rectangle. But when debugging a signature that appears at the wrong size or position, checking the CTM in the appearance stream’s content is a valuable diagnostic step.

chapter.title: Signature Appearance Rectangles and Page Coordinates
chapter.simpara: The annotation rectangle (/Rect) positions the signature on the page. The appearance stream’s BBox defines the drawing canvas. The relationship between them determines how the visual signature is rendered.
Here is the complete chain:

listitem.simpara: The page has a coordinate system defined by its MediaBox (or CropBox).
The annotation’s /Rect specifies where on the page the annotation sits, in page coordinates.
The appearance stream has a /BBox that defines its internal drawing area.
The viewer computes a transformation from the BBox to the Rect — scaling and translating the appearance stream content to fill the annotation rectangle.
If the page has a /Rotate value, the viewer rotates the entire visual output after all of the above.

chapter.simpara: This chain explains why a signature appearance that looks correct on one page can appear distorted on another: if the target page has different dimensions or a different rotation, and you reuse the same appearance stream without adjusting the annotation rectangle, the scaling transformation changes.

chapter.title: PDFBox Coordinate Handling
chapter.simpara: PDFBox provides PDRectangle as the primary class for working with rectangles in PDF user space. The key methods:

note.simpara: PDRectangle predefined constants include PDRectangle.A4 (595.28 x 841.89) and PDRectangle.LETTER (612 x 792). These are useful for creating new pages but should never be used as the reference for positioning on an existing page — always query the actual page’s CropBox.

section.title: Practical Example: Placing a Visible Signature
section.simpara: The following complete example places a visible signature rectangle at a specific visual position on a page, correctly handling rotation:
Call this with visual coordinates — "200 points from the left, 50 points from the top, 180 wide, 40 tall" — and it returns the correct PDF rectangle regardless of rotation.

chapter.title: Common Coordinate Bugs
chapter.simpara: Over years of building PDF signing tools, we have seen the same bugs recur. Here is a catalog.

section.title: Flipped Signatures (Y-axis Inversion)
section.simpara: Symptom: The signature appears at the vertical opposite of where it should be. A signature placed near the top of the page appears near the bottom.
Cause: Using screen-space y-coordinates (origin top-left) directly as PDF y-coordinates (origin bottom-left) without inverting.
Fix: Apply the formula pdfY = cropBox.getUpperRightY() - screenY - signatureHeight.

section.title: Signatures on the Wrong Page Position (Rotated Pages)
section.simpara: Symptom: The signature appears in a completely unexpected location — not just vertically flipped, but seemingly random — and only on certain documents.

Cause: The page has a non-zero /Rotate value, and the coordinate calculation does not account for it. Many developers test exclusively with non-rotated pages and never encounter this until a customer submits a rotated document.
Fix: Always check page.getRotation() and apply the rotation-aware coordinate transformation shown above.

section.title: Zero-Area Annotation (Invisible Signature Becomes Truly Invisible)
section.simpara: Symptom: The signature field exists (it shows in the signature panel), but nothing is visible on the page.

Cause: The annotation rectangle has zero width or height, typically because the lower-left and upper-right corners are identical, or because a coordinate calculation produced a negative dimension that the viewer clamped to zero.
Fix: Always validate that the computed rectangle has positive width and height before creating the annotation. Log the rectangle values during development:

section.title: Signature Appears Off-Page
section.simpara: Symptom: The signature field exists, the signature validates, but nothing is visible. Unlike the zero-area case, the rectangle has positive dimensions — it’s just outside the visible CropBox.
Cause: Coordinates were computed against the MediaBox, but the CropBox is smaller. Or the CropBox has a non-zero origin that was not accounted for.
Fix: Always compute against page.getCropBox() and include the CropBox’s lower-left offset in your calculations.

section.title: Distorted Signature Appearance
section.simpara: Symptom: The signature image or text appears stretched or squashed.
Cause: The appearance stream’s BBox has a different aspect ratio than the annotation rectangle. The viewer scales the BBox to fit the Rect, distorting the content.
Fix: Ensure the appearance stream’s BBox dimensions match the annotation rectangle’s width and height.

chapter.title: Debugging Coordinates with QPDF and RUPS
chapter.simpara: When a signature appears in the wrong place, you need to inspect the raw PDF objects. Two tools are indispensable.

section.title: QPDF
section.simpara: QPDF (https://qpdf.sourceforge.io/) is a command-line tool that can dump every object in a PDF in a human-readable JSON format:
section.programlisting: qpdf --json input.pdf > /tmp/structure.json
section.simpara: Then search for the annotation rectangle:
section.programlisting: grep -A2 '"Rect"' /tmp/structure.json
section.simpara: You will see the raw /Rect array values. Compare them against the page’s /MediaBox and /CropBox to verify they fall within the visible area.
QPDF can also show page-level dictionaries:
section.programlisting: qpdf --show-object=3 input.pdf # show object 3 (a page, for example)

section.title: RUPS (Reading and Updating PDF Syntax)
section.simpara: RUPS is a Java Swing application from the iText team that provides a tree view of every PDF object. It is invaluable for visual debugging because you can navigate the object tree, inspect annotation rectangles, and see the page boxes side by side.
To inspect a signature’s coordinates in RUPS:

listitem.simpara: Open the PDF in RUPS.
Navigate to Pages → Page N → Annots (the annotations array).
Find the signature widget annotation.
Check the /Rect value — this is the annotation rectangle in page coordinates.
Go back to the page and check /MediaBox, /CropBox, and /Rotate.
Verify that the Rect falls within the CropBox and that you’ve accounted for the rotation.

tip.simpara: RUPS also lets you inspect the appearance stream’s BBox. Navigate into the annotation’s /AP → /N and check the /BBox entry. If the BBox dimensions don’t match the Rect dimensions, you’ve found your distortion bug.

section.title: PDFBox Programmatic Inspection
section.simpara: You can also inspect coordinates programmatically, which is useful in automated tests:

caution.simpara: When writing integration tests for signature placement, always test with at least four documents: one for each rotation value (0, 90, 180, 270). Also test with a document that has a CropBox smaller than its MediaBox. These five test cases catch the vast majority of coordinate bugs before they reach production.

chapter.title: Summary
chapter.simpara: The PDF coordinate system is a solved problem — the rules are clear and have not changed since PDF 1.0. The difficulty lies entirely in the mismatch between PDF’s bottom-left origin and the top-left origin of every UI framework, compounded by page rotation’s effect on visual layout.
For signature placement, remember these principles:

listitem.simpara: Always use the CropBox (via page.getCropBox()) as your reference frame.
Always invert the y-axis when converting from screen coordinates: pdfY = cropBoxTop - screenY - height.
Always check page.getRotation() and transform coordinates accordingly.
Always ensure the appearance stream BBox matches the annotation rectangle dimensions.
Always test with rotated pages and non-standard CropBoxes.

chapter.simpara: These are not complex algorithms. They are simple arithmetic applied consistently. The bugs come from skipping a step, not from the math being hard.

partintro.simpara: Fonts are the invisible infrastructure of every PDF document. When they work, nobody notices. When they break, the document becomes unreadable — and if that document contains a digital signature, the signature appearance becomes a row of blank rectangles or missing-glyph squares that make your legally binding signature look like a rendering error.

This chapter explains how fonts work inside PDF files, how they get embedded (or not), what goes wrong when they don’t, and how all of this matters specifically for digital signatures. We cover the font types defined by ISO 32000-2:2020, the encoding models, the mechanics of embedding and subsetting, and the practical reality of getting text to render correctly in signature appearance streams across different viewers.

If you have ever seen a signature block that shows .notdef squares where a signer’s name should be, or a PDF where the signature text vanishes entirely on one viewer but renders fine on another, this chapter explains why.

chapter.title: Why Fonts Matter for Digital Signatures
chapter.simpara: A PDF digital signature has two independent representations: the cryptographic representation (the CMS SignedData structure, the byte range, the certificate chain) and the visual representation (the appearance stream that shows the signer’s name, date, and reason on the page). Chapter 15 covers how appearance streams are structured; this chapter covers the font infrastructure that makes the text inside those appearances render correctly.

The visual appearance of a signature is a Form XObject — a miniature PDF page with its own resource dictionary, its own content stream, and its own font references. When that content stream contains a Tj or TJ operator to draw text, the PDF viewer must resolve the font reference, find the glyph outlines, and render them. If any step in that chain fails — the font reference points to a font that isn’t embedded, the encoding doesn’t map the character code to the right glyph, the font descriptor is missing, the font file stream is corrupt — the text doesn’t render.
This matters more for signatures than for regular document text, for three reasons:

listitem.simpara: Signature appearances are generated programmatically. When a human creates a PDF in a word processor, the word processor embeds the fonts it uses. When your signing code creates a signature appearance, your code is responsible for font handling. If you get it wrong, nobody catches the error until a user reports that the signature looks broken.

Signature appearances must survive across viewers.
