A signature created on a server using PDFBox must render correctly in Adobe Acrobat, Foxit Reader, browser-based viewers using pdf.js, LibreOffice, and Preview.app. Each viewer has its own font fallback behavior, its own interpretation of encoding, and its own handling of missing fonts. The only way to guarantee consistent rendering is to embed the font.

Signature text often contains names with non-ASCII characters. Signer names like "Muller" are fine. Signer names like "Muller" with an umlaut, "Gonzalez" with an accent, or any East Asian, Arabic, or Cyrillic name will fail if the font and encoding aren’t set up correctly. In a global signing system, "works for ASCII names" is not good enough.

chapter.title: Font Types in PDF
chapter.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 9.6 defines several font types. The distinctions matter because each type has different embedding rules, different encoding models, and different failure modes.

section.title: Type 1 Fonts (PostScript Era)
section.simpara: Type 1 fonts are the original PostScript outline fonts, defined by Adobe in 1984. They use cubic Bezier curves for glyph outlines and a custom PostScript-based encoding scheme.

In PDF, a Type 1 font dictionary has /Subtype /Type1 and references a font program (the glyph outlines) via the /FontDescriptor dictionary’s /FontFile entry. The font program is a stream containing the PostScript font data in PFB (Printer Font Binary) format.

Type 1 fonts are limited to a maximum of 256 glyphs per font instance — a consequence of the single-byte encoding model inherited from PostScript. This makes them unsuitable for CJK scripts, complex scripts like Arabic or Devanagari, or any language that needs more than 256 distinct glyphs.

note.simpara: Adobe officially ended support for Type 1 fonts in January 2023, removing the ability to author content with Type 1 fonts in their Creative Cloud applications. Existing PDFs with embedded Type 1 font programs still render correctly (the outlines are in the file), but this signals the industry’s long-term direction: TrueType and OpenType are the future.

section.title: The Standard 14 Fonts
section.simpara: PDF defines fourteen fonts that conforming readers are expected to have available without embedding. These are the Standard 14 fonts, specified in ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 9.6.2.2:

entry.simpara: Sans-serif. The default choice for most programmatic PDF generation.
Serif. Common in academic and legal documents.
Monospace. Used for code listings and form data.

section.simpara: The critical word is "expected." ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 9.6.2.2 states that conforming readers should provide these fonts, but PDF 2.0 actually recommends embedding even the Standard 14 fonts to ensure consistent rendering. The practical reality is that most viewers do provide metrics-compatible substitutes for these fonts, but the substitutes are not identical — a document typeset with Helvetica on one viewer may render with Arial or Liberation Sans on another, causing slight differences in character widths, line breaks, and text positioning.

For signature appearances, these differences are usually cosmetic and acceptable. But if your signature appearance calculates text positions based on precise Helvetica metrics (e.g., centering text in the signature rectangle), a substitute font with slightly different widths will misalign the text.

tip.simpara: For signature appearances, the Standard 14 fonts are "good enough" for ASCII text. They are available everywhere, they don’t require embedding, and the rendering differences between substitute fonts are typically invisible at the font sizes used in signature blocks (8-12 points).

For non-ASCII text — accented characters, umlauts, CJK characters — the Standard 14 fonts are not sufficient, because their built-in encoding (WinAnsiEncoding) cannot represent characters outside the Windows-1252 code page. If your signers have names with characters outside that range, you must embed a TrueType or OpenType font with the appropriate glyph coverage.

section.title: PDFBox 3.x and the Standard 14 API Change
section.simpara: If you’ve migrated from PDFBox 2.x to 3.x, you’ve likely encountered this:
section.programlisting: // PDFBox 2.x -- this no longer compiles in 3.x PDFont font = PDType1Font.HELVETICA;
section.simpara: In PDFBox 2.x, the Standard 14 fonts were available as static constants on PDType1Font. PDFBox 3.x removed these constants and replaced them with an enum-based API:

The reason for this change is that the old static constants were shared mutable objects — every caller that obtained PDType1Font.HELVETICA got the same instance, which could cause subtle threading and state pollution issues when multiple documents were processed concurrently. The new constructor creates a fresh instance each time, eliminating the shared-state problem.

warning.simpara: If you search the internet for PDFBox font examples, the majority of results still show the 2.x API (PDType1Font.HELVETICA). These examples will not compile with PDFBox 3.0.0. Always verify that code examples match the major version of PDFBox you are using.

section.title: TrueType Fonts
section.simpara: TrueType fonts, developed jointly by Apple and Microsoft in the late 1980s as a competitor to Adobe’s Type 1, use quadratic Bezier curves for glyph outlines. They are the most common font format in modern computing and the recommended choice for PDF font embedding.

In PDF, a TrueType font dictionary has /Subtype /TrueType. The font program is embedded via the /FontDescriptor dictionary’s /FontFile2 entry, which contains the raw TrueType font data (a .ttf file, essentially).

TrueType fonts in PDF can be either simple (single-byte encoding, max 256 glyphs per instance) or embedded as part of a composite font (Type 0 + CIDFont, described below) for full Unicode coverage. When used as a simple font, TrueType has the same 256-glyph limitation as Type 1. The composite font mechanism removes this limitation.

PDType0Font.load() is the PDFBox 3.x method for loading TrueType and OpenType fonts. Despite the name suggesting Type 0, this method creates the full composite font structure (Type 0 wrapper + CIDFont descendant) needed for Unicode text support. The font file is embedded in the document, and PDFBox automatically subsets it on save.

section.title: Type 0 / CIDFont (Composite Fonts)
section.simpara: Type 0 fonts — also called composite fonts — are the mechanism PDF uses to support large character sets (CJK scripts, Arabic, Devanagari, and any script requiring more than 256 glyphs).
A Type 0 font consists of two linked dictionaries:

listitem.simpara: The Type 0 font dictionary (/Subtype /Type0): the "wrapper" that the content stream references. It contains a /DescendantFonts array pointing to the CIDFont dictionary and a /Encoding entry specifying the CMap that maps character codes to CIDs (Character IDs).

The CIDFont dictionary (/Subtype /CIDFontType0 or /CIDFontType2): the actual font data. CIDFontType0 wraps a PostScript CID-keyed font; CIDFontType2 wraps a TrueType font. In practice, CIDFontType2 (TrueType-based) is overwhelmingly more common.

section.screen:
% Type 0 composite font structure in a PDF 10 0 obj << /Type /Font /Subtype /Type0 /BaseFont /ABCDEF+DejaVuSans /Encoding /Identity-H /DescendantFonts [11 0 R] /ToUnicode 12 0 R >> endobj
11 0 obj << /Type /Font /Subtype /CIDFontType2 /BaseFont /ABCDEF+DejaVuSans /CIDSystemInfo << /Registry (Adobe) /Ordering (Identity) /Supplement 0 >> /FontDescriptor 13 0 R /W [3 [278] 36 [722] 68 [556 500]] >> endobj

callout.para: The /BaseFont name includes a subset prefix (ABCDEF+) — we’ll explain this below
/Identity-H means character codes map directly to CIDs (horizontal writing mode)
The descendant CIDFont dictionary
The /ToUnicode CMap enables text extraction — critical for accessibility and search
CIDFontType2: the actual glyph data comes from an embedded TrueType font
The /W (Widths) array defines per-glyph widths for precise text positioning

section.simpara: When PDFBox’s PDType0Font.load() embeds a TrueType font, it creates exactly this structure. You don’t need to build it manually, but understanding it helps when debugging font issues in existing PDFs.

chapter.title: Font Embedding vs. Referencing
chapter.simpara: There are two ways a PDF can use a font: embed it (include the font program inside the PDF file) or reference it (include only the font name and metrics, relying on the viewer to provide the actual glyphs).

section.title: Referenced Fonts
section.simpara: A referenced font includes the font dictionary and metrics but not the font program. The viewer must find a matching font on the system or use a fallback. The Standard 14 fonts are the canonical example — they’re designed to be used without embedding because every viewer is expected to have compatible substitutes.

Referenced non-standard fonts are a recipe for rendering failures. If your PDF references "CompanyBrandFont-Regular" without embedding it, the viewer will substitute whatever it can find — usually a generic serif or sans-serif font — and the text will look wrong. Character widths will differ, glyphs may be missing, and text positioning will be off.
For signature appearances, never reference non-standard fonts. The signing server has the font file; the recipient’s PDF viewer probably doesn’t.

section.title: Embedded Fonts
section.simpara: An embedded font includes the complete font program (or a subset of it) as a stream inside the PDF. The viewer uses the embedded data to render glyphs, regardless of what fonts are installed on the system.

Embedding guarantees consistent rendering across all viewers and all platforms. It increases the PDF file size — a full TrueType font can be several megabytes — but subsetting (described below) reduces the impact dramatically.
The font program is stored as a stream referenced by the /FontDescriptor dictionary:

table.title: Font embedding streams by font type — ISO 32000-2:2020 Table 123

entry.simpara: PFB (Printer Font Binary) data — the Type 1 font program
CFF (Compact Font Format) data, or OpenType font program. The stream’s /Subtype distinguishes: /CIDFontType0C for CID-keyed CFF, /Type1C for simple CFF, /OpenType for OpenType.

important.simpara: The distinction between /FontFile, /FontFile2, and /FontFile3 is not just metadata — the stream contents are in fundamentally different binary formats. A viewer that tries to parse a CFF stream as TrueType (or vice versa) will get corrupted glyph outlines or crash. Getting the key wrong is a common source of font rendering failures in programmatically generated PDFs.

chapter.title: Font Subsetting
chapter.simpara: Embedding a full font file — especially a CJK font with tens of thousands of glyphs — adds significant bulk to the PDF. Font subsetting solves this by embedding only the glyphs actually used in the document.

A typical signature appearance uses fewer than 100 distinct glyphs (the signer’s name, a date, a reason string). Subsetting a 2 MB TrueType font down to just those glyphs produces a subset of perhaps 10-30 KB.

section.title: The Subset Prefix Convention
section.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 9.6.4 defines a naming convention for subsetted fonts: the /BaseFont name is prefixed with six uppercase letters followed by a plus sign.
section.screen: /BaseFont /ABCDEF+DejaVuSans
section.simpara: The prefix "ABCDEF+" indicates that this is a subset — it does not contain all glyphs from the original DejaVuSans font. The six letters are arbitrary (PDFBox generates them randomly); their only purpose is to make the subset’s /BaseFont name unique within the document, preventing different subsets of the same font from being confused with each other.
A font without the subset prefix:
section.screen: /BaseFont /DejaVuSans
section.simpara: This indicates the full font is embedded (or the font is referenced without embedding).

tip.simpara: When debugging font issues, the subset prefix is your first clue. If you see /ABCDEF+Helvetica, the font is subsetted. If a glyph is missing from the rendered output, it may not have been included in the subset — meaning the glyph was not used (or not known to be used) when the subset was created. This is a common bug when signature text is modified after the font was subsetted.

section.title: How PDFBox Handles Subsetting
section.simpara: PDFBox 3.0.0 performs automatic font subsetting when saving a document. When you load a TrueType font with PDType0Font.load(), PDFBox tracks which glyphs are used during text drawing operations and includes only those glyphs in the final embedded subset.

There is one catch. If you write raw content stream operators directly (as we must for signature appearance Form XObjects in PDFBox 3.x, since PDPageContentStream does not support Form XObjects), PDFBox may not automatically track glyph usage. In this case, you must encode the text through the font to ensure the glyph tracking is triggered:

warning.simpara: If you bypass PDFBox’s text encoding and write raw character codes directly into the content stream, the subset may not include all required glyphs. The result: some characters render as .notdef (the missing glyph symbol, typically a small rectangle) in viewers. This is one of the most common causes of broken signature appearances.

chapter.title: Encoding
chapter.simpara: Encoding is the mapping between character codes (the byte values in the content stream) and glyphs (the visual shapes the viewer renders). Getting encoding right is essential for correct text rendering, and getting it wrong is the most common cause of garbled or missing text in PDF documents.

section.title: Simple Font Encoding (Single-Byte)
section.simpara: For simple fonts (Type 1 and simple TrueType), each character code is a single byte (0-255). The encoding maps each byte value to a glyph name, and the font program maps glyph names to outlines.
PDF defines two built-in encodings:

WinAnsiEncoding (ISO 32000-2:2020 Table D.1): Corresponds closely to Windows-1252. Covers Western European characters: basic Latin, accented characters (a, e, u, n, etc.), and common symbols. This is the default for most PDF generators and the encoding used by the Standard 14 fonts.
MacRomanEncoding (ISO 32000-2:2020 Table D.2): The classic Macintosh character set. Rarely used in modern PDFs, but you’ll encounter it in documents created by older Mac applications.
A font dictionary can also specify a custom encoding via the /Encoding key, using a /Differences array that overrides individual code-to-glyph mappings:
section.screen: /Encoding << /Type /Encoding /BaseEncoding /WinAnsiEncoding /Differences [ 128 /Euro 160 /space /exclamdown /cent /sterling ] >>

callout.para: Maps byte value 128 to the Euro sign glyph, overriding the WinAnsiEncoding mapping

section.title: The 256-Glyph Wall
section.simpara: Single-byte encoding means a simple font can address at most 256 glyphs. This is fine for English and most Western European languages, but it’s a hard wall for:

listitem.simpara: CJK languages: Chinese alone requires thousands of glyphs
Arabic and Hebrew: need contextual glyph shaping (the same character looks different depending on position in a word)
Devanagari, Thai, Georgian: scripts with large glyph inventories
Mixed-script text: a signature appearance showing a name in Cyrillic with a date in Latin

section.simpara: For any of these, you need a composite font (Type 0 + CIDFont) with a multi-byte encoding.

section.title: Composite Font Encoding
section.simpara: Composite fonts use a CMap (Character Map) to map multi-byte character codes to CIDs. The most common CMap in modern PDFs is /Identity-H, which maps character codes directly to CIDs as a simple identity function — code 0 maps to CID 0, code 1 to CID 1, and so on.

With /Identity-H, the character codes in the content stream are raw CID values encoded as two-byte big-endian integers. This gives access to the full 65,536 CID range, more than enough for any single font.

The downside of /Identity-H is that the character codes have no inherent semantic meaning — they’re just glyph indices. Without additional mapping information, a viewer cannot extract the text content or determine what Unicode characters the glyphs represent. That’s where the /ToUnicode CMap comes in.

chapter.title: The ToUnicode CMap
chapter.simpara: The /ToUnicode entry in a font dictionary maps character codes to Unicode values. It is not used for rendering — the viewer uses the font’s glyph outlines for that. Instead, it is used for text extraction: copy-paste, search, and accessibility.
For signature appearances, the /ToUnicode CMap matters because:

listitem.simpara: Accessibility tools read the /ToUnicode CMap to extract text from the appearance stream. A signature appearance without /ToUnicode mappings will appear as empty or garbled text to screen readers.

PDF validators (including veraPDF) check for the presence of /ToUnicode CMaps as a compliance requirement for PDF/A. If your signed PDFs must be PDF/A-compliant, the fonts in your signature appearance must have proper /ToUnicode entries.
Text search in the document depends on /ToUnicode. Without it, searching for the signer’s name will not find the text in the signature appearance.

chapter.simpara: A /ToUnicode CMap is a PDF stream containing a CMap program in PostScript-like syntax:
chapter.screen: /CIDInit /ProcSet findresource begin 12 dict begin begincmap /CIDSystemInfo << /Registry (Adobe) /Ordering (UCS) /Supplement 0 >> def /CMapName /Adobe-Identity-UCS def /CMapType 2 def 1 begincodespacerange <0000> <FFFF> endcodespacerange 5 beginbfchar <0024> <0044> % CID 36 -> U+0044 (D) <004C> <0069> % CID 76 -> U+0069 (i) <004E> <0067> % CID 78 -> U+0067 (g) endbfchar endcmap CMapName currentdict /CMap defineresource pop end end
chapter.simpara: PDFBox generates the /ToUnicode CMap automatically when you use PDType0Font.load() and write text through the font’s encoding methods. You do not need to create it manually — but understanding the structure helps when you’re debugging a PDF where text extraction returns garbled results.

important.simpara: In PDFBox 3.x, the method PDFont.getToUnicodeCMap() is not publicly accessible. To inspect the /ToUnicode CMap of a font, you must access the COS dictionary directly: font.getCOSObject().getDictionaryObject(COSName.TO_UNICODE). This is a common stumbling block when writing font inspection or diagnostic tools.

chapter.title: Font Descriptors
chapter.simpara: Every embedded font (and many referenced fonts) has a font descriptor dictionary (/Type /FontDescriptor) that provides metrics and metadata about the font. The font descriptor is essential — it tells the viewer how to render the font even before it looks at the glyph outlines.

table.title: Key entries in the font descriptor — ISO 32000-2:2020 Table 122

entry.simpara: The PostScript name of the font (e.g., /DejaVuSans)
Bit field describing the font: FixedPitch (bit 1), Serif (bit 2), Symbolic (bit 3), Script (bit 4), Nonsymbolic (bit 6), Italic (bit 7), AllCap (bit 17), SmallCap (bit 18), ForceBold (bit 19)
The bounding box of all glyphs: [llx lly urx ury] in glyph coordinate space
Angle of the italic slant (0 for upright fonts)
The maximum height above the baseline
The maximum depth below the baseline (typically negative)
The dominant stem width (vertical strokes) — affects rendering weight
CFF or OpenType font program (with /Subtype to distinguish)

note.simpara: A font descriptor with all metrics but no font file stream describes a referenced font — the viewer must find a substitute. If the descriptor includes a font file stream, the font is embedded. Checking whether /FontFile, /FontFile2, or /FontFile3 exists is the definitive way to determine if a font is embedded.

chapter.title: PDFBox Font Handling in 3.x
chapter.simpara: PDFBox 3.0.0 made several breaking changes to its font API compared to 2.x. Understanding these changes is essential for writing correct font handling code.

section.title: Loading Fonts
section.simpara: The false parameter to PDType0Font.load() disables subsetting. This is rarely needed, but can be useful during debugging to rule out subsetting as the cause of missing glyphs.

section.title: The getEncoding() Removal
section.simpara: PDFBox 2.x provided a PDFont.getEncoding() method that returned the font’s encoding object. PDFBox 3.x removed this method.
If you need to inspect a font’s encoding in PDFBox 3.0.0, you must access the COS dictionary directly:

section.title: Inspecting Font Embedding Programmatically
section.simpara: A common diagnostic task is checking whether a font in a PDF is embedded. Here’s a complete example:

chapter.title: Why Signature Text Shows as Squares or Blanks
chapter.simpara: This section addresses the most common font-related failures in signature appearances, what causes them, and how to fix them.

section.title: Missing Font
section.simpara: Symptom: The signature block is empty, or shows substituted text in a different font than intended.
Cause: The signature appearance references a font that is not embedded and not available on the viewer’s system.

Fix: Always embed fonts used in signature appearances. For basic ASCII text, the Standard 14 fonts are a safe choice because viewers provide substitutes. For anything else, embed a TrueType font using PDType0Font.load().

section.title: Wrong Encoding
section.simpara: Symptom: Some characters render correctly, but accented characters (e, u, n) show as wrong characters or .notdef squares.

Cause: The text contains characters outside the font’s encoding range. WinAnsiEncoding covers codes 32-255 corresponding to Windows-1252, which includes most Western European accented characters. But characters outside this range — em-dash at certain positions, certain ligatures, or any non-Latin script — will fail.
Fix: Use a composite font (Type 0) with /Identity-H encoding and an embedded TrueType font. This provides full Unicode coverage. In PDFBox: use PDType0Font.load() instead of the Standard 14 fonts.

section.title: Unembedded Font with Non-Standard Glyphs
section.simpara: Symptom: Text renders as squares or blank space on some viewers but works on others.

Cause: The font is referenced but not embedded. Viewers that happen to have a compatible font installed render the text correctly; viewers without the font substitute a generic font that may not have the required glyphs.
Fix: Embed the font.

section.title: Subset Missing Glyphs
section.simpara: Symptom: Most text renders correctly, but specific characters are missing.

Cause: The font was subsetted, but the subset does not include all required glyphs. This typically happens when text is written to the content stream after the font subsetting decision was made, or when glyph usage tracking was bypassed.

Fix: Ensure all text is written through PDFBox’s font encoding methods before saving. If the problem persists, try loading the font with subsetting disabled (PDType0Font.load(document, file, false)) to confirm subsetting is the cause.

caution.simpara: When writing raw content stream operators for a Form XObject (which PDFBox 3.x requires for signature appearances), make sure to encode text through the font before writing the encoded bytes to the stream. Skipping this step means PDFBox won’t know which glyphs are needed and may produce a subset that’s missing characters.

chapter.title: Font Licensing and the fsType Flag
chapter.simpara: TrueType and OpenType fonts contain a licensing flag called fsType in the OS/2 table. This flag indicates what the font vendor permits in terms of embedding and editing:

entry.simpara: No restrictions. The font may be embedded and used freely.
The font must not be embedded in documents. Period.
The font may be embedded in read-only documents. The recipient can view and print but should not edit.
The font may be embedded, and the recipient may edit the document.

chapter.simpara: Most free and open-source fonts (DejaVu, Noto, Liberation) have fsType 0x0000 (Installable). Many commercial fonts (fonts you pay for) have restrictive fsType values.
PDF generators typically respect the fsType flag — PDFBox will emit a warning (but not throw an exception) when embedding a font with restrictive fsType. Some tools refuse to embed the font entirely.
For signature appearances:

listitem.simpara: Use fonts with fsType 0x0000 (Installable) to avoid any licensing complications.

The Google Noto font family (Noto Sans, Noto Serif, Noto Sans CJK) covers virtually every script in Unicode and is Apache-2.0 licensed with fsType 0x0000. It is the best general-purpose choice for internationalized signature text.
DejaVu Sans covers Latin, Greek, Cyrillic, Armenian, Georgian, and many symbols. Free, well-tested, and widely deployed.
Liberation Sans/Serif/Mono are metrics-compatible with Arial/Times New Roman/Courier New. Licensed under SIL Open Font License.

warning.simpara: Even if your PDF generator embeds a font with restrictive fsType, the PDF viewer will render it. The fsType flag is a licensing statement, not a technical enforcement mechanism. But distributing PDFs with improperly embedded commercial fonts exposes your organization to licensing claims. For server-side signing operations that produce thousands of PDFs, use only fonts whose license explicitly permits embedding.

chapter.title: Practical Advice for Signature Appearances
chapter.simpara: Drawing from everything in this chapter, here is the decision framework for choosing and configuring fonts in signature appearance streams:

section.title: For ASCII-Only Signatures
section.simpara: If your signer names, dates, and reasons are guaranteed to be ASCII only (a-z, A-Z, 0-9, basic punctuation):
section.programlisting: // Simple, reliable, zero-cost approach PDFont font = new PDType1Font(Standard14Fonts.FontName.HELVETICA);
section.simpara: This works everywhere. No embedding needed, no licensing concerns, no subsetting complications. The text will render in Helvetica or a metrics-compatible substitute on every viewer.

section.title: For International Signatures
section.simpara: If signer names may contain non-ASCII characters (accented Latin, Cyrillic, CJK, Arabic, or anything beyond Windows-1252):

Bundle the font file with your application (in src/main/resources/fonts/ for a Maven project). Do not rely on system-installed fonts — the signing server may not have the same fonts as your development machine.

section.title: Font Size Guidelines
section.simpara: Signature appearance text is typically rendered at small sizes (8-12 points) in a rectangle that’s often only 150-300 points wide. Keep these guidelines in mind:

listitem.simpara: 10pt is a good default for signer name and date
8pt works for secondary information (reason, location, certificate details)
Never go below 7pt — some viewers render small text poorly, and the text becomes unreadable when printed
Calculate text width before rendering to ensure it fits the signature rectangle; truncation or overflow beyond the bounding box results in clipped text that viewers may or may not display

chapter.title: Debugging Font Issues
chapter.simpara: When a signature appearance renders incorrectly, the following diagnostic steps will identify the root cause.

section.title: Step 1: Identify the Font
section.simpara: Open the PDF in a tool that shows the raw object structure (PDFalyzer, QPDF, or PDFDebugger from PDFBox’s tools module). Navigate to the signature field’s widget annotation, then to /AP /N (the normal appearance stream), then to /Resources /Font. Note the font name, subtype, and whether a font descriptor with a font file stream exists.

section.title: Step 2: Check Embedding
section.simpara: Look for /FontFile, /FontFile2, or /FontFile3 in the font descriptor. If none exists, the font is not embedded — that’s likely the problem.

section.title: Step 3: Check Encoding
section.simpara: For simple fonts, check the /Encoding entry. Is it /WinAnsiEncoding? If the problematic characters are outside Windows-1252, the encoding is the cause.
For composite fonts, check the /Encoding entry on the Type 0 dictionary. It should be /Identity-H for a properly structured modern PDF.

section.title: Step 4: Check the ToUnicode CMap
section.simpara: If the text renders correctly but copy-paste or search returns garbled results, the /ToUnicode CMap is missing or incorrect.

section.title: Step 5: veraPDF Validation
section.simpara: veraPDF provides font-specific validation rules that catch a wide range of font issues:
section.programlisting: # Run veraPDF validation focusing on font issues verapdf --format text --flavour 2b input.pdf 2>&1 | grep -i font
section.simpara: veraPDF checks for embedded fonts (required for PDF/A), valid /ToUnicode mappings, correct font descriptor entries, and consistent glyph widths. Its validation messages are often the fastest way to identify exactly what’s wrong with a font configuration.

section.title: Step 6: PDFBox Font Inspection
section.simpara: For programmatic inspection, PDFBox can enumerate all fonts in a page or appearance stream:

tip.simpara: When debugging font issues in production, always capture the raw bytes of the appearance stream and the font dictionary’s COS structure. The visual symptom (squares, blanks, wrong characters) tells you something is wrong; the COS structure tells you what. A tool that shows both the rendered appearance and the underlying object tree — the way PDFalyzer does — saves enormous debugging time compared to flipping between a viewer and a hex editor.

chapter.title: Summary
chapter.simpara: Fonts in PDF are a deceptively deep topic. For digital signatures specifically, the key takeaways are:

listitem.simpara: Embed your fonts. The Standard 14 fonts are safe for ASCII text; everything else must be embedded.
Use composite fonts (Type 0) for non-ASCII text. Simple fonts are limited to 256 glyphs.
Encode text through the font API to ensure glyph tracking for subsetting works correctly.
Use freely licensed fonts (Noto Sans, DejaVu, Liberation) to avoid fsType complications.
Test in multiple viewers — what works in Adobe Acrobat may fail in pdf.js or LibreOffice.
When debugging, look at the COS structure, not just the rendered output. The font dictionary, font descriptor, encoding, and /ToUnicode CMap tell the full story.

chapter.simpara: The next chapter — Chapter 12 — moves from the document infrastructure we’ve built up across Part II into the core subject of this book: the architecture of PDF digital signatures themselves.

abstract.simpara: This chapter dissects the internal architecture of PDF digital signatures as specified in ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8. We examine every dictionary, every key, and every byte — from the AcroForm field tree down to the raw CMS SignedData blob embedded in /Contents.
But more importantly, we talk about what actually matters when you’re staring at a broken signature at 2 AM, wondering why Adobe says "invalid" while your code says "fine."

section.title: Overview: What a PDF Signature Actually Is
section.simpara: Here’s the thing most people get wrong about PDF signatures: they think of them as something attached to a document, like a wax seal on an envelope. They’re not.

A PDF digital signature is a set of PDF objects embedded within the document itself. The signature is part of the document. This seemingly minor architectural choice has profound consequences — it’s why PDF signatures are simultaneously powerful and maddening, why they survive copy operations but break on re-saves, and why you can have a perfectly valid signature on a document that’s been modified after signing (yes, really).
The four pieces of a PDF signature:

listitem.simpara: A signature field (PDSignatureField) in the AcroForm field tree — the form field that "holds" the signature
A signature dictionary (/Type /Sig) — the actual cryptographic payload and metadata
A widget annotation — the visual rectangle on a page (may be zero-size for invisible signatures)
An appearance stream (/AP) — what the viewer renders in the signature rectangle

note.simpara: The signature field is a form field like any other (/FT /Sig). The signature dictionary is the value of that field (/V). An unsigned field has /V absent or null — the field exists but has no signature value yet.

Think of it like an empty checkbox on a paper form. The box is there, waiting to be checked. A document author can place signature fields in a PDF template, and signers fill them in later — each one independently, at different times, possibly on different continents.

tip.simpara: When you encounter a PDF with signature problems, start here. Open the document in a COS-level viewer (like PDFalyzer, or even a hex editor) and check:

listitem.simpara: Does the AcroForm exist? (Some PDFs have signature fields floating without an AcroForm — instant headache.)
Does /SigFlags include bit 2 (AppendOnly)? If not, any editor can destroy all signatures with a simple save.
Is the /V entry present on the signature field? If absent, the field is unsigned.
Does the widget actually have a page reference? Some tools create orphaned widgets.

tip.simpara: This five-minute checklist saves hours of debugging.

section.title: Signature Fields in the AcroForm Tree

blockquote.simpara: A signature field (field type Sig) shall represent a digital signature that may or may not have a signature value.

section.simpara: Signature fields live in the document’s interactive form structure:

section.title: The SigFlags Trap
section.simpara: The /SigFlags entry is one of those things that’s easy to overlook and devastating to get wrong.

entry.simpara: Tells the viewer "this document contains at least one signature field." Conforming readers shall not allow saving in a way that invalidates existing signatures.
The document shall be saved using incremental updates only. This is the mechanism that protects existing signatures from being destroyed by a full rewrite.

important.simpara: Setting /SigFlags to 3 (both bits) is mandatory when a document contains signatures. If bit 2 is missing, a naive PDF editor may do a full save and destroy all signatures. Many real-world PDFs get this wrong.

section.simpara: Here’s what happens in practice: someone creates a PDF with LibreOffice, adds a signature field, signs it — and LibreOffice doesn’t set bit 2. The document gets emailed to a colleague who opens it in some random PDF editor to add an annotation, hits save, and the editor does a full rewrite. Every signature is now invalid. The colleague has no idea what happened. The signer gets an angry phone call.

The fix is always the same: if you’re building a tool that creates signature fields, set /SigFlags to 3 the moment any field has a value. And if you’re building a tool that edits PDFs, check for /SigFlags bit 2 before saving — if it’s set, you must use incremental save. If your tool doesn’t support incremental save…​ well, you need to support it now. Welcome to PDF.

section.title: Field Dictionary Structure
section.simpara: A signature field’s COS dictionary looks like this. Every key matters, and I’ll explain why:

Here is the complete COS dictionary of a typical signature field. In most PDFs, the field and its widget annotation are merged into a single object — the spec (ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.5.6.19) allows splitting them, but few tools bother:
section.screen: << /Type /Annot /Subtype /Widget /FT /Sig /T (Signature1) /V 42 0 R /Rect [100 500 300 550] /AP << /N 43 0 R >> /P 7 0 R /Lock << /Type /SigFieldLock /Action /All >> /SV << /Type /SV /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite /SubFilter [/adbe.pkcs7.detached /ETSI.CAdES.detached] /DigestMethod [/SHA256 /SHA384 /SHA512] /Reasons [(Approved)(Reviewed)] /MDP << /P 2 >> >> >>
section.simpara: The keys fall into three groups: annotation/widget keys (how it looks and where it lives), signature field keys (what it is and what it points to), and sub-dictionaries that constrain signing behavior.

entry.simpara: Always /Widget — this is a widget annotation.
Widget rectangle in page coordinates: [llx lly urx ury]. If [0 0 0 0], the signature is invisible.
Page reference — which page this widget is on. Some poorly generated PDFs omit this, and then viewers have to guess. Don’t be that tool.
Appearance stream dictionary. /N is the normal appearance — what the viewer draws. Without this, many viewers show a generic placeholder. Adobe is particularly opinionated about appearances.
Field type — /Sig marks this as a signature field. Non-negotiable. If you see /FT /Tx on something that’s supposed to be a signature, someone made a mistake.
Partial field name — used to identify the field programmatically. This is how your code finds "the signature field called Signature1."

Reference to the signature dictionary (/Type /Sig) — the cryptographic payload. If absent, the field is unsigned. When present, this is the key entry that connects the form field to the actual signature. The signature dictionary is detailed in .

section.simpara: The remaining two keys — /Lock and /SV — are sub-dictionaries that deserve their own tables.

entry.simpara: What to lock after signing: /All (lock everything), /Include (lock fields listed in /Fields), or /Exclude (lock everything except fields listed in /Fields). This is how approval signatures can lock specific fields — see .
Array of field names to include or exclude from locking. Required when /Action is /Include or /Exclude.

section.title: Seed Value Dictionary (/SV): The Unsung Hero

blockquote.simpara: The seed value dictionary provides constraint information that shall be used at the time the signature is applied.

section.simpara: The seed value dictionary is one of the most powerful and underused features in the entire PDF signature specification. Most people don’t even know it exists, which is a shame, because it solves a problem that plagues every organization deploying PDF signatures at scale: how do you ensure signers use the right algorithm, the right format, and the right handler?

entry.simpara: Required signature handler (e.g., /Adobe.PPKLite). Use this to ensure your documents work with specific software.
Acceptable signature formats. Set this to [/ETSI.CAdES.detached] if you need PAdES compliance. The signer’s software must use one of these formats.
Acceptable hash algorithms. Set this to [/SHA256 /SHA384 /SHA512] — and for the love of all that is holy, do not include /SHA1. It’s 2026. SHA-1 has been broken since 2017.
Pre-defined reason strings the signer must choose from. Great for audit trails — instead of "asdf" in the reason field, signers pick from "Approved", "Reviewed", "Authorized".
Legal attestation strings — rarely used, but available if you need legal boilerplate attached to the signature.
Flags indicating which seed values are required (vs. recommended). If bit 1 is set on a constraint, it’s mandatory — the signer’s software must enforce it, not just suggest it.

section.simpara: The /MDP and /TimeStamp entries are sub-dictionaries with their own structure:

entry.simpara: The DocMDP permission level this signature must enforce: 1 (no changes), 2 (form fills + signatures), 3 (form fills + signatures + annotations). This is how a template author says "whoever signs this field must also lock the document."
URL of the RFC 3161 timestamp server.
If bit 1 is set, timestamping is required (not just recommended). If you need PAdES-B-T, set this. The signer’s software will contact the TSA automatically.

tip.simpara: If you’re creating PDF templates that others will sign, use seed values aggressively. Don’t trust signers to pick the right options — they won’t. A financial services company once sent us PDFs where half the signatures used SHA-1 because some signers had old software and nobody constrained the algorithm. Those signatures were technically valid but would fail compliance audits.
Set /DigestMethod to [/SHA256], set /SubFilter to [/ETSI.CAdES.detached], and set the /Ff flags to make them mandatory. Your future self will thank you.

section.title: The Signature Dictionary (/Type /Sig)
section.simpara: The signature dictionary is the heart of a PDF digital signature. It’s referenced from the field’s /V entry, and it’s where the real action is. Everything else — the field, the widget, the appearance — is just scaffolding. This dictionary is where the cryptographic proof lives.
Here is the complete structure of a typical signature dictionary — every key you’ll encounter in production:
section.screen: << /Type /Sig /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite /SubFilter /ETSI.CAdES.detached /ByteRange [0 15000 15500 34500] /Contents <308206A7...00000000> /M (D:20260306120000+00'00') /Name (John Doe) /Location (Vienna, Austria) /Reason (Approved) /ContactInfo (john@example.com) /Reference [ << /Type /SigRef /TransformMethod /DocMDP /TransformParams << /Type /TransformParams /P 2 /V /1.2 >> /DigestMethod /SHA256 >> ] /Prop_Build << /App << /Name /PDFalyzer >> /Filter << /Name /Adobe.PPKLite >> >> >>
section.simpara: The table below dissects each key. The core cryptographic keys come first, then metadata, then sub-dictionaries.

section.title: Core Signature Keys
section.simpara: These five keys define the cryptographic signature itself. Get any of them wrong and the signature is broken.

table.title: Core keys of the Signature Dictionary (/Type /Sig) — ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1, Table 255

entry.simpara: Always /Sig when present. Identifies this dictionary as a signature value. Optional per the spec, but always include it — defensive parsers rely on it.

The signature handler name. In theory, this identifies which software can process the signature. In practice, it’s always /Adobe.PPKLite. Always. Even when the signing software has nothing to do with Adobe. The name stuck because Adobe defined the original signature spec in 1999 and everyone just…​ went with it.
The actual encoding format of the signature value. This is the key that determines how /Contents is structured — see for the full history and current recommendation.

Four integers [offset1 length1 offset2 length2] defining which bytes of the file are signed. The gap between the two ranges is where /Contents lives. The most important array in the entire dictionary — a deep dive is in .

Hex-encoded CMS SignedData blob (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §5), padded with zeros. This is the actual cryptographic signature. Everything else in this dictionary is metadata. This is the payload. See .

section.title: Metadata Keys
section.simpara: These keys carry informational data about the signing event. They are part of the signed bytes and cannot be changed after signing — but they are set by whoever controls the signing process, not verified against any external authority.

table.title: Metadata keys of the Signature Dictionary — ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1, Table 255

entry.simpara: Signing time in PDF date format. Do not trust this. It’s the signer’s local clock. Use the CMS timestamp attribute for authoritative time.
Signer’s name. Do not trust this either. It’s free text. The authoritative name is in the X.509 certificate inside /Contents.
Contact info. Informational, optional, you see the pattern.

warning.simpara: The /M, /Name, /Location, /Reason, and /ContactInfo fields are unauthenticated metadata. Yes, they’re part of the signed bytes. Yes, they can’t be changed after signing. But they’re set before signing, by whoever controls the signing process. If a malicious signer puts "Barack Obama" in the /Name field and signs with a self-signed certificate, the signature will be structurally valid. The only trustworthy identity information is the X.509 certificate in /Contents. Never display /Name to users as if it were verified identity. This mistake is shockingly common in PDF viewer implementations.

section.title: Signature Reference Dictionary (/Reference)
section.simpara: The /Reference entry is an array of signature reference dictionaries (/Type /SigRef) that define transform methods — rules about what can and cannot change after signing. This is the mechanism behind certification signatures and field-level locking (covered in depth in ).

entry.simpara: The transform method: /DocMDP (document modification detection and prevention), /FieldMDP (field-level locking), or /UR (usage rights — deprecated in PDF 2.0). DocMDP is by far the most common. See .
Parameters for the transform method. For DocMDP, this contains /P (permission level: 1 = no changes, 2 = form fills + signatures, 3 = form fills + signatures + annotations) and /V (version).
Hash algorithm for this reference (e.g., /SHA256). Must match the algorithm used in the CMS structure.

section.title: Build Properties Dictionary (/Prop_Build)
section.simpara: The /Prop_Build dictionary identifies the software that created the signature. It’s optional and purely informational — but surprisingly useful for debugging.

entry.simpara: Application that created the signature. Contains /Name (e.g., /PDFalyzer, /AdobeAcrobatDC).
Public security handler info. Rarely populated.

simpara.emphasis: The /Prop_Build dictionary is your friend when debugging.

tip.simpara: When a signature doesn’t validate, the first thing I check is /Prop_Build. It tells you which software created the signature. If it says /iText with a version from 2015, you know you might be dealing with an old CMS structure. If it says /Adobe Acrobat and the signature is invalid, something is genuinely wrong because Adobe’s own tool should produce signatures that Adobe validates. If /Prop_Build is missing entirely, the signing software was lazy — which tells you something about the quality of the rest of the signature, too.

section.title: SubFilter Values: A Brief History of Getting It Right
section.simpara: The /SubFilter value determines how the /Contents bytes are structured. Getting this right is the difference between "signature works everywhere" and "signature works in Adobe but nowhere else" (or vice versa).
The history of SubFilter values is essentially the history of PDF signatures trying — and sometimes failing — to keep up with cryptographic best practices:

The first two SubFilter values are deprecated — they use SHA-1, which has been broken for collision resistance since the SHAttered attack of February 2017. You will encounter them in legacy documents, but you should never use them for new signatures.

table.title: Deprecated SubFilter Values (do not use for new signatures)

entry.simpara: Do not use. Raw RSA signature with the certificate in a separate /Cert key instead of inside /Contents. No CMS wrapper. SHA-1 only. This was PDF’s first attempt at digital signatures in 1999. It made sense at the time. It doesn’t anymore.

Do not use. CMS SignedData where the SHA-1 digest of the byte range is encapsulated in the CMS. This is the worst of both worlds: SHA-1 (broken) and encapsulated content (unnecessary complexity). Nobody misses this format.

section.simpara: The following three values are the current standard. For new signatures, use /ETSI.CAdES.detached unless you have a specific reason not to.

entry.simpara: CMS SignedData with detached content. The workhorse format — widely supported since PDF 1.3. The signed bytes are the PDF byte range, not embedded in CMS. If you need maximum compatibility with older software, this is your format.

CMS SignedData with mandatory CAdES signed attributes, specifically the signing-certificate-v2 attribute. Required for PAdES-B-B and higher. This is the modern standard. Use this. The CMS structure is identical to /adbe.pkcs7.detached at the container level — the difference is one signed attribute (~50 bytes).
RFC 3161 timestamp token — not a signature, but a document-level timestamp. Used for PAdES-B-T and long-term archival. You’ll encounter these when dealing with LTV (Long-Term Validation).

caution.simpara: Here’s a trap that catches people all the time: Adobe Acrobat and most viewers accept all SubFilter values for backward compatibility. Your signature will "work" in Acrobat regardless of which SubFilter you use. But only /ETSI.CAdES.detached is valid for PAdES-compliant signatures under eIDAS. If you need legal standing in the EU — and increasingly, that’s everyone — this is the only acceptable value. Don’t let "it works in Acrobat" lull you into using /adbe.pkcs7.detached for new signatures. Acrobat is not the arbiter of legal validity; the eIDAS regulation is.

tip.simpara: "Can I use /ETSI.CAdES.detached if my users have old PDF readers?"

Yes. The CMS structure is identical to /adbe.pkcs7.detached at the container level. Old readers that don’t understand the SubFilter name will typically fall back to parsing the /Contents as generic CMS/PKCS#7 — and it will work. The difference is one signed attribute (signing-certificate-v2), which old readers simply ignore.

The only scenario where it breaks: truly ancient software (pre-2010) that strictly checks the SubFilter name against a hardcoded whitelist and rejects unknown values. If your users are running PDF readers from 2008, they have bigger problems than SubFilter compatibility.

section.title: The /Contents Value: CMS SignedData
section.simpara: The /Contents entry is a hex-encoded byte string containing a DER-encoded CMS SignedData structure (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §5).
This is where the actual cryptographic signature lives. Everything else in the signature dictionary is metadata. This is the payload.

section.title: Why Hex-Encoded?
section.simpara: PDF strings can be literal (…​) or hex <…​>. The hex form is used because CMS blobs contain arbitrary binary data (DER-encoded ASN.1), and literal strings would need extensive escaping for bytes like \(, \), \\. Hex encoding doubles the size but is safe and unambiguous.

A typical /Contents value looks like <308206A7…​several thousand hex characters…​0000000000>. The leading bytes (3082…​) are the DER-encoded ASN.1 SEQUENCE tag, and the trailing zeros are padding. Which brings us to one of the more elegant hacks in the PDF specification:

section.title: Pre-allocation: The Chicken-and-Egg Problem
section.simpara: Here’s the fundamental problem with PDF signatures:

listitem.simpara: The signature covers the file’s bytes (including the signature dictionary itself)
But the signature dictionary must contain the CMS blob
But the CMS blob can only be computed after you know which bytes to sign
But you can’t know which bytes to sign until the dictionary is written

section.simpara: The solution is a trick that every PDF signing implementation uses: pre-allocate a large enough /Contents value filled with zeros, write the file, then overwrite the zeros with the actual CMS bytes:

important.simpara: The pre-allocated size must be estimated accurately, and this is where things go wrong in the real world:

listitem.simpara: Too small — the CMS blob doesn’t fit — signing fails. You’ll get an exception from PDFBox and no output file. This is the most common "my signing suddenly broke" bug, usually triggered by adding a certificate chain or a timestamp that makes the CMS blob bigger than expected.
Too large — wasted bytes, but the signature is valid. A few KB of zeros at the end of the file never hurt anyone.

important.simpara: Always err on the side of too large. Here are sensible defaults:

I’ve seen production systems fail because someone used the PDFBox default (0x2500) for PAdES-B-T signatures. The first 500 signatures worked fine because the TSA responses were small. Then the TSA renewed its certificate with a longer chain, the responses grew by 2 KB, and every signing operation started failing on a Friday afternoon. Set it to 0x6000 minimum and forget about it.

section.title: CMS SignedData: The Cryptographic Core
section.simpara: The CMS blob inside /Contents follows RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §5. If you’ve worked with S/MIME email signatures or code signing, this structure will look familiar — it’s the same SignedData format used across the entire PKI ecosystem:
section.programlisting: SignedData ::= SEQUENCE { version CMSVersion, digestAlgorithms DigestAlgorithmIdentifiers, encapContentInfo EncapsulatedContentInfo, certificates [0] IMPLICIT CertificateSet OPTIONAL, crls [1] IMPLICIT RevocationInfoChoices OPTIONAL, signerInfos SignerInfos }

callout.para: Version: 1 for basic CMS, 3 if attribute certificates are present
Set of digest algorithms used (typically just {SHA-256})

Content type: id-data (1.2.840.113549.1.7.1) with absent content — this is what "detached" means. The signed data (the PDF byte range) is not inside the CMS structure. It’s outside, in the PDF file itself.

Certificates — the signer’s certificate and optionally the full chain (intermediates, possibly root). Always include the full chain. I know it makes the blob bigger. I know storage is a concern. Do it anyway. A signature without a chain is a signature that can only be validated by someone who already has all your certificates. That’s almost nobody.
CRLs — rarely embedded here in the modern PAdES world. In PAdES-B-LT and later, revocation data goes in the DSS dictionary instead.
Signer information — the actual signature value and signed attributes. This is where the cryptography lives.

section.title: SignerInfo: Where the Cryptography Lives
section.programlisting: SignerInfo ::= SEQUENCE { version CMSVersion, sid SignerIdentifier, digestAlgorithm DigestAlgorithmIdentifier, signedAttrs [0] IMPLICIT SignedAttributes OPTIONAL, signatureAlgorithm SignatureAlgorithmIdentifier, signature SignatureValue, unsignedAttrs [1] IMPLICIT UnsignedAttributes OPTIONAL }

callout.para: Identifies the signer’s certificate — either by issuer DN + serial number, or by subject key identifier. Both work. Issuer+serial is more traditional; subject key identifier is more robust when certificates are reissued.
Hash algorithm used to digest the PDF byte range. SHA-256 is the minimum acceptable. SHA-384 or SHA-512 if you’re paranoid or work in defense.
Signed attributes — authenticated metadata. See the critical attributes table below. These are signed, meaning any modification invalidates the signature.

Signature algorithm (e.g., SHA256withRSA, SHA256withECDSA). Note: the signature is computed over the DER-encoded signed attributes, not directly over the PDF bytes. This is a CMS subtlety that trips up many implementers.
The actual cryptographic signature bytes.
Unsigned attributes — unauthenticated extensions. Timestamps go here.

section.title: The Signed Attributes That Matter
section.simpara: The first two attributes are required by CMS whenever signed attributes are present. The remaining attributes add PAdES compliance and defense-in-depth.

table.title: Required CMS Signed Attributes — RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §11

entry.simpara: Must be id-data (1.2.840.113549.1.7.1). Required by CMS when signed attributes are present. Forgetting this is a classic implementation bug — the signature appears valid in some tools but fails in others.
SHA-256 hash of the PDF byte range. This is what’s actually verified during validation. If this hash doesn’t match the file bytes, the signature is invalid. Full stop.

Hash of the signer’s certificate (SHA-256). Required for PAdES / CAdES. This single attribute prevents certificate substitution attacks — an attacker can’t swap in a different certificate without breaking the signature. It’s ~50 bytes. There is no reason not to include it.
Claimed signing time. Not authoritative — it’s whatever the signer’s clock said. For authoritative time, you need a timestamp (see unsigned attributes). Optional, but almost everyone includes it.
Protects against algorithm substitution attacks by binding the digest and signature algorithms into the signed attributes. Good hygiene to include.

simpara.emphasis: The signing-certificate-v2 attribute is what makes the difference between /adbe.pkcs7.detached and /ETSI.CAdES.detached.

important.simpara: Without it: plain CMS (PKCS#7) — vulnerable to certificate substitution attacks. With it: CAdES — the signer’s certificate is bound to the signature by hash.
This single attribute is the gateway to PAdES compliance. It’s about 50 bytes in the CMS structure. There is no reason not to include it, even if you’re using /adbe.pkcs7.detached as your SubFilter.

Certificate substitution attacks are not theoretical. In 2019, researchers at Ruhr University Bochum demonstrated practical attacks against multiple PDF viewers that exploited the absence of certificate binding. The fix was trivially simple: check signing-certificate-v2. Several major viewers had to be patched.

section.title: Unsigned Attributes: The Afterthought That Became Essential

table.title: Unsigned Attributes — RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §11

entry.simpara: RFC 3161 — Internet X.509 PKI Time-Stamp Protocol (TSP), ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04) §5.3

RFC 3161 timestamp token over the signature value. Provides authoritative proof of signing time from a trusted TSA. This is the only way to prove when a signature was made. The timestamp token has its own signature from the TSA, making it self-authenticating — which is why it’s safe as an unsigned attribute.
Full certificate chain values. Used in CAdES-X-Long. In the PAdES world, these typically go in the DSS dictionary instead (see ).
OCSP responses and CRLs. Used in CAdES-X-Long. In PAdES, these go in the DSS dictionary instead.

section.simpara: The timestamp attribute deserves special attention. Unsigned attributes are, by definition, not protected by the signature — they can be added after signing without invalidating anything. This is by design. A timestamp proves that the signature existed at a certain time, and it’s intentionally added after the signature is created (because you need the signature value to timestamp it).
This is also why you should never put anything security-critical in unsigned attributes that isn’t self-authenticating (like a timestamp token, which has its own signature from the TSA).

section.title: The Signing Process: Step by Step
section.simpara: The process of creating a PDF digital signature is intricate because the signature must cover bytes that include the signature dictionary itself — a chicken-and-egg problem solved by the /Contents pre-allocation we discussed above.
Here’s the full sequence, and understanding it is essential for debugging:

entry.simpara: Create or locate the signature field. If the document already has an unsigned field (/FT /Sig with no /V), use it. Otherwise, create a new field in the AcroForm /Fields array with a widget annotation on the desired page. Practical note: if the document was prepared by someone else (a template), always prefer using existing unsigned fields. Creating new fields in a document that already has signatures is technically possible via incremental save, but it makes some validators nervous.

Build the signature dictionary with placeholder values. /ByteRange is set to [0 0 0 0] (will be overwritten). /Contents is filled with 00 bytes at the estimated maximum size. All other keys (/Filter, /SubFilter, /M, /Name, /Reason, /Reference) are set to their final values now — they become part of the signed bytes and can’t change later.

Write the PDF using incremental save. The new objects (signature dict, updated field, updated xref) are appended after the existing %%EOF. This is critical — a full rewrite would invalidate any existing signatures. This is non-negotiable. Even if the document has no prior signatures, use incremental save. It’s the only safe habit.

Compute actual byte offsets. After writing, the exact byte positions of the /Contents hex string are known. Update /ByteRange to [0, contentsStart, contentsEnd, fileLength - contentsEnd]. The two segments cover everything except the /Contents value itself.

Read the signed bytes. Concatenate file[0 .. contentsStart-1] and file[contentsEnd .. fileLength-1]. These are the exact bytes the signature covers — everything except the /Contents hex value. Debug tip: if you suspect byte range issues, dump these bytes to a file and hash them manually. Compare with the message-digest attribute in the CMS.
Hash the signed bytes. Compute SHA-256 (or SHA-384/512) over the concatenated byte ranges. This hash becomes the message-digest signed attribute in the CMS structure.

Sign the hash. The private key (RSA or ECDSA) signs the DER-encoded signed attributes (which include the hash). This may happen locally or via a remote HSM/signing service. In the remote case, only the hash leaves your system — the private key never does. More on remote signing in .

Build the CMS SignedData structure. Include: digest algorithm, signer certificate, certificate chain, signed attributes (content-type, message-digest, signing-certificate-v2), signature value. DER-encode the result.

Write CMS bytes into /Contents. Hex-encode the DER bytes and write them at the exact byte offset of the /Contents value in the file. Remaining space is left as 00 padding. No other bytes in the file are modified.

caution.simpara: Step 10 is a raw byte write — not a PDF object update. The file is opened as a random-access binary stream, the cursor is positioned at the /Contents hex string’s start offset, and the hex chars are overwritten in place. If the CMS blob is larger than the pre-allocated space, signing fails. If even a single byte is written at the wrong offset, the signature is invalid and the PDF may be corrupted. This is why you don’t implement PDF signing from scratch — you use PDFBox or a similar library that handles the byte-level bookkeeping for you.

section.title: Incremental Save: The Foundation of Multi-Signature
section.simpara: Every signature operation uses incremental save (ISO 32000-2:2020 §7.5.6). The original file bytes are never modified — new objects are appended after the existing %%EOF.

This is the mechanism that makes multiple independent signatures possible. Without incremental save, adding a second signature would modify the bytes covered by the first signature, invalidating it. With incremental save, the first signature’s bytes remain untouched — the second signature’s data is simply appended at the end.
Each signature’s /ByteRange covers everything up to and including its own incremental update — but not the updates that come after:

listitem.simpara: Signature 1’s byte range: [0, …​, …​, M] — covers original + update #1
Signature 2’s byte range: [0, …​, …​, P] — covers original + update #1 + update #2

section.simpara: This is why Signature 1 remains valid after Signature 2 is added: Signature 2’s bytes are appended after the range that Signature 1 covers. From Signature 1’s perspective, those bytes are "trailing" — present in the file but outside the signed range.

simpara.emphasis: "The file has been modified since signing" — don’t panic.

tip.simpara: This is the single most common false alarm in PDF signature validation. A validator detects trailing bytes after a signature’s byte range and flags it as "modified." But in the vast majority of cases, those trailing bytes are simply a later incremental update — another signature, a form fill, or an annotation.

listitem.simpara: Is the trailing data a valid incremental update? (Look for xref table + trailer + %%EOF.)
Does it correspond to an expected operation? (Another signature, a form field being filled.)
Did the byte range change? (The original signature’s bytes at their original offsets should be byte-for-byte identical.)

tip.simpara: If the answers are yes, yes, and no — the signature is fine. The "modification" is just the normal operation of PDF’s append-only architecture.

listitem.simpara: Trailing bytes that aren’t a valid incremental update — raw data appended to the file
An incremental update that modifies objects covered by an existing signature in unexpected ways (e.g., page content changes)
Multiple %%EOF markers where the trailing section isn’t a proper PDF update

section.title: Widget Annotations and Page Placement
section.simpara: Every signature field has an associated widget annotation (ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.5.6.19) that defines its visual presence on a page.

section.title: The /Rect Coordinate System
section.simpara: The widget’s /Rect array specifies [llx, lly, urx, ury] in default user space coordinates:

important.simpara: PDF coordinates have origin at lower-left with Y increasing upward. This is the opposite of screen coordinates (origin top-left, Y increasing downward). This will bite you. It bites everyone. You will write the conversion wrong the first time. Here it is, so you can copy it:

callout.para: X translates directly (both coordinate systems have X increasing rightward)
Y must be flipped: PDF Y=0 is bottom, screen Y=0 is top. Note the bbox[1] + bbox[3] — that’s lower-left-Y plus height, giving the top edge in PDF space, which maps to the top edge in screen space.
Width scales linearly
Height scales linearly

important.simpara: Where bbox is [lowerLeftX, lowerLeftY, width, height] as extracted by PDFBox’s PDRectangle. Note that PDFBox gives you [x, y, width, height] while the raw PDF /Rect is [llx, lly, urx, ury] — PDFBox does the conversion for you. Don’t convert twice.

section.title: Invisible Signatures
section.simpara: A signature can be invisible — having no visual appearance on the page:

listitem.simpara: Zero-size rect: /Rect [0 0 0 0] — the widget exists but has no area
No widget at all: The field has no annotation entry (valid per spec, but some viewers complain)

section.simpara: Invisible signatures are common for:

listitem.simpara: Certification signatures — the document author certifies the document without cluttering the layout
Machine-generated signatures — automated workflows where nobody’s looking at the PDF
Counter-signatures — additional approval signatures in complex chains where visual space is limited

simpara.emphasis: Should your signature be visible or invisible?

tip.simpara: For approval signatures intended for human signers: make them visible. Users expect to see their signature on the page. A visible signature provides a visual anchor — "I signed here" — and helps in audit scenarios.
For certification signatures and automated workflows: invisible is usually fine. The certification status is shown in the viewer’s signature panel, not on the page.
For machine-to-machine: always invisible. No human is looking at the page. Adding a visual appearance just wastes space and processing time.

One exception: if you’re in a regulated industry where auditors review printed copies of signed PDFs (yes, this still happens in 2026), visible signatures are essential because auditors can’t check digital signatures on paper. In that case, include the signer’s name, the signing time, and ideally a note like "Digitally signed — verify electronically."

section.title: AcroForm /SigFlags: Document-Level Signature Metadata

blockquote.simpara: If the document contains signature fields, the AcroForm dictionary shall include a SigFlags entry.

entry.simpara: At least one signature field has a value. Readers should present signature-specific UI (e.g., the blue bar at the top of Adobe Acrobat). Must be set whenever any signature field contains a /V entry.

The document shall only be saved incrementally. A conforming reader that allows editing must not do a full save — it must append. This is the single most important flag for signature preservation. Without it, a naive editor may do a full rewrite and destroy every signature.

section.simpara: In practice, both bits should be set (/SigFlags 3) whenever the document contains any signed signature field. This was already discussed above, but it bears repeating: if you get this wrong, signatures will be destroyed by innocent save operations, and the blame will be on your tool.

section.title: PDFBox API Mapping
section.simpara: For those of you who skipped here — I don’t blame you, this is the section with the code — here’s how the spec concepts map to PDFBox 3.x classes:

section.title: Minimal Signing Code (Key Lines)
section.simpara: The essential PDFBox signing code in ~20 lines. This is the skeleton — the full, production-ready version is in Appendix A, but these are the lines that matter:

callout.para: Always load from bytes. Don’t create a new PDDocument() and try to saveIncremental() — PDFBox 3.x will throw IllegalStateException: document was not loaded from a file or a stream. Yes, this error message has confused every single person who’s ever encountered it.
Filter is always Adobe.PPKLite for interoperability. Don’t be creative here.
SubFilter: use ETSI.CAdES.detached for PAdES compliance. See the earlier section for why.
Pre-allocate 24KB for the CMS blob. Not the PDFBox default of 9KB. See the pre-allocation table above.
Visual appearance — set this to null for invisible signatures. Or provide an InputStream of a pre-built appearance.
Register the signature and the SignatureInterface. The interface’s sign() method will be called during saveIncremental().
Incremental save. There is no other option. Do not call save() on a document you’re signing.

section.title: The SignatureInterface: Your Extension Point

callout.para: Called by PDFBox during saveIncremental(). The InputStream provides the concatenated byte ranges — this is the data you need to hash and sign. Your implementation builds the CMS SignedData blob and returns the DER-encoded bytes.

section.simpara: This is the only thing you need to implement. PDFBox handles the PDF structure, the byte range calculation, the incremental save, the widget creation, the appearance stream embedding. You handle the cryptography.

simpara.emphasis: Keep your SignatureInterface implementation stateless and testable.

tip.simpara: Don’t put PDF logic in there. Don’t reference the PDDocument. Your sign() method receives an InputStream of bytes and returns CMS bytes. That’s it. This makes it trivially testable — pass in any byte stream, check that the output is valid CMS. It also makes it easy to swap between local signing (BouncyCastle) and remote signing (HSM, cloud) without touching any PDF code.

section.title: Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
section.simpara: After years of working with PDF signatures, the same problems come up over and over. Here’s the hit list:

entry.simpara: Trusting /Name for signer identity. Many systems display the /Name field from the signature dictionary as if it’s the verified signer name. It’s not. It’s free-text set by whoever controls the signing process.
Always extract the signer’s identity from the X.509 certificate in the CMS structure. Display the certificate’s Subject CN (or the full Subject DN for enterprise contexts).
Using PDDocument.save() instead of saveIncremental(). Full save rewrites the entire file, destroying all existing signatures.
Always use saveIncremental(). Make it a code review checklist item: "Does this code path call save() on a signed document? Reject."
Pre-allocating too little space for /Contents. Works fine in testing with simple certificates, breaks in production when chains get longer or timestamps are added.
Use 0x6000 (24KB) minimum. For PAdES-B-LT, use 0x18000 (96KB). Test with your actual TSA and certificate chain, not with test certificates.
Forgetting signing-certificate-v2 in the CMS attributes. Technically valid as PKCS#7, but not PAdES-compliant and vulnerable to certificate substitution.
Use BouncyCastle’s JcaSignerInfoGeneratorBuilder with the correct DigestCalculatorProvider. It adds the attribute automatically when configured correctly. See the code in Appendix A.
Not including the certificate chain. A signature with just the signer’s certificate can’t be validated by anyone who doesn’t already have the issuer certificates.
Always include the full chain (signer + intermediates). You can omit the root CA — validators typically have their own trust stores. But include everything else.
Creating a fresh PDDocument and calling saveIncremental(). PDFBox 3.x requires the document to have been loaded from bytes.
Create a blank PDF, save it to bytes, load it back, then sign. See the test code in this chapter.
Setting /SigFlags to 1 instead of 3. Bit 1 alone means "signatures exist" but doesn’t enforce append-only saving.

Modifying the PDF between adding the signature and calling saveIncremental(). Any change to the document graph between addSignature() and saveIncremental() may change byte offsets, breaking the signature.
Set up everything before calling addSignature(). Once you’ve added the signature, the very next call should be saveIncremental(). Nothing in between.

listitem.simpara: A PDF signature = signature field + signature dictionary + widget + appearance stream — all embedded in the document
The signature dictionary’s /Contents holds a CMS SignedData blob (hex-encoded, zero-padded)
/ByteRange defines exactly which file bytes are signed — everything except /Contents itself
/SubFilter determines the signature format: use /ETSI.CAdES.detached for PAdES compliance — there’s no good reason not to
The signing-certificate-v2 attribute is what distinguishes CAdES from plain PKCS#7 — and what prevents certificate substitution attacks
All signatures use incremental save — original bytes are never modified, ever
/SigFlags must be set to 3 (SignaturesExist + AppendOnly) to protect existing signatures — getting this wrong destroys other people’s signatures
Seed value dictionaries (/SV) can constrain how a field is signed — use them in templates to prevent signer mistakes
Widget /Rect uses PDF coordinates (origin lower-left, Y up) — you will get the conversion wrong the first time, and that’s OK
PDFBox’s SignatureInterface.sign(InputStream) is the single extension point for all signing logic — keep it clean, stateless, and testable
When in doubt, pre-allocate more space for /Contents — wasted bytes are cheap, failed signatures are expensive
"The file has been modified since signing" is usually not a problem — it’s usually just a later incremental update

bibliomixed.bibliomisc: [iso32000-2] ISO 32000-2:2020, Document management — Portable document format — Part 2: PDF 2.0, §12.8 (Digital Signatures), §12.7 (Interactive Forms), §7.5.6 (Incremental Updates)
[rfc5652] RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS), Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS), §5 (Signed-data Content Type), §11.1 (Content Type), §11.2 (Message Digest)
[rfc5035] RFC 5035, Enhanced Security Services (ESS) Update: Adding CertID Algorithm Agility, §3 (signing-certificate-v2)
[rfc6211] RFC 6211, Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) Algorithm Identifier Protection Attribute
[etsi-pades] ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04), Electronic Signatures and Infrastructures (ESI); PAdES digital signatures, §5.1 (PAdES-CMS), §5.4 (Document timestamps)
[adobe-digsig] Adobe Systems, PDF Reference, sixth edition (2006), §8.7 (Digital Signatures) — historical reference for the original /adbe.pkcs7.detached and /adbe.x509.rsa_sha1 formats

[pdf-insecurity] Mladenov, Mainka, Kampmann, Somorovsky, Schwenk, 1 Trillion Dollar Refund: How To Spoof PDF Signatures and subsequent papers, Ruhr University Bochum, 2019–2021 — comprehensive attack taxonomy on PDF signature validation; required reading for anyone implementing signature verification

partintro.simpara: The byte range is where PDF signatures live or die. Everything in Chapter 12 — the /Sig dictionary, the /SubFilter, the certificate chain, the CMS structure — all of it is meaningless if the byte range is wrong. A single off-by-one error in the /ByteRange array, and the signature is cryptographically invalid. A subtle gap in coverage, and an attacker can inject content that the signature appears to endorse.

This chapter takes the byte range apart byte by byte. We will trace exactly which bytes are signed, where the gap is, why the gap exists, what lives in the gap, and what goes wrong when any of these assumptions are violated. We will look at hex dumps of real PDFs, visualize the byte range geometry, walk through multi-signature scenarios, and examine the exact attacks that exploit byte range weaknesses.
If Chapter 12 taught you what a byte range is, this chapter teaches you how it actually works in the raw file — and how to verify that it works correctly.

section.title: What the Spec Says
section.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1 defines the /ByteRange array as:

blockquote.simpara: An array of pairs of integers (starting byte offset, length in bytes) that shall describe the exact byte range for the digest computation. Multiple discontiguous byte ranges shall be used to describe a digest that does not include the signature value itself.

section.simpara: In practice, every PDF signature uses exactly one pair of byte ranges — two segments — with a gap between them. The gap contains the /Contents value (the hex-encoded CMS signature). The two segments cover everything else in the file.
The /ByteRange array always has exactly four integers:
section.screen: /ByteRange [offset1 length1 offset2 length2]
section.simpara: Where:

listitem.simpara: offset1 = 0 (always — the signed region starts at the first byte of the file)
length1 = number of bytes from the start of the file to the opening < of the /Contents hex string
offset2 = byte offset immediately after the closing > of the /Contents hex string
length2 = number of bytes from offset2 to the end of the file

section.simpara: The math is simple:

listitem.simpara: Signed bytes = [0, length1) + [offset2, offset2 + length2)
Unsigned gap = [length1, offset2) — this contains only the /Contents hex string value
Total file size = offset2 + length2

section.title: Visualizing the Byte Range
section.simpara: To make this concrete, let’s look at a real signed PDF. Suppose we have a signed file of 45,000 bytes with a 32KB (32,768 hex characters = 16,384 bytes) reserved /Contents value:

The gap is slightly larger than 16,384 bytes because it includes the < and > delimiters that bracket the hex string, plus the `/Contents ` key prefix. The exact gap size depends on how PDFBox (or whatever library produced the file) serializes the signature dictionary.

section.title: Why the Gap Exists: The Chicken-and-Egg Problem
section.simpara: The gap exists because of a fundamental bootstrapping problem:

listitem.simpara: To compute the signature, you need to hash the bytes of the file
To write the file, you need to include the signature (inside /Contents)
But the signature is part of the file — so how can you hash a file that doesn’t exist yet?

section.simpara: The solution: exclude the signature value from the hash. The /Contents hex string is the one part of the file that is not covered by the signature. Everything else — the PDF header, all objects, the signature dictionary itself (including /ByteRange, /Filter, /SubFilter, and all other keys), the cross-reference table, and the trailer — is signed.
This means the signing process works like this:

listitem.simpara: Write the entire PDF file, including the /Sig dictionary, but leave the /Contents value as a placeholder (all zeros)
Record the byte offsets of where the placeholder starts and ends
Compute the hash over everything except the placeholder: hash = SHA256(segment1 || segment2)
Construct the CMS SignedData using this hash
Hex-encode the CMS bytes and write them into the placeholder space
If the CMS is shorter than the reserved space (it almost always is), pad the remainder with zeros

section.simpara: This is why PDFBox’s SignatureInterface.sign(InputStream) receives an InputStream — that stream contains the concatenation of segment 1 and segment 2 (the bytes outside the gap), ready to be hashed.

important.simpara: The /ByteRange array itself is part of the signed region.
