This is critical for security: if an attacker could modify the /ByteRange after signing, they could redirect the signature to cover different content. The byte range is committed before the hash is computed, and any change to it invalidates the signature.

section.title: What’s Actually in the Gap
section.simpara: Let’s look at a hex dump of the gap region. In a properly signed PDF, the gap contains exactly one thing: the hex-encoded CMS SignedData, padded with zeros:
section.screen: Byte 14000: 3C -- opening '<' Byte 14001: 30 38 30 36 30 39 ... -- CMS bytes (hex-encoded) ... ... (several KB of hex characters) ... Byte 28500: 30 30 30 30 30 30 30 30 30 30 30 30 ... -- zero padding ("000000...") ... Byte 30407: 3E -- closing '>'
section.simpara: The hex encoding doubles the size: a 4,096-byte CMS signature becomes 8,192 hex characters. The reserved space is typically 16KB (32,768 hex characters = 16,384 bytes of actual CMS capacity), which is enough for most PAdES-B-B signatures.

warning.simpara: PAdES-B-LTA signatures are much larger than PAdES-B-B. A B-LTA signature includes:

listitem.simpara: The CMS SignedData itself (~2–4 KB)
The signing certificate and full chain (~2–5 KB)
The timestamp token from the TSA (~2–4 KB)
OCSP responses for each certificate in the chain (~1–3 KB each)
CRL data if OCSP is not available (~5–50 KB)

warning.simpara: A B-LTA signature can easily reach 20–30 KB, which requires 40–60 KB of hex-encoded space. If you’re producing B-LTA signatures, reserve at least 64 KB for /Contents:

If the CMS exceeds the reserved space, PDFBox throws an IOException — the signing fails silently in the sense that no file is produced. This is the #1 cause of "signing worked in development but fails in production" — development certificates have short chains and no timestamps, production certificates have full chains, OCSP responses, and timestamps that exceed the default buffer.

section.title: The Zero-Padding Question
section.simpara: The zero padding at the end of the /Contents hex string is not part of the CMS. It’s just filler — the hex string 00 decodes to a null byte, and CMS parsers stop reading at the end of the DER-encoded ASN.1 structure, ignoring trailing zeros.
Some validators are stricter than others about this padding:

listitem.simpara: Adobe Acrobat: Ignores trailing zero padding — works fine
LibreOffice: Ignores trailing zeros — works fine

Some custom validators: May compute the hash of the entire /Contents hex string (including padding) and fail because the hash doesn’t match what the signer computed over just the CMS bytes. This is a bug in the validator, not in the signed PDF.

tip.simpara: When writing validation code, always parse the /Contents hex string as DER and stop at the end of the ASN.1 structure. Do not hash the raw hex string — decode it to binary, find the end of the DER sequence (the total length indicated in the ASN.1 header), and ignore everything after that.

chapter.title: Incremental Saves and the Byte Range

section.title: How Signing Creates an Incremental Update
section.simpara: When PDFBox signs a PDF, it performs an incremental save — it appends new data to the end of the existing file rather than rewriting the entire file. This is not optional for signing; it is how signing must work.
An incrementally saved PDF looks like this:

figure.title: PDF file before and after signing — incremental update appends new objects without modifying the original file

section.simpara: The "new objects" include the signature dictionary (or modifications to an existing signature field), any new annotation objects for the visual appearance, and updated AcroForm entries. The "new xref" is a cross-reference section that records the locations of these new objects, and the "new trailer" points to the new xref.

The entire appended region — including the new %%EOF — is covered by the byte range of the signature. The byte range starts at byte 0 (the original file’s header) and extends to the end of the appended data, with only the /Contents gap excluded.
This means:

listitem.simpara: The signature covers the original file in its entirety
The signature covers the new objects added during signing (the signature dictionary, appearances, etc.)
The signature covers the new cross-reference table and new trailer
The only thing not covered is the CMS signature value itself

section.title: What Happens After Signing: The Trailing Bytes Problem
section.simpara: Here is one of the most security-critical aspects of byte range handling: what happens to bytes that appear after the signed region?
Consider this scenario:

figure.title: Byte range coverage — the signature covers two segments with a gap for /Contents

section.simpara: If offset2 + length2 equals the total file size, the signature covers everything — good.
But what if someone appends data after the signed region?

figure.title: Trailing bytes after the signed region — the Incremental Saving Attack surface

section.simpara: The byte range still validates — the hash over the signed segments hasn’t changed. But the file now contains unsigned data at the end. If this unsigned data is a valid incremental update (with its own xref, trailer, and %%EOF), a PDF viewer might load it and display the attacker’s content while showing the original signature as valid.

This is exactly the Incremental Saving Attack (ISA) that the Bochum team demonstrated in 2019 (Chapter 24). The attack works because PDF viewers before the patches would happily load incremental updates appended after the signed byte range, display the new content, and still report the signature as valid — because the signed bytes hadn’t been modified.

simpara.emphasis: The trailing bytes check is the most critical validation step.

caution.simpara: When verifying a PDF signature, you must verify that:

If the file is larger than the signed region, something was appended after signing. This doesn’t necessarily mean the file was tampered with — a second legitimate signature would also append data — but it means you must validate the second revision’s signature independently.

If there is unsigned trailing data that is not a valid subsequent signature revision, the document has been modified after signing and the signature should be treated as covering only the original content.
Chapter 24 provides the full attack taxonomy and defense code.

section.title: Multi-Signature Scenarios
section.simpara: Enterprise PDF workflows often involve multiple signatures — a document might be signed by the author, countersigned by a manager, and certified by a compliance officer. Each signature creates its own incremental update.
The byte range geometry for a multiply-signed PDF looks like this:

figure.title: Multi-signature byte range nesting — each subsequent signature covers everything except its own gap

section.simpara: Each signature covers everything before its own gap (including previous signatures' gaps — the hex bytes of /Contents1 and /Contents2 are part of the signed data for signatures 2 and 3). This nesting means:

listitem.simpara: Signature 1 covers the original PDF + the objects and xref added during the first signing

Signature 2 covers everything that signature 1 covers PLUS the objects added during the second signing. This means signature 2 implicitly endorses signature 1’s existence (though not necessarily its validity)
Signature 3 covers everything, providing the broadest coverage

important.simpara: Each signature is a snapshot of the document at the time it was applied. Signature 1 saw the document in state S1. Signature 2 saw it in state S2 (which includes signature 1). If you need to know what the document looked like when signature 1 was applied, you must extract revision 1 by truncating the file at the end of signature 1’s byte range — everything after that was added later.
PDFBox provides this capability:

section.title: Permission-Based Restrictions: What Can Change After Signing?
section.simpara: Not all changes after signing are attacks. The PDF specification defines three modification detection and prevention (MDP) permission levels that control what changes are allowed after a certification signature:

entry.simpara: No changes whatsoever are allowed. Any modification — including filling form fields or adding annotations — invalidates the signature. This is the most restrictive level.

Users may fill form fields and apply additional signatures. Other changes (adding pages, modifying text, changing form field definitions) invalidate the signature. This is the most common level for government forms.
Users may fill form fields, sign, and add annotations (comments, highlights, stamps). This is the most permissive level while still detecting unauthorized modifications.

section.simpara: When validating a signature with MDP restrictions, you must check not only whether the byte range is intact, but also whether the content of subsequent incremental updates falls within the allowed permissions. This is where validation gets complex — you need to compare the PDF objects in the signed revision against the current revision and determine whether the differences are permitted.

Adobe Acrobat does this analysis automatically and shows warnings like "The document has been altered since the signature was applied, but the changes are permitted." Building this analysis yourself requires understanding of the PDF object model at a level covered in Chapters 7 and 8.

chapter.title: Size Estimation: How Much Space to Reserve

section.title: The Problem
section.simpara: Before you can sign a PDF, you must write it to disk with a placeholder for /Contents. The placeholder must be large enough to hold the final CMS signature. If it’s too small, the signing fails. If it’s too large, you waste space (and the file contains kilobytes of zero padding).
The challenge: the exact CMS size depends on:

listitem.simpara: The signing certificate — larger key sizes (RSA-4096 vs RSA-2048) produce larger signatures
The certificate chain — more intermediates = more bytes
The signed attributes — PAdES requires ESS-signing-certificate-v2, content-type, message-digest; more attributes = more bytes
Timestamps — a TSA response is itself a CMS SignedData, typically 2–4 KB
OCSP responses — each certificate in the chain may have a 1–3 KB OCSP response
CRL data — if a certificate’s revocation information is provided via CRL instead of OCSP, CRLs can be 5–500 KB depending on the CA’s revocation volume

section.simpara: You won’t know the exact sizes until you’ve fetched the timestamp and revocation data — but you need to commit the space before computing the hash.

section.title: Estimation Strategies
section.simpara: Reserve a fixed buffer that’s large enough for worst-case scenarios:

This wastes some space (a B-B signature typically needs only 4–8 KB of the 16 KB reserved), but the waste is negligible compared to the content of a typical PDF. A 100 KB PDF with 16 KB of zero padding in /Contents is 116 KB — nobody cares.

simpara.emphasis: Strategy 2: Pre-compute exact size (complex, rarely necessary)

section.simpara: If you’re generating millions of signed PDFs per day and storage costs matter, you can:

listitem.simpara: Fetch the timestamp and revocation data before creating the placeholder
Compute the CMS size exactly
Reserve only the exact space needed (plus a small safety margin)

section.simpara: This is significantly more complex because you need to construct a "dry run" CMS to measure it, and the timestamp/OCSP data might change between the dry run and the actual signing. In practice, this optimization is almost never worth the complexity.

tip.simpara: Just use 64 KB (65536 bytes) as your default. It’s enough for PAdES-B-LT in the vast majority of cases, it wastes a trivial amount of space, and it eliminates the entire class of "signature too large for reserved space" failures. Only reduce it if you have a specific reason (embedded systems with extreme storage constraints) and only increase it if you’re producing B-LTA signatures with CRLs from large CAs.

section.title: What Happens When the Estimate Is Wrong
section.simpara: If the CMS signature is smaller than the reserved space: no problem. The remaining space is filled with zero bytes. All conforming validators ignore the padding.
If the CMS signature is larger than the reserved space: the signing fails. PDFBox throws an IOException with a message like:
section.screen: IOException: Can't write signature, not enough space
section.simpara: When this happens:

listitem.simpara: The output file is corrupted (partial write) — discard it
Increase SignatureOptions.setPreferredSignatureSize() and retry
To debug: log the actual CMS size from your SignatureInterface.sign() method and compare it to the reserved size

chapter.title: Byte Range Verification: How to Check It

section.title: The Verification Algorithm
section.simpara: When validating a signature, the byte range verification follows these steps:

listitem.simpara: Read the /ByteRange array from the signature dictionary: [offset1, length1, offset2, length2]
Verify structural sanity:
offset1 must be 0
length1 must be positive
offset2 must equal length1 + gap size (where gap size = the length of the /Contents hex string including delimiters)
length2 must be positive
offset2 + length2 should equal the file size (or the size of the revision, for multi-signature documents)
Verify the gap contains only /Contents: the bytes in [length1, offset2) should be a hex string containing the CMS signature value
Extract the signed bytes: concatenate [0, length1) and [offset2, offset2 + length2)
Compute the hash: hash = SHA-256(signed_bytes) (or whatever digest algorithm the CMS specifies)

Verify the CMS: parse the CMS SignedData from /Contents, extract the signed messageDigest attribute, and compare it to the hash you just computed. If they match, and the CMS cryptographic verification passes, the byte range is intact.

section.title: Implementation in Java

warning.simpara: The code above verifies that the hash matches — it does not verify the CMS signature (the RSA/ECDSA computation over the signed attributes). Full verification requires both:

listitem.simpara: Byte range hash matches CMS messageDigest attribute (shown above)
CMS signature over signedAttrs verifies with the signer’s certificate

warning.simpara: Chapter 31 provides complete verification code that covers both steps plus certificate chain validation.

chapter.title: Hex Dump Walkthrough: A Real Signed PDF
chapter.simpara: Let’s trace the byte range through an actual signed PDF, byte by byte. This is the kind of analysis you do when debugging — you open a hex editor (or use xxd, hexdump, or QPDF) and look at the raw bytes.

section.title: Finding the Signature Dictionary
section.simpara: First, locate the signature dictionary in the file. With QPDF:
section.programlisting: qpdf --show-object=15 signed-document.pdf
section.simpara: (Where object 15 is the signature dictionary — find it by tracing from the AcroForm → Fields → [signature field] → /V.)
Output:
section.screen: 15 0 obj << /ByteRange [0 14231 47007 1849] /ContactInfo () /Contents <3082...0000> /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite /Location () /M (D:20260308120000+01'00') /Name (Test Signer) /Reason () /SubFilter /adbe.pkcs7.detached /Type /Sig >> endobj
section.simpara: Now we know:

listitem.simpara: Segment 1: bytes [0, 14231) — 14,231 bytes from file start
Gap: bytes [14231, 47007) — 32,776 bytes containing /Contents
Segment 2: bytes [47007, 48856) — 1,849 bytes at file end
Total file size: 47,007 + 1,849 = 48,856 bytes

section.title: Examining the Gap Boundaries
section.simpara: Let’s look at the bytes right around the gap:
section.programlisting: xxd -s 14220 -l 30 signed-document.pdf
section.simpara: Output:

We can see /Contents < at offset ~14,223. The < delimiter is at the byte just before the hex string begins. The /ByteRange says segment 1 ends at offset 14,231 — which is the < character. Actually, the exact offset of < depends on how PDFBox serializes the dictionary.
Now the end of the gap:
section.programlisting: xxd -s 47000 -l 20 signed-document.pdf
section.simpara: Output:
The > delimiter is at byte 47,007 (approximately), followed by a newline and the next dictionary key. Segment 2 starts immediately after the >.

section.title: Verifying No Trailing Bytes
section.programlisting: wc -c signed-document.pdf # Output: 48856 signed-document.pdf
section.simpara: Byte range says: offset2 + length2 = 47,007 + 1,849 = 48,856. File size = 48,856. They match — no trailing unsigned bytes. The signature covers the entire file (minus the gap).

section.title: Problem 1: Trailing Bytes After Signed Region
section.simpara: Symptom: Signature validates cryptographically, but the file contains content not covered by the signature.
Cause: Something was appended to the file after signing — possibly a legitimate incremental update (second signature, form fill), or possibly an Incremental Saving Attack.
Detection:

Resolution: Parse the trailing data. If it contains a valid cross-reference section and trailer pointing to a subsequent signature, it’s likely a legitimate multi-signature document. If it contains modified objects (changed page content, replaced fonts, new pages), it may be an attack. Chapter 24 provides detailed analysis code.

section.title: Problem 2: /Contents Size Exceeded
section.simpara: Symptom: IOException: Can’t write signature, not enough space during signing.
Cause: The CMS SignedData (after hex encoding) exceeds the reserved /Contents space.
Fix: Increase SignatureOptions.setPreferredSignatureSize(). See the size estimation section above for recommended values by PAdES profile.

section.title: Problem 3: ByteRange Doesn’t Start at Zero
section.simpara: Symptom: Validator reports "invalid byte range" or "signature does not cover document header."
Cause: Extremely rare — would indicate a buggy signing library. The spec requires that the signed region start at byte 0 to cover the PDF header.
Resolution: This is unfixable after the fact — the PDF must be re-signed.

section.title: Problem 4: Gap Contains More Than Just /Contents
section.simpara: Symptom: Validator reports "unexpected data in signature gap" or hash mismatch.

Cause: The bytes between the two signed segments should contain only the /Contents hex string. If any other data exists in this gap — for example, if a tool inserted extra whitespace or a stray dictionary key — the byte range geometry is broken.
Detection: Parse the gap region and verify it matches the pattern <[0-9A-Fa-f]+> with optional zero padding.

section.title: Problem 5: Hash Algorithm Mismatch
section.simpara: Symptom: Computed hash doesn’t match CMS messageDigest attribute, even though the byte range looks correct.

Cause: You’re hashing with a different algorithm than the one specified in the CMS. The CMS SignerInfo.digestAlgorithm tells you which algorithm was used — it might be SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512, or (for legacy documents) SHA-1.
Fix: Always read the digest algorithm from the CMS before computing the hash:

listitem.simpara: The byte range divides a signed PDF into exactly two signed segments with one unsigned gap between them
The gap contains only the hex-encoded CMS signature value (the /Contents string) — nothing else
offset2 + length2 must equal the file size; any trailing bytes are unsigned and potentially malicious
Each signature in a multi-signature document covers all previous signatures, creating a nested coverage model
Always reserve generous space for /Contents — 64 KB is a safe default for most PAdES profiles
The /ByteRange array itself is signed — it cannot be modified after signing without detection
Byte range verification is the first step of signature validation — if it fails, there is no need to check the CMS
The Incremental Saving Attack (ISA) exploits the trailing bytes problem; the trailing bytes check is your primary defense

partintro.simpara: PDF digital signatures come in two fundamentally different flavors, and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes in signature implementation. An approval signature says "I have reviewed and agree with this document." A certification signature says "I am the author of this document, and I am defining what changes are allowed after I sign it."

The difference is not just semantic — it is structural. A certification signature carries a /DocMDP (Modification Detection and Prevention) transform that defines permission levels, restricts what subsequent signers and form fillers can do, and triggers different validation behavior in viewers. Getting this wrong means your signatures either restrict too much (form fields that can’t be filled after signing) or too little (no protection against unauthorized modifications).
This chapter explains both signature types, the DocMDP permission model, Lock and FieldMDP dictionaries, and the interaction rules that govern what happens when multiple signatures coexist.

section.title: Approval Signatures
section.simpara: An approval signature is what most people think of when they hear "digital signature." It means: "I, the signer, have reviewed the document and give my approval."
Technically, an approval signature is a /Sig dictionary with:

listitem.simpara: /Filter: /Adobe.PPKLite
/SubFilter: adbe.pkcs7.detached or ETSI.CAdES.detached
/ByteRange: covers the document up to this point
No /TransformMethod in any associated signature reference dictionary

section.simpara: Approval signatures have these properties:

listitem.simpara: Multiple allowed: A document can have any number of approval signatures. Each one adds an incremental update.

No permission restrictions: An approval signature does not limit what subsequent users can do with the document. After you apply an approval signature, someone else can add another signature, fill form fields, add annotations, or even modify the document (though modifications will cause the signature to show as "modified since signing").

Order matters: Each approval signature covers everything up to the point it was applied. Signature 2 covers signature 1. If the document is modified between signature 1 and signature 2, signature 1 will show as "document modified" while signature 2 will show as "valid" (because it was applied after the modification).

section.title: Certification Signatures
section.simpara: A certification signature (also called an "author signature" or "MDP signature") is structurally different. It carries a /TransformMethod of /DocMDP in its signature reference dictionary, which embeds a set of permission rules into the signature.
Technically, a certification signature includes:

listitem.simpara: Everything an approval signature has
A /Reference array in the signature dictionary containing a /SigRef (Signature Reference) dictionary
The /SigRef dictionary specifies /TransformMethod /DocMDP
A /TransformParams dictionary with a /P key (the permission level)

section.simpara: And at the document level:

listitem.simpara: The document catalog’s /Perms dictionary includes a /DocMDP entry pointing to the certifying signature

section.simpara: Certification signatures have these properties:

listitem.simpara: Only one per document: A document can have at most one certification signature. It is always the first signature applied. If you try to add a certification signature to a document that already has one, the behavior is undefined — some viewers will reject it, others will ignore the previous one.

Defines permissions: The /P value (1, 2, or 3) controls what changes are allowed after certification. This is the defining feature that makes certification signatures different from approval signatures.
First in the chain: The certification signature is always the foundational signature. All subsequent signatures are approval signatures that build on the certified baseline.

section.screen: << /Type /Sig /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite /SubFilter /adbe.pkcs7.detached /ByteRange [0 14231 47007 1849] /Contents <3082...> /Reference [ << /Type /SigRef /TransformMethod /DocMDP /TransformParams << /Type /TransformParams /P 2 /V /1.2 >> >> ] /Name (Document Author) /M (D:20260308120000+01'00') >>
section.simpara: The document catalog connects this signature to the document-level permission system:
section.screen: << /Type /Catalog /AcroForm << ... >> /Perms << /DocMDP 15 0 R >> % Reference to the certifying signature object /Pages 3 0 R >>

section.title: Permission Level 1: No Changes Allowed
section.simpara: /P 1 is the strictest level. After certification with P=1, no changes whatsoever are allowed to the document. This means:

listitem.simpara: No form fields can be filled
No additional signatures can be added
No annotations can be created
No pages can be added or removed
Any incremental update invalidates the certification

section.simpara: This level is appropriate for documents that are complete and final — archived records, published regulations, completed certificates. In practice, it is rarely used because most signing workflows require at least form filling or countersigning.

warning.simpara: P=1 is so restrictive that it prevents the document from being countersigned. If your workflow requires multiple signatures (author signs, then reviewer countersigns), do not use P=1. Use P=2 instead — it allows additional signatures while preventing content modifications.

section.title: Permission Level 2: Form Filling and Signing Only
section.simpara: /P 2 is the default and most common permission level. After certification with P=2:

listitem.simpara: Allowed: Filling form fields, applying additional (approval) signatures, adding signature appearances
Not allowed: Modifying page content, adding pages, removing pages, changing form field definitions (adding/removing fields), modifying JavaScript actions
Any change beyond form filling and signing invalidates the certification

section.simpara: P=2 is the standard for government forms, contracts, and regulated documents. The document author sets up the form (fields, layout, instructions), certifies it, and then the form recipient fills in the fields and applies their own approval signature — without being able to alter the form structure or content.
This is the level you should use unless you have a specific reason to choose P=1 or P=3.

section.title: Permission Level 3: Form Filling, Signing, and Annotations
section.simpara: /P 3 is the most permissive certification level. After certification with P=3:

listitem.simpara: Allowed: Everything P=2 allows, plus adding annotations (comments, highlights, stamps, sticky notes, text markup)
Not allowed: Modifying page content, adding or removing pages, changing form field definitions

section.simpara: P=3 is appropriate for review workflows where multiple people need to comment on a certified document without altering its content. Think of a contract that needs review comments from legal, compliance, and management before final signatures.

section.title: What "Invalidates the Certification" Actually Means
section.simpara: When we say a change "invalidates the certification," we mean:

listitem.simpara: The byte range of the certification signature remains intact — the hash still matches
The CMS signature still verifies cryptographically
But a conforming viewer detects that the modifications in subsequent revisions exceed the allowed permissions and displays a warning

section.simpara: In Adobe Acrobat, this shows as: "The document has been altered or corrupted since the Signature was applied." In LibreOffice, it shows as: "Document has been modified since it was last signed."

The exact wording varies by viewer, and — this is important — not all viewers enforce MDP permissions correctly. Some viewers only check whether the byte range is intact and don’t analyze the incremental updates for permission compliance. This is one of the weaknesses that the Bochum team exploited in the Incremental Saving Attack (Chapter 24).

section.title: What Lock Dictionaries Do
section.simpara: Beyond document-level MDP permissions, PDF supports field-level locking via the /Lock dictionary on a signature field. A Lock dictionary specifies which form fields should be locked (made read-only) once this particular signature is applied.
The Lock dictionary lives in the signature field dictionary (not the signature value dictionary), and it applies when the field is signed:
section.screen: << /Type /Annot /Subtype /Widget /FT /Sig /T (SignatureField1) /V 15 0 R % Reference to the /Sig dictionary /Lock << /Type /SigFieldLock /Action /Include % or /Exclude or /All /Fields [(FieldA) (FieldB)] >> >>

entry.simpara: Lock all fields in the document. No /Fields array is needed. After this signature is applied, every form field in the document becomes read-only. This is the simplest but most restrictive lock.

Lock only the fields listed in the /Fields array. The listed fields become read-only; all other fields remain fillable. Use this when you want to protect specific fields (e.g., "Price" and "Total") while allowing others (e.g., "Customer Signature") to be filled.

Lock all fields except those listed in the /Fields array. The listed fields remain fillable; everything else is locked. Use this when most fields should be locked but a few specific fields must remain editable (e.g., a "Date Received" field that an administrator fills after the signer has signed).

section.title: The /Fields Array
section.simpara: The /Fields array in the Lock dictionary contains the fully qualified field names of the fields to include or exclude. In a hierarchical field tree, the fully qualified name is the concatenation of parent and child names separated by periods:
section.screen: /Fields [(PersonalInfo.FirstName) (PersonalInfo.LastName) (Signature2)]

warning.simpara: Field name matching is case-sensitive and must be exact. A typo in the Lock dictionary’s /Fields array means the field won’t be locked — and there will be no error message. The signature applies successfully, the Lock dictionary is present, but the misspelled field remains editable.
Always test your Lock configurations by:

listitem.simpara: Signing the document with the Lock dictionary
Opening it in Adobe Acrobat
Attempting to edit the fields that should be locked
Verifying that Acrobat prevents editing and shows the fields as read-only

section.title: FieldMDP vs. DocMDP
section.simpara: While DocMDP controls what categories of changes are allowed (form filling, annotations, etc.), FieldMDP controls which specific fields can be modified. It is a more targeted mechanism.
FieldMDP uses the same /Reference array mechanism as DocMDP but with /TransformMethod /FieldMDP:
section.screen: << /Type /SigRef /TransformMethod /FieldMDP /TransformParams << /Type /TransformParams /Action /Include /Fields [(ProtectedField1) (ProtectedField2)] /V /2.0 >> >>
section.simpara: The /Action and /Fields keys work identically to Lock dictionary actions — /Include, /Exclude, and /All with the same semantics.
The difference from Lock dictionaries:

listitem.simpara: Lock dictionary: Controls whether a field is editable in the viewer UI (read-only vs. writable). It’s a presentation-level restriction — the viewer should prevent the user from typing in a locked field.

FieldMDP: Controls whether modifications to specific fields are detected during validation. If a FieldMDP-protected field is changed after signing, the signature shows as modified. It’s a validation-level mechanism.

section.simpara: In practice, Lock and FieldMDP are often used together: Lock prevents casual editing in a UI, and FieldMDP ensures that any bypass of the Lock (via direct file manipulation) is detected during validation.

chapter.title: Interaction Rules: When Multiple Signatures Coexist

section.title: The Certification + Approval Pattern
section.simpara: The most common multi-signature pattern is:

listitem.simpara: Author creates the document with form fields
Author certifies with P=2 (form filling and signing allowed) and optionally locks specific fields
Recipient fills form fields
Recipient applies an approval signature (which adds an incremental update with the filled values and the new signature)

section.simpara: This works because:

listitem.simpara: The certification (P=2) allows form filling and additional signatures
The approval signature is applied to a document that already has a certification
The certification signature remains valid because the changes (form fill + new signature) are within the P=2 permissions

section.title: What Happens When Permissions Conflict
section.simpara: Things get complicated when:

Technically, any signature can be applied to any document. If someone applies an approval signature first and then a certification signature second, the certification signature covers (signs over) the approval signature. But the certification’s MDP permissions only apply to changes after the certification — they don’t retroactively validate the approval signature’s changes.
In practice, this scenario is unusual and may confuse validators. Always apply the certification signature first.

simpara.emphasis: Scenario 2: Lock dictionary conflicts with MDP

section.simpara: If the certification says P=2 (form filling allowed) but a Lock dictionary locks all fields — the Lock takes precedence for the locked fields. The MDP permission defines the ceiling (what’s possible), and Lock dictionaries further restrict within that ceiling.

simpara.emphasis: Scenario 3: Multiple Lock dictionaries from different signatures

section.simpara: Each signature field can have its own Lock dictionary. When multiple signatures with Lock dictionaries coexist:

listitem.simpara: Locks are cumulative — each new signature can lock additional fields
Locks are permanent — once a field is locked by any signature, no subsequent signature can unlock it
A field is locked if any Lock dictionary in the chain locks it

section.simpara: This means the first signer can lock fields A and B, the second signer can lock fields C and D, and after both signatures are applied, fields A, B, C, and D are all locked.

section.title: Creating a Certification Signature

note.simpara: PDFBox’s API for certification signatures evolved between versions. In PDFBox 2.x, you used doc.addSignature() with a SignatureOptions and separately configured the MDP via the catalog’s /Perms dictionary. In PDFBox 3.x, the AccessPermission class provides a cleaner API, but the underlying mechanism is the same — writing the /DocMDP reference into the catalog.

section.title: Adding Lock Dictionaries
section.simpara: Lock dictionaries are configured on the signature field before signing:

chapter.title: Seed Value Dictionaries: Constraining How a Signature Field Is Signed

section.title: Purpose of Seed Values
section.simpara: A seed value dictionary (/SV) on a signature field constrains how the field can be signed. It does not sign anything itself — it provides instructions to the signing tool about what certificate to use, what algorithm to use, which SubFilter is required, and other signing parameters.

This is the mechanism that government form creators use to ensure that form fillers sign with the correct certificate type, use the correct algorithm, and produce signatures that will pass validation.

entry.simpara: The signature handler to use. In practice, always /Adobe.PPKLite. If set, the signing tool should use this handler — if a different handler is selected, the signing tool should warn the user.
Array of acceptable SubFilter values. The signing tool should use one of these SubFilter values. Example: [/adbe.pkcs7.detached /ETSI.CAdES.detached] means either detached format is acceptable.
Array of acceptable digest algorithms. Example: [/SHA256 /SHA384 /SHA512]. If set, the signing tool must use one of these algorithms. /SHA1 is deprecated and should not be included.

Array of text strings presenting acceptable signing reasons. The signing tool should let the user choose from this list. Example: [(I approve this document) (I have reviewed this document) (I am the authorized signatory)].
The MDP parameters dictionary, specifying the default DocMDP permission level for signatures applied to this field.

Timestamp seed value dictionary specifying a timestamp authority URL and whether a timestamp is required. If the /Ff flag is set to 1 (required), the signing tool must include a timestamp from the specified TSA.

Flags indicating which seed values are mandatory (bit 1 set) vs. recommended (bit 1 clear). If a seed value’s corresponding Ff bit is set, the signing tool must respect the constraint — failure to do so should prevent the signature from being applied.
Array of legal attestation strings. The signer may be required to accept one of these attestations as part of the signing ceremony.

section.title: Timestamp Seed Values
section.simpara: The /TimeStamp sub-dictionary in seed values is particularly important for PAdES compliance:
section.screen: /SV << /TimeStamp << /URL (http://tsa.example.com/timestamp) /Ff 1 % 1 = timestamp required, 0 = optional >> /SubFilter [/ETSI.CAdES.detached] /DigestMethod [/SHA256 /SHA384 /SHA512] /Ff 1 % All seed values are mandatory >>
section.simpara: This seed value dictionary says: "When signing this field, you must use CAdES detached format, SHA-256 or better, and you must include a timestamp from tsa.example.com. All of these requirements are mandatory (Ff=1)."

A conforming signing tool (Adobe Acrobat, for example) will enforce these constraints automatically. PDFBox-based signing code must read and respect these seed values manually — the library does not enforce them automatically.

tip.simpara: Always read the seed value dictionary before signing. If you’re building a signing service that signs arbitrary PDFs, the seed values may impose constraints that your default configuration doesn’t meet. Ignoring them produces a signature that appears valid cryptographically but violates the form author’s intent — and may fail validation in systems that check seed value compliance.

section.title: Mistake 1: Using Certification When Approval Is Needed
section.simpara: Scenario: A developer creates a multi-signature workflow where three people sign sequentially. They make the first signature a certification signature with P=1 (no changes allowed).
Result: The second and third signatures fail or invalidate the certification, because P=1 prohibits additional signatures.
Fix: Use P=2 for the first (certification) signature if additional signatures are needed. Or use approval signatures for all three if no MDP restrictions are needed.

section.title: Mistake 2: Not Setting MDP When It’s Needed
section.simpara: Scenario: A government form is meant to be filled and signed by the citizen, with no other modifications allowed. The form creator signs it as a plain approval signature without MDP.

Result: The form has no modification restrictions. Anyone can modify the form structure, add fields, remove fields, or alter content — and the original signature only indicates "document has been modified" without specifying what was allowed.
Fix: Use a certification signature with P=2 to allow form filling while preventing structural changes.

section.title: Mistake 3: Lock Dictionary with Wrong Field Names
section.simpara: Scenario: The signature is configured to lock fields "Price", "Total", and "Terms". But the actual field names in the PDF are "price_field", "total_amount", and "contract_terms" (because the form designer used different naming).
Result: The Lock dictionary is present and syntactically correct, but no fields are actually locked — the names don’t match. There is no error message or warning.
Fix: Enumerate all field names before configuring the Lock dictionary. In PDFBox:

section.title: Mistake 4: Assuming All Viewers Enforce MDP
section.simpara: Scenario: A developer relies on MDP P=2 to prevent content modifications, assuming all viewers will refuse to let users edit non-form content after certification.

Result: Adobe Acrobat enforces MDP correctly. Foxit enforces it mostly correctly. LibreOffice enforces it partially. PDFBox (when used as a library) does not enforce MDP at all — it allows any modification programmatically. Browser-based PDF viewers (Chrome, Firefox) often ignore MDP entirely because they don’t support editing.

Lesson: MDP is a validation mechanism, not an access control mechanism. It tells you whether changes were permitted, not whether they were prevented. A determined user with a hex editor or a programmatic tool can always modify a PDF — MDP ensures you can detect that the modification occurred.

listitem.simpara: Approval signatures: "I approve this document." Multiple allowed, no restrictions on subsequent changes, each covers all previous content.
Certification signatures: "I authored this document and define what can change." Only one per document, must be first, carries DocMDP permissions (P=1/2/3).
P=2 is the default and most common permission level — allows form filling and additional signatures while preventing content modifications.
Lock dictionaries control which fields become read-only after a specific signature is applied. Cumulative and permanent.
FieldMDP provides validation-level detection of changes to specific fields — complements Lock dictionaries.
Seed value dictionaries constrain how a signature field must be signed — SubFilter, digest algorithm, timestamp requirements.
MDP is a detection mechanism, not a prevention mechanism. Not all viewers enforce it. Always verify compliance programmatically.
Always apply certification signatures first, then approval signatures. The certification establishes the permission framework.

partintro.simpara: A digital signature has two faces. The cryptographic face — the CMS SignedData, the byte range, the certificate chain — is what makes the signature valid. The visual face — what the user sees in the PDF viewer — is what makes the signature trustworthy to a human reader. These two faces are entirely independent, and understanding their independence is critical for both security and usability.

The visual appearance of a PDF signature is an annotation appearance stream — a miniature PDF drawing that shows the signer’s name, date, reason, and optionally a handwritten signature image, a company logo, or other graphical elements. This appearance stream is part of the signed bytes (it lives inside the signature field’s widget annotation), but it is not cryptographically bound to the CMS in any meaningful way. A valid CMS signature does not guarantee that the visual appearance accurately represents the signer — it only guarantees that the bytes haven’t changed.

This distinction is exactly what Shadow Attacks (Chapter 24) exploit: an attacker can create a PDF where the visual appearance shows one thing to the signer and something different to the recipient, with the signature validating correctly in both cases.
This chapter explains how signature appearances work, how to create them with PDFBox, what the layer model means, and how different viewers handle appearance rendering.

section.title: What an Appearance Stream Is
section.simpara: Every annotation in a PDF — not just signatures — can have an appearance stream. An appearance stream is a Form XObject (a /Type /XObject /Subtype /Form stream) that contains the drawing instructions for how the annotation looks.
For a signature widget annotation, the appearance stream is stored under the /AP (Appearance) key:
section.screen: << /Type /Annot /Subtype /Widget /FT /Sig /T (SignatureField1) /V 15 0 R % the /Sig dictionary /Rect [100 600 300 650] % position on the page (x1 y1 x2 y2) /AP << /N 20 0 R % Normal appearance stream >> >>
section.simpara: The /N key holds the normal appearance — the one shown when the annotation is not being interacted with. Annotations can also have /R (rollover — shown on mouse hover) and /D (down — shown when clicked), but signature appearances only use /N.
The appearance stream (object 20 in this example) is a Form XObject:
section.screen: 20 0 obj << /Type /XObject /Subtype /Form /BBox [0 0 200 50] % bounding box in the stream's coordinate space /Resources << /Font << /F1 21 0 R >> >> /Length 142 >> stream BT /F1 10 Tf 0 35 Td (Digitally signed by: Test Signer) Tj 0 -15 Td (Date: 2026-03-08 12:00:00 UTC) Tj 0 -15 Td (Reason: I approve this document) Tj ET endstream endobj
section.simpara: This is a complete miniature PDF page: it has a bounding box, font resources, and drawing instructions using the standard PDF content stream operators (BT/ET for text blocks, Tf for font selection, Td for positioning, Tj for showing text).

section.title: Visible vs. Invisible Signatures
section.simpara: Not all signatures need a visual appearance. A signature can be:
Visible: The signature field has a non-zero /Rect (annotation rectangle) and an appearance stream. The user sees a signature block on a specific page.

Invisible: The signature field either has a zero-size /Rect ([0 0 0 0]), or has no appearance stream, or is not associated with any page. The signature exists cryptographically but doesn’t appear on any page.

Invisible signatures are common in automated signing workflows where the PDF is being signed programmatically (e.g., a server signing all outgoing invoices) and there’s no need for a visual indicator on the document. The signature is still listed in the viewer’s signature panel — the user can still inspect it, verify the certificate, and check the validation status.

tip.simpara: Even for invisible signatures, some viewers (notably Adobe Acrobat) display a blue banner at the top of the document saying "Signed and all signatures are valid" or similar. This banner is not part of the PDF content — it’s the viewer’s UI.

chapter.title: The Layer Model: n0, n2, and n4

section.title: Adobe’s Legacy Layer System
section.simpara: Adobe Acrobat uses a layer-based approach to compose signature appearances. This system was designed by Adobe for their own implementation and is not formally part of the ISO 32000 specification — but because Adobe Acrobat is the dominant viewer, understanding it matters.
The layers are:

listitem.simpara: n0 (Layer 0 — Background): Contains the background graphic and a static validity indicator. Adobe Acrobat uses this to show the blue "valid" checkmark or the red "invalid" X. This layer is generated and updated by Acrobat itself — it is not typically set by the signing application.

n2 (Layer 2 — Content): Contains the signer’s information — name, date, reason, location, and optionally a handwritten signature image or company logo. This is the layer you create when building a visual signature appearance.
n4 (Layer 4 — Text overlay): An optional text overlay, rarely used.

section.simpara: In practice, most signing applications (including PDFBox-based implementations) create a single appearance stream that serves as the entire appearance — they don’t use the n0/n2/n4 layer decomposition. Adobe Acrobat, when it encounters a signature without layer-based appearances, creates its own composite appearance using the provided stream as the content layer and adding its own validity indicators.

note.simpara: The layer model is an implementation detail of Adobe Acrobat, not a spec requirement. When you create a signature appearance with PDFBox, you create a single Form XObject for the /AP /N entry. Acrobat will overlay its own validity graphics on top. Other viewers (LibreOffice, Foxit, browser-based viewers) each handle the appearance differently — some show your appearance as-is, some add their own decorations.

section.title: What This Means for Your Implementation
section.simpara: When building a signature appearance with PDFBox:

listitem.simpara: Create a single Form XObject with the signer’s information
Don’t worry about n0/n2/n4 layers — the single appearance works across all viewers
Set reasonable fonts and sizes — the appearance will be rendered in the space defined by the signature field’s /Rect
Include key information: signer’s name, signing date/time, reason (if applicable)
Test in multiple viewers — Acrobat, Foxit, LibreOffice, and a browser-based viewer — to ensure the appearance renders correctly in each

section.title: Basic Text Appearance
section.simpara: The simplest appearance shows the signer’s name and date:

warning.simpara: In PDFBox 3.x, you cannot use PDPageContentStream to write to a Form XObject. PDPageContentStream is strictly for page content. For Form XObjects, you must write raw PDF operators to the stream’s output stream, as shown above.

This is one of the breaking changes from PDFBox 2.x, where PDPageContentStream could be used with Form XObjects via a constructor overload that accepted a PDFormXObject parameter. That constructor no longer exists in 3.x.

section.title: Appearance with Signature Image
section.simpara: Many signing workflows include a handwritten signature image — either captured via a canvas on a web page or tablet, or uploaded as a PNG/JPEG file. Adding an image to the appearance requires embedding it as a PDF image XObject:

section.title: Attaching the Appearance to the Signature
section.simpara: After creating the appearance Form XObject, attach it to the signature field’s widget annotation:

section.title: How Different Viewers Handle Appearances
section.simpara: One of the most frustrating aspects of PDF signature appearances is that different viewers render them differently — even when the appearance stream is identical.

table.title: Signature appearance rendering behavior across major viewers

entry.simpara: Overlays its own validity indicator (blue checkmark / red X) on layer n0. Your appearance stream is rendered on layer n2. Acrobat may resize your content to fit its layer model. If /NeedAppearances is true in the AcroForm, Acrobat may regenerate appearances from scratch.
Renders the appearance stream mostly as provided. Adds a small validity icon in the corner. Generally the most faithful rendering of custom appearances.

Renders the appearance stream as-is. Does not overlay validity indicators on the appearance itself — shows validation status in a separate panel. Good for testing that your raw appearance looks correct.
Renders appearance streams but does not validate signatures. No validity indicators. The appearance is what you see — useful for checking visual layout.
Renders appearance streams. Does not validate signatures (though experimental validation support has been discussed). Like Chrome, useful for layout testing.
Renders appearances and provides signature validation. May show validity status differently from other viewers.

important.simpara: Always test your signature appearances in at least three viewers — Adobe Acrobat, a non-Adobe desktop viewer (Foxit or LibreOffice), and a browser. Appearances that look perfect in Acrobat may render differently in other viewers due to:

listitem.simpara: Different font fallback behavior (if the font isn’t embedded)
Different handling of the coordinate system and bounding box
Different overlay/decoration behavior
Different treatment of transparency and blending

section.title: The /NeedAppearances Problem
section.simpara: The AcroForm dictionary has a boolean flag /NeedAppearances that tells viewers whether to regenerate form field appearances from scratch. If this flag is true, some viewers (notably Adobe Acrobat) will ignore the existing appearance streams and generate their own based on the field’s value.

For signature fields, this is usually not what you want. If /NeedAppearances is true and Acrobat regenerates the signature’s appearance, your carefully crafted visual (with the signer’s image, custom layout, etc.) gets replaced with Acrobat’s default rendering.
Always set /NeedAppearances to false (or remove the key entirely — the default is false) before signing:

section.title: The Coordinate System
section.simpara: PDF uses a bottom-left origin coordinate system where (0, 0) is at the lower-left corner of the page. The y-axis goes up, not down. This is the opposite of most screen coordinate systems and is a common source of positioning errors.
The signature field’s /Rect array defines the annotation rectangle as [x1 y1 x2 y2], where:

listitem.simpara: (x1, y1) is the lower-left corner
(x2, y2) is the upper-right corner

section.simpara: So a signature field at the bottom-right of an A4 page (595 x 842 points) might have:
section.screen: /Rect [395 50 575 100] % 180pt wide, 50pt tall, near bottom-right

warning.simpara: A common mistake: specifying the rectangle with screen coordinates (origin at top-left, y going down). If you compute y = pageHeight - desiredY, make sure you do this for both y1 and y2, and that y1 < y2.
Debug tip: If your signature appearance shows up in the wrong position, check:

listitem.simpara: Is the page rotated? (A /Rotate key on the page dictionary changes the visible coordinate system)
Is the MediaBox the expected size? (Custom page sizes shift everything)
Did you flip y1 and y2? (The lower-left corner must have the smaller y value)

section.title: Handling Page Rotation
section.simpara: PDF pages can have a /Rotate key that specifies a rotation angle (0, 90, 180, or 270 degrees). This rotation affects how the page is displayed but does not change the coordinate system of the page content or annotations.

This means that on a 90-degree rotated page (landscape orientation achieved via rotation), the annotation rectangle coordinates are still in the unrotated coordinate space. To place a signature at the visual bottom-right of a rotated page, you need to account for the rotation.

section.title: The Appearance/Content Disconnect
section.simpara: The visual appearance of a signature is stored in the PDF’s object structure, which is covered by the byte range. But the appearance is not cryptographically bound to the identity of the signer. The appearance can show anything — "Signed by Alice" while the CMS is signed by Bob’s certificate.

A conforming viewer should display the certificate information from the CMS (not from the appearance) in its signature properties panel. But what the user sees on the page is the appearance — and most users trust the visual appearance more than the signature properties panel.

This is a deliberate design trade-off in PDF: the signer (or the signing application) controls what the appearance looks like, and the viewer overlays validity information. The validity indicators (checkmarks, status banners) come from the viewer’s own analysis of the CMS — they don’t come from the appearance stream.

section.title: Shadow Attacks and Visual Appearances
section.simpara: Shadow Attacks (Chapter 24) exploit the gap between what a signer sees and what the document contains. In the Replace variant, the attacker creates a form field with a specific visual appearance, then changes the field’s value (and thus its appearance) after the signer certifies the document. Because the certification allows form filling (P=2 or P=3), the change is permitted — but the visual content of the document has changed dramatically.

The defense is the same as for all Shadow Attacks: validate not just the cryptographic integrity but also the semantic integrity — what changed between revisions, and were those changes consistent with the displayed content?

tip.simpara: For maximum security: When validating a signed PDF, don’t just check the signature’s validity. Extract the revision that was signed (by truncating the file at the byte range end — see Chapter 13) and compare its visual rendering to the current document. If they differ, the document has been modified since signing — even if the modifications were technically "permitted" by the MDP level.

section.title: Problem: Blank Signature Rectangle
section.simpara: Symptom: The signature field shows as an empty box in the viewer.
Causes:

listitem.simpara: No appearance stream set (the /AP dictionary is missing)
/NeedAppearances is true, and the viewer regenerated an empty appearance
The Form XObject has an empty stream (no drawing instructions)
The bounding box (/BBox) is [0 0 0 0] or has zero dimensions

section.title: Problem: Text Appears Clipped or Off-Screen
section.simpara: Symptom: Only part of the signature text is visible, or the text appears outside the signature rectangle.
Causes:

listitem.simpara: The text positioning (Td operators) exceeds the Form XObject’s /BBox
The font size is too large for the available space
The /BBox doesn’t match the annotation’s /Rect dimensions

section.simpara: Fix: Ensure the Form XObject’s /BBox width and height match the annotation /Rect dimensions, and that all drawing instructions fall within the /BBox.

section.title: Problem: Font Not Rendering
section.simpara: Symptom: Text appears as squares, dots, or is completely invisible.
Causes:

listitem.simpara: The font resource is not declared in the Form XObject’s /Resources
The font name in the content stream (/F1) doesn’t match the resource key
Non-ASCII characters used with a non-embedded Standard 14 font (Standard 14 fonts only support WinAnsiEncoding)

section.simpara: Fix: For text beyond basic Latin characters (accented letters, CJK, etc.), embed a TrueType or Type0 font. Standard 14 fonts (Helvetica, Times, Courier, etc.) only support a limited character set.

listitem.simpara: Signature appearance is an annotation appearance stream — a Form XObject with drawing instructions
The appearance is visually independent from the cryptographic signature — they can show different things
Invisible signatures have zero-size rectangles or no appearance stream — they exist cryptographically but don’t show on the page
The n0/n2/n4 layer model is Adobe’s implementation detail, not a spec requirement — create a single Form XObject for maximum compatibility
PDFBox 3.x requires writing raw PDF operators to Form XObject streams — PDPageContentStream only works for pages
Always set /NeedAppearances to false before signing to prevent viewers from regenerating your custom appearance
Test in multiple viewers — Acrobat, Foxit, LibreOffice, and browsers all render appearances differently
The coordinate system is bottom-left origin with y going up — account for page rotation when positioning
Visual appearances are a security surface — Shadow Attacks exploit the disconnect between appearance and content

partintro.simpara: Signing a PDF is the easy part. Validating a signed PDF — correctly, securely, and in a way that matches what other validators produce — is where the real complexity lives. A signature can fail validation for dozens of reasons, and the error messages from different viewers are often vague or contradictory. Adobe says "valid." LibreOffice says "signature cannot be verified." Your custom validator throws a CMSException. Which one is right?

This chapter walks through the complete validation process, from the first byte range check to the final certificate chain verification. We cover what each validation step checks, what can go wrong at each step, how different viewers handle each step, and how to debug validation failures systematically.

chapter.title: The Five Steps of Signature Validation
chapter.simpara: Signature validation is not a single check — it is a pipeline of five independent verifications, each of which must pass. A failure at any step means the signature is invalid (or at least questionable), regardless of whether the other steps pass.

section.title: Step 1: Structural Integrity (Byte Range)
section.simpara: Question answered: "Does the signature cover the expected bytes of the file?"
What to check:

listitem.simpara: The /ByteRange array has exactly 4 elements: [offset1 length1 offset2 length2]
offset1 is 0 (signature covers from the start of the file)
offset2 + length2 equals the file size (or revision size for multi-signature documents)
The gap [length1, offset2) contains only the /Contents hex string
No trailing unsigned bytes exist after the signed region (unless they are a subsequent legitimate revision)

section.simpara: Common failures:

listitem.simpara: Trailing bytes after the signed region — possible Incremental Saving Attack (Chapter 24)
ByteRange doesn’t start at 0 — broken signing implementation
Gap contains unexpected data — corrupted or manually edited PDF

section.simpara: Chapter 13 covers this step in exhaustive detail.

section.title: Step 2: Hash Verification (Digest Match)
section.simpara: Question answered: "Does the hash of the signed bytes match what the CMS claims was signed?"
What to check:

listitem.simpara: Read the digest algorithm from the CMS SignerInfo.digestAlgorithm
Compute the hash over the byte range segments: hash = algo(segment1 || segment2)
Extract the messageDigest attribute from the CMS signedAttrs
Compare the computed hash to the CMS-provided hash

section.simpara: Common failures:

listitem.simpara: Hash algorithm mismatch — you computed SHA-256 but the CMS used SHA-384
Byte range computation error — you read the wrong bytes
Modified file — someone changed the file after signing

section.title: Step 3: Cryptographic Verification (CMS Signature)
section.simpara: Question answered: "Was the CMS SignedData actually created by the claimed signer’s private key?"
What to check:

listitem.simpara: Extract the signer’s certificate from the CMS SignedData.certificates collection
Verify that the SignerInfo.signatureAlgorithm matches the certificate’s key type (RSA cert with RSA signature, ECDSA cert with ECDSA signature)
Verify the signature over the signedAttrs using the signer’s public key
Verify that the signed attributes include the required fields: contentType, messageDigest, and (for PAdES) ESS-signing-certificate-v2

section.simpara: Common failures:

listitem.simpara: Wrong certificate used for verification — the CMS contains multiple certificates, and you picked an intermediate instead of the signer
Algorithm mismatch — rare, but can happen with misconfigured BouncyCastle providers
Missing ESS-signing-certificate-v2 — not a cryptographic failure, but a PAdES compliance failure

section.title: Step 4: Certificate Chain Validation
section.simpara: Question answered: "Is the signer’s certificate trustworthy? Was it valid at the time of signing?"
What to check:

listitem.simpara: Build the certificate chain from the signer’s certificate up to a trusted root
Verify each certificate’s signature (each cert is signed by its issuer)
Check validity periods — was each certificate valid at the signing time?
Check revocation status — was any certificate revoked at or before the signing time?
Check key usage — does the signer’s certificate allow digital signatures (digitalSignature or nonRepudiation key usage)?
Check basic constraints — are intermediate certificates properly marked as CA certificates?

section.simpara: Common failures:

listitem.simpara: Self-signed certificate: The signer’s certificate is not issued by a recognized CA. This is technically valid (the CMS signature will verify) but the certificate is not trusted. Most viewers show "signature is valid but the identity of the signer could not be verified."

Expired certificate: The certificate was valid when the signature was created, but has since expired. With timestamps, this is not a problem (the timestamp proves the signature was created before expiration). Without timestamps, viewers may warn about an expired certificate.

Revoked certificate: A certificate in the chain was revoked (compromised, or the CA issued it in error). Revocation checking requires OCSP or CRL lookup — which may fail if the revocation servers are unreachable.
Missing intermediate: The CMS doesn’t include all intermediate certificates, and the validator can’t build the chain. This is the most common real-world certificate chain failure.

Not in trust store: The root certificate is not in the validator’s trust store. Different validators have different trust stores — Adobe uses AATL, operating systems use their own, and EU validators use the EUTL.

section.title: Step 5: Trust Evaluation
section.simpara: Question answered: "Do I trust the entity that signed this document?"
This is the most subjective step, and it’s where validators diverge the most. Trust evaluation is not a purely technical question — it depends on context:

Adobe Acrobat trust model: Acrobat trusts certificates that chain to a root in the Adobe Approved Trust List (AATL). If the certificate chains to an AATL root, Acrobat shows a green checkmark. If not — even if the certificate is perfectly valid, issued by a reputable CA, and chains to a root in the operating system’s trust store — Acrobat shows a yellow warning: "At least one signature has problems."

This is one of the most frustrating aspects of PDF signature validation for developers. Your signature is cryptographically valid, the certificate is issued by a recognized CA, the certificate is not expired or revoked — but Acrobat shows a warning because the CA is not on Adobe’s list. Getting on the AATL requires the CA to apply to Adobe and pay fees.

EU validation model: EU validators (like the DSS demonstration webapp, or validators used by EU government services) trust certificates that chain to a root in the EU Trusted List (EUTL). The EUTL is the authoritative registry of Qualified Trust Service Providers. A signature is a "qualified electronic signature" — with the legal weight of a handwritten signature under eIDAS — only if the certificate chains to an EUTL-listed QTSP.

Operating system trust model: Some applications (like LibreOffice) use the operating system’s certificate store for trust evaluation. This means the set of trusted roots varies by OS and by the user’s system configuration.

Custom trust model: Your application can define its own trust store — a specific set of root certificates that you trust for your use case. This is common in enterprise environments where the signing certificates are issued by an internal CA.

simpara.emphasis: When debugging trust failures, ask three questions:

listitem.simpara: What trust store is the validator using? (AATL, EUTL, OS store, custom)
Is the signer’s root certificate in that trust store?
Are all intermediates available? (Either embedded in the CMS or fetchable via the certificate’s Authority Information Access extension)

tip.simpara: The answer to question 1 determines everything else. A signature can be "valid" in one validator and "untrusted" in another, purely because of trust store differences.

chapter.title: Validation Differences Across Viewers
chapter.simpara: One of the most confusing aspects of PDF signature validation is that different viewers produce different results for the same PDF. This is not a bug — it’s a consequence of the five-step validation model, where each viewer makes different choices at each step.

table.title: Validation behavior comparison across major PDF viewers

chapter.simpara: "Manual" for PDFBox means the library provides the primitives (signature extraction, byte range access, CMS parsing) but does not perform the validation automatically — you must write the validation logic yourself. This is by design: PDFBox is a PDF library, not a validation service.
EU DSS provides the most complete automated validation, including ETSI-compliant validation reports.

section.title: The Systematic Approach
section.simpara: When a signature fails validation, resist the urge to guess. Instead, walk through the five steps systematically:

simpara.emphasis: Step 1: Is the byte range intact?

section.programlisting: # Check byte range with QPDF qpdf --show-object=<sig-obj-number> document.pdf # Look at /ByteRange — does offset2 + length2 == file size? wc -c document.pdf
// Compute the hash yourself and compare to the CMS messageDigest // (Code in Chapter 13)

# Extract /Contents, decode hex, verify with OpenSSL openssl cms -inform DER -in signature.der -verify \ -content signed-bytes.bin -noverify -out /dev/null # -noverify skips certificate verification — tests only the CMS math

simpara.emphasis: Step 4: Is the certificate chain valid?

section.programlisting: # Extract the signer's certificate and verify the chain openssl cms -inform DER -in signature.der -noout -cmsout -print | \ grep -A 5 "cert:" openssl verify -CAfile trusted-roots.pem signer-cert.pem
section.simpara: Check the signer’s root against the relevant trust store (AATL list, EUTL, OS store).

section.title: Common Error Messages and Their Meaning

entry.simpara: Hash mismatch or CMS verification failure. The document was modified after signing, or the CMS is malformed.
The certificate does not chain to an AATL root. The signature may be cryptographically perfect — Acrobat just doesn’t trust the CA.
"Document has been altered or corrupted since the Signature was applied" (Acrobat)
Either the byte range doesn’t cover the full file (trailing bytes), or modifications after signing exceed the MDP permissions.
"Signature is valid, but the identity of the signer could not be verified" (Acrobat)
CMS is valid, but the certificate chain is incomplete or the root is not trusted.
Generic error — LibreOffice doesn’t distinguish well between CMS failure and trust failure. Check the signature details panel.
CMSException: message-digest attribute value does not match calculated value (BouncyCastle)
Hash mismatch. The bytes you passed to the verifier don’t match what was signed.
A certificate in the chain has expired.
Revocation checking failed because the CRL distribution point is unreachable or the CRL is not cached.

section.title: Why Timestamps Matter for Validation
section.simpara: A digital signature proves that a specific certificate holder signed specific bytes. But it doesn’t prove when the signing happened. Without a timestamp, you can only say "the signature was created sometime before now."
This matters because certificates expire and can be revoked. If a certificate was valid on March 1 but expired on March 31, and you validate the signature on April 15:

listitem.simpara: Without timestamp: The certificate is expired. Some validators will flag the signature as questionable.
With timestamp: The timestamp proves the signature was created on March 1, when the certificate was valid. The signature remains valid despite the certificate’s expiration.

section.title: Verifying the Timestamp
section.simpara: A signature timestamp is embedded as an unsigned attribute (id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken, OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.14) in the CMS SignerInfo:

section.title: Timestamp Chain of Trust
section.simpara: The timestamp itself is a CMS signature — signed by the TSA’s certificate. This certificate has its own chain and trust requirements:

listitem.simpara: The TSA certificate must have the id-kp-timeStamping extended key usage (OID 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.8)
The TSA certificate chain must be valid at the time of verification
The TSA’s root must be in your trust store

section.simpara: If the TSA’s certificate has also expired by the time you validate (years later), you need long-term validation (LTV) — which is the subject of Chapter 18.

section.title: Architecture
section.simpara: A production validation pipeline typically follows this structure:
section.screen: Input: PDF file │ ├─→ Step 1: Extract all signatures │ └─→ For each signature: │ ├─→ Step 2: Byte range check │ ├─→ Step 3: Hash verification │ ├─→ Step 4: CMS verification │ ├─→ Step 5: Certificate chain │ ├─→ Step 6: Revocation check │ ├─→ Step 7: Timestamp verification (if present) │ ├─→ Step 8: MDP compliance (if certification sig) │ └─→ Step 9: Trust evaluation │ └─→ Output: Validation report ├─→ Per-signature results ├─→ Overall document status └─→ Detailed diagnostics
section.simpara: Chapter 31 provides a complete Java implementation of this pipeline.

section.title: When to Use EU DSS Instead
section.simpara: If you need to produce ETSI-compliant validation reports (ETSI TS 119 102-2) — for example, because you’re building a validation service for a government or regulated industry — use the EU DSS framework (Chapter 23) instead of building the pipeline yourself. EU DSS handles all validation steps, produces standards-compliant reports, and has been tested against the full range of PAdES profiles and edge cases.
Building your own pipeline is appropriate when:

listitem.simpara: You need custom trust evaluation logic
You need to validate signatures in a specific context (e.g., your own application’s signing workflow)
You want to understand every step of the validation process (which is the point of this book)
You’re building a specialized tool (forensic analysis, security research)
Signature validation is a five-step pipeline: byte range → hash → CMS → certificate chain → trust
Each step is independent — a failure at any step means the signature is invalid or questionable
Different viewers produce different results because they use different trust stores and enforce different rules
Adobe Acrobat uses AATL for trust; EU validators use EUTL; LibreOffice uses the OS trust store
The most common real-world failure is trust evaluation — the signature is cryptographically perfect but the root CA isn’t in the validator’s trust store
Timestamps prove when a signature was created — essential for handling expired certificates and building long-term validation
Debug systematically: walk through the five steps in order, using QPDF, OpenSSL, and BouncyCastle to isolate the failure
EU DSS provides the most complete automated validation with ETSI-compliant reports
PDFBox provides the primitives for validation but does not automate the pipeline — you build it yourself

partintro.simpara: ISO 32000-2 defines how a digital signature is embedded in a PDF. PAdES defines what that signature must contain to be legally meaningful in Europe — and, increasingly, everywhere else.

PAdES — PDF Advanced Electronic Signatures — is a set of standards published by the European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI) that constrain PDF signatures to meet specific compliance requirements. A PAdES signature is always a valid ISO 32000-2 signature, but the reverse is not true. PAdES adds mandatory signed attributes, requires specific SubFilter values, mandates timestamp handling, and defines four progressively more complete signature profiles.

Understanding PAdES is not optional if your signatures need to be validated by European government services, if your customers require eIDAS-compliant signatures, or if you want your signatures to remain verifiable for years or decades. Even if you’re not operating in the EU, PAdES profiles have become the de facto standard for "properly structured" PDF signatures worldwide — Adobe Acrobat increasingly favors PAdES-structured signatures in its validation logic.
This chapter explains each PAdES profile, what it requires, how the profiles relate to each other, and how to produce each one.

chapter.title: The Four PAdES Baseline Profiles
chapter.simpara: PAdES defines four baseline profiles, each building on the previous one. They are formally named PAdES-B-B, PAdES-B-T, PAdES-B-LT, and PAdES-B-LTA. The naming follows the pattern: PAdES-B(aseline)-<level>.

entry.simpara: The minimum PAdES signature. CMS SignedData with mandatory signed attributes including ESS-signing-certificate-v2. The signature itself — no timestamps, no revocation data.
B-B + a signature timestamp from a trusted TSA. Proves when the signature was created. The timestamp is an unsigned CMS attribute (id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken).

B-T + embedded revocation data (OCSP responses and/or CRLs) and the full certificate chain in the PDF’s DSS (Document Security Store) dictionary. Everything needed to validate the signature offline, without contacting any external servers.

B-LT + a document timestamp signature that covers the entire file (including the DSS dictionary). Enables re-timestamping decades later, creating an indefinite chain of trust that outlives any individual certificate.

section.title: The Progression: Why Four Levels?
section.simpara: Each profile addresses a specific failure mode:

listitem.simpara: B-B fails when: the signing certificate expires (validators may warn), or when you need to prove when the signature was created (there’s no timestamp)
B-T fails when: the OCSP responder or CRL distribution point goes offline years later, and you can’t check revocation status
B-LT fails when: the timestamp’s certificate, or the signing certificate’s root CA, expires or is removed from trust stores decades later
B-LTA handles all of these by providing a renewable chain of timestamps

section.simpara: In practice:

listitem.simpara: B-B is sufficient for short-lived documents that will be validated within the signing certificate’s validity period (typically 1-3 years)
B-T is the minimum for any document with legal significance — you need to prove when it was signed
B-LT is required for long-term archival (10+ years) where you can’t assume external services will remain available
B-LTA is the gold standard for documents that must be verifiable indefinitely — government records, notarized documents, regulatory filings

section.title: Requirements
section.simpara: A PAdES-B-B signature must include:

listitem.simpara: SubFilter: Must be ETSI.CAdES.detached (not adbe.pkcs7.detached)
Mandatory signed attributes in CMS:
content-type (OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.3) — must be id-data
message-digest (OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.4) — the hash of the signed bytes
ESS-signing-certificate-v2 (OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.47) — identifies the signing certificate by its hash, preventing certificate substitution attacks
Signing certificate embedded in CMS: The signer’s certificate (and ideally the full chain up to but not including the root) must be embedded in the CMS SignedData.certificates collection
Digest algorithm: Must be SHA-256 or stronger (SHA-384, SHA-512). SHA-1 is not PAdES-compliant.

section.title: The ESS-signing-certificate-v2 Attribute
section.simpara: This attribute is the key difference between a basic ISO 32000-2 signature and a PAdES-B-B signature. It binds the CMS signature to a specific certificate by including a hash of the certificate in the signed attributes.

Why this matters: without ESS-signing-certificate-v2, an attacker could theoretically substitute a different certificate into the CMS certificates collection — one with the same public key but different identity information. The CMS signature would still verify (same key), but the identity would be wrong. The ESS attribute prevents this by cryptographically committing to a specific certificate.

warning.simpara: A common mistake: using ESS-signing-certificate (v1, OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.12) instead of ESS-signing-certificate-v2 (OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.47). The v1 attribute uses SHA-1 for the certificate hash, which is deprecated. PAdES requires v2, which uses SHA-256 by default.
iText 2.x (and old versions of some other libraries) produced v1 attributes. If you encounter a PDF with ESS-signing-certificate (v1), it’s likely from a legacy signing implementation.

section.title: SubFilter: ETSI.CAdES.detached vs. adbe.pkcs7.detached
section.simpara: PAdES requires the SubFilter value ETSI.CAdES.detached. This is structurally identical to adbe.pkcs7.detached — both produce a detached CMS signature embedded in the /Contents hex string. The difference is semantic: ETSI.CAdES.detached signals that the signature follows the CAdES (CMS Advanced Electronic Signatures) profile, which includes the ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute and other CAdES requirements.
