In practice, many validators accept both SubFilter values for PAdES validation. Adobe Acrobat treats ETSI.CAdES.detached and adbe.pkcs7.detached similarly. But for strict PAdES compliance — especially when dealing with EU government validators — use ETSI.CAdES.detached.

section.title: What B-T Adds
section.simpara: PAdES-B-T is PAdES-B-B plus a signature timestamp — a timestamp token from a Trusted Timestamp Authority (TSA) that proves when the signature was created.

The timestamp is embedded as an unsigned attribute (id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken, OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.14) in the CMS SignerInfo. It’s unsigned because it’s applied after the signature — you sign first, then request a timestamp over the signature value.

section.title: How Timestamping Works
section.simpara: The timestamp workflow:

listitem.simpara: Sign the document — produce the CMS SignedData with all signed attributes (B-B level)
Hash the signature value — compute SHA-256 over the CMS SignerInfo.signature bytes
Send a timestamp request — send the hash to a TSA using RFC 3161 (HTTP POST to the TSA’s URL)
Receive the timestamp response — the TSA returns a TimeStampToken (which is itself a CMS SignedData signed by the TSA’s certificate)
Embed the timestamp — add the TimeStampToken as an unsigned attribute in the CMS

section.title: Choosing a TSA
section.simpara: Choosing a TSA is a non-trivial decision. The TSA’s certificate must be trusted by the validators that will check your signatures. Key considerations:

listitem.simpara: For Adobe Acrobat: The TSA’s root must be in the AATL, or the timestamp will show a warning
For EU compliance: The TSA should be a Qualified Trust Service Provider (QTSP) listed in the EUTL
For availability: The TSA must be reachable when you sign — if the TSA is down, your signing fails (or you produce a B-B signature without a timestamp)
For cost: Some TSAs are free (FreeTSA, DigiCert’s free tier), others charge per timestamp or per certificate

section.simpara: Chapter 18 covers timestamps in depth, including TSA selection, failover strategies, and the Document Security Store.

section.title: What B-LT Adds
section.simpara: PAdES-B-LT is PAdES-B-T plus long-term validation data embedded directly in the PDF. This data ensures that the signature can be validated years from now, even if the signing certificate’s CA has gone out of business, the OCSP responder has been decommissioned, and the CRL distribution points return 404.
The long-term data is stored in the DSS (Document Security Store) dictionary, which is added to the document catalog:
section.screen: << /Type /Catalog /AcroForm << ... >> /DSS << /Certs [30 0 R 31 0 R 32 0 R] % Certificate chain as DER-encoded streams /OCSPs [33 0 R] % OCSP responses as DER-encoded streams /CRLs [34 0 R] % CRLs as DER-encoded streams /VRI << /A1B2C3D4... << % Hash-keyed VRI (Validation Related Info) /Cert [30 0 R 31 0 R] /OCSP [33 0 R] /TU (D:20260308120000Z) % Time of validation >> >> >> >>

entry.simpara: DER-encoded certificates — the full chain for every signature and timestamp in the document. Include the signer’s certificate, all intermediates, and optionally the root.
DER-encoded OCSP responses for certificate revocation checking. Each response covers a specific certificate at a specific point in time.
DER-encoded Certificate Revocation Lists. Used when OCSP is not available for a certificate.
Validation Related Information — a dictionary keyed by uppercase hex SHA-1 hashes of signature values, mapping each signature to its specific validation data.

section.title: Why B-LT Matters
section.simpara: Without B-LT, validating a signature requires contacting external servers:

listitem.simpara: OCSP responder to check revocation status
CRL distribution point to download the revocation list
CA’s AIA (Authority Information Access) URL to fetch intermediate certificates

section.simpara: These servers may be unreachable years from now. CAs shut down, merge, change URLs, or decommission infrastructure. With B-LT, everything needed for validation is embedded in the PDF itself — the document is self-contained.

section.title: What B-LTA Adds
section.simpara: PAdES-B-LTA is PAdES-B-LT plus a document timestamp — a timestamp signature that covers the entire PDF file, including the DSS dictionary.
This timestamp is not part of the original signature’s CMS — it’s a separate signature dictionary with /SubFilter /ETSI.RFC3161:
section.screen: << /Type /Sig /Filter /Adobe.PPKLite /SubFilter /ETSI.RFC3161 /ByteRange [0 52000 84776 2100] /Contents <3082...> >>
section.simpara: This timestamp signature covers the entire file (including the original signature, the DSS, and everything else) and proves that all of this data existed at the time the timestamp was created.

section.title: The Archival Chain
section.simpara: The power of B-LTA is that it creates a renewable chain of trust:

listitem.simpara: The original signature (signed by the signer’s certificate, valid for 1-3 years)
The signature timestamp (signed by the TSA’s certificate, valid for 10-20 years)
The archival timestamp (signed by the TSA’s certificate, covers everything)
Years later: add another archival timestamp (covers the previous archival timestamp)
Repeat indefinitely

section.simpara: Each archival timestamp extends the provable lifetime of the signature. Even after the original signing certificate has expired, been revoked, and the CA has gone out of business — the chain of archival timestamps proves that the signature was valid at the time it was created.
This is the mechanism that national archives use to preserve the legal validity of digitally signed documents for decades.

section.title: Re-Timestamping
section.simpara: To extend a B-LTA signature’s validity:

listitem.simpara: Fetch fresh OCSP responses / CRLs for the archival timestamp’s certificate chain
Add them to the DSS dictionary
Apply a new archival timestamp (document timestamp) over the entire file

section.simpara: This can be done without the original signer’s involvement — it’s a maintenance operation performed by the document custodian.

tip.simpara: Automated re-timestamping should be a scheduled process in any document management system that stores B-LTA signed documents. Before the current archival timestamp’s TSA certificate expires (check the certificate’s notAfter date), apply a new archival timestamp. This keeps the chain of trust alive.

chapter.title: Migrating Between Profiles
chapter.simpara: One of the elegant properties of PAdES profiles is that you can upgrade a signature’s profile without re-signing:

listitem.simpara: B-B → B-T: Add a timestamp (unsigned attribute to the CMS). Requires the original file and the CMS.
B-T → B-LT: Fetch revocation data and certificates, create the DSS dictionary, add as incremental update.
B-LT → B-LTA: Add a document timestamp covering the entire file.

chapter.simpara: This progression means you can sign with B-B today (fast, no TSA dependency) and upgrade to B-LTA later as a batch operation. EU DSS provides methods for exactly this:

listitem.simpara: PAdES constrains ISO 32000-2 signatures for legal and regulatory compliance — a PAdES signature is always a valid PDF signature, but not vice versa
Four profiles: B-B (basic), B-T (timestamped), B-LT (long-term data), B-LTA (archival)
ESS-signing-certificate-v2 is the mandatory attribute that makes a signature PAdES-compliant — without it, you have a valid ISO 32000-2 signature but not PAdES
SubFilter ETSI.CAdES.detached is required for strict PAdES compliance
The DSS dictionary (B-LT and above) embeds certificates, OCSP responses, and CRLs directly in the PDF for offline validation
B-LTA provides indefinite validity through renewable archival timestamps
Profiles can be upgraded without re-signing — B-B → B-T → B-LT → B-LTA as incremental updates
For EU/eIDAS compliance, B-LTA is the target — anything less may not meet long-term archival requirements

partintro.simpara: A digital signature without a timestamp is like a contract without a date. You can prove who signed and what they signed, but not when. And in the world of digital signatures, "when" is often the most important question — because certificates expire, keys get compromised, algorithms get broken, and the difference between "signed before the certificate expired" and "signed after the certificate expired" can be the difference between a legally binding document and an invalid one.

This chapter covers everything about timestamps and long-term validation (LTV): how RFC 3161 timestamps work, how they’re embedded in PDF signatures, how the Document Security Store (DSS) enables offline validation, how archival timestamps create indefinite validity, and how to build a complete LTV infrastructure.

section.title: What a Timestamp Proves
section.simpara: An RFC 3161 timestamp is a cryptographic proof that a specific piece of data existed at a specific point in time. The proof comes from a Trusted Timestamp Authority (TSA) — a server operated by a trusted third party that:

listitem.simpara: Receives a hash of the data to be timestamped
Combines the hash with the current time
Signs the combination with the TSA’s private key
Returns the signed timestamp token

section.simpara: The timestamp token is a CMS SignedData structure (yes, a timestamp is itself a CMS signature) containing a TSTInfo (Timestamp Token Info) structure with:

listitem.simpara: The hash algorithm and hash value of the timestamped data
The timestamp time (UTC)
The TSA’s name
A serial number (unique per timestamp)
An accuracy indication (how precise the time is)

section.title: The Timestamp Request/Response Flow
section.simpara: The protocol uses HTTP POST with content type application/timestamp-query for the request and application/timestamp-reply for the response. The request and response are DER-encoded ASN.1 structures defined in RFC 3161.

section.title: Implementation with BouncyCastle
TSA Selection Criteria
section.simpara: Choosing the right TSA is critical. A timestamp is only as trustworthy as the TSA that produced it.

table.title: TSA selection criteria for different use cases

entry.simpara: For Adobe Acrobat: TSA’s root must be in AATL. For EU compliance: TSA must be a QTSP in the EUTL. For general use: TSA’s root should be widely trusted.
TSAs can go down. For production signing, use a TSA with a published SLA. Consider a fallback TSA if the primary is unreachable.

Free TSAs exist (FreeTSA.org, some DigiCert endpoints) but may not be suitable for production. Commercial TSAs charge per timestamp or per annual subscription. Costs range from free to several thousand euros per year.
TSAs report their time accuracy (e.g., ±1 second). For most use cases, sub-second accuracy is not critical.
Some regulations require the TSA to be operated within specific jurisdictions. EU regulations may require a TSA operated by an EU-based QTSP.
Network latency + TSA processing time. Free TSAs may be slower. For high-volume signing (thousands per minute), TSA response time matters.
The TSA’s signing certificate has an expiry date. Longer-lived TSA certificates mean your timestamps are verifiable for longer before re-timestamping is needed.

warning.simpara: Never hardcode a single TSA URL without a fallback strategy. If the TSA is unreachable when your application needs to sign, the signing fails. At minimum, implement:

listitem.simpara: A configurable TSA URL (not hardcoded)
A reasonable timeout (10-30 seconds)
A retry with backoff (the TSA may be temporarily overloaded)
Optionally, a fallback TSA URL
A graceful degradation path: if no TSA is available, produce a B-B signature and log a warning for later B-T upgrade

section.title: Why You Need Probing
section.simpara: Before your application signs a document, you need to know which TSAs are actually reachable. A signing operation that fails at the timestamping step — after the user has already authenticated, after the CSC API has consumed a SAD token, after the CMS signature has been computed — is one of the most frustrating failure modes in digital signing. You can avoid it by probing TSAs in the background.

section.title: Building a TSA Probe
section.simpara: A TSA probe sends a minimal RFC 3161 timestamp request — using a dummy hash, with certReq=false to minimize response size — and measures whether the server responds correctly:

important.simpara: Content-Type validation is essential. Some TSA endpoints sit behind reverse proxies or WAFs that return an HTTP 200 response with an HTML error page when the backend is down. Without checking Content-Type: application/timestamp-reply, your application might accept a garbage response and only fail later when trying to parse it as ASN.1. Always validate the response Content-Type before parsing.

section.title: Probe Strategy
section.simpara: For production applications, run probes on a schedule:

listitem.simpara: At startup: Probe all configured TSAs concurrently using a thread pool. Mark each as "online" or "offline" with its measured response time.
Periodically: Re-probe every 5-15 minutes. TSA availability can change — maintenance windows, network issues, certificate rotations.

On failure: If a signing operation fails at the timestamping step, immediately probe all TSAs and update their status. The failure may be transient (timeout) or permanent (certificate expired, endpoint removed).

section.simpara: Present the probe results to the user: a green/red status indicator with response time helps them choose the fastest available TSA for their signing operation.

section.title: Why Caching Matters
section.simpara: Timestamp and OCSP requests are network calls to external servers. In a high-volume signing scenario — batch signing hundreds of documents — making a fresh TSA request and OCSP lookup for every single signature is wasteful and slow. More importantly, it creates an availability dependency: if the TSA or OCSP responder goes down mid-batch, the entire batch fails.

section.title: TSA Response Caching
section.simpara: TSA responses are not cacheable in the traditional sense — each timestamp token contains a unique serial number and a specific timestamp, making every response unique by design. However, you can optimize for batch scenarios:

listitem.simpara: Connection pooling: Reuse HTTP connections to the TSA server. TLS handshakes are expensive; a keep-alive connection eliminates that overhead for subsequent requests.

Pre-warming: Establish the connection before the signing flow begins. The first request to a TSA often takes 2-5x longer than subsequent requests due to DNS resolution, TLS negotiation, and server-side session setup.

Batch authorization: Some CSC providers and TSA operators support batch requests — signing multiple hashes or timestamping multiple values in a single API call. If your TSA supports it, prefer batch requests over individual calls.

warning.simpara: Never reuse a timestamp token across multiple signatures. Each timestamp proves existence at a specific instant for a specific hash. Sharing a timestamp token between two signatures is technically invalid (the hash won’t match) and a compliance violation.

section.title: OCSP Response Caching
section.simpara: Unlike timestamps, OCSP responses are cacheable — and you should cache them aggressively. An OCSP response contains a thisUpdate and nextUpdate field that define a validity window. Within that window, the response is authoritative: the CA is asserting that the certificate’s revocation status has not changed since thisUpdate and will not change before nextUpdate.
Typical OCSP response validity windows:

listitem.simpara: Commercial CAs: 1-4 hours (short, to limit exposure from compromised responder)
Government CAs: 4-24 hours (longer, to reduce load on centralized infrastructure)
Qualified CAs (eIDAS): 24 hours maximum (ETSI EN 319 411-2 requirement)

section.simpara: For batch signing of 100 documents with the same certificate, a cached OCSP response eliminates 99 round trips to the OCSP responder.

section.title: CRL Caching
section.simpara: CRLs are even more cacheable than OCSP responses. Every CRL contains a nextUpdate date, and some CAs publish CRLs on fixed schedules (every 6, 12, or 24 hours). Cache the CRL bytes keyed by the CRL Distribution Point URL and check nextUpdate before re-fetching:

tip.simpara: Cache warming for DSS construction. When building a DSS dictionary (for PAdES-B-LT or B-LTA), you need OCSP responses and CRLs for the signer’s entire certificate chain plus the TSA’s certificate chain. A single signature can require 4-8 revocation data lookups. Pre-fetch and cache this data before the signing flow begins — the DSS construction should be an offline operation that reads from cache, not a live network operation.

chapter.title: Signature Timestamps vs. Document Timestamps
chapter.simpara: PDF supports two kinds of timestamps, and they serve different purposes:

section.title: Signature Timestamps
section.simpara: A signature timestamp is embedded inside the CMS SignedData as an unsigned attribute of the SignerInfo. It timestamps the signature value — proving when the signature was created.

listitem.simpara: Where it lives: CMS SignerInfo.unsignedAttrs.id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken
What it timestamps: The bytes of the SignerInfo.signature field
PAdES level: Required for PAdES-B-T and above
Who creates it: The signing application, immediately after computing the CMS signature

section.title: Document Timestamps
section.simpara: A document timestamp is a separate signature in the PDF — a /Sig dictionary with /SubFilter /ETSI.RFC3161 — that timestamps the entire document.

listitem.simpara: Where it lives: A separate signature dictionary in the PDF, applied as an incremental update
What it timestamps: The entire PDF file (via byte range), including all previous signatures and the DSS
PAdES level: Required for PAdES-B-LTA (the archival timestamp)
Who creates it: The document management system, as a maintenance operation

section.simpara: The key difference: a signature timestamp proves when a specific signature was created. A document timestamp proves that the entire document (including all signatures and validation data) existed at a specific time.
section.screen:
// Signature timestamp (inside CMS) /Sig << /SubFilter /ETSI.CAdES.detached /Contents <CMS SignedData: SignerInfo: signedAttrs: [contentType, messageDigest, ESS-signing-certificate-v2] signature: <RSA/ECDSA signature bytes> unsignedAttrs: [ signatureTimeStampToken: <TimeStampToken> ← THIS ] > >>
// Document timestamp (separate signature) /Sig << /SubFilter /ETSI.RFC3161 ← THIS /ByteRange [0 ... ... ...] ← Covers entire file /Contents <TimeStampToken as CMS> >>

section.title: Purpose
section.simpara: The DSS is a dictionary in the document catalog that stores all the data needed to validate every signature in the document offline — without contacting any external servers.
Without a DSS, validating a signature requires:

listitem.simpara: Fetching the signer’s certificate chain from the CMS and/or AIA URLs
Contacting OCSP responders to check revocation status
Downloading CRLs from distribution points

section.simpara: All of these require network access and functioning servers. Years from now, these servers may not exist. The DSS solves this by embedding everything locally.

section.title: Building the DSS
section.simpara: Building a DSS requires collecting all the validation data and embedding it as an incremental update:

section.title: VRI: Validation Related Information
section.simpara: The DSS can optionally include a /VRI sub-dictionary that maps each signature to its specific validation data. The VRI keys are the uppercase hex SHA-1 hash of the signature value (the bytes in /Contents):
section.screen: /DSS << /Certs [...] /OCSPs [...] /VRI << /A1B2C3D4E5F6... << % SHA-1 hash of signature 1's /Contents /Cert [30 0 R 31 0 R] % Certs for this specific signature /OCSP [33 0 R] % OCSP for this specific signature /TU (D:20260308120000Z) % Time of validation >> /F6E5D4C3B2A1... << % SHA-1 hash of signature 2's /Contents /Cert [30 0 R 32 0 R] /OCSP [34 0 R] /TU (D:20260309140000Z) >> >> >>
section.simpara: VRI is useful for multi-signature documents where each signature has different validation data. Without VRI, a validator must try all certificates and OCSP responses in the DSS against each signature to find the right match. With VRI, the mapping is explicit.

note.simpara: The VRI key is a SHA-1 hash — yes, SHA-1. This is a historical artifact from when the DSS was first specified. SHA-1 is used here only as a key derivation function (to generate a unique dictionary key from the signature value), not for cryptographic security. The security of the DSS does not depend on SHA-1 collision resistance.

section.title: OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol)
section.simpara: OCSP is the preferred mechanism for checking certificate revocation. You send a request to the CA’s OCSP responder with the certificate’s serial number, and it responds with "good," "revoked," or "unknown."

section.title: CRL (Certificate Revocation List)
section.simpara: CRLs are the fallback when OCSP is not available. A CRL is a signed list of all revoked certificates issued by a CA.

warning.simpara: CRLs can be very large — some CAs have CRLs of 500 KB or more. Embedding a large CRL in the DSS significantly increases the PDF file size. Prefer OCSP when available — OCSP responses are typically 1-3 KB.

Also be aware of delta CRLs — some CAs publish only the changes since the last full CRL. If the certificate’s CRL Distribution Point specifies a delta CRL, you may need both the full CRL and the delta CRL for complete validation.

chapter.title: The Complete B-LTA Workflow
chapter.simpara: Putting it all together, here’s the end-to-end workflow for producing a PAdES-B-LTA signature:
chapter.screen:
1. Create PAdES-B-B signature └── CMS with ESS-signing-certificate-v2, SHA-256, SubFilter ETSI.CAdES.detached
2. Add signature timestamp → PAdES-B-T └── Request RFC 3161 timestamp over the signature value └── Embed as unsigned CMS attribute
3. Fetch validation data └── Build certificate chain (signer → intermediates → root) └── Fetch OCSP response for each certificate └── Fetch CRL as fallback where OCSP unavailable └── Fetch TSA's certificate chain and revocation data too
4. Create DSS dictionary → PAdES-B-LT └── Embed all certificates as streams └── Embed all OCSP responses as streams └── Embed all CRLs as streams └── Optionally create VRI entries └── Add DSS to document catalog as incremental update
5. Add document timestamp → PAdES-B-LTA └── Request RFC 3161 timestamp over the entire file (including DSS) └── Apply as separate /Sig dictionary with SubFilter ETSI.RFC3161 └── Save as incremental update
chapter.simpara: Each step is an incremental update to the PDF. The final file contains the original document, the signature, the DSS, and the document timestamp — everything needed for indefinite validation.

listitem.simpara: RFC 3161 timestamps are CMS-signed proofs that data existed at a specific time — issued by Trusted Timestamp Authorities
Signature timestamps (in CMS unsigned attributes) prove when a specific signature was created
Document timestamps (separate /Sig with SubFilter ETSI.RFC3161) prove when the entire document existed — used for archival
The DSS dictionary embeds certificates, OCSP responses, and CRLs directly in the PDF for offline validation
VRI entries map specific signatures to their validation data using SHA-1 hash keys
OCSP is preferred over CRLs for revocation checking (smaller, more specific)
TSA selection matters: trust store compatibility (AATL/EUTL), availability, cost, and response time
PAdES-B-LTA is the complete package: signature + timestamp + validation data + archival timestamp
Re-timestamping extends validity indefinitely — a scheduled maintenance operation for archival documents
Always implement TSA failover — a TSA outage should not prevent signing
Probe TSAs in the background at startup and periodically — validate Content-Type headers, not just HTTP 200
Cache OCSP responses aggressively — they have explicit validity windows (thisUpdate/nextUpdate) and eliminate redundant network calls in batch scenarios
Cache CRLs by distribution point URL — CRLs change infrequently and can be very large; avoid re-downloading within the validity window
TSA responses cannot be cached — each is unique by design — but connection pooling and pre-warming significantly reduce latency

partintro.simpara: In 2014, the European Union did something that no other jurisdiction had managed: it created a legal framework where a digital signature — a sequence of bytes produced by a cryptographic algorithm — carries the same legal weight as ink on paper, in all 27 member states simultaneously. That framework is called eIDAS, and whether you love it or hate it, if your software produces or validates digital signatures that touch Europe, you need to understand it.

The Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) — its full title is "Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 23 July 2014 on electronic identification and trust services for electronic transactions in the internal market" — is a 38-page regulation that created the legal and institutional infrastructure for electronic signatures, seals, timestamps, and related trust services across the entire European Union. It replaced a failed directive that had been limping along for 15 years, and it did so by making one critical architectural decision: it chose to be a regulation, not a directive.
That distinction matters enormously, and understanding why requires a brief detour through European law.

chapter.title: A Brief History: The Electronic Signatures Directive

section.title: Directive 1999/93/EC
section.simpara: Before eIDAS, there was the Electronic Signatures Directive (Directive 1999/93/EC), adopted on December 13, 1999. The directive was born in the heady optimism of the dot-com era, when legislators across the world were scrambling to give electronic commerce a legal foundation. The United States had passed the E-Sign Act. UNCITRAL had published a Model Law on Electronic Signatures. Europe wanted its own framework, and it wanted one that would work across all (then 15) member states.

The directive’s core idea was sound: define what an "advanced electronic signature" is, define what a "qualified certificate" is, establish that qualified electronic signatures should not be denied legal effect, and require member states to set up supervisory bodies for certification service providers.

section.title: Why Directives Fail
section.simpara: Here is where European institutional mechanics become relevant to your code.

In EU law, a directive tells member states what result to achieve but lets each country decide how to achieve it through national legislation. A regulation, by contrast, is directly applicable in all member states without any national transposition — it becomes law everywhere on the same day, with the same text.

The Electronic Signatures Directive was a directive. This meant that each member state had to transpose it into national law, and each one did so differently. Germany created the Signaturgesetz (Signature Law). France created le decret n. 2001-272. Italy created the Codice dell’Amministrazione Digitale. Each country interpreted the directive’s requirements through its own legal tradition, its own bureaucratic structures, and its own technical preferences.

The result was exactly what you would predict: 15 (later 28) different national implementations of the "same" legal framework, with subtly different definitions of what counted as an "advanced" signature, different requirements for certification service providers, different supervisory structures, and — critically for developers — different technical standards that qualified certificates had to meet.

note.simpara: The Electronic Signatures Directive used the term "Certification Service Provider" (CSP). eIDAS renamed these to "Trust Service Provider" (TSP) and, for the supervised ones, "Qualified Trust Service Provider" (QTSP). The terminology change was deliberate: eIDAS covers more than just certificates — it covers timestamps, seals, website authentication, and electronic delivery services. If you encounter "CSP" in older documentation, read it as "TSP."

section.title: Cross-Border Failure
section.simpara: The most damaging consequence of the directive’s fragmentation was the failure of cross-border recognition. In theory, a qualified electronic signature from Germany should have been accepted in France, and vice versa. In practice, it often was not. Each country’s government procurement portals, tax systems, and regulatory filing platforms had been built to recognize only their own national qualified certificates. A German doctor trying to sign a cross-border prescription electronically for a French patient would find that the French system did not recognize the German qualified certificate.

A 2012 study by the European Commission found that cross-border recognition of electronic signatures was "marginal at best." Of 13 member states surveyed, only 5 had any mechanism at all for recognizing qualified certificates from other EU countries. The directive had been in force for 13 years, and the internal market for electronic signatures was essentially non-existent.
This was the political context in which eIDAS was born.

section.title: The Lessons Learned
section.simpara: The Commission identified several root causes for the directive’s failure:

listitem.simpara: Directive vs. Regulation: national transposition created divergent implementations
No mandatory trust infrastructure: there was no EU-wide list of qualified providers that all member states were required to accept
Vague technical requirements: the directive referenced "secure signature creation devices" without specifying what that meant technically
No supervision framework: how supervisory bodies should operate, what they should check, and what consequences non-compliance should carry were all left to national discretion
Certificates only: the directive covered only electronic signatures, ignoring related services like timestamps, seals, and electronic delivery

section.simpara: eIDAS addressed every single one of these failures.

section.title: Regulation, Not Directive
section.simpara: eIDAS is a regulation. Article 288 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union says: "A regulation shall have general application. It shall be binding in its entirety and directly applicable in all Member States."

This means: - The text is the same in all 27 member states (plus EEA countries through incorporation) - No national transposition is needed or permitted — the regulation is the law - National courts apply the regulation directly; citizens and businesses can invoke it directly - Divergent national interpretations can be challenged at the Court of Justice of the European Union

For developers, this has a profound practical consequence: the rules are the same everywhere. When eIDAS says that a qualified electronic signature has the legal effect equivalent to a handwritten signature, it means exactly that in Germany, France, Italy, Estonia, and all other member states. You do not need to check 27 different national laws to know what your signature is worth.

section.title: Scope and Structure
section.simpara: The eIDAS regulation has two main parts:

Chapter II: Electronic Identification (Articles 6-12) — establishes a framework for mutual recognition of national electronic identification schemes (eID). This is the part that deals with government-issued digital identities (like the German Personalausweis or the Estonian ID-kaart) and how they should be recognized across borders. We will not cover this in detail because it relates to identity management rather than document signing.
Chapter III: Trust Services (Articles 13-45) — this is where the electronic signature framework lives, and it is the focus of this chapter and the next four chapters.

The trust services chapter covers: - Electronic signatures (Articles 25-27) - Electronic seals (Articles 35-40) - Electronic timestamps (Articles 41-42) - Electronic delivery services (Articles 43-44) - Website authentication certificates (Article 45) - General provisions on trust service providers (Articles 13-24)

important.simpara: eIDAS does not mandate how electronic signatures must be implemented technically. It defines legal categories and legal effects, then defers to ETSI and CEN for the technical standards. The regulation deliberately separates legal requirements from technical implementation — a wise decision that has allowed the technical standards (PAdES, CAdES, XAdES) to evolve independently of the legal framework.

When eIDAS says "advanced electronic signature," it is defining a legal concept with four properties. When ETSI EN 319 142 says "PAdES-B-T," it is defining a technical implementation that satisfies those properties. The mapping between legal categories and technical standards is documented in Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2015/1506.

chapter.title: The Three Tiers of Electronic Signatures
chapter.simpara: The heart of eIDAS for developers is its three-tier classification of electronic signatures. Each tier has progressively stricter requirements and progressively stronger legal effects.

section.title: Tier 1: Electronic Signature (Article 3(10))
section.simpara: The broadest tier. Article 3(10) defines an electronic signature as:

blockquote.simpara: "electronic signature" means data in electronic form which is attached to or logically associated with other data in electronic form and which is used by the signatory to sign

section.simpara: That is it. An electronic signature under eIDAS can be: - A scanned image of a handwritten signature pasted into a PDF - A typed name at the bottom of an email - A checkbox on a web form saying "I agree" - A click of a "Sign" button that records user intent
There is no cryptographic requirement. There is no certificate requirement. There is no requirement for any specific technology at all.

The legal effect of a basic electronic signature is defined in Article 25(1): it "shall not be denied legal effect and admissibility as evidence in legal proceedings solely on the grounds that it is in an electronic form." This is a non-discrimination principle — courts cannot reject evidence just because it is electronic. But it says nothing about the weight of that evidence. A typed name has much less evidentiary value than a cryptographic signature with a qualified certificate.

tip.simpara: For developers building signing workflows: many business processes do not require more than a basic electronic signature. Click-to-sign, draw-to-sign, and type-to-sign all count as electronic signatures under eIDAS. If your use case is internal corporate approvals, non-binding agreements, or informal consent, a basic electronic signature may be legally sufficient. Check with your legal team, but do not assume you always need cryptography.

section.title: Tier 2: Advanced Electronic Signature (Article 3(11) and Article 26)
section.simpara: The middle tier. Article 3(11) defines an advanced electronic signature as an electronic signature that meets the requirements set out in Article 26.
Article 26 specifies four requirements:

blockquote.simpara: An advanced electronic signature shall meet the following requirements:
(a) it is uniquely linked to the signatory;
(b) it is capable of identifying the signatory;
(c) it is created using electronic signature creation data that the signatory can, with a high level of confidence, use under his sole control; and
(d) it is linked to the data signed therewith in such a way that any subsequent change in the data is detectable.

section.simpara: Let us examine each requirement from a technical perspective.

section.title: (a) Uniquely linked to the signatory
section.simpara: The signature must be produced using data (typically a private key) that is uniquely associated with one specific natural person. In practice, this means: a private key that belongs to exactly one person, used to produce a cryptographic signature that can only have been created with that key.
A shared private key used by multiple employees would fail this requirement. A department-level signing key would fail this requirement. The key must be tied to a specific individual.

section.title: (b) Capable of identifying the signatory
section.simpara: The signature (together with its associated data, such as a certificate) must make it possible to determine who signed. A raw RSA or ECDSA signature over a hash does not, by itself, identify anyone — it just proves that whoever held the private key produced the signature. Combined with an X.509 certificate that binds the public key to an identity (name, organization, email), the requirement is satisfied.

section.title: (c) Sole control
section.simpara: The signatory must be the only person who can use the signing key. "With a high level of confidence" is the qualifier — absolute sole control is impossible to guarantee (keys can be stolen), but the system must make unauthorized use difficult.

Sole control mechanisms include: - Password-protected PKCS#12 keystore: the signer must know the password - Smart card with PIN: the signer must possess the card and know the PIN - HSM with authentication: the signer must authenticate (username + password + OTP) before the HSM will sign - Biometric activation: fingerprint or face recognition to authorize signing

warning.simpara: "Sole control" is where many implementations fail the advanced signature test. If a signing key is stored in a server-side keystore and an application signs on behalf of users without meaningful per-signature authentication, the resulting signatures are arguably not advanced electronic signatures — because the user does not have sole control over the key.

The fact that the user logged into the application is not sufficient. The authentication must be linked to the signing action, not just the session. This is the distinction that the SCAL (Sole Control Assurance Level) framework addresses — more on this in Chapter 22.

section.title: (d) Linked to the data
section.simpara: Any change to the signed data must be detectable. This is the integrity property that every digital signature scheme provides: if you change even one bit of the signed data, the signature will no longer verify. For PDF signatures, the byte range mechanism (Chapter 13) ensures this linkage.

section.title: Tier 3: Qualified Electronic Signature (Article 3(12))
section.simpara: The highest tier, and the most consequential. Article 3(12) defines a qualified electronic signature as:

blockquote.simpara: "qualified electronic signature" means an advanced electronic signature that is created by a qualified electronic signature creation device, and which is based on a qualified certificate for electronic signatures

section.simpara: A QES is an advanced electronic signature (all four Article 26 properties) that additionally: 1. Uses a Qualified Signature Creation Device (QSCD) — certified hardware that protects the signing key 2. Is based on a Qualified Certificate — issued by a Qualified Trust Service Provider (QTSP) listed in the EU Trusted List

The legal effect of a QES is defined in Article 25(2): "A qualified electronic signature shall have the equivalent legal effect of a handwritten signature." This is not a non-discrimination principle — it is an equivalence rule. A QES is legally equal to pen on paper. No court in any EU member state can treat it differently.

Furthermore, Article 25(3) establishes mutual recognition: "A qualified electronic signature based on a qualified certificate issued in one Member State shall be recognised as a qualified electronic signature in all other Member States." This is the cross-border recognition that the 1999 directive failed to achieve.

section.title: Article 25: Legal Effects of Electronic Signatures
section.simpara: Article 25 is the most cited provision in eIDAS, and it deserves careful reading.

entry.simpara: Electronic signatures shall not be denied legal effect solely because they are electronic
Non-discrimination: courts cannot reject electronic evidence just because it is not paper
QES shall have the equivalent legal effect of a handwritten signature
Legal equivalence: a QES is a handwritten signature for all legal purposes
QES based on a qualified certificate from one member state shall be recognized in all member states
Cross-border recognition: no country can reject another country’s QES

section.simpara: Note what Article 25 does not say: it does not say that advanced electronic signatures (AdES) have the legal effect of handwritten signatures. An AdES benefits only from the non-discrimination rule of 25(1) — it cannot be rejected solely because it is electronic, but a court is free to assign it less evidentiary weight than a handwritten signature based on the circumstances.

This creates a clear hierarchy: - QES: automatic legal equivalence to handwritten (Article 25(2)) - AdES: cannot be dismissed as evidence just for being electronic (Article 25(1)), but the court decides its weight - Basic electronic signature: same as AdES — cannot be dismissed for being electronic, but its evidentiary weight is at the court’s discretion

important.simpara: Many developers (and their managers) misunderstand Article 25(1) as meaning "all electronic signatures are legally valid." That is not what it says. It says they cannot be denied legal effect solely because they are electronic. A typed name at the bottom of an email is an electronic signature under eIDAS, but it provides very little evidence about who actually sent the email, whether they intended to be bound by its contents, or whether the email was tampered with. A court will weigh this evidence accordingly.
Only QES gets automatic equivalence. Everything else is a matter of evidence and judicial discretion.

section.title: Article 28: Qualified Certificates for Electronic Signatures
section.simpara: Article 28 requires that qualified certificates contain specific information, listed in Annex I of the regulation. A qualified certificate must contain:

listitem.simpara: An indication (at least in machine-processable form) that the certificate is a qualified certificate
A set of data representing the QTSP, including the member state where it is established and its name
The signatory’s name (or a pseudonym, clearly identified as such)
Electronic signature validation data (the public key) corresponding to the electronic signature creation data (the private key)
The period of validity
The certificate serial number
The advanced electronic signature or seal of the issuing QTSP
The location where the certificate supporting the QTSP’s signature/seal is available free of charge
The location of the QTSP’s certificate revocation status services

section.simpara: In X.509 terms, this translates to specific extensions and naming conventions: - The certificate policies extension must contain the OID for qualified certificates (0.4.0.194112.1.2 for QES) - The subject DN must contain the signatory’s name - The issuer DN identifies the QTSP - CRL Distribution Points and/or OCSP responder URLs must be present - The QCStatements extension (OID 0.4.0.1862.1) carries machine-readable qualification information

section.title: Article 29: Requirements for Qualified Signature Creation Devices
section.simpara: Article 29 states that qualified electronic signature creation data (the private key) must be managed by a QSCD that meets the requirements of Annex II.
Annex II requires that QSCDs must:

listitem.simpara: Ensure the confidentiality of the electronic signature creation data
Ensure that the electronic signature creation data used for signature creation can occur only once (practical uniqueness)
Ensure that the electronic signature creation data cannot be derived with reasonable assurance, and the signature is reliably protected against forgery using currently available technology
Ensure that the electronic signature creation data can be reliably protected by the legitimate signatory against use by others

section.simpara: In practice, this means: the private key must be generated on, and never leave, a certified hardware device. The certification is typically Common Criteria EAL4+ (or equivalent) for the device, plus specific Protection Profiles defined by CEN and ETSI.

Examples of certified QSCDs: - Smart cards (e.g., IDPrime MD from Thales, CardOS from Atos) - USB tokens (e.g., SafeNet eToken 5110) - Hardware Security Modules (e.g., Thales Luna Network HSM, Utimaco SecurityServer) - Remote QSCDs operated by a QTSP (the key lives in an HSM in a data center, accessed via API)

note.simpara: The rise of remote QSCDs has been the biggest practical change since eIDAS entered into force. The original assumption was that QES required a physical device in the signer’s possession (a smart card in their wallet). But eIDAS Annex II does not require physical possession — it requires sole control. A private key in an HSM operated by a QTSP, accessible only through strong multi-factor authentication specific to the signer and the signing operation, satisfies the requirement. This opened the door to remote signing (Chapter 22), which is now the dominant model for QES in practice.

section.title: Article 35-40: Electronic Seals
section.simpara: Electronic seals are the organizational counterpart to electronic signatures. While a signature is created by a natural person (a human), a seal is created by a legal person (a company, government agency, association).

The seal articles mirror the signature articles almost exactly: - Article 35(1): non-discrimination for electronic seals - Article 35(2): qualified electronic seal enjoys a presumption of integrity and origin - Article 36: AdESeal requirements (same four properties as AdES, adapted for legal persons) - Article 38: qualified certificates for electronic seals (same as Art. 28, but for organizations)

The key difference: Article 35(2) says a qualified seal enjoys a "presumption of integrity of the data and of correctness of the origin of that data." Note the careful wording — it says nothing about the legal intent behind the seal. A signature expresses consent or approval. A seal proves origin and integrity. Chapter 21 explores this distinction in depth.

section.title: Within the EU
section.simpara: Article 25(3) establishes that a QES based on a qualified certificate from one member state is recognized as a QES in all member states. Article 35(3) does the same for qualified electronic seals.
This means: - An Austrian QES is valid in Portugal - A Lithuanian qualified seal is valid in Spain - An Estonian qualified timestamp is valid in Germany

Member states cannot impose additional requirements for cross-border recognition. They cannot require that QES certificates be issued by domestic QTSPs. They cannot require additional technical features beyond what eIDAS specifies.

The mechanism that makes this work is the EU Trusted List (EUTL) — a machine-readable, cryptographically signed registry of all QTSPs in the EU. If a certificate chains to a QTSP on the EUTL, it qualifies for cross-border recognition. Chapter 20 covers the EUTL in detail.

warning.simpara: In practice, cross-border recognition is still imperfect. While the legal framework is clear, many national IT systems have not been updated to consume the EUTL and accept certificates from other member states. A 2021 European Commission survey found that while most member states had updated their legal frameworks, actual system-level interoperability lagged significantly.

If your software validates signatures against a fixed list of trusted certificates (rather than dynamically checking the EUTL), you are likely rejecting valid QES from other member states. This is a compliance failure under eIDAS — Article 25(3) does not have exceptions for "we haven’t updated our trust store yet."

section.title: Non-EU Recognition
section.simpara: eIDAS Article 14 allows the European Commission to adopt implementing acts recognizing electronic identification schemes from third countries, provided they meet equivalent requirements.

In practice, non-EU recognition is sparse: - Switzerland has a bilateral agreement with the EU that provides for mutual recognition of qualified trust services under the ZertES (Swiss Electronic Signatures Act) - United Kingdom post-Brexit: the UK retained eIDAS as domestic law (the UK eIDAS regulation) but mutual recognition lapsed; UK QES are not automatically recognized in the EU and vice versa - United States: no formal equivalence arrangement; US digital signatures have no special status under eIDAS (and eIDAS signatures have no special status under US law)

For developers serving global markets, this means: do not assume that a qualified certificate from a non-EU QTSP will be recognized as "qualified" in the EU, even if the QTSP provides technically identical services. Qualification is a legal status granted by an EU member state’s supervisory body, not a technical property of the certificate.

chapter.title: eIDAS 2.0: Regulation (EU) 2024/1183
chapter.simpara: On April 11, 2024, the revised eIDAS regulation — commonly called eIDAS 2.0 or Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0) — entered into force. The revision keeps the trust services framework largely intact but adds a transformative new component: the European Digital Identity Wallet (EUDI Wallet).

section.title: What Changed
section.simpara: The key changes in eIDAS 2.0:

listitem.simpara: EUDI Wallet: Every member state must provide a digital identity wallet to its citizens and residents by 2026. The wallet will carry government-issued Person Identification Data (PID) and Qualified Electronic Attestations of Attributes (QEAAs). Crucially, the wallet will be able to create QES — making qualified signatures accessible to every EU citizen from their smartphone.

Qualified Electronic Attestations of Attributes (QEAAs): A new trust service type. QEAAs are digitally signed attestations of specific attributes (age, professional qualifications, university degrees) that can be presented through the EUDI wallet. Think of them as digital equivalents of physical certificates and credentials.

Remote QSCD in the wallet: The EUDI wallet can use a remote QSCD operated by a QTSP for creating qualified signatures. The private key lives in an HSM in the QTSP’s data center, but the wallet provides the authentication and sole control mechanisms.

Free QES for citizens: Article 45e requires that natural persons be offered QES creation through the wallet free of charge for non-professional use. This is a dramatic change — until now, QES required purchasing a certificate from a commercial QTSP, which typically costs 50-300 EUR per year.
Qualified Electronic Ledgers: A new trust service type for distributed ledger technology (blockchain). ETSI is currently developing the technical standards.

section.title: Impact on Developers
section.simpara: For PDF signature developers, eIDAS 2.0 means:

listitem.simpara: Expect a surge in QES volume: Free QES through the wallet will dramatically increase the number of qualified signatures in circulation. Your validation code needs to handle EUTL lookups efficiently.

Wallet-initiated signing flows: The EUDI wallet defines a new interaction pattern where the wallet app initiates a signing flow with a remote QSCD. If your application accepts signed documents, you need to support the signature formats the wallet produces.

QEAA validation: You may need to validate not just the signature on a document, but also QEAAs presented alongside it (e.g., a doctor signing a prescription presents their medical qualification attestation).

tip.simpara: The EUDI wallet’s Architecture Reference Framework (ARF) is publicly available and under active development. As of early 2026, large-scale pilots are running in multiple sectors across the EU. The technical specifications are not yet final, but the direction is clear: QES will move from a niche enterprise technology to a consumer feature on smartphones. Plan your architecture accordingly.

chapter.title: The Gap Between Regulation and Reality

section.title: What the Regulation Says
section.simpara: eIDAS paints an elegant picture: three clearly defined tiers, qualified trust services supervised by competent national bodies, mutual recognition across borders, machine-readable trusted lists that software can consume programmatically.

section.title: What Actually Happens
section.simpara: The reality is more complicated.

National implementation varies despite the regulation’s direct applicability. While member states cannot transpose the regulation differently (it is directly applicable), they can adopt supplementary national measures. Germany’s Vertrauensdienstegesetz (Trust Services Act), France’s decret relatif a la signature electronique, and Italy’s Codice dell’Amministrazione Digitale all contain national provisions that fill gaps the regulation left open. These national provisions sometimes create practical differences in how QES is implemented and accepted.

EUTL consumption is not universal. Many government and enterprise systems still validate signatures against hardcoded trust stores rather than dynamically checking the EUTL. This means they accept domestic QES but reject equally valid QES from other member states.

QSCD certification is slow and expensive. Getting a signature creation device certified as a QSCD takes 12-24 months and costs hundreds of thousands of euros. This limits the number of available QSCDs and concentrates the market among a few hardware vendors.

The "qualified" premium. Qualified certificates cost significantly more than standard certificates — typically 50-300 EUR per year for individuals, and more for organizations. This price barrier has limited adoption, particularly among small businesses and individuals. eIDAS 2.0’s free QES through the wallet aims to address this, but the wallet is not yet widely deployed.

Interoperability testing is ongoing. The European Commission funds regular interoperability testing events (called "plugtests") through ETSI, where QTSPs and software vendors test their implementations against each other. These events consistently reveal interoperability issues — signatures that validate in one tool but fail in another, timestamps that are accepted by some validators but rejected by others, EUTL entries that are ambiguous about the services a QTSP provides.

section.title: "eIDAS makes all digital signatures legal"
section.simpara: No. eIDAS creates a framework with three tiers. Only QES gets automatic legal equivalence to handwritten signatures. Basic electronic signatures and advanced electronic signatures benefit only from the non-discrimination principle — they cannot be rejected solely for being electronic, but their legal weight is a matter for the court to decide.

section.title: "If my signature uses a certificate, it’s advanced"
section.simpara: Not necessarily. An advanced electronic signature must satisfy all four requirements of Article 26: uniquely linked to the signatory, capable of identifying them, under sole control, and linked to the data. A certificate-based signature where the private key is stored in a shared server-side keystore, accessible to multiple system administrators, arguably fails the "sole control" requirement.

section.title: "PAdES = eIDAS compliance"
section.simpara: Not automatically. PAdES defines technical profiles for PDF signatures. Using PAdES-B-LTA with a qualified certificate on a QSCD produces a QES. Using PAdES-B-LTA with a self-signed certificate produces a technically well-structured signature with zero eIDAS qualification. The eIDAS tier depends on who issued the certificate and where the key is stored, not on the PDF signature format alone.

section.title: "My country’s signatures are recognized everywhere in the EU"
section.simpara: Only if they are qualified. A non-qualified signature from Germany has no special status in France — it is just evidence, to be evaluated by the French court on its merits. Only QES enjoys automatic cross-border recognition under Article 25(3).

section.title: "eIDAS applies outside the EU"
section.simpara: eIDAS is EU law. It applies within the EU, the EEA (through the EEA Agreement), and countries with specific bilateral arrangements (Switzerland). It does not apply in the United States, China, Japan, or anywhere else, unless those jurisdictions choose to recognize eIDAS-qualified signatures under their own legal frameworks.

However, eIDAS has become a de facto global reference model. Countries designing their own electronic signature frameworks often look to eIDAS for inspiration. The three-tier model (basic, advanced, qualified) has been adopted in various forms by multiple non-EU jurisdictions.

chapter.title: What Your Code Needs to Do
chapter.simpara: For developers building PDF signature creation or validation software, eIDAS imposes specific technical requirements depending on the tier you are targeting:

section.title: For Basic Electronic Signatures
section.simpara: Nothing beyond what PDF already requires. Any valid PDF signature — even a simple /SubFilter /adbe.pkcs7.detached with a self-signed certificate — is an electronic signature under Article 3(10).

section.title: For Advanced Electronic Signatures
section.simpara: Your signing workflow must ensure: 1. Unique linkage: one private key per signer (never share keys) 2. Identification: the certificate must contain the signer’s identity (name, at minimum) 3. Sole control: meaningful per-signature authentication (not just session login) 4. Data linkage: the signature must cover the entire document (standard byte range coverage)
Technically, this means: PAdES-B-B or higher, with a certificate issued to a specific individual, and a signing flow that authenticates the signer for each signature.

section.title: For Qualified Electronic Signatures
section.simpara: All of the above, plus: 1. Qualified certificate: issued by a QTSP listed on the EUTL 2. QSCD: the private key must be on a certified qualified signature creation device 3. Recommendation: PAdES-B-LTA: while not strictly required by eIDAS, PAdES-B-LTA ensures long-term verifiability

section.title: For Validation
section.simpara: If your software validates signatures and needs to determine the eIDAS tier:

caution.simpara: The code above is a simplified illustration. Production eIDAS tier determination is significantly more complex because:

listitem.simpara: EUTL lookup requires parsing the trusted list XML, validating its signature, and traversing the LOT hierarchy
Certificate chain validation must check revocation status for every certificate in the chain
QSCD status may need to be inferred from the certificate’s QCStatements rather than verified directly
Some QTSPs issue certificates that are qualified for some services but not others

caution.simpara: For production use, the EU DSS framework (Chapter 23) provides a complete, tested implementation of eIDAS tier determination. Do not implement this from scratch unless you have very specific requirements that EU DSS cannot meet.

listitem.simpara: eIDAS (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS)) is an EU regulation — directly applicable in all member states, no national transposition required
It replaced the failed Electronic Signatures Directive (1999/93/EC), which suffered from fragmented national implementations

Three tiers: basic electronic signature (any electronic indication of signing intent), advanced electronic signature (four properties: unique linkage, identification, sole control, data linkage), qualified electronic signature (advanced + QSCD + qualified certificate)
Only QES has automatic legal equivalence to a handwritten signature (Article 25(2))
Cross-border recognition is mandatory for QES (Article 25(3)) — enforced through the EU Trusted List
eIDAS 2.0 (Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0)) adds the EUDI wallet, making QES accessible to all EU citizens via smartphone
The gap between regulation and implementation is real — code that hardcodes trust stores instead of checking the EUTL violates the mutual recognition principle
For production eIDAS validation, use the EU DSS framework (Chapter 23) rather than implementing from scratch

partintro.simpara: A certificate is just a data structure — some ASN.1 fields, a public key, and a signature from someone who claims to be a Certificate Authority. What makes a certificate qualified is not a technical property you can detect by parsing bytes alone. It is a legal status, conferred by a government supervisory body, published in a machine-readable trust infrastructure, and backed by an institutional apparatus that most developers have never heard of, let alone queried programmatically.

This chapter takes you through that entire apparatus: the Qualified Trust Service Providers that issue qualified certificates, the EU Trusted List that publishes who they are, the technical requirements that separate a qualified certificate from an ordinary one, the QSCD hardware that protects the keys, and the Java code you need to actually parse and verify all of this. If Chapter 19 was the legal framework, this chapter is the plumbing.

section.title: What a QTSP Is
section.simpara: A Qualified Trust Service Provider is an organization that has been audited, approved, and listed by a national supervisory body within the EU as meeting the requirements of Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Articles 20-24. The word "qualified" is doing serious work here — it is not a marketing term, not a self-declaration, and not something you obtain by installing software. It is a legal status, analogous to a bank receiving a banking license: you cannot call yourself a QTSP unless a government body says you are one.
A QTSP can provide one or more qualified trust services:

listitem.simpara: Qualified certificates for electronic signatures (natural persons)
Qualified certificates for electronic seals (legal persons)
Qualified timestamps (RFC 3161 tokens from a qualified TSA)
Qualified electronic delivery services (certified email equivalents)
Qualified website authentication certificates (the controversial Article 45, rewritten in eIDAS 2.0)
Qualified electronic attestations of attributes (new in Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0))

section.simpara: The qualification is per-service, not per-organization. A provider might be qualified for timestamps but not for certificates, or for seals but not for signatures. This matters when you are parsing the EU Trusted List — you need to check not just whether the provider is listed, but which services are qualified.

section.title: How to Become a QTSP
section.simpara: The path to becoming a QTSP is neither quick nor cheap. Here is the process, simplified but accurate:

listitem.simpara: Establish the infrastructure. You need HSMs (certified to Common Criteria EAL4+ or equivalent), a secure data center (typically ISO 27001 certified), redundant systems, trained personnel, and documented processes for every aspect of certificate lifecycle management — issuance, suspension, revocation, key generation, key destruction.

Write a Certificate Practice Statement (CPS). This is the document that describes, in exhaustive detail, how you operate: identity verification procedures, key management, certificate profiles, revocation handling, disaster recovery, incident response. A CPS for a QTSP is typically 80-150 pages long.

Get a conformity assessment. An accredited Conformity Assessment Body (CAB) evaluates your infrastructure, processes, and CPS against Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) and the applicable ETSI standards (primarily ETSI EN 319 401 for general TSP requirements and ETSI EN 319 411-1/411-2 for certificate issuance). The assessment produces a Conformity Assessment Report (CAR).

Submit to the national supervisory body. Each EU member state designates a supervisory body for QTSPs: in Germany, the Bundesnetzagentur (Federal Network Agency); in France, ANSSI; in Italy, AgID (Agenzia per l’Italia Digitale). You submit the CAR, your CPS, and supporting documentation.
Supervisory body review and approval. The supervisory body reviews everything, may request additional information or on-site inspections, and eventually grants or denies qualified status.

Listing on the national Trusted List. Once approved, the supervisory body adds your service(s) to the national Trusted Service Status List (TSL) — the country’s portion of the EU Trusted List. Your qualified status takes effect when the updated TSL is published.

Ongoing supervision. Qualification is not a one-time event. Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article 20(1) mandates audits at least every 24 months. Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article 19(2) requires QTSPs to report security incidents to the supervisory body within 24 hours.

warning.simpara: The entire process from initial infrastructure setup to EUTL listing typically takes 18-36 months and costs several hundred thousand to several million euros, depending on the scope of services and the jurisdiction. This is by design: the barrier to entry ensures that only well-resourced, professionally operated organizations can issue qualified certificates.

If someone offers you "qualified certificates" from a provider that is not on the EUTL, they are either confused about what "qualified" means or deliberately misleading you. Always verify against the EUTL.

section.title: The Supervision Model
section.simpara: The eIDAS supervision model is a two-layer structure:

National layer: Each member state’s supervisory body is responsible for QTSPs established in its territory. The supervisory body grants and revokes qualified status, maintains the national TSL, conducts or commissions audits, handles incident reports, and imposes sanctions for non-compliance.

EU layer: The European Commission maintains the List of Trusted Lists (LOTL) — a master document that points to every national TSL. The Commission does not supervise individual QTSPs; it supervises the supervisory bodies indirectly, through the mutual evaluation mechanism and through the eIDAS Expert Group.

entry.simpara: Maintains LOTL, sets technical standards via implementing acts, coordinates mutual evaluations
Grants/revokes qualified status, maintains national TSL, audits QTSPs, handles incidents
Operates trust services, issues certificates, complies with ETSI standards, reports incidents

section.title: Architecture: The LOT Hierarchy
section.simpara: The EUTL is not a single list — it is a hierarchy of cryptographically signed XML documents:

listitem.simpara: The LOTL (List of Trusted Lists) is the root. Maintained by the European Commission, published at a well-known URL. The LOTL contains pointers (URLs) to every national TSL, along with the certificates used to verify each national TSL’s signature.

Each national TSL (Trusted Service Status List) lists the trust service providers in that country, their services, the status of each service (granted, withdrawn, deprecated), and the CA certificates associated with each service.
Each TSL is digitally signed using an XML Advanced Electronic Signature (XAdES) — the same trust-no-one verification model applied to the trust infrastructure itself.

section.simpara: The LOTL URL is:
section.screen: https://ec.europa.eu/tools/lotl/eu-lotl.xml

note.simpara: This URL has been stable for years, but production systems should make it configurable. The official reference for the LOTL location is Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2015/1505. The LOTL itself contains a NextUpdate field indicating when the next version will be published. National TSLs are updated on varying schedules — some monthly, some quarterly, some whenever a change occurs.

section.title: XML Schema
section.simpara: The TSL XML schema is defined in ETSI TS 119 612 (replacing the older ETSI TS 102 231). The schema is verbose, deeply nested, and frankly exhausting to read — but it is well-structured and consistent.
section.programlisting:
<!-- Simplified LOTL structure --> <TrustServiceStatusList xmlns="http://uri.etsi.org/02231/v2#"> <SchemeInformation> <TSLVersionIdentifier>5</TSLVersionIdentifier> <TSLSequenceNumber>321</TSLSequenceNumber> <TSLType> http://uri.etsi.org/TrstSvc/TrustedList/TSLType/EUlistofthelists </TSLType> <SchemeOperatorName> <Name xml:lang="en">European Commission</Name> </SchemeOperatorName> <ListIssueDateTime>2026-02-15T00:00:00Z</ListIssueDateTime> <NextUpdate> <dateTime>2026-08-15T00:00:00Z</dateTime> </NextUpdate> </SchemeInformation>
<TrustServiceProviderList> <TrustServiceProvider> <TSPInformation> <TSPName><Name xml:lang="en">Germany</Name></TSPName> </TSPInformation> <TSPServices> <TSPService> <ServiceInformation> <ServiceTypeIdentifier> http://uri.etsi.org/TrstSvc/Svctype/TLIssuer </ServiceTypeIdentifier> <ServiceSupplyPoints> <ServiceSupplyPoint> https://www.bundesnetzagentur.de/.../TrustedList.xml </ServiceSupplyPoint> </ServiceSupplyPoints> </ServiceInformation> </TSPService> </TSPServices> </TrustServiceProvider> <!-- ... 26 more countries ... --> </TrustServiceProviderList> </TrustServiceStatusList>
section.simpara: Each national TSL then contains the actual QTSP entries with their service certificates:
section.programlisting: <!-- Simplified national TSL structure (Germany) --> <TrustServiceStatusList xmlns="http://uri.etsi.org/02231/v2#"> <TrustServiceProviderList> <TrustServiceProvider> <TSPInformation> <TSPName> <Name xml:lang="en">D-Trust GmbH</Name> </TSPName> </TSPInformation> <TSPServices> <TSPService> <ServiceInformation> <ServiceTypeIdentifier> http://uri.etsi.org/TrstSvc/Svctype/CA/QC </ServiceTypeIdentifier> <ServiceStatus> http://uri.etsi.org/TrstSvc/TrustedList/Svcstatus/granted </ServiceStatus> <StatusStartingTime>2018-07-01T00:00:00Z</StatusStartingTime> <ServiceDigitalIdentity> <DigitalId> <X509Certificate>MIIE...base64...</X509Certificate> </DigitalId> </ServiceDigitalIdentity> <ServiceInformationExtensions> <Extension Critical="true"> <Qualifications> <QualificationElement> <Qualifiers> <Qualifier uri="http://uri.etsi.org/TrstSvc/ TrustedList/SvcInfoExt/QCWithQSCD"/> </Qualifiers> </QualificationElement> </Qualifications> </Extension> </ServiceInformationExtensions> </ServiceInformation> </TSPService> </TSPServices> </TrustServiceProvider> </TrustServiceProviderList> </TrustServiceStatusList>

table.title: EUTL service type identifiers for PDF-relevant services

entry.simpara: CA issuing qualified certificates (check qualifiers for esign/eseal/web)
Currently qualified — this is what you are looking for
Not EU-qualified, but recognized under national law
Service deprecated by the supervisory body

important.simpara: A common mistake in EUTL parsing is to check only the current status. You also need to check the service history. A service that was granted from 2019-01-01 to 2023-06-30 and then withdrawn was qualified during that window. A certificate issued during the granted period is still a qualified certificate, even though the service is now withdrawn.

The TSL includes a ServiceHistory element with ServiceHistoryInstance entries recording every status change. Your validation logic must check whether the certificate was issued (and the signature created) during a period when the service status was granted.

section.title: Cryptographic Signing of the TSL
section.simpara: Every TSL (including the LOTL) is signed with an XAdES signature. To trust the contents, you must:

listitem.simpara: Verify the XML signature on the TSL document
Verify the signing certificate against the LOTL (for national TSLs) or against the Commission’s well-known certificate (for the LOTL itself)
Check freshness — the TSL must not be past its NextUpdate date (or not past it by more than a configurable grace period)

section.simpara: The LOTL signing certificate is published by the European Commission at a well-known URL. When the signing certificate rotates, the new certificate is announced in advance in the LOTL itself — this is the pivot mechanism, and it is one of the trickiest parts of EUTL parsing to implement correctly.

tip.simpara: For production systems, implement a scheduled refresh — daily or weekly — that re-downloads, re-verifies, and re-parses the entire EUTL hierarchy. The EU DSS library (Chapter 23) provides a TrustedListsCertificateSource that handles all of this automatically, including caching, refresh scheduling, signature verification, and the pivot mechanism.

chapter.title: Qualified Certificates: What Makes Them Qualified
chapter.simpara: A certificate is "qualified" when three conditions are simultaneously true:

listitem.simpara: It is issued by a CA whose certificate issuance service has granted status in the EUTL
The certificate contains specific technical indicators (the QCStatements extension)
The CA followed the procedures defined in its CPS, which was approved during the conformity assessment

chapter.simpara: Condition 3 is not something you can verify programmatically — it is an institutional guarantee, backed by the supervisory body’s ongoing oversight. Conditions 1 and 2 are what your code checks.

section.title: The QCStatements Extension
section.simpara: The technical indicator of a qualified certificate is the QCStatements extension (OID 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.3), defined in ETSI EN 319 412-5. This extension contains a sequence of statements, each identified by an OID:

table.title: QCStatements OIDs relevant to PDF signatures

entry.simpara: This certificate is issued as a qualified certificate under Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS)
The private key resides on a Qualified Signature/Seal Creation Device
The type: esign (…​6.1 = signature), eseal (…​6.2 = seal), web (…​6.3)
How long the CA retains registration information after certificate expiry
URL of the PKI Disclosure Statement (human-readable CPS summary)

warning.simpara: Do not trust the QCStatements extension alone. Anyone can put id-etsi-qcs-QcCompliance in a self-signed certificate. The QCStatements assertion is only meaningful when the issuing CA is listed in the EUTL with granted status for a CA/QC service.
The correct validation logic is:

listitem.simpara: Check that the issuing CA’s certificate matches one in the EUTL with ServiceTypeIdentifier of CA/QC and status granted
Check the period: the certificate’s notBefore date must fall within the service’s granted status period
Then check QCStatements for the specific type (esign, eseal, web)
Then check for QcSSCD if you need to verify QSCD usage

warning.simpara: Steps 1-2 without steps 3-4: you know the CA is qualified, but not necessarily this specific certificate. Steps 3-4 without steps 1-2: you have a self-asserted claim with no institutional backing. You need all four.

section.title: Certificate Policy OIDs
section.simpara: Qualified certificates also carry specific Certificate Policy OIDs in the certificatePolicies extension (OID 2.5.29.32), defined in ETSI EN 319 411-2:

table.title: Certificate Policy OIDs for qualified certificates

entry.simpara: QCP-n: Qualified Certificate Policy for natural persons (signatures)
QCP-l: Qualified Certificate Policy for legal persons (seals)
QCP-n-qscd: Natural person certificates with QSCD
QCP-l-qscd: Legal person certificates with QSCD

section.simpara: The -qscd variants indicate that the CA has verified that the subscriber’s private key is on a QSCD. This is a stronger assertion than the QcSSCD statement alone — the policy OID says "the CA has verified the key is on a QSCD according to the procedures defined in ETSI EN 319 411-2."

section.title: QSCD Requirements
section.simpara: A Qualified Signature Creation Device (or Qualified Seal Creation Device — QSCD covers both) must meet the requirements of Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Annex II:

listitem.simpara: The private key must be generated on the device and never leave it
The signing operation must occur on the device itself
Access to the private key must require authentication of the legitimate holder (PIN, biometric, etc.)
The private key cannot be derived from signatures or the public key with reasonable assurance

section.simpara: Certification path: Common Criteria evaluation (ISO/IEC 15408) at EAL4+ against Protection Profiles defined in EN 419 211 (for smart cards and tokens) or EN 419 221 (for HSMs and remote signing). A national certification body (BSI in Germany, ANSSI in France) performs the evaluation.

listitem.simpara: Smart cards (Thales IDPrime MD, Atos CardOS): private key generated on chip, never exported, signing performed on-card
USB tokens (SafeNet eToken 5110, YubiKey 5 FIPS): same security model as smart cards, USB form factor
HSMs (Thales Luna, Utimaco SecurityServer): rack-mounted devices for server-side signing; basis for remote signing (Chapter 22)

caution.simpara: Not every HSM is a certified QSCD. An HSM may be FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or Common Criteria EAL4+ certified for general cryptographic operations without being certified as a QSCD under the specific EN 419 211/221 Protection Profiles. The QSCD certification must reference the eIDAS-specific Protection Profiles.

chapter.title: Real-World QTSPs
chapter.simpara: When you are debugging why a particular qualified certificate does or does not validate, knowing who issued it and where they are listed is invaluable. Here are the QTSPs you are most likely to encounter:

D-Trust GmbH (Germany) — a subsidiary of the Bundesdruckerei (the organization that prints German passports). Listed in the German TSL, supervised by the Bundesnetzagentur. Dominant in German government and enterprise contexts.

DigiCert / QuoVadis (Netherlands, Belgium) — DigiCert acquired QuoVadis in 2019, inheriting QTSP status in multiple EU countries. One of the few providers with both AATL and eIDAS qualified status — useful when documents must validate in both Adobe Acrobat and European government systems.
GlobalSign (Belgium) — listed as a QTSP in Belgium, supervised by the FPS Economy. Strong enterprise presence. AATL membership plus EUTL listing gives broad compatibility.

Swisscom Trust Services (Austria, Switzerland) — the Swiss telecoms giant operates a QTSP listed in the Austrian TSL (not Swiss — because Switzerland is not in the EU) plus ZertES qualification in Switzerland. Their remote signing service is popular for high-volume enterprise signing.
InfoCert (Italy) — one of the largest European QTSPs by volume. Listed in the Italian TSL, supervised by AgID. Part of the Tinexta Group. Deeply embedded in Italian public administration.
Asseco Data Systems / Certum (Poland) — the dominant QTSP in Central/Eastern Europe. If you encounter qualified certificates from Polish organizations, Certum is the likely issuer.

note.simpara: This list is not exhaustive — there are over 200 qualified trust services listed across the EUTL. The EU maintains a browsable version at https://eidas.ec.europa.eu/efda/tl-browser/ where you can filter by country, service type, and status.

section.title: Downloading and Parsing the LOTL
Matching Certificates Against the EUTL

caution.simpara: The code above is educational — it demonstrates the mechanics of EUTL parsing and certificate matching. A production implementation must also:

listitem.simpara: Verify the XML signatures on the LOTL and every national TSL
Handle the LOTL pivot mechanism when the signing certificate rotates
Parse service history to support time-based qualification checks
