Cache and refresh the hierarchy on a schedule (not on every validation)
Parse QCStatements from the signer’s certificate to determine the qualification type

caution.simpara: For production use, the EU DSS library (Chapter 23) provides TrustedListsCertificateSource, which handles all of this correctly. Do not reimplement this from scratch unless you have a very specific reason.

listitem.simpara: A QTSP is not a self-declared status — it requires conformity assessment, supervisory body approval, and listing in the EUTL; the process takes 18-36 months and significant investment
The EUTL is a hierarchy: the LOTL (Commission) points to national TSLs (supervisory bodies); every document is cryptographically signed with XAdES
A certificate is qualified when: its issuer is in the EUTL with granted status for CA/QC, and the certificate contains the QcCompliance QCStatement
QCStatements carry machine-readable qualification data: QcCompliance, QcSSCD, QcType (esign/eseal/web)
Certificate Policy OIDs from ETSI EN 319 411-2 encode the qualification policy: QCP-n, QCP-l, with -qscd variants
QSCDs require Common Criteria EAL4+ certification against EN 419 211/221 Protection Profiles — not every HSM qualifies
Check service history when validating: a certificate issued during a granted period is qualified even if the service is now withdrawn
Real-world QTSPs include D-Trust (DE), DigiCert/QuoVadis (NL/BE), GlobalSign (BE), Swisscom (AT/CH), InfoCert (IT), Certum (PL), and over 200 others
For production EUTL validation, use the EU DSS framework’s TrustedListsCertificateSource — do not reimplement the parsing, pivot handling, and signature verification from scratch

partintro.simpara: Here is a question that trips up almost every developer the first time they encounter it: if an electronic signature and an electronic seal use exactly the same cryptographic mechanism — the same RSA or ECDSA algorithm, the same CMS SignedData structure, the same PAdES profile, the same qualified certificate from the same QTSP, stored on the same model of QSCD — what, exactly, is the difference?

The answer is not technical. It is legal. A signature is created by a natural person (a human being) and expresses that person’s intent. A seal is created by a legal person (a company, government agency, association) and proves the origin and integrity of data. Same bytes, same math, different legal meaning — and getting it wrong can mean the difference between a legally binding contract and an authenticated invoice.

section.title: Signatures: Natural Persons
section.simpara: An electronic signature under Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) is always attributed to a natural person. Article 3(10) defines an electronic signature as "data in electronic form which is attached to or logically associated with other data in electronic form and which is used by the signatory to sign." Article 3(9) defines "signatory" as "a natural person who creates an electronic signature."

A company cannot sign. A department cannot sign. A server cannot sign. Only a human can sign, and a signature expresses that human’s intent — consent, approval, acknowledgment, or whatever the signing context requires.

section.title: Seals: Legal Persons
section.simpara: An electronic seal is attributed to a legal person — a company, government agency, or any entity with legal personality. Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article 3(25) defines an electronic seal as "data in electronic form, which is attached to or logically associated with other data in electronic form to ensure the latter’s origin and integrity."

Note the careful wording. A seal does not express intent. It proves origin (the data came from this organization) and integrity (the data has not been tampered with). When Acme Corp seals an invoice, the seal says: "This invoice was produced by Acme Corp, and it has not been modified since." It says nothing about which employee generated it or whether anyone reviewed it.

section.title: Article 25: Signatures

listitem.simpara: 25(1): Non-discrimination — an electronic signature shall not be denied legal effect solely because it is electronic
25(2): A qualified electronic signature has the equivalent legal effect of a handwritten signature
25(3): A QES from one member state is recognized in all member states

section.simpara: The critical phrase in 25(2) is "equivalent legal effect." This is the strongest possible legal recognition — a QES is a handwritten signature for all legal purposes.

listitem.simpara: 35(1): Non-discrimination (same principle as 25(1))
35(2): A qualified electronic seal enjoys a presumption of integrity of the data and of correctness of the origin
35(3): Mutual recognition across member states
Article 36: Advanced seal requirements (same four properties as Art. 26, adapted for legal persons)
Article 38: Qualified certificates for seals must meet Annex III requirements
Article 40: Member states may define additional legal effects beyond the presumption

entry.simpara: Yes — the signer intends to be bound
No — proves origin and integrity only

important.simpara: The difference between "legal equivalence" and "presumption of integrity and origin" is not academic. A QES provides non-repudiation of commitment — the signer personally committed to the content. A QESeal provides non-repudiation of origin — the data came from the stated organization.

If a contract requires a signature, a seal will not suffice — even a qualified seal. A seal on a contract proves only that the document came from the company, not that any authorized representative agreed to its terms. Conversely, for automated invoices, a seal is the correct choice — attributing personal intent to a batch process would be legally inappropriate.

section.title: What Differs in the Certificate
section.simpara: From a cryptographic perspective, there is zero difference. Both are X.509 certificates, both use the same algorithms, both can be issued by the same QTSP. The differences are in the metadata:

section.title: Creating a Seal vs a Signature in Java
section.simpara: The code is identical. The only difference is which certificate and private key you load.

callout.para: The reason field typically differs: "Contract approval by Alice Smith" for a signature vs "Authenticated by Acme Corp" for a seal

section.title: Detecting Signature vs Seal During Validation

tip.simpara: If the QcType statement is absent (some older certificates predate ETSI EN 319 412-5), fall back to the certificate policy OID: 0.4.0.194112.1.2 / …​1.1 indicate signature certificates, 0.4.0.194112.1.3 / …​1.4 indicate seal certificates. As a last resort, check the subject DN — a certificate with only an organization name (no personal CN) is likely a seal, though this heuristic is not definitive.

chapter.title: Use Cases: When to Sign, When to Seal

section.title: Contracts and Agreements: Signature
section.simpara: Any document requiring personal commitment — contracts, NDAs, medical prescriptions, regulatory filings, court submissions — needs a signature. The signer is the specific human being who agrees. Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article 27 requires member states that mandate handwritten signatures for public services to accept QES.

section.title: Invoices and Automated Documents: Seal
section.simpara: Documents needing organizational authentication without personal approval — invoices, bank statements, payslips, automated reports — are candidates for seals. EU VAT Directive 2006/112/EC (Article 233) recognizes electronic seals as meeting invoice authenticity and integrity requirements.

section.title: Hybrid Workflows
section.simpara: Many real-world workflows combine both:

listitem.simpara: An organization seals a document template (e.g., a bank seals a loan agreement)
A natural person signs to express personal agreement (e.g., the customer signs the loan)
The organization’s representative signs to accept (a natural person acting as agent)

section.simpara: The seal and signatures coexist as separate signature dictionaries in the same PDF, each with its own byte range and CMS structure. Validators should display both, clearly distinguishing "Sealed by Acme Corp" from "Signed by Alice Smith."

chapter.title: Server-Side Seal Creation and Batch Sealing
chapter.simpara: Because seals represent organizations rather than individuals, they are ideal for automated, server-side creation. No human needs to be present at the moment of sealing.

section.title: The Architecture

listitem.simpara: The organization holds a qualified seal certificate with its private key on an HSM (certified as a Qualified Seal Creation Device)
An application server generates documents (invoices, reports, certificates)
The server calls the HSM to sign each document — no human interaction per document
The HSM authenticates the application server (mutual TLS, API key), not a person

section.simpara: This is the critical difference from signatures: for QES, the signatory must authenticate per signing operation (sole control). For seals, the organization’s security policies ensure only authorized systems can trigger sealing. This makes batch processing feasible.

warning.simpara: At high volumes, watch for two bottlenecks:

listitem.simpara: HSM throughput: a single signing operation takes 1-10ms (RSA-2048 is slower than ECDSA P-256). At 50,000 documents per minute, you may need multiple HSM partitions or load balancing.
TSA throughput: if adding qualified timestamps (PAdES-B-T), the TSA becomes the constraint. Most commercial TSAs handle hundreds of requests per second, but verify against your volume requirements.

section.title: Cost Implications
section.simpara: Signature certificates are per-person, per-year: 50-300 EUR per natural person. For 500 employees, the annual cost is substantial.
Seal certificates are per-organization, per-year. A single seal certificate can produce millions of seals at a fixed cost. The per-document cost approaches zero at scale.
This asymmetry drives the architectural decision: seals for volume, signatures for commitment.

section.title: Displaying Seals as Signatures
section.simpara: If your validation UI says "Signed by Acme Corp" when it encounters a seal, you are misleading the user. The correct display is "Sealed by" or "Authenticated by" — language that communicates origin without implying personal intent.

section.title: Using Signature Certificates for Automation
section.simpara: If your batch pipeline signs 10,000 documents per hour with Alice’s personal qualified certificate, you have a problem. Alice is not exercising sole control over each operation, and may not even be aware of most of them. Use a seal certificate for automation and reserve signature certificates for workflows requiring individual human approval.

section.title: Ignoring the Certificate Type in Code
section.simpara: Because the signing code is identical, it is easy to accidentally use a seal certificate for a signature workflow or vice versa. Your application must validate the certificate before signing: check QcType, reject mismatches, and never rely on the user to "use the right certificate."

caution.simpara: Some older qualified certificates lack a QcType statement entirely. For these, you must fall back to the EUTL service entry’s qualifiers or the certificate policy OID. This is yet another reason to use the EU DSS library (Chapter 23), which handles these edge cases.

listitem.simpara: Signatures are for natural persons and express intent — Article 25(2) gives QES the legal equivalence of a handwritten signature
Seals are for legal persons and prove origin and integrity — Article 35(2) gives qualified seals a presumption of integrity and correct origin, not equivalence to a signature
The cryptographic mechanism is identical — same CMS, same PAdES, same QSCD; the only difference is the certificate (QcType esign vs eseal, certificate policy QCP-n vs QCP-l)
Use signatures for contracts, agreements, approvals — anything requiring personal human commitment
Use seals for invoices, automated reports, official publications — organizational authentication without personal approval
Server-side batch sealing is the dominant seal use case: HSM-backed, no per-operation human auth, millions of documents at fixed cost
Enforce the certificate type in code — do not allow seal certs for signature workflows or vice versa
Display seals correctly: "Sealed by" not "Signed by" — the legal distinction matters
Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0) focuses on personal QES via the EUDI wallet; seals continue on HSM infrastructure, with rising volume from QEAAs

partintro.simpara: For the first two decades of digital signatures, the assumption was simple: the private key lives with the signer. A smart card in a wallet, a PKCS#12 file on a laptop, a USB token plugged into a desktop. The signer has physical custody of their key material, they authenticate locally (enter a PIN, type a password), and the signing happens on their device.
That model is dead — or at least, it is dying.

In the remote signing model, the private key never leaves a server-side Hardware Security Module (HSM). The signer authenticates remotely — via OAuth 2.0, via SMS OTP, via biometric verification on their phone — and the server performs the cryptographic signing operation on their behalf. The signer never touches the key, never downloads it, never even knows the key’s technical parameters. They authorize a signing operation, and the server produces the signature.

This chapter covers the architecture of remote signing: why it exists, how the CSC API standardizes it, how HSMs and Signature Activation Modules work together, what the SCAL levels mean for sole control, and how to integrate with remote signing services in practice. If your application needs to produce qualified electronic signatures (QES) at scale — for thousands of users who do not have smart cards and do not want USB tokens — remote signing is how you get there.

section.title: The Smart Card Problem
section.simpara: The traditional QES model required every signer to have:

listitem.simpara: A qualified certificate issued by a QTSP
A Qualified Signature Creation Device (QSCD) — a certified smart card or USB token
A card reader connected to their computer
Driver software for the card reader and the QSCD
Middleware that bridges the QSCD to the signing application (typically PKCS#11 or Microsoft CNG)

section.simpara: This worked in tightly controlled enterprise environments — banks, government agencies, regulated industries where IT departments could provision hardware to every employee. It failed completely for consumer scenarios, mobile scenarios, and any context where "plug in a USB device and install a driver" is not feasible.
The practical failure modes were numerous:

listitem.simpara: Driver hell: Smart card drivers are notoriously platform-dependent. A card that works on Windows 10 may not work on Windows 11 without a driver update. macOS support is inconsistent. Linux support depends on the specific combination of card, reader, and PKCS#11 module.

Mobile impossibility: Smartphones have no USB-A ports, no smart card readers, and limited support for external cryptographic hardware. The NFC-based smart card reading that some phones support is slow and unreliable.

Certificate lifecycle: Qualified certificates typically expire after 1-3 years. Renewal requires physically visiting a Registration Authority (for identity verification) and provisioning a new certificate onto the QSCD. Users forget, certificates expire, signing fails.

Cost: A qualified certificate costs 50-300 EUR per year. A smart card costs 20-50 EUR. A card reader costs 15-40 EUR. For an organization with 10,000 employees, the hardware cost alone is significant — before you account for help desk tickets from users who cannot get their cards to work.

section.title: The Remote Alternative
section.simpara: Remote signing eliminates every one of these problems:

listitem.simpara: No hardware: The signer needs only a web browser or a mobile app. No drivers, no readers, no tokens.
Mobile-native: Authentication happens through the phone itself — push notifications, biometric confirmation, SMS OTP. The signing experience on a smartphone is identical to the desktop experience.
Managed lifecycle: The QTSP manages the key lifecycle entirely. Certificate issuance, renewal, and revocation happen server-side. The signer never handles key material.
Lower per-user cost: While the QTSP charges per signature or per subscription, the elimination of hardware provisioning and support often makes remote signing cheaper at scale.

note.simpara: Remote signing does not lower the security bar — it moves the security boundary. Instead of trusting the signer’s device to protect the key (which, for a password-protected PKCS#12 on a laptop, is a dubious proposition), you trust the QTSP’s HSM infrastructure and authentication system. For most threat models, a professionally managed HSM with multi-factor authentication provides stronger key protection than a smart card with a four-digit PIN that the user wrote on a sticky note.

section.title: eIDAS and Remote QSCDs
section.simpara: The Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) does not require physical possession of a QSCD — it requires sole control (Article 26(c)) and that the device meet the requirements of Annex II. An HSM operated by a QTSP, where the signer’s key partition is accessible only through strong multi-factor authentication tied to the specific signing operation, satisfies both requirements.
The European Committee for Standardization (CEN) formalized this in EN 419 241, which defines requirements for trustworthy systems supporting server signing:

listitem.simpara: EN 419 241-1: General requirements for server signing
EN 419 241-2: Protection profile for QSCDs for server signing

section.simpara: These standards, combined with the SCAL framework (discussed below), provide the certification path for remote QSCDs. By 2024, remote signing had become the dominant model for QES in practice — more qualified signatures are produced via remote HSMs than via local smart cards.

section.title: Why Send the Hash, Not the Document
section.simpara: Remote signing uses a hash-signing model: the client computes the hash of the data to be signed and sends only the hash to the server. The server applies the private key to the hash (RSA-PSS, ECDSA, etc.) and returns the raw signature value. The client then embeds the signature value into the CMS structure and the PDF.
This design has three critical advantages:

listitem.simpara: Privacy: The document content never leaves the client. The server sees only a 32-byte (SHA-256) or 64-byte (SHA-512) hash. It cannot reconstruct the document from the hash. For sensitive documents — medical records, legal contracts, financial statements — this is essential.
Bandwidth: Sending a 32-byte hash is dramatically faster than sending a 50 MB PDF over a network connection. For mobile clients on cellular networks, the difference matters.

Separation of concerns: The client handles PDF parsing, byte range computation, CMS structure building, and timestamp embedding. The server handles key management, access control, and the raw cryptographic operation. Neither needs to understand the other’s domain.

section.title: The Flow Step by Step

figure.title: Remote signing flow — client and server responsibilities

section.simpara: Steps 1-3 and 11-14 happen entirely on the client. Only steps 4, 6, and the server-side steps 5-10 involve the remote server. The document never crosses the network.

important.simpara: The "hash" sent to the server in step 6 is not the hash of the document bytes. It is the hash of the DER-encoded signed attributes of the CMS SignerInfo. The signed attributes include the messageDigest attribute, which itself contains the hash of the document bytes. This two-level hashing is standard CMS behavior (defined in RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5.4) and is easy to get wrong.

If you hash the document bytes and send that to the remote server, the resulting signature will not verify — because CMS validators expect the signature to be over the signed attributes, not the raw data.

section.title: What the CSC Is
section.simpara: The Cloud Signature Consortium (CSC) is an industry body that developed an open standard API for remote signing. Before the CSC API, every remote signing provider had its own proprietary API — different authentication flows, different request/response formats, different error codes. Integrating with Swisscom AIS required completely different code than integrating with DigiCert, which required completely different code than integrating with InfoCert.
The CSC API v2.0 (the current version, published in 2019 and updated since) standardizes:

listitem.simpara: Authentication: OAuth 2.0 with specific grant types
Credential discovery: listing available signing credentials for a user
Signature activation: the mechanism by which the signer authorizes a specific signing operation
Hash signing: the actual cryptographic operation

section.simpara: The API is RESTful, uses JSON for request and response bodies, and is designed to be consumed by web and mobile clients.

section.title: API Endpoints
section.simpara: The CSC API defines the following core endpoints:

entry.simpara: Authenticate the user with the remote signing service. Returns an OAuth 2.0 access token.
List the signing credentials available to the authenticated user. Returns credential IDs, certificate chains, key algorithms, and SCAL levels.
Get detailed information about a specific credential, including the certificate chain and supported algorithms.
Authorize a specific credential for signing. For SCAL2 credentials, this triggers explicit user authentication (OTP, biometric) and returns a Signature Activation Data (SAD) token.
Send a one-time password to the user (SMS, email) as part of SCAL2 authorization.
Sign one or more hashes using a previously authorized credential. Returns the raw signature values.
Request an RFC 3161 timestamp. Some providers expose timestamping through the CSC API as well.

section.title: Authentication: OAuth 2.0
section.simpara: The CSC API uses OAuth 2.0 for authentication. The specific grant type depends on the deployment scenario:

listitem.simpara: Authorization Code Grant: For web applications where the user authenticates in a browser. The signing application redirects the user to the QTSP’s authorization server, the user logs in, and the application receives an authorization code that it exchanges for an access token.
Client Credentials Grant: For server-to-server scenarios where a system (not a human) needs to sign. The application authenticates with its own client ID and secret.
Resource Owner Password Grant: For scenarios where the application collects the user’s credentials directly. Discouraged by OAuth 2.0 best practices but still supported by some providers.

section.title: Credential Discovery
section.simpara: Once authenticated, the client can discover what signing credentials are available:

The credential response includes the full certificate chain (Base64-encoded), which the client needs to build the CMS structure before requesting the hash signature. This is a critical detail: the client must know the signer’s certificate at CMS construction time (for the ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute), not just at signing time.

section.title: Credential Authorization and SAD
section.simpara: For SCAL2 credentials (see below), the client must explicitly authorize the credential before signing. This triggers an additional authentication step — the QTSP sends an OTP to the signer, or the signer confirms via a mobile app, or biometric verification occurs.

The result of successful authorization is a Signature Activation Data (SAD) token — an opaque string that the client presents with the signing request to prove that the signer authorized this specific operation.

warning.simpara: The SAD token has a limited lifetime (typically 5-10 minutes) and a limited number of uses (specified in numSignatures). If you request authorization for 1 signature, the SAD can only be used once. If you need to sign multiple documents in a batch, request a higher numSignatures value — but be aware that some QTSPs limit the maximum.
If the SAD expires before you use it, you must re-authorize. Design your signing flow so that SAD acquisition happens immediately before hash signing, not minutes before.

section.title: Hash Signing
section.simpara: The core operation — sending a hash and receiving a signature:
The OIDs in the request body specify the algorithms:

table.title: Common algorithm OIDs for CSC signHash

entry.simpara: Hash algorithm. The hash you send must use this algorithm.
RSA signature with SHA-256. The server applies PKCS#1 v1.5 padding.
RSA-PSS signature. Preferred over PKCS#1 v1.5 for new deployments.
ECDSA signature with SHA-256. Shorter signatures, equivalent security.

section.title: The Problem SCAL Solves
section.simpara: The Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS) Article 26(c) requires that an advanced electronic signature be "created using electronic signature creation data that the signatory can, with a high level of confidence, use under his sole control."

For local signing with a smart card, sole control is straightforward: the signer physically possesses the card and knows the PIN. For remote signing, sole control is more nuanced. The key lives in a server-side HSM. Multiple users' keys may reside in the same physical HSM. The QTSP’s system administrators have physical access to the HSM. How do you prove that the signer — and only the signer — authorized a specific signing operation?
The SCAL (Sole Control Assurance Level) framework, defined in CEN EN 419 241-1, provides two levels of assurance:

section.title: SCAL1: Server-Side Sole Control
section.simpara: In SCAL1, the remote signing server ensures sole control through its own authentication and authorization mechanisms, but there is no explicit per-signature authorization from the user beyond the initial session authentication.

Characteristics: - The signer authenticates to the service (username + password, possibly with MFA) - Once authenticated, the service can sign on the signer’s behalf for the duration of the session - The service’s access control mechanisms prevent unauthorized use - There is no additional user interaction for each signing operation

SCAL1 is suitable for: - Advanced electronic signatures (AdES): The sole control requirement of Article 26(c) can be met if the authentication is sufficiently strong - Automated signing workflows: Where a human authorizes a batch of signatures upfront - Internal enterprise signing: Where the trust model accepts session-level authorization
SCAL1 is not suitable for QES, because the sole control assurance is not strong enough — the server could theoretically sign without the user’s knowledge during an active session.

section.title: SCAL2: Explicit Per-Signature Authorization
section.simpara: In SCAL2, every signing operation requires explicit authorization from the signer, independent of session authentication. The authorization is linked to the specific data being signed.

Characteristics: - The signer authenticates to the service (session-level) - For each signing operation, the signer must provide additional authorization: OTP, biometric confirmation, mobile app push approval - The authorization produces a SAD (Signature Activation Data) token that is bound to the specific credential and limited in scope (time, number of signatures) - The HSM will not perform the signing operation without a valid SAD

SCAL2 is required for: - Qualified electronic signatures (QES): The per-signature authorization satisfies Article 26(c) with a high level of confidence - Any scenario where you need to prove that the signer actively authorized each specific signature

entry.simpara: Easy (sign N documents in a loop)
Possible but requires batch SAD (N signatures authorized at once)

important.simpara: The distinction between SCAL1 and SCAL2 is not merely theoretical — it determines whether the resulting signature can be a QES. If your application needs to produce qualified electronic signatures via remote signing, you must implement a SCAL2 flow. A SCAL1 flow, no matter how strong the session authentication, will not produce a QES because it lacks per-signature authorization.

This is the single most common mistake in remote signing integrations: assuming that a strong login (username + password + TOTP) is sufficient for QES. It is not. The authentication must be linked to the signing operation, not just the session.

section.title: The HSM (Hardware Security Module)
section.simpara: The HSM is the physical device that stores private keys and performs cryptographic operations. In a remote signing deployment, the HSM is typically:

listitem.simpara: FIPS 140-2 Level 3 or Level 4 certified (US standard)
Common Criteria EAL4+ certified (international standard)
Deployed in a physically secured data center with access logging, video surveillance, and multi-person access controls
Connected to the signing server via a high-speed internal network (never directly exposed to the internet)

section.simpara: Popular HSMs for remote signing: - Thales Luna Network HSM: Widely used in European QTSPs. Supports PKCS#11, JCE (Java Cryptography Extension), and Microsoft CNG. Available in various throughput tiers. - Utimaco SecurityServer: German manufacturer, popular with European banks and government agencies. Strong PKCS#11 support. - Entrust nShield Connect: Used by large enterprises and some QTSPs. Offers a proprietary "Security World" key management architecture alongside standard PKCS#11. - AWS CloudHSM / Azure Dedicated HSM: Cloud-hosted HSMs. Convenient for cloud-native architectures, but may not meet all QSCD certification requirements (check the specific certification status).

section.title: The SAM (Signature Activation Module)
section.simpara: The SAM sits between the remote signing API and the HSM. Its job is to enforce sole control:

listitem.simpara: Receive the signing request with the signer’s credentials and SAD
Verify the SAD — check that it is valid, not expired, bound to the correct credential, and that the number of allowed signatures has not been exhausted
Authorize the HSM operation — unlock the signer’s key partition in the HSM for exactly one (or N) signing operations
Return the signature value to the API layer

section.simpara: The SAM is the component that makes SCAL2 possible. Without a SAM, the signing server could theoretically use any key in the HSM at any time. The SAM ensures that keys are only activated when the legitimate signer has provided explicit, verifiable authorization.
section.screen:
Architecture of a Remote Signing Service
┌─────────────────────┐ Internet │ Load Balancer / │ ─────────────────────│ API Gateway │ └──────────┬───────────┘ │ ┌──────────┴───────────┐ │ CSC API Server │ │ (REST endpoints) │ │ OAuth 2.0 token │ │ validation │ └──────────┬───────────┘ │ ┌──────────┴───────────┐ │ Signature │ │ Activation Module │ │ (SAM) │ │ • SAD validation │ │ • SCAL enforcement │ │ • Audit logging │ └──────────┬───────────┘ │ PKCS#11 ┌──────────┴───────────┐ │ Hardware Security │ │ Module (HSM) │ │ • Key storage │ │ • Crypto operations │ │ • Tamper-evident │ └──────────────────────┘

section.title: PKCS#11: The HSM Interface
section.simpara: PKCS#11 (also called Cryptoki) is the standard API for communicating with HSMs and smart cards. It is a C-based API with Java bindings available through the JCE SunPKCS11 provider or through dedicated libraries.
In a remote signing server, the PKCS#11 flow for signing looks like this:

tip.simpara: The JCE SunPKCS11 provider requires a configuration that specifies the PKCS#11 library path, slot number, and other HSM-specific parameters. This configuration is typically loaded from a file or a dynamic InputStream:
Different HSM vendors provide different PKCS#11 shared libraries (.so on Linux, .dll on Windows). The library path is the most common source of "HSM not found" errors in deployments.

section.title: Key Lifecycle in an HSM
section.simpara: Private keys in an HSM follow a strict lifecycle:

listitem.simpara: Generation: The key pair is generated inside the HSM. The private key never exists outside the HSM’s tamper-resistant boundary. This is a fundamental requirement for QSCD certification.
Activation: The key is associated with a certificate (issued by the QTSP’s CA) and made available for signing. For SCAL2, activation for each signing operation requires a valid SAD.
Use: The HSM performs signing operations using the key. Each use is logged for audit purposes.
Suspension: The key can be temporarily suspended (e.g., if the signer reports a compromised authentication factor). The certificate is not revoked, but signing is disabled.
Revocation: The key is permanently disabled, and the associated certificate is revoked. The key material may be destroyed or archived depending on the QTSP’s policy.

note.simpara: For QSCD certification under EN 419 241-2, the HSM must guarantee that private keys are generated internally and can never be extracted — not even by the QTSP’s own administrators. This is what "the key never leaves the HSM" means in practice. The HSM must be designed so that there is no API, no debug mode, no firmware backdoor that could extract key material. Physical attacks (opening the HSM casing) must trigger key destruction.

section.title: Major Remote Signing Providers
section.simpara: The remote signing market is dominated by a handful of European QTSPs, each offering CSC-compliant APIs with varying degrees of completeness:

entry.simpara: Integrated with Swiss law (ZertES). Strong mobile authentication via Mobile ID. High throughput for batch signing.
Global presence. QuoVadis (acquired by DigiCert) provides the EU-qualified certificates. AATL-listed roots.
Largest QTSP in Europe by volume. SPID integration for Italian digital identity. Strong in Italian government sector.
Focus on document workflow integration. Offers both remote signing and a full document management platform.
Mobile-first approach. Signing via smartphone app with biometric authentication. Popular in Baltic states.
German Federal Printing Office subsidiary. Strong in German government and healthcare sectors.

section.title: The Ascertia/ADSS Pattern
section.simpara: A notable infrastructure pattern in the CSC ecosystem is the widespread use of Ascertia ADSS Server as the underlying platform. Many QTSPs — especially regional and government-affiliated providers — do not build their own CSC API implementation. Instead, they deploy Ascertia’s ADSS (Automated Digital Signature Server), which exposes the CSC API through a consistent base path:
section.screen: https://<provider-domain>/adss/service/ras/csc/v1/
section.simpara: This creates a recognizable fingerprint. If you encounter a CSC endpoint with /adss/service/ras/ in the URL, you are almost certainly talking to an Ascertia deployment, regardless of the provider’s branding. The practical consequence: the CSC behavior, error formats, and quirks will be identical across all Ascertia-based providers, even though they are operated by different organizations in different countries.
Common variations include:

listitem.simpara: https://signinghub.ascertia.com/adss/service/ras/csc/v1/ (Ascertia’s own hosted service)
https://ras.<provider>.com/adss/service/ras/csc/v1/ (white-labeled deployments)
https://sign.<provider>.com/adss/service/ras/csc/v1/ (government integrations)

section.simpara: This pattern matters for integration testing: if you have tested against one Ascertia-based provider, you have effectively tested against all of them. Conversely, if you encounter an Ascertia-specific bug, expect it to appear at every provider using the same platform.

section.title: Sandbox and Test Endpoints
section.simpara: Most CSC providers offer sandbox environments for development and integration testing. These sandboxes are invaluable — they let you develop and test the entire signing flow without purchasing production credentials or risking real certificates.

table.title: CSC Provider Sandbox and Test Endpoints

entry.simpara: Free sandbox with demo credentials. Publicly documented test accounts available. Good starting point for CSC integration.
Demo environment for Document Trust Manager. Requires DigiCert partnership account.
Development endpoint. Mobile-first — requires ZealiD test app.
End-to-end test environment. Belgian Mobile ID integration testing.

tip.simpara: Start with SSL.com’s sandbox. It is the most accessible CSC test environment — no partnership agreements, no special hardware, publicly documented demo credentials. Build your integration against their sandbox first, then adapt for your production provider. The CSC API surface is standardized enough that switching providers later requires only URL and credential changes, not code changes.

section.title: National eID and CSC Integration
section.simpara: Several European countries have integrated their national electronic identity systems with CSC-compatible remote signing. This means citizens can use their existing government-issued digital identity to create qualified electronic signatures without obtaining separate signing credentials.

table.title: National eID systems with CSC-compatible remote signing

entry.simpara: InfoCert and Namirial both offer SPID-authenticated remote signing. Citizens use their existing SPID identity to access signing credentials hosted by the QTSP.
BankID provides remote signing through bank-operated infrastructure. High adoption rate (~4.5 million active users).
PrimeSign (Cryptas) infrastructure. Mobile phone-based signing via the national eID system.
Integrates with the Estonian national ID card and Mobile-ID systems for remote signing.
Swisscom Trust Services. Qualified under Swiss law (ZertES) rather than eIDAS, but cross-recognized.
FNMT (Fábrica Nacional de Moneda y Timbre) provides remote signing tied to the national certificate infrastructure.

section.simpara: For integrators, the key implication is authentication: when the CSC provider is backed by a national eID system, the OAuth 2.0 flow typically redirects through the national identity provider (OpenID Connect federation). Your application’s authentication UI must support this redirect — it is not a simple username/password flow.

section.title: CSC Endpoint Discovery
section.simpara: Because the CSC specification requires a public /info endpoint that responds without authentication, CSC servers can be discovered by probing for known URL patterns:
section.screen: GET /csc/v2/info → Most CSC v2 providers GET /csc/v1/info → Legacy CSC v1 providers GET /adss/service/ras/csc/v1/info → Ascertia ADSS deployments
section.simpara: A successful response returns the service capabilities:
section.programlisting: { "specs": "CSC API v2", "name": "Remote Signing Service", "authType": ["oauth2"], "methods": ["credentials/list", "credentials/info", "credentials/authorize", "signatures/signHash"] }
section.simpara: This discovery mechanism is useful for integration testing (verifying that a provider’s endpoint is reachable and responsive) and for building provider registries. The /info endpoint also reveals which CSC API methods the provider supports — not all providers implement the full specification.

section.title: Choosing a Provider
section.simpara: When selecting a remote signing provider, consider:

listitem.simpara: Trusted list presence: The provider must be a QTSP listed in the EUTL of the member state where they are supervised. Verify this at the EU’s official Trusted List Browser.

AATL listing: If your signed PDFs will be opened in Adobe Acrobat, the provider’s root certificate should be in the Adobe Approved Trust List (AATL). Without AATL listing, Acrobat will show a warning even for EU-qualified signatures.

Geographic coverage: Some providers issue certificates qualified in one member state; others have qualified status in multiple states. For cross-border use, mutual recognition under eIDAS Article 25(3) applies, but AATL listing is a separate concern.

API completeness: Not all providers implement the full CSC API. Some support only SCAL1, some have proprietary extensions, some are still on CSC v1.x. Request the provider’s API documentation and test against their sandbox environment.

Authentication options: SCAL2 requires per-signature authorization. Providers offer different mechanisms: SMS OTP, mobile app push, biometric (fingerprint/face), hardware token. Choose based on your users' capabilities.

Pricing model: Per-signature pricing (typically 0.10-2.00 EUR per signature), per-certificate pricing (annual fee per signer), or volume-based tiers. Calculate your total cost based on expected signing volume.
SLA and availability: For production signing, you need a provider with a published SLA. Remote signing is a synchronous operation in most workflows — if the provider is down, your users cannot sign.

warning.simpara: Do not assume that all CSC API implementations are interchangeable. The CSC specification leaves room for provider-specific behavior in areas like:

listitem.simpara: OTP delivery mechanisms (some providers trigger OTP automatically, others require an explicit /credentials/sendOTP call)
SAD token format and lifetime
Batch signing limits (how many hashes can be signed with a single SAD)
Error response formats and error codes

warning.simpara: Always develop and test against the specific provider’s sandbox before going to production. The CSC specification is a standard, but implementations have edges.

chapter.title: Complete Integration Example
chapter.simpara: The following example shows a complete remote signing flow — from PDF preparation through CSC API calls to final PDF assembly:

caution.simpara: The code above is a simplified illustration of the remote signing flow. A production implementation must also handle:

listitem.simpara: Error recovery: What happens if the CSC API call fails after SAD authorization? The SAD may be consumed. You may need to re-authorize.
Timeout handling: Network calls to the CSC API and TSA can fail. Implement timeouts and retries.

Content size estimation: PDFBox’s addSignature requires you to pre-estimate the size of the /Contents value. If you underestimate, the CMS will not fit. Set SignatureOptions.setPreferredSignatureSize() generously (e.g., 16384 bytes).
Thread safety: If multiple users sign concurrently, your CSC API client must handle concurrent requests correctly. Use separate HTTP connections per request.

caution.simpara: For production remote signing with 3.0.0, consider using the EU DSS framework (Chapter 23), which provides a complete, tested implementation of the remote signing flow with CSC API support.

section.title: Transport Security
section.simpara: All communication with the CSC API must use TLS 1.2 or later. The access token is a bearer token — anyone who intercepts it can use it to sign. In production:

listitem.simpara: Pin the CSC API server’s TLS certificate (or at least its root CA) to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks
Use short-lived access tokens (minutes, not hours)
Transmit tokens only in the Authorization header, never in query parameters or request bodies
Log all API calls for audit purposes, but never log access tokens or SAD values

section.title: Hash Manipulation
section.simpara: The remote signing server signs whatever hash you send it. It does not — and cannot — verify that the hash corresponds to any particular document. This means: if an attacker compromises the client application, they can send arbitrary hashes to the server and obtain valid signatures over arbitrary data.

Mitigations: - SCAL2 authorization: The signer must actively authorize each signing operation, limiting the window for automated abuse - Audit logging: Every signing operation is logged on the server, creating a forensic trail - Rate limiting: Unusual signing patterns (hundreds of signatures per minute from a single user) should trigger alerts - Document description in SAD: Some CSC implementations allow including a document description in the authorization request, which the signer can verify before approving

section.title: Key Compromise
section.simpara: If an HSM is compromised, all keys stored in it are compromised. This is the catastrophic failure mode of remote signing — a single breach affects all users.

HSM-level mitigations: - Physical security (data center access controls, tamper detection) - Multi-person key ceremony for HSM initialization - Regular firmware updates - Separate HSMs for different security levels (production vs. test) - Geographic redundancy with key replication

Operational mitigations: - Certificate revocation within minutes of detection - Automatic alerts for anomalous signing patterns - Regular penetration testing of the signing infrastructure - Insurance against breach liability (some QTSPs carry specific cyber insurance)

listitem.simpara: Remote signing moves the private key from the signer’s device to a server-side HSM operated by a QTSP — the signer authenticates remotely, the server signs
The hash-signing model preserves document confidentiality: only a 32-byte hash crosses the network, never the document content
The CSC API v2.0 standardizes remote signing: OAuth 2.0 authentication, credential discovery, signature activation, hash signing
SCAL1 provides session-level sole control (suitable for AdES); SCAL2 provides per-signature authorization (required for QES)
The SAD token is the proof that the signer authorized a specific signing operation — it has limited lifetime and use count
HSMs store keys and perform cryptographic operations; SAMs enforce sole control and verify SAD tokens
PKCS#11 is the standard interface between the signing server and the HSM — vendor-specific libraries are the most common deployment issue
Major providers (Swisscom AIS, DigiCert/QuoVadis, InfoCert, Namirial, ZealID, D-Trust) all offer CSC-compliant APIs with varying degrees of completeness
Always test against the specific provider’s sandbox — CSC API implementations have provider-specific behaviors
Many providers run Ascertia ADSS as their CSC platform — if you’ve tested one ADSS deployment, the behavior will be identical across all of them
National eID integration (SPID, BankID, SwissID) routes authentication through the national identity provider via OpenID Connect — not a simple username/password flow
The CSC /info endpoint enables endpoint discovery — it responds without authentication and reveals the provider’s capabilities and supported methods
For production PDF signing via remote services, consider the EU DSS framework (Chapter 23), which provides tested CSC API integration

partintro.simpara: Throughout this book, we have been building PDF signing and validation code from the ground up — constructing CMS structures by hand with BouncyCastle, computing byte ranges manually, assembling DSS dictionaries one stream at a time. This is the right approach for understanding what is happening, but it is the wrong approach for production software.

The European Digital Signature Services (EU DSS) framework is the European Commission’s open-source reference implementation for digital signature creation, extension, and validation. It is maintained by the European Commission’s ISA2 programme (now the Digital Europe Programme), developed primarily by the Luxembourg company Nowina Solutions, and used by dozens of EU member states in their national validation services. It handles PAdES, CAdES, XAdES, and JAdES. It validates against the EU Trusted List. It implements every PAdES profile from B-B through B-LTA. It supports remote signing via the CSC API. And it is free — released under the LGPL 2.1 license.

If you are building production software that creates or validates digital signatures in a European context, EU DSS is not just an option — it is the benchmark against which your implementation will be measured. This chapter covers everything you need to use it: architecture, core modules, the signing flow, validation, Spring Boot integration, and the honest trade-offs between using EU DSS and rolling your own.

section.title: Origins
section.simpara: The EU DSS project began in 2011 as part of the European Commission’s ISA programme (Interoperability Solutions for European Public Administrations). The goal was to provide member states with a reusable software library that correctly implemented the ETSI signature standards — so that each country would not have to write its own implementation from scratch and risk incompatibilities.

The project was motivated by the same cross-border recognition failures that led to eIDAS itself (Chapter 19). Even when countries agreed on the legal framework, their software implementations often produced or validated signatures differently. A common reference implementation was the practical answer to "why doesn’t my signature validate in your system?"

section.title: Development
section.simpara: The framework has been developed primarily by Nowina Solutions (formerly Nowina Technologies), a Luxembourg-based company specializing in electronic signatures and trust services. Nowina won the European Commission’s procurement for DSS development and has been the principal technical contributor since 2015.

The source code is hosted on the European Commission’s GitHub organization at github.com/esig/dss and on the Commission’s own code repository at ec.europa.eu/digital-building-blocks/code/projects/ESIG.

entry.simpara: First widely adopted version. PAdES, CAdES, XAdES support. Java 7/8.
Major rewrite. Modular architecture (separate Maven modules). Java 8 minimum. Introduced pluggable token architecture.
JAdES (JSON Advanced Electronic Signatures) support. ETSI EN 319 102-1 validation policy. Improved EU Trusted List parsing.
Java 11 minimum. CSC API support for remote signing. Updated BouncyCastle dependency (1.70+).
Improved PKCS#11 token support. PSD2 (Payment Services Directive) certificate validation. Updated CRL/OCSP handling.
Java 17 minimum. Module system (JPMS) support. Restructured Maven coordinates (groupId changed from eu.europa.ec.joinup.sd-dss to eu.europa.dss). Updated 1.80 dependency.
Java 17+. Enhanced EUDI wallet integration. Improved TSL (Trusted List) refresh mechanisms. This is the current stable release as of early 2026.

note.simpara: The groupId change in version 6.0 is a common source of confusion. If your project depends on eu.europa.ec.joinup.sd-dss:dss-pades, that is DSS 5.x. If it depends on eu.europa.dss:dss-pades, that is DSS 6.x. The two groupIds are not interchangeable — do not mix modules from different major versions.

section.title: Design Principles
section.simpara: EU DSS is built around three core architectural principles:

listitem.simpara: Format independence: The same signing and validation workflow works for PAdES (PDF), CAdES (CMS), XAdES (XML), and JAdES (JSON). The format-specific logic is encapsulated in format modules; the core signing flow is shared.

Pluggable token architecture: The private key can come from anywhere — a PKCS#12 file, a PKCS#11 smart card, a Microsoft CNG keystore, a remote CSC API service, or a custom SignatureTokenConnection implementation. The signing flow does not need to know where the key is.

Policy-based validation: Validation is driven by a configurable policy (an XML file) that defines which checks to perform and what their outcomes mean. You can use the default ETSI validation policy, or you can define your own — accepting certain non-conformities for your use case while rejecting others.

section.title: Module Overview
section.simpara: EU DSS is organized into Maven modules, each with a specific responsibility. You do not need all of them — include only what you use.

entry.simpara: Service Provider Interface. Core abstractions: DSSDocument, SignatureTokenConnection, CertificateToken, DSSPrivateKeyEntry. All other modules depend on this.
Data model classes: SignatureLevel, DigestAlgorithm, EncryptionAlgorithm, TimestampType, etc. Enumerations and DTOs used across the framework.
Token implementations: Pkcs12SignatureToken, Pkcs11SignatureToken, MSCAPISignatureToken, KeyStoreSignatureToken. These connect to key material.
PAdES (PDF) signing and validation. Depends on Apache PDFBox internally. Produces and validates PAdES-B-B through B-LTA.
CAdES (CMS) signing and validation. Used internally by dss-pades for the CMS structures inside PDF signatures.
XAdES (XML) signing and validation. Not needed for PDF work unless you also handle XML signatures.
JAdES (JSON) signing and validation. The newest format support.
EU Trusted List (TSL/EUTL) parsing, validation, and caching. Downloads the trusted lists, verifies their signatures, and builds a trust store that other modules use for validation.
Higher-level service classes: OnlineOCSPSource, OnlineCRLSource, OnlineTSPSource, CommonsDataLoader (HTTP client). Implements the data-fetching operations that validation requires.
Document-level abstractions: SignatureParameters, ToBeSigned, SignatureValue, SignedDocumentValidator. The signing flow API lives here.
JAXB model for the diagnostic data XML that validation produces. Useful for detailed validation reports.
JAXB model for the detailed validation report. Human-readable validation results.
JAXB model for the simple validation report. Summary-level pass/fail results.

section.title: Maven Coordinates
section.simpara: For a typical PAdES signing and validation project with EU DSS 6.2, you need:
section.programlisting:
<properties> <dss.version>6.2</dss.version> </properties>
<dependencies> <!-- Core signing and validation for PAdES --> <dependency> <groupId>eu.europa.dss</groupId> <artifactId>dss-pades</artifactId> <version>${dss.version}</version> </dependency>
<!-- Token support (PKCS#12, PKCS#11) --> <dependency> <groupId>eu.europa.dss</groupId> <artifactId>dss-token</artifactId> <version>${dss.version}</version> </dependency>
<!-- Online sources for OCSP, CRL, TSA --> <dependency> <groupId>eu.europa.dss</groupId> <artifactId>dss-service</artifactId> <version>${dss.version}</version> </dependency>
<!-- EU Trusted List validation --> <dependency> <groupId>eu.europa.dss</groupId> <artifactId>dss-tsl-validation</artifactId> <version>${dss.version}</version> </dependency> </dependencies>

tip.simpara: EU DSS depends on 3.0.0 internally for PDF operations. If your project also uses PDFBox directly, ensure version alignment — having two different PDFBox versions on the classpath causes unpredictable behavior. Check the DSS BOM (Bill of Materials) for the exact PDFBox version it expects.

section.title: The Two-Phase Signing Model
section.simpara: EU DSS uses a two-phase signing model that maps directly to the hash-signing model described in Chapter 22. This separation is what makes remote signing possible — the private key operation can happen anywhere, including on a remote HSM via the CSC API.

simpara.emphasis: Phase 1: Compute what needs to be signed

section.simpara: The ToBeSigned object contains the bytes that must be signed — specifically, the DER-encoded signed attributes of the CMS SignerInfo, exactly as described in Chapter 22’s hash-signing flow. EU DSS has already computed the message digest over the PDF byte range, built the ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute, and assembled the signed attributes.

simpara.emphasis: Phase 2: Sign the hash and finalize

section.simpara: The beauty of this design: step 5 is the only step that touches the private key. Everything else — PDF parsing, CMS construction, timestamp embedding, incremental save — is handled by EU DSS. If you want to use a remote signing service instead of a local key, you replace step 5 with a CSC API call and everything else stays the same.

section.title: SignatureTokenConnection Implementations
section.simpara: The SignatureTokenConnection interface abstracts access to the private key. EU DSS ships with several implementations:
For remote signing via CSC API, you implement SignatureTokenConnection with HTTP calls to the CSC endpoints. The sign() method sends the hash to /signatures/signHash and returns the signature value.

chapter.title: Complete PAdES-B-T Signing Example
chapter.simpara: Here is a complete, working example that signs a PDF with PAdES-B-T using a PKCS#12 keystore and a TSA:

That is roughly 60 lines of code — including logging, comments, and error handling setup — to produce a fully compliant PAdES-B-T signature. Compare this to the hundreds of lines required to build the CMS structure manually with BouncyCastle (Chapter 12), and the value proposition of EU DSS becomes clear.

chapter.title: Extending Signatures
chapter.simpara: One of EU DSS’s most powerful features is signature extension: upgrading a signature’s PAdES profile without re-signing.
The extendDocument method handles all the complexity described in Chapter 17 (PAdES Profiles) and Chapter 18 (Timestamps and LTV):

listitem.simpara: Parses the existing signature to determine its current level
Fetches OCSP responses and CRLs for every certificate in the chain
Builds the DSS dictionary with all validation data
Requests an archival timestamp from the TSA
Saves everything as an incremental update

chapter.simpara: This is the operation you would run as a scheduled job to keep archived documents verifiable indefinitely.

section.title: Basic Validation
section.simpara: Validation is where EU DSS provides the most value. Building a correct signature validator from scratch requires implementing the entire ETSI EN 319 102-1 validation algorithm — certificate chain building, revocation checking at the correct point in time, timestamp validation, DSS consumption, policy evaluation. EU DSS does all of this:

section.title: Validation Reports
section.simpara: EU DSS produces three levels of validation reports:

listitem.simpara: Simple Report: A summary of each signature’s validity — pass/fail, signer name, signing time, signature level. Suitable for end-user display.

Detailed Report: A structured breakdown of every validation check — certificate chain validation, revocation checking, timestamp validation, policy conformance. Each check has an indication (PASSED, FAILED, INDETERMINATE) and a sub-indication explaining why.

Diagnostic Data: The raw data collected during validation — all certificates encountered, all revocation data fetched, all timestamps found. This is the lowest level and is useful for debugging validation failures.

section.title: EU Trusted List Integration
section.simpara: For validating qualified certificates, EU DSS can load and parse the EU Trusted List (EUTL):

warning.simpara: The EU Trusted List is large — the LOTL (List of the Lists) references 27+ national trusted lists, each containing dozens of trust service providers and their certificates. The initial download and parsing can take 30-60 seconds.

In production: - Load the EUTL at application startup, not on the first validation request - Cache the downloaded lists on disk (use FileCacheDataLoader) - Refresh periodically (every 24 hours is typical) rather than on every request - If the EUTL is unreachable, fall back to the cached version
Never skip EUTL validation — if you validate qualified signatures without checking the EUTL, you are accepting certificates that may not be qualified.

chapter.title: Spring Boot Integration
chapter.simpara: EU DSS provides a Spring Boot starter module that auto-configures most of the framework:
chapter.programlisting: <dependency> <groupId>eu.europa.dss</groupId> <artifactId>dss-spring-boot-starter</artifactId> <version>${dss.version}</version> </dependency>

section.title: Configuration Properties
section.simpara: The starter exposes configuration through application.properties:
section.programlisting:
# TSA configuration dss.tsp.url=http://timestamp.digicert.com dss.tsp.digest-algorithm=SHA256
# EU Trusted List dss.lotl.url=https://ec.europa.eu/tools/lotl/eu-lotl.xml dss.lotl.cache-directory=/var/cache/dss/tl dss.lotl.cache-expiration=86400000
# OCSP/CRL timeouts dss.ocsp.connect-timeout=5000 dss.ocsp.read-timeout=10000 dss.crl.connect-timeout=5000 dss.crl.read-timeout=30000
# Proxy (if behind a corporate proxy) dss.proxy.https.host=proxy.example.com dss.proxy.https.port=8443

section.title: Auto-Configured Beans
section.simpara: The starter auto-configures the following Spring beans:

listitem.simpara: CommonCertificateVerifier — pre-configured with OCSP and CRL sources
OnlineTSPSource — configured from dss.tsp.url
OnlineOCSPSource and OnlineCRLSource — with timeout configuration
TrustedListsCertificateSource — loaded from the EUTL on startup
TLValidationJob — scheduled to refresh the EUTL periodically
CommonsDataLoader — HTTP client with proxy and timeout support

section.simpara: You can inject these directly into your services:

chapter.title: EU DSS vs. Rolling Your Own

section.title: When to Use EU DSS
section.simpara: Use EU DSS when:

listitem.simpara: You need eIDAS compliance: EU DSS is the reference implementation. If your validation disagrees with EU DSS, regulators will assume your code is wrong, not EU DSS.
You validate qualified signatures: EUTL parsing, QCStatements interpretation, SCAL assessment — EU DSS handles all of this. Implementing it correctly from scratch is months of work.
You need multiple signature formats: If your application handles PAdES, CAdES, and XAdES, EU DSS’s format-independent architecture saves enormous effort.
You need long-term maintenance: EU DSS is actively maintained with a clear release cadence. ETSI standards evolve, new algorithms are added, old ones deprecated. EU DSS tracks these changes.

section.title: When to Consider Alternatives
section.simpara: EU DSS may not be the right choice when:

listitem.simpara: You need total control over CMS construction: EU DSS abstracts away the CMS building process. If your use case requires non-standard CMS attributes, custom signature dictionary entries, or unusual byte range handling, EU DSS may fight you. In these cases, using BouncyCastle directly (as shown throughout this book) gives you full control.

You have strict dependency constraints: EU DSS brings a significant dependency tree — PDFBox, BouncyCastle, Apache HttpClient, JAXB, SLF4J, and dozens of transitive dependencies. If your project has strict dependency policies, the EU DSS footprint may be too large.

You only create signatures, never validate: If you only sign PDFs and never validate them, EU DSS brings a lot of validation machinery you do not need. A leaner approach with PDFBox + BouncyCastle may be more appropriate.

You need to support PDF features beyond signing: EU DSS is focused on digital signatures. It does not help with form filling, content editing, font embedding, or other PDF operations. You will still need PDFBox or another PDF library for those.

section.title: The Hybrid Approach
section.simpara: In practice, many projects use a hybrid approach:

listitem.simpara: EU DSS for validation: Use SignedDocumentValidator to validate incoming signed PDFs. This gives you correct ETSI EN 319 102-1 validation with EUTL support.

PDFBox + BouncyCastle for signing: Use PDFBox for PDF manipulation and BouncyCastle for CMS construction. This gives you full control over the signing process while still producing standard-compliant signatures.

EU DSS for extension: Use PAdESService.extendDocument() to upgrade signatures from B-B/B-T to B-LTA. This is complex to implement manually (DSS dictionary construction, archival timestamp embedding) and EU DSS does it reliably.

section.simpara: This hybrid model gives you the best of both worlds: full control where you need it, and battle-tested reference code where correctness is paramount.

tip.simpara: If you adopt the hybrid approach, be mindful of PDFBox version alignment. EU DSS uses a specific PDFBox version internally. If your direct PDFBox dependency uses a different version, classloader conflicts can cause subtle, hard-to-diagnose bugs — wrong method signatures, missing classes, or (worst case) silent data corruption. Always align your PDFBox version with the one in EU DSS’s dependency tree.

section.title: 1. Forgetting to Set the Certificate Chain
section.simpara: Without the certificate chain, the CMS certificates collection will contain only the signer’s certificate. Validators that do not have access to the intermediate certificates (from AIA or a local store) will fail to build the chain.

section.title: 2. Not Configuring a Certificate Verifier
section.simpara: An unconfigured verifier cannot check certificate revocation. EU DSS will still produce a signature, but validation will report INDETERMINATE for revocation checking.

section.title: 3. Underestimating /Contents Size
section.simpara: When EU DSS constructs the PDF signature, it must pre-allocate space for the /Contents hex string. If the allocated space is too small, the CMS signature (with certificates, timestamps, etc.) will not fit. EU DSS handles this internally with reasonable defaults, but if you are building very large certificate chains or using multiple timestamps, you may need to increase the allocation:

section.title: 4. Blocking the EUTL Load on First Request
section.simpara: The EUTL download takes 30-60 seconds. If you trigger it lazily (on the first validation request), your first user will experience a 30-second delay. Always load the EUTL eagerly at application startup:

section.title: 5. Ignoring Validation Sub-Indications
section.simpara: A common mistake is checking only the top-level Indication (TOTAL_PASSED, TOTAL_FAILED, INDETERMINATE) and ignoring the SubIndication. The sub-indication tells you why the validation reached that conclusion:

listitem.simpara: NO_SIGNING_CERTIFICATE_FOUND — the signer’s certificate is missing
REVOKED — a certificate in the chain has been revoked
EXPIRED — a certificate has expired and there is no proof that signing occurred before expiry
TRY_LATER — revocation data could not be fetched (OCSP/CRL unreachable)
CRYPTO_CONSTRAINTS_FAILURE — weak algorithms detected (e.g., SHA-1)
HASH_FAILURE — the message digest does not match (document has been modified)

section.simpara: Always log the sub-indication alongside the indication for debugging.

listitem.simpara: EU DSS is the European Commission’s open-source reference implementation for digital signature creation, extension, and validation — LGPL 2.1 licensed, actively maintained
The two-phase signing model (ToBeSigned / SignatureValue) cleanly separates CMS construction from the private key operation, enabling local and remote signing through the same API
SignatureTokenConnection abstracts key access: PKCS#12, PKCS#11, MS CAPI, or custom (CSC API) — swap the token, keep the signing flow
EU DSS handles PAdES-B-B through B-LTA creation and the full ETSI EN 319 102-1 validation algorithm, including EUTL lookup for qualified certificate determination
Signature extension (extendDocument) upgrades a signature’s PAdES profile without re-signing — the most practical feature for archival workflows
The Spring Boot starter auto-configures certificate verifiers, TSA sources, and EUTL loading — production-ready with minimal configuration
Validation reports come in three levels: Simple (pass/fail), Detailed (per-check breakdown), Diagnostic (raw collected data)
The EUTL must be loaded at startup and cached on disk — lazy loading causes unacceptable latency, missing EUTL data causes incorrect qualified status determination
Hybrid approach is common in practice: EU DSS for validation and extension, PDFBox + BouncyCastle for signing with full CMS control
Always align PDFBox versions between your direct dependency and EU DSS’s transitive dependency — version mismatches cause classloader conflicts

chapter.title: PDF Signature Attacks: A Complete Technical Analysis

blockquote.attribution: Vladislav Mladenov et al. 1 Trillion Dollar Refund
blockquote.simpara: We found 21 out of 22 desktop viewer applications and 5 out of 7 online validation services to be vulnerable to at least one of our attacks.

chapter.simpara: For nearly two decades, the PDF signature ecosystem operated on an assumption that turned out to be wrong: that viewers actually verified what they claimed to verify. Between 2019 and 2021, a research team at Ruhr University Bochum systematically dismantled this assumption. They showed that an attacker could take a validly signed PDF, modify it in specific ways, and present the result to any major PDF viewer — Adobe Acrobat, Foxit Reader, Nitro, LibreOffice, Okular, dozens of others — and the viewer would display a green checkmark and the text "Signature is valid."
The signed content had been altered. The viewer said it hadn’t. Every viewer was wrong.

This chapter is a complete technical analysis of every major attack against PDF digital signatures — from the Bochum research program to earlier CVEs, from the theoretical mechanics to working code that demonstrates each attack class. The purpose is not to enable attacks but to make them impossible to misunderstand. If you build or maintain a PDF signature validation system, you need to understand exactly how these attacks work so that your code cannot be fooled by them. If you are a security researcher, this chapter gives you the foundation to find the next generation of PDF signature vulnerabilities — because the Bochum team found the obvious ones, but the attack surface is far from fully explored.

section.title: The Researchers
section.simpara: The PDF signature attacks of 2019–2021 were the work of the Chair of Network and Data Security (Lehrstuhl für Netz- und Datensicherheit) at Ruhr University Bochum, Germany, led by Prof. Dr. Jörg Schwenk.
The core research team:

listitem.simpara: Vladislav Mladenov — PhD researcher, co-first author of the CCS 2019 paper, led the implementation and testing of the USF, ISA, and SWA attack variants
Christian Mainka — post-doctoral researcher, co-first author of both the CCS 2019 and NDSS 2021 papers, designed the Shadow Attack taxonomy and coordinated responsible disclosure
Karsten Meyer zu Selhausen — researcher and co-founder of Hackmanit GmbH, the security consultancy that spun out of the Bochum group; contributed to attack implementation and vendor coordination
Martin Grothe — researcher, contributed to the CCS 2019 paper’s systematic evaluation across 22 viewers
Simon Rohlmann — researcher, co-author of the NDSS 2021 Shadow Attacks paper, developed the Hide-and-Replace variant
Prof. Dr. Jörg Schwenk — senior author on both papers, provided the research direction and the formal security model for PDF signatures

section.simpara: The team maintained a coordinated disclosure website at pdf-insecurity.org, where they published proof-of-concept PDFs, detailed attack descriptions, and a viewer-by-viewer vulnerability matrix. Hackmanit GmbH later developed PDF Detector, a tool for checking PDFs against known attack patterns.
The two landmark papers:

listitem.simpara: "1 Trillion Dollar Refund: How To Spoof PDF Signatures" — presented at ACM Conference on Computer and Communications Security (CCS), November 2019, London. This paper introduced three attack classes (USF, ISA, SWA) and tested them against 22 desktop viewers and 7 online validation services.

"Shadow Attacks: Hiding and Replacing Content in Signed PDFs" — presented at Network and Distributed Systems Security Symposium (NDSS), February 2021 (virtual). This paper introduced a fundamentally different attack model where malicious content is prepared before the document is signed.

section.simpara: Both conferences are top-tier academic venues in computer security. The CCS 2019 paper won a Distinguished Paper Award — a recognition given to only a handful of papers at each conference.

section.title: Before Bochum: The State of PDF Signature Validation
section.simpara: To understand why the Bochum attacks worked, you need to understand what PDF viewers were doing when they claimed to "validate" a signature.
A correct signature validation must verify at least the following:

listitem.simpara: Structural integrity: The byte range specified in the /ByteRange array must cover the entire file except the gap reserved for the /Contents value. There must be no unaccounted bytes before, between, or after the byte ranges.

Cryptographic verification: The CMS SignedData structure in /Contents must contain a valid signature over the hash of the byte range data. The digest algorithm, signing algorithm, and signed attributes must all be consistent.

Certificate chain validation: The signer’s certificate must chain to a trusted root. The chain must be valid at the time of signing (or at the time of a trusted timestamp). Revocation status must be checked.

Modification detection: If additional data has been appended to the file after the signature (an incremental update), the viewer must determine whether those changes are permissible under the signature’s /TransformMethod (e.g., DocMDP permission level) or whether they indicate tampering.

section.simpara: What the Bochum team discovered was that most viewers implemented step 3 reasonably well (they checked certificate chains) but failed catastrophically at steps 1, 2, and 4. Some viewers didn’t check the cryptographic signature value at all. Some didn’t verify that the byte range covered the entire file. Some allowed arbitrary changes after signing without any warning.

The result: a perfectly executed certificate chain validation was protecting…​ nothing. The viewers were carefully verifying who signed the document, while completely ignoring whether the document had been changed since they signed it.

section.title: The Concept
section.simpara: Universal Signature Forgery is the simplest and most devastating of the three original attack classes. The premise is almost embarrassingly straightforward: what happens if the CMS signature value in /Contents is wrong?
A correct implementation should reject the signature immediately — if the cryptographic value doesn’t verify, nothing else matters. What the Bochum team found was that some viewers never checked.

In the most extreme cases, the /Contents value could be empty (all zeros), filled with random bytes, or contain a valid CMS structure that signed completely different data — and the viewer would still display "Signature is valid" with a green checkmark.

section.title: How It Works
section.simpara: The attack requires no special tools and no knowledge of the signer’s private key. The attacker takes a legitimately signed PDF, modifies the document content (changes text, replaces pages, adds content), and re-calculates any internal structures (cross-reference table, object offsets) needed to keep the file syntactically valid. The /Contents value — the CMS SignedData blob — is left unchanged, even though it now contains a signature over the original document bytes, not the modified ones.
In a correct implementation, the viewer would:

listitem.simpara: Read the /ByteRange to determine which bytes are signed
Hash those bytes using the digest algorithm specified in the CMS structure
Extract the signature value from the CMS SignerInfo
Verify the signature over the hash using the signer’s public key
If verification fails → reject the signature

section.simpara: Vulnerable viewers skipped step 4, step 3, or in some cases both. They parsed the CMS structure to extract the signer’s certificate (for display purposes), checked that the certificate chained to a trust anchor, and then displayed the result — without ever verifying that the signature value was mathematically valid for the current document content.

section.title: The Variants
section.simpara: The Bochum team identified three USF variants with increasing severity:

formalpara.para: The SignerInfo.signature value (the actual RSASSA-PKCS1-v1_5 or ECDSA bytes) is replaced with random data. A correct implementation rejects this immediately during RSA/ECDSA verification. Vulnerable viewers never reached that step.

The outer CMS ContentInfo wrapping is modified so the SignedData structure is structurally invalid — wrong OIDs, missing fields, or inconsistent version numbers. A correct implementation rejects this during CMS parsing. Vulnerable viewers used lenient parsing that silently ignored structural errors.

The entire /Contents hex string is replaced — either with zeros, with a truncated CMS structure, or with a CMS structure copied from a different document. This tests whether the viewer checks the relationship between the CMS content and the actual document bytes at all.

section.title: Demonstration: Detecting USF Vulnerability
section.simpara: The following code demonstrates how to detect whether a signed PDF’s CMS signature actually validates cryptographically — the check that USF-vulnerable viewers failed to perform. This is the defense, not the attack: if you are building a validator, this is the minimum you must implement.

callout.para: Extracts the bytes covered by /ByteRange — this is the data that was supposedly signed
Extracts the raw CMS SignedData blob from the /Contents hex string
Creates a CMS structure with the signed content as the detached data — BouncyCastle will use this for digest comparison
If no certificate matches the SignerInfo.sid, the CMS structure is malformed — reject immediately

This is the critical line. signer.verify() performs the actual RSA/ECDSA verification: it computes the digest over the signed content, verifies the signed attributes, and checks the signature value against the signer’s public key. USF-vulnerable viewers never called the equivalent of this method.

warning.simpara: If your signature validation code does not include a step equivalent to signer.verify() — an actual cryptographic verification of the signature value against the document bytes — your code is vulnerable to Universal Signature Forgery. This is non-negotiable. It must happen before you display any "valid" indicator to the user.

section.title: Constructing a USF Attack (Proof of Concept)
section.simpara: To understand USF fully, here is how an attacker constructs one. This is not production exploit code — it is a minimal demonstration of the mechanics that every PDF signature validator must defend against.

callout.para: Modifying the document structure after it was signed — adding a page, changing text, anything
The attacker can insert any content they want

A full save() (not saveIncremental()) rewrites the entire file, changing byte offsets everywhere. The original /ByteRange and /Contents are preserved structurally, but the bytes they reference have changed. The CMS signature now signs data that no longer exists at those byte positions.

important.simpara: This code produces a PDF that any correct validator will reject. The point is that in 2019, most validators were not correct. The digest computed over the new byte range content will not match the digest inside the CMS SignerInfo.digestAlgorithm — the very first check in proper CMS verification detects this. USF worked because viewers displayed the signer’s certificate information without performing this check.

section.title: The Concept
section.simpara: The Incremental Saving Attack is more subtle than USF and exploits a feature that is fundamental to how PDF signatures work: incremental updates.
When a PDF is signed, the signing process:

listitem.simpara: Appends the signature dictionary and any new objects to the end of the file
