Appends a new cross-reference section pointing to the new/modified objects
Appends a new trailer
The /ByteRange in the signature covers everything from the beginning of the file through the end of this new revision — with a gap for the /Contents value

section.simpara: The critical insight is that PDF is designed to allow additional incremental updates after signing. This is intentional — you need to be able to add a second signature to an already-signed document, or fill in form fields on a signed form. ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1 explicitly allows certain modifications after signing, depending on the signature’s /TransformMethod and permission level.
The question is: does the viewer correctly distinguish between permissible incremental updates (adding another signature) and impermissible ones (changing the page content)?
The Bochum team found that most viewers did not.

section.title: How It Works
section.simpara: The attacker takes a legitimately signed PDF and appends a new incremental update — exactly the way PDFBox’s saveIncremental() method does. But instead of adding a second signature or filling in a form field, the attacker appends objects that change the visible content: modified page content streams, replaced images, rewritten text, or entirely new pages.

The original signature remains intact — its byte range still covers the original revision, and its CMS signature is still mathematically valid over those bytes. The appended revision contains the modifications, complete with a new cross-reference section and trailer that redirect the viewer to the modified objects.
A correct validator must:

listitem.simpara: Verify the signature is cryptographically valid (steps from USF defense)
Determine the document’s revision at the time of signing (the byte range endpoint)
Compare the current document state to the signed revision
If there are changes beyond the signed revision, evaluate whether those changes are permissible under the signature’s DocMDP permissions
If impermissible changes are detected, display a clear warning: "The document has been modified after signing"

section.simpara: Vulnerable viewers either:

listitem.simpara: Did not compare the current document state to the signed revision at all
Compared but accepted all incremental updates as permissible
Only checked whether the appended data was "syntactically valid PDF" rather than whether it changed the visible content

title.emphasis: ISA Variant 1: Content Replacement via Incremental Update

formalpara.para: The attacker appends a new revision that redefines existing page content stream objects. The cross-reference in the new revision points to new objects at the end of the file that replace the original content. The viewer renders the new content; the signature covers the old content.

The attacker appends a new revision that modifies the page tree — adding new pages, removing existing pages, or reordering them. The /Pages dictionary in the new revision points to a different set of page objects.

title.emphasis: ISA Variant 3: Annotation and Form Field Manipulation

formalpara.para: The attacker appends annotations or modifies form field values. This variant is particularly insidious because some annotation changes are legitimately allowed after signing (e.g., adding signature fields for subsequent signers). The boundary between "allowed" and "not allowed" is defined by the DocMDP permission level, and most viewers did not enforce it correctly.

section.title: Demonstration: ISA Attack Mechanics

callout.para: AppendMode.OVERWRITE replaces the existing content stream. PDFBox tracks this as a modified object, which will be written in the incremental update
The attacker-controlled content — can be anything

saveIncremental() appends to the existing file bytes without modifying them. The original signature’s /ByteRange and /Contents remain at their original byte positions. The new revision simply adds new objects and a new cross-reference section after the original %%EOF

section.title: Detecting ISA: Revision Comparison
section.simpara: The defense against ISA requires comparing the document as rendered from the signed revision to the document as rendered from the current (potentially modified) revision.

callout.para: /ByteRange is an array of four integers: [offset1, length1, offset2, length2]. The two ranges cover everything before and after the /Contents gap
The signed content ends at the start of range 2 plus its length — this is the last byte the signature covers
If the file extends beyond this point, there is appended content that the signature does not cover. This is the ISA detection point
Extracting the signed revision as a standalone PDF allows you to render it separately and compare to the current document state

tip.simpara: A naive "does the file extend beyond the byte range?" check catches obvious ISA attacks, but it is not sufficient. A legitimate second signature also extends the file. The real check requires parsing the appended incremental update and determining what changed — was it another signature (allowed), a form fill (allowed under DocMDP P=3), or a content modification (never allowed)? This is the hard part of ISA defense, and it is where most viewers failed in 2019.

section.title: The Concept
section.simpara: The Signature Wrapping Attack is the most technically sophisticated of the three original attack classes. Where USF exploits missing cryptographic verification and ISA exploits missing modification detection, SWA exploits the way viewers locate the signature within the file.

The core insight: the /ByteRange array tells the viewer which bytes are signed, but nothing prevents an attacker from moving the signed bytes to a different location in the file — as long as the byte values at the /ByteRange offsets still produce the same hash.

Actually, SWA is even simpler than that. The attacker doesn’t need the same hash. The attacker modifies the /ByteRange array itself to point to a different region of the file — one that contains attacker-controlled content — while keeping the original CMS signature that was computed over the original content.

Wait — if the /ByteRange is modified, wouldn’t the signature be invalid? Yes, if the viewer reads the /ByteRange from the signed revision. But many viewers read the /ByteRange from the current cross-reference table, which may have been modified by an incremental update. The attacker appends a new revision that:

listitem.simpara: Redefines the signature dictionary object to point to a new /ByteRange
Places attacker-controlled content at the byte positions specified by the new /ByteRange
Ensures the CMS signature (in the original /Contents) is structurally present

section.simpara: The viewer follows the new cross-reference to the redefined signature dictionary, reads the attacker’s /ByteRange, hashes the attacker’s content, and — if it doesn’t actually verify the hash against the CMS signature (shades of USF) — displays "valid."

In some variants, the viewer does check the hash, but the attacker has been clever about which bytes are at the new /ByteRange positions, potentially including a copy of the original signed content or exploiting hash-algorithm weaknesses.

section.title: How It Works
section.screen:
Original file layout: [header ... objects ... page content ... ] [signature dict: /ByteRange [0 840 3000 1200] /Contents <CMS...>] [xref ... trailer ... %%EOF]
Bytes 0-839 and 3000-4199 are signed. Bytes 840-2999 are the /Contents gap (CMS signature value). The CMS signature is valid over hash(bytes[0..839] + bytes[3000..4199]).
Attacker appends an incremental update: [... %%EOF] <-- original file ends here [new object: modified signature dict <-- attacker redefines the sig dict /ByteRange [0 840 5000 1200] <-- points to NEW byte positions /Contents <original CMS bytes>] <-- keeps the original CMS value [attacker content at byte offset 5000] <-- whatever the attacker wants [new xref pointing to new sig dict] [new trailer ... %%EOF]
section.simpara: The viewer follows the new xref, finds the redefined signature dictionary, reads /ByteRange [0 840 5000 1200], and hashes bytes at those positions. If it checks the CMS signature against this hash, it will fail (because the CMS signed the original byte range content). But if the viewer is also vulnerable to USF (skips the hash verification), the attack succeeds.
SWA is often combined with elements of ISA and USF. The Bochum team found that many real-world exploits used a combination of attack techniques.

section.title: Demonstration: Detecting SWA

callout.para: The first byte range must start at offset 0. If it doesn’t, there are unsigned bytes at the beginning of the file — a clear SWA indicator
The gap between the two ranges must be positive and should correspond to the /Contents hex string. A zero or negative gap indicates structural manipulation
The gap should contain the hex-encoded CMS value. Checking that it starts with < and ends with > is a basic sanity check

caution.simpara: SWA defense requires more than just checking /ByteRange arithmetic. A sophisticated attacker can construct a valid-looking byte range that passes arithmetic checks but points to carefully crafted content. The complete defense is: (a) verify /ByteRange covers the file correctly, (b) verify the CMS signature cryptographically (USF defense), and (c) verify that the /ByteRange values come from the signed revision’s cross-reference, not from an appended revision.

section.title: A Fundamentally Different Threat Model
section.simpara: The three attacks from CCS 2019 (USF, ISA, SWA) all shared a common assumption: the attacker modifies a PDF after it has been legitimately signed. The signed document is genuine; the attacker’s manipulation comes afterward.

Shadow Attacks, published at NDSS 2021, inverted the model. The attacker prepares the PDF before it is signed, embedding hidden malicious content that the signer cannot see during the signing process. The signer reviews the document, sees nothing suspicious, and signs it. The signed PDF is then sent to the recipient, who opens it — and sees different content from what the signer saw.
The signature is cryptographically valid. The signer genuinely signed those exact bytes. The bytes just don’t mean what the signer thought they meant.

This is not a cryptographic failure. It is a semantic failure: the gap between what a human sees when they review a document and what the bytes actually encode. And it is far harder to defend against than USF, ISA, or SWA.

section.title: Shadow Attack Variant 1: Hide
section.simpara: The attacker creates a PDF with two layers of content — one visible, one hidden. The hidden content is placed behind the visible content using PDF features like:

listitem.simpara: Overlapping objects: A white rectangle drawn on top of text, hiding it. The text is in the content stream; the rectangle covers it. After signing, the attacker removes the rectangle via an incremental update, revealing the hidden text.

Optional Content Groups (OCG): PDF’s layer mechanism. Content can be tagged with /OC (Optional Content) markers and made visible or invisible via the /OCProperties dictionary. The attacker sets a layer to invisible before signing, then sets it to visible after signing.
Referenced XObjects: Content referenced as Form XObject but not actually rendered on any page. After signing, an incremental update adds the XObject to a page’s content stream.

section.simpara: The key constraint: all of the hidden content must be present in the file at the time of signing, so that it falls within the signed byte range. The attacker only changes the visibility via an incremental update after signing — which some viewers allowed without warning.

section.title: Shadow Attack Variant 2: Replace
section.simpara: The attacker creates a PDF where the visible content can be replaced by different visible content without modifying any objects in the signed byte range. This exploits interactive form features:

listitem.simpara: A text field with a default value ("Contract for $1,000") and a different actual value ("Contract for $1,000,000") — the default is displayed initially, but changing which value is rendered requires only an annotation flag change
Font references that can be swapped — the same Unicode code points render as different glyphs depending on which font is active
Images referenced by name from a page’s Resources dictionary — changing which image a name resolves to requires only modifying the Resources dictionary

section.simpara: The Replace variant is particularly dangerous because it exploits features that are designed to change document appearance (form filling, annotation updates) — the viewer must distinguish between a legitimate form fill and an attacker swapping content.

section.title: Shadow Attack Variant 3: Hide-and-Replace
section.simpara: The most powerful variant combines both techniques. The attacker creates a PDF that:

listitem.simpara: Contains the content the signer will see (layer A)
Contains the content the recipient will see (layer B), hidden behind layer A
Is structured so that a single incremental update (changing a few bytes in an annotation dictionary or OCG configuration) switches from layer A to layer B

section.simpara: After signing, the attacker appends the minimal incremental update needed to switch layers. If the viewer allows annotation or OCG changes after signing (as many did, because these are often considered "permissible" changes), the attack succeeds.

section.title: Demonstration: Shadow Attack (Hide Variant) Preparation

callout.para: The hidden text is written to the content stream first. In PDF rendering order, later operators paint over earlier ones on the same area
A white filled rectangle covers the hidden text completely. The signer sees only white space at this position
The visible text is drawn on top of the white rectangle. This is what the signer reads when reviewing the document before signing

caution.simpara: Shadow Attacks are fundamentally harder to prevent than USF, ISA, or SWA because the malicious content is part of the signed data. The signature is cryptographically valid over the exact bytes the signer intended to sign. The defense must go beyond byte-level verification into semantic analysis: does the document contain hidden content? Are there overlapping objects? Are there unused Form XObjects? Are there Optional Content Groups with suspicious visibility settings? This is an open research problem with no complete solution.

section.title: The Scope of Shadow Attack Vulnerability
section.simpara: The Bochum team tested 29 PDF viewers against the Shadow Attack variants. The results were comprehensive:

table.title: Shadow Attack test results across PDF viewers (NDSS 2021)

note.simpara: This table is a representative subset. The full results covering 29 viewers are available in the NDSS 2021 paper and at pdf-insecurity.org. Chrome’s built-in PDF viewer does not validate signatures at all (it displays them but does not indicate validity), so Shadow Attacks are not applicable in the traditional sense — but the user also receives no warning that the document has been signed and potentially tampered with.

section.title: Pre-Bochum Vulnerabilities: Earlier PDF Signature Issues
section.simpara: The Bochum research was the most systematic analysis of PDF signature security, but it was not the first time vulnerabilities were discovered. Several earlier issues laid the groundwork — and reveal that the category of bug (failing to verify what you claim to verify) has been a persistent pattern in PDF signature implementations.

section.title: CVE-2018-16042 — Adobe Acrobat Signature Bypass (2018)
section.simpara: In 2018, Adobe patched CVE-2018-16042 in Acrobat DC and Acrobat Reader DC (versions before 2019.008.20081). The vulnerability allowed a crafted PDF to display a valid signature indicator even when the document had been modified.

The technical details were not fully disclosed (Adobe’s advisory was characteristically brief), but the vulnerability class — signature validation bypass via document manipulation — is the same category as the ISA attacks that Bochum would systematize a year later. This CVE was one of several signals that the PDF signature validation code in major viewers had not been subject to rigorous adversarial testing.

Affected versions: Acrobat DC/Reader DC before 2019.008.20081 Impact: Signature validation bypass — modified documents displayed as validly signed Fix: Updated signature validation logic in the January 2019 security update

section.title: CVE-2018-4993 — Adobe Acrobat NTLM Credential Theft (2018)
section.simpara: While not strictly a signature bypass, CVE-2018-4993 demonstrated a related risk: a crafted PDF could trigger NTLM authentication requests to an attacker-controlled server when opened in Adobe Acrobat. Combined with a forged signature (via any of the bypass techniques), this creates a compound attack: the victim opens a "validly signed" PDF, and their Windows credentials are silently exfiltrated.

Affected versions: Acrobat DC/Reader DC before 2018.011.20040 Impact: NTLM hash theft via SMB relay when opening a crafted PDF Fix: Restricted external resource loading in the May 2018 security update

section.title: SHAttered and the SHA-1 Deprecation (2017)
section.simpara: In February 2017, researchers from Google and CWI Amsterdam published SHAttered — the first practical collision attack against SHA-1. They produced two different PDF documents with the same SHA-1 hash, proving that SHA-1 could no longer be relied upon for collision resistance.

For PDF signatures, this had immediate implications. The adbe.pkcs7.sha1 SubFilter — which embeds a SHA-1 hash of the entire document as the CMS content data — was now theoretically vulnerable to a collision attack: an attacker could prepare two documents with the same SHA-1 hash, have the victim sign one, and then substitute the other.

The practical impact was limited (the collision required approximately 263 computations and the two colliding documents had to be carefully constructed), but the theoretical break was sufficient to trigger industry-wide deprecation:

listitem.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 deprecated adbe.pkcs7.sha1 entirely
Adobe Acrobat began showing warnings for SHA-1 signed documents
The CA/Browser Forum required SHA-256 as the minimum for all new certificates
PAdES profiles already mandated SHA-256 as the minimum digest algorithm

entry.simpara: Theoretical weakness in SHA-1 published (Wang et al.)
NIST deprecated SHA-1 for digital signatures in federal use
Microsoft announced SHA-1 certificate sunset for 2016
SHAttered: first practical SHA-1 collision (Google/CWI). Two PDFs with identical SHA-1 hashes publicly demonstrated
SHA-1 collisions reduced to 263 operations (Leurent and Peyrin, "SHA-1 is a Shambles"), making chosen-prefix collisions feasible

warning.simpara: If your code still generates signatures using SHA-1 (either via adbe.pkcs7.sha1 or using SHA-1 as the digestAlgorithm in the CMS SignerInfo), stop immediately. SHA-1 is broken. Use SHA-256 or SHA-384. There is no legitimate reason to use SHA-1 for any new signature created after 2017.

section.title: The Comodo Registration Authority Breach (2011)
section.simpara: In March 2011 — the same month as DigiNotar, though discovered slightly earlier — an attacker compromised a registration authority (RA) affiliated with Comodo, one of the world’s largest certificate authorities at the time.

The attacker used the compromised RA account to issue nine fraudulent SSL certificates for high-profile domains: login.live.com, mail.google.com, www.google.com, login.yahoo.com, login.skype.com, addons.mozilla.org, and three others. The certificates were issued by Comodo’s legitimate intermediate CA, meaning they chained correctly to a trusted root.

The breach was discovered quickly because the attacker tested one of the certificates against a live server, and certificate pinning in Google Chrome detected the mismatch. Without Chrome’s certificate pinning, the fraudulent certificates could have been used for undetectable man-in-the-middle attacks.

For PDF signatures: The Comodo breach demonstrated that even without compromising a root CA’s private key, an attacker could obtain legitimate-looking certificates through a registration authority compromise — a weaker link in the chain. A document signed with one of these fraudulent certificates would have validated correctly in any PDF viewer that trusted Comodo’s root. The signer would appear to be "Google" or "Microsoft" or "Yahoo" — entities that obviously did not sign the document.
The incident led to:

listitem.simpara: Mandatory two-factor authentication for all RA accounts at major CAs
Domain control validation (DCV) requirements tightened by the CA/Browser Forum
Increased scrutiny of the RA model, eventually leading several CAs to eliminate external RAs entirely

section.title: The Symantec Distrust (2017–2018)
section.simpara: Between 2015 and 2017, Google’s Chrome security team and the CA/Browser Forum discovered that Symantec (which at that time owned the Thawte, GeoTrust, VeriSign, and RapidSSL certificate brands) had mis-issued thousands of certificates — including certificates for domains whose owners had not authorized them.
The problems were systemic:

listitem.simpara: Symantec had delegated certificate issuance to third-party partners (RAs) without adequate oversight
Test certificates for real domains were issued to unauthorized parties
Audit logs were incomplete or missing
Symantec initially denied the scope of the problem, then acknowledged it in stages

section.simpara: Google announced a graduated distrust of all Symantec-issued certificates:

listitem.simpara: Chrome 66 (April 2018): distrusted certificates issued before June 1, 2016
Chrome 70 (October 2018): distrusted all remaining Symantec certificates

section.simpara: Mozilla Firefox and Apple Safari followed with their own distrust timelines. Symantec sold its CA business to DigiCert, which re-issued certificates for affected customers under DigiCert’s infrastructure.

For PDF signatures: Symantec was a major CA in the AATL (Adobe Approved Trust List). Documents signed with Symantec-issued certificates before the distrust cutoff dates would validate correctly when signed, but might show warnings or failures in PDF viewers that updated their trust stores to reflect the browser distrust. This created a time-dependent validation problem: a PDF signed with a Symantec certificate in 2015 was "valid" when signed, "suspect" in 2018, and — depending on the viewer’s trust configuration — might show as "invalid" or "valid with warnings" today.

This is exactly the problem that PAdES-B-LTA (long-term validation with archival timestamps) was designed to solve: by embedding the full certificate chain and revocation data at the time of signing, and then protecting it with periodic archival timestamps, the signature’s validity at the time of signing can be verified even after the issuing CA is no longer trusted.

section.title: Exploits in the Wild
section.simpara: A natural question: were any of these attacks exploited before or after the Bochum disclosures?
The honest answer is: we don’t know for certain.

PDF signatures are used in financial contracts, government documents, legal filings, tax returns, insurance claims, and medical records. If an attacker successfully forged a signature on a financial contract, the victim might not discover it until a dispute arises — and even then, the forensic analysis might not identify the specific attack technique.
What we do know:

listitem.simpara: The "1 Trillion Dollar Refund" title was not hyperbole. The paper’s title referenced the fact that tax authorities in multiple countries accept signed PDFs as official submissions. The German tax system (ELSTER), for example, processes millions of electronically signed documents per year. If USF or ISA could forge signatures on tax submissions, the financial exposure was enormous. The Bochum team coordinated with CERT-Bund (Germany’s national CERT) before publication.

Adobe’s response was unusually fast. Adobe issued CVE-2020-9592 and CVE-2020-9596 and released patches within weeks of the responsible disclosure. For a company that typically operates on monthly security update cycles, the accelerated timeline suggests Adobe assessed the risk as immediate and severe — consistent with either active exploitation or intelligence indicating exploitation was imminent.

The EU Digital Signature Service (DSS) was vulnerable. The EU’s own reference implementation for signature validation — the tool member states use to validate eIDAS-compliant signatures — was vulnerable to ISA attacks. If the EU’s own validator could be fooled, signatures submitted to government services across 27 member states could potentially have been forged without detection.

Commercial signing services had no detection capability. Before the Bochum research, no commercial PDF signing or validation service tested for these attack classes. There were no automated tools to detect ISA, SWA, or Shadow Attack patterns in submitted PDFs. Any exploitation that occurred before 2019 would have been invisible to the existing security infrastructure.

section.title: Post-Disclosure Exploitation Risk
section.simpara: After the papers were published and patches released, the risk shifted:

listitem.simpara: Unpatched viewers remain vulnerable. Not all users update their PDF viewers. Enterprise environments, government agencies with slow update cycles, and users in regions with limited internet access may still be running vulnerable versions of Acrobat, Foxit, or other viewers.
The attack techniques are public. The Bochum team published detailed descriptions and proof-of-concept PDFs. Any attacker with basic PDF knowledge can reproduce the attacks.

Shadow Attacks remain partially unresolved. While most viewers now detect the Hide and Replace variants, the fundamental problem — that PDF semantics allow content to be visually hidden — is inherent to the format. New variants of Shadow Attacks are likely still discoverable.

section.title: Adobe’s Response
section.simpara: Adobe’s patches for CVE-2020-9592 and CVE-2020-9596 (released in the May 2020 Acrobat security update, version 2020.006.20042) implemented:

listitem.simpara: Cryptographic signature verification — closing the USF vulnerability by ensuring the CMS signature value is always verified against the document hash
Byte range coverage validation — checking that the /ByteRange covers the entire file from offset 0 to the file’s end (minus the /Contents gap), closing the SWA vulnerability
Incremental update analysis — warning users when an incremental update modifies content beyond what the signature’s permission level allows, closing the ISA vulnerability
Shadow content detection — partial detection of Hidden content patterns (overlapping objects, OCGs with suspicious visibility settings)

section.simpara: Acrobat’s signature panel now displays more granular information:

listitem.simpara: "Signed and all signatures are valid" — no modifications detected
"Signed but the document has been modified since signing" — incremental updates detected, with details about what changed
"At least one signature has problems" — cryptographic or structural verification failed
"The document has been altered or corrupted since the signature was applied" — clear indication of tampering

section.title: Foxit’s Response
section.simpara: Foxit Reader patched its signature validation in version 10.1 (released October 2020), implementing similar checks: CMS verification, byte range validation, and incremental update analysis. Foxit was notably slower than Adobe in responding — roughly five months after Adobe’s patch.

section.title: LibreOffice’s Response
section.simpara: LibreOffice (which uses the NSS library for cryptographic operations) patched its PDF signature validation in version 7.0. The fix was more complex because LibreOffice’s PDF import/export pipeline differs significantly from dedicated PDF viewers — it converts PDF to an internal format for editing, which complicates byte-level signature verification.

section.title: The Broader Industry
section.simpara: By mid-2021, most actively maintained PDF viewers had released patches addressing the three original attack classes (USF, ISA, SWA). Shadow Attack mitigations were slower to arrive and remain incomplete in some viewers as of 2026.
The Bochum research also influenced the standards bodies:

listitem.simpara: ISO TC 171 (the committee responsible for ISO 32000-2:2020) incorporated the attack findings into the errata and clarifications for the second edition of PDF 2.0
ETSI updated the PAdES standard (EN 319 142-1 V1.2.1, published 2022) to include explicit requirements for byte range coverage verification and incremental update validation
The EU Digital Signature Service (DSS) — the open-source reference validator maintained by the European Commission — was patched and now includes specific test cases for each attack class

section.title: Building a Robust Validator: Lessons from the Attacks
section.simpara: Every attack in this chapter exploits a specific gap between what a viewer should check and what it actually checks. Here is the complete checklist, informed by every vulnerability discussed above.

entry.simpara: Parse the CMS SignedData structure and verify it is well-formed (correct OIDs, consistent version numbers, all required fields present)
Verify the SignerInfo.signature value cryptographically against the signer’s public key and the computed digest
Verify the message digest in the signed attributes matches the hash of the byte range content
Verify /ByteRange gap contains the /Contents hex string
Verify /ByteRange covers the entire file (for the signed revision) — no unaccounted bytes before, between, or after the ranges
If the file extends beyond the signed byte range, parse the incremental update and determine what changed

Verify that any changes in the incremental update are permissible under the signature’s DocMDP permission level (P=1: no changes; P=2: form fills + signatures; P=3: form fills + signatures + annotations)
Verify the certificate chain from the signer’s certificate to a trusted root, checking revocation (OCSP/CRL) and validity dates
If a timestamp is present, verify the TSA certificate chain independently and check that the timestamp covers the correct data
Check for hidden content patterns: overlapping objects, Optional Content Groups with invisible layers, unreferenced Form XObjects, form fields with discrepant default/actual values
Ensure the digest algorithm is SHA-256 or stronger — reject SHA-1

tip.simpara: Checks 1–6 are non-negotiable — any validator that skips them is vulnerable to at least one of USF, ISA, or SWA. Checks 7–10 are necessary for real-world security but require more complex implementation. Checks 11–12 are defense-in-depth against attacks that remain partially unresolved.

important.simpara: No checklist is complete. The Bochum team found these attack classes by thinking adversarially about the gap between what the spec requires and what implementations do. There will be more attacks. The defense is not just implementing these twelve checks — it is building a culture of adversarial testing into your validation code.

section.title: A Note on Responsible Disclosure
section.simpara: The Bochum team followed responsible disclosure for both papers. They notified affected vendors months before publication and provided proof-of-concept PDFs for testing.
For the CCS 2019 paper, the disclosure timeline was approximately:

listitem.simpara: March–April 2019: Initial vendor notifications
May–August 2019: Back-and-forth with vendors; some requested extended timelines; some did not respond
November 2019: Paper presented at CCS; public disclosure
May 2020: Adobe patches released (CVE-2020-9592, CVE-2020-9596)
October 2020: Foxit patches released

section.simpara: The six-month gap between public disclosure and Adobe’s patch — and the eleven-month gap before Foxit’s patch — is notable. The Bochum team publicly criticized the slow response, particularly from viewers that took more than six months to patch after detailed proof-of-concept material was shared privately.
For the NDSS 2021 paper (Shadow Attacks), the disclosure process was similar but vendors responded faster, having already been through the CCS 2019 cycle.

The research materials — including proof-of-concept PDFs, the vulnerability matrix, and detailed attack descriptions — remain available at pdf-insecurity.org. The Hackmanit GmbH PDF Detector tool (available as a web service and standalone library) can check PDFs for known attack patterns.

section.title: Summary
section.simpara: PDF signature security before 2019 was, to put it bluntly, an illusion. The cryptographic primitives were sound — RSA, ECDSA, SHA-256, CMS are all well-designed and well-analyzed. The failure was entirely in how PDF viewers used those primitives. They verified certificates meticulously and signatures not at all.
The Bochum research (CCS 2019, NDSS 2021) exposed four attack classes:

listitem.simpara: USF: The viewer doesn’t check the cryptographic signature value — forge anything
ISA: The viewer doesn’t check for modifications after signing — append anything
SWA: The viewer doesn’t validate the byte range — redirect it to anything
Shadow Attacks: The viewer doesn’t analyze what the signed content means — hide anything

section.simpara: Every major PDF viewer was vulnerable. The patches took months to years. Some Shadow Attack variants remain partially unresolved.

Earlier events — the DigiNotar breach (2011), the Comodo RA compromise (2011), the Symantec distrust (2017–2018), and the SHAttered SHA-1 collision (2017) — had already weakened the trust infrastructure underneath PDF signatures. The Bochum attacks showed that even if you trusted the infrastructure, the validators themselves couldn’t be trusted.

The lesson for implementers is clear: verify everything, trust nothing, and test adversarially. The validation checklist in this chapter is the minimum. The next attack class is already being researched.

partintro.simpara: Every PDF digital signature carries an implicit assertion: "I trust the certificate authority that issued this certificate." That assertion is not a mathematical fact — it is an organizational, operational, and political judgment. And like all judgments, it can turn out to be catastrophically wrong.

This chapter chronicles the most consequential certificate authority failures of the past two decades. Not because they are historical curiosities, but because each one contains lessons that directly affect how you should build, validate, and maintain PDF digital signatures today. A CA breach does not just compromise web traffic. It retroactively undermines every document signed with certificates issued by that CA — contracts, tax filings, court submissions, medical records. The signatures do not become invalid in a cryptographic sense; the math still checks out. They become untrustworthy, which in the real world is worse.

chapter.title: Why CA Breaches Matter for PDF Signatures
chapter.simpara: When you sign a PDF, the recipient validates your signature by chaining your signing certificate back to a trusted root. That chain passes through one or more intermediate CAs, each vouching for the next, until it reaches a root certificate that the validator explicitly trusts — either because it is in Adobe’s AATL, in the EU Trusted List, in the operating system’s trust store, or in a custom trust configuration.

The entire chain is only as strong as its weakest link. If any CA in the chain is compromised, an attacker can issue fraudulent certificates — certificates that look legitimate but are controlled by the attacker. Documents signed with those fraudulent certificates will pass validation in any viewer that trusts the compromised CA.

Worse, when a compromised CA is discovered and revoked, the collateral damage extends to every legitimate certificate that CA ever issued. If you signed a contract last year with a certificate from CA-X, and CA-X is discovered to be compromised this year, your signature’s trustworthiness is now in question — not because your key was compromised, but because the entity vouching for your identity can no longer be trusted.

This is the fundamental fragility of the PKI trust model: trust is transitive, delegated, and revocable. You cannot sign a document and walk away confident that the signature will still be trusted in five years, because you cannot predict whether the CA that issued your certificate will still be trusted in five years.

important.simpara: The only mitigation for CA compromise is embedding revocation evidence at signing time. A PAdES-B-LTA signature includes OCSP responses and CRLs captured when the signature was created. If the CA is later revoked, the embedded evidence proves that the certificate was valid at the time of signing — before the breach was discovered. Without LTV data, a validator checking the signature after the CA revocation has no way to distinguish between a legitimate pre-breach signature and a fraudulent one.

chapter.title: DigiNotar (2011): The Breach That Broke a Country

section.title: Background: A Small CA With a Big Responsibility
section.simpara: DigiNotar was a Dutch certificate authority founded in 1998, headquartered in Beverwijk, a small town near Amsterdam. The company was originally an offshoot of the Dutch notarial profession — the name is a portmanteau of "Digital Notary" — and it carved out a niche issuing certificates for Dutch government services, notaries, and businesses.

In 2010, VASCO Data Security International (now OneSpan) acquired DigiNotar for approximately 10 million euros. At the time, DigiNotar was one of the root CAs trusted by every major browser and operating system. Its certificates were embedded in the trust stores of Windows, macOS, Firefox, Chrome, and by extension, in the trust stores used by PDF viewers.

DigiNotar also operated PKIoverheid (PKI for Government) — the Dutch government’s public key infrastructure. PKIoverheid certificates were used across Dutch government digital services, including the DigiD electronic identity system, tax filing, and — critically for our purposes — official government document signing.

section.title: The Attack
section.simpara: In June 2011, an attacker gained access to DigiNotar’s certificate issuance infrastructure. The attack was later attributed to a state-sponsored actor operating from Iran, though the attacker also posted messages claiming to be a lone Iranian hacker named "Comodohacker" — the same handle used in the Comodo breach earlier that year.
The attacker exploited multiple vulnerabilities in DigiNotar’s infrastructure:

listitem.simpara: The CA’s servers ran outdated, unpatched software
Network segmentation between the public-facing servers and the certificate issuance systems was inadequate
The HSMs (Hardware Security Modules) that should have required physical authorization for certificate issuance were configured to allow automated remote issuance
Audit logs were incomplete and inconsistent — DigiNotar did not have reliable records of which certificates had been issued

section.simpara: Once inside, the attacker issued over 531 fraudulent certificates for high-value domains. The confirmed targets included:

table.title: Known fraudulently issued DigiNotar certificates (partial list)

entry.simpara: Gmail, Google Search — primary communication tool for millions

section.title: The Use: Surveillance of Iranian Citizens
section.simpara: The fraudulent certificates were not issued for financial fraud. They were used for mass surveillance.

In July and August 2011, the fraudulent google.com certificate was deployed in man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks targeting Iranian internet users. The Iranian government (or an actor with access to Iranian internet infrastructure) intercepted HTTPS connections to Google services — primarily Gmail — by presenting the fraudulent DigiNotar certificate. Because DigiNotar was a trusted root CA, browsers accepted the certificate without warning.

The victims were Iranian citizens communicating via Gmail during a period of significant political tension. The Arab Spring protests had swept through Tunisia, Egypt, Libya, and Syria. Iran’s Green Movement, which had erupted after the disputed 2009 presidential election, was still active. The surveillance targeted people who thought their communications were private.

The scale of the interception was significant. Fox-IT, the Dutch forensics firm that later investigated the breach, estimated that approximately 300,000 unique IP addresses — almost all Iranian — had been subjected to MITM attacks using the fraudulent Google certificate.

section.title: Discovery: How Certificate Pinning Saved the Day
section.simpara: The attack was discovered on August 28, 2011, by an Iranian user of Google Chrome.

Chrome has a feature called certificate pinning (also known as HPKP, HTTP Public Key Pinning, or in Chrome’s case, a hardcoded pin set). For a small number of high-value domains — including google.com — Chrome maintains a built-in list of expected certificate public keys. If the presented certificate’s public key does not match any of the pinned keys, Chrome refuses the connection and displays a prominent error, even if the certificate otherwise validates correctly against the trust store.

When the Iranian Chrome user attempted to connect to Google, Chrome detected that the presented certificate (signed by DigiNotar) did not match Google’s pinned public keys. Chrome displayed an error. The user reported the error to Google.

note.simpara: Certificate pinning was not designed as a comprehensive defense against CA compromise. It only worked for the handful of domains that Google had pre-configured pins for. If the attacker had only issued certificates for domains that Chrome did not pin — the intelligence agency websites, the Iranian opposition news site, the Yahoo and Skype domains — the attack might have continued undetected for months.

The lesson is humbling: the discovery of one of the most significant CA compromises in internet history was essentially an accident — it depended on one user, in one country, using one specific browser, connecting to one specific domain that happened to have certificate pinning enabled.

section.title: The Response: Death of a CA
section.simpara: Once Google confirmed the fraudulent certificate, events moved rapidly:

listitem.simpara: August 29, 2011: Google published a Chrome update blocking the specific DigiNotar intermediate certificate used to sign the fraudulent Google certificate
August 30, 2011: Mozilla pushed a Firefox update removing DigiNotar’s root from the trust store entirely
September 2, 2011: The Dutch government’s GovCERT (Government Computer Emergency Response Team) issued an advisory acknowledging the breach
September 3, 2011: Google, Microsoft, and Apple all pushed updates removing DigiNotar from their trust stores
September 5, 2011: The Dutch government formally revoked DigiNotar’s authorization to issue PKIoverheid certificates
September 20, 2011: DigiNotar’s parent company VASCO filed for DigiNotar’s bankruptcy in Dutch court

section.simpara: The entire collapse — from discovery to bankruptcy — took less than four weeks.

Fox-IT’s forensic investigation, published in their report "DigiNotar Certificate Authority breach — 'Operation Black Tulip'" (named with Dutch irony), found that the breach was far more extensive than initially reported. The attacker had been inside DigiNotar’s systems since at least June 17, 2011, had compromised multiple CA servers, and had access to issuing infrastructure for both DigiNotar’s commercial certificates and the PKIoverheid government certificates.

section.title: Impact on PDF Signatures
section.simpara: The immediate impact on PDF signatures was severe and irreversible.

Any PDF document signed with a DigiNotar-issued certificate became unverifiable the moment browsers and operating systems removed DigiNotar from their trust stores. Adobe Acrobat, which uses the Windows certificate store (on Windows) or its own AATL (on macOS), stopped trusting DigiNotar certificates. A signed PDF that showed a green checkmark on September 2 showed a warning on September 4.

The documents themselves had not changed. The signatures had not been tampered with. The signing keys had not been compromised (in most cases — the legitimate certificate holders were not the ones attacked). But the trust anchor for those certificates no longer existed.

For Dutch government agencies, this was particularly painful. PKIoverheid certificates were used to sign official government documents, notarial deeds, and legal filings. The Dutch government had to issue emergency replacement certificates through other CAs and re-sign critical documents — a process that took months.

warning.simpara: This is the nightmare scenario for long-lived signed documents. A contract signed in 2010 with a DigiNotar certificate and stored in an archive had its trust chain severed in 2011. If the signature did not include LTV data (OCSP responses and CRLs embedded at signing time, as in PAdES-B-LTA), there was no way to prove after the fact that the certificate was valid when the signature was created. The document was, for all practical purposes, unsigned.
This is why PAdES-B-LTA exists. This is why you embed revocation data. This is not a theoretical concern — it happened.

section.title: Lessons from DigiNotar
section.simpara: The DigiNotar breach taught the PKI community several painful lessons:

listitem.simpara: A single CA compromise can retroactively invalidate thousands of signatures. Trust is not permanent. It is not even stable. It can be revoked at any time, with retrospective effect.

CA security is only as strong as the weakest operational practice. DigiNotar had HSMs, but they were misconfigured. They had audit logs, but they were incomplete. They had network segmentation, but it was insufficient. The technology existed to prevent the breach; the organization failed to deploy it correctly.
Certificate pinning is a band-aid, not a solution. It helped detect this breach, but it does not scale. You cannot pin every domain’s certificate in every application.

Government PKI is not immune. DigiNotar operated critical Dutch government infrastructure. Government CAs are not inherently more trustworthy than commercial ones — they are subject to the same operational failures.

Bankruptcy is the final revocation. When a CA goes bankrupt, its certificates do not gracefully expire. They are abruptly distrusted. There is no migration path, no deprecation period, no gentle sunset. One day the certificates work; the next day they do not.

section.title: The Attack
section.simpara: Just five months before DigiNotar’s collapse, in March 2011, a similar attack targeted Comodo — at the time, one of the world’s largest commercial CAs.

The attacker compromised registration authority (RA) accounts at Comodo’s reseller partners — specifically, the Italian firm GlobalTrust.it and the Argentine firm InstantSSL.it. Registration authorities are entities authorized to perform identity verification on behalf of a CA; they can request certificate issuance without having direct access to the CA’s root key.
Using the compromised RA credentials, the attacker obtained nine fraudulent certificates for high-value domains:

listitem.simpara: login.live.com (Microsoft)
mail.google.com (Google)
www.google.com (Google)
login.yahoo.com (Yahoo, three separate certificates)
login.skype.com (Skype)
addons.mozilla.org (Mozilla)
An additional "global trustee" certificate

section.simpara: The target list was remarkably similar to the DigiNotar targets — because the same attacker was behind both breaches.

section.title: The Response: How Comodo Got It Right
section.simpara: Comodo’s response was dramatically faster and more effective than DigiNotar’s would be five months later.

Comodo detected the fraudulent issuance within hours — their monitoring systems flagged the unusual certificate requests from the compromised RA accounts. The nine certificates were revoked within the same day. Comodo published a detailed incident report, contacted browser vendors, and cooperated fully with the investigation.
The key differences between Comodo’s response and DigiNotar’s later failure:

table.title: Comodo vs DigiNotar incident response comparison

section.simpara: Comodo survived because it detected the breach quickly, responded transparently, and demonstrated that the compromise was limited to reseller accounts — not the root CA infrastructure. The attackers got nine certificates; at DigiNotar, they got the keys to the kingdom.

section.title: The Attacker’s Manifesto
section.simpara: The attacker posted a manifesto online claiming to be a 21-year-old Iranian, acting alone, motivated by anger at Western governments' treatment of Iran. The manifesto, written in broken English, contained enough technical detail about both the Comodo and DigiNotar breaches to be credible. Whether the attacker was truly a lone wolf or a front for state-sponsored activity remains debated. The operational sophistication of the MITM infrastructure deployed against Iranian citizens (requiring cooperation with or access to Iranian ISP infrastructure) suggests state involvement, regardless of who wrote the code.

tip.simpara: From a PDF signing perspective, the Comodo incident reinforces a critical point: your signing certificate’s trustworthiness depends not only on your CA, but on every reseller, registration authority, and partner in the CA’s issuance chain. When selecting a CA for document signing certificates, ask about their RA model. How many resellers can request certificate issuance? What monitoring exists for anomalous issuance patterns? How quickly can fraudulent certificates be detected and revoked?

section.title: Background: The World’s Largest CA
section.simpara: If DigiNotar was a small CA that died quickly, Symantec was a giant that died slowly.

Through a series of acquisitions — VeriSign (2010), Thawte (via VeriSign), GeoTrust (via VeriSign), and RapidSSL (via GeoTrust) — Symantec had become the world’s largest certificate authority by 2015. At its peak, Symantec and its subsidiary brands had issued approximately 30% of all TLS certificates on the public internet.
That market share made Symantec’s CA operation systemically important. It also made it a tempting target for scrutiny.

section.title: The Mis-Issuance Scandal
section.simpara: In September 2015, Google’s Certificate Transparency (CT) monitoring detected that Symantec had issued several test certificates for google.com domains — without Google’s authorization. Symantec claimed these were test certificates issued by employees during internal testing, and that they had been revoked quickly.
Google investigated further and discovered that the problem was much larger:

listitem.simpara: Symantec had issued at least 164 certificates for domains without proper authorization
Some were issued by Symantec employees, others by RA partners (including CrossCert, a Korean RA)
The unauthorized issuance spanned multiple years
Symantec’s internal controls had failed to detect the mis-issuances
When confronted, Symantec’s initial disclosures underreported the scope of the problem

section.simpara: In March 2017, Google engineers Ryan Sleevi and Devon O’Brien published a detailed analysis on the Chromium bug tracker, proposing a phased distrust of all Symantec-issued certificates. The proposal was unprecedented: no one had ever suggested distrusting certificates from the world’s largest CA.

section.title: The Distrust Timeline
section.simpara: What followed was a two-year negotiation between Google, Mozilla, Symantec, and the broader CA/Browser Forum. The timeline:

listitem.simpara: March 2017: Google proposes phased Symantec distrust in Chrome
April 2017: Symantec contests the proposal, arguing the issues were limited
July 2017: Google, Mozilla, and Symantec reach a compromise plan: Symantec must transition to a new infrastructure operated by a different CA
August 2017: DigiCert announces acquisition of Symantec’s CA business for approximately $950 million
December 2017: DigiCert completes the acquisition and begins re-issuing certificates under DigiCert roots
April 2018: Chrome 66 distrusts Symantec certificates issued before June 1, 2016
October 2018: Chrome 70 distrusts ALL remaining Symantec certificates
October 2018: Firefox follows with similar distrust in Firefox 63

section.simpara: The distrust was progressive: older certificates were distrusted first, giving website operators time to obtain replacement certificates. But the endpoint was absolute: after October 2018, no Symantec-issued certificate was trusted by Chrome or Firefox.

section.title: Impact on PDF Signatures
section.simpara: The Symantec distrust had a less dramatic but more insidious impact on PDF signatures than the DigiNotar breach.

DigiNotar’s collapse was sudden — trust was revoked in days, and the relatively small number of affected certificates limited the blast radius. Symantec’s distrust was gradual, but the scale was enormous.

listitem.simpara: Thousands of document signing certificates had been issued under Symantec, VeriSign, Thawte, and GeoTrust brands
Many of these certificates were in Adobe’s AATL (Adobe Approved Trust List)
As Symantec certificates were distrusted, PDF signatures chaining to those certificates showed warnings in validators that followed Chrome/Firefox trust decisions
Adobe’s trust store (AATL) moved independently from browsers — Adobe did not automatically distrust Symantec certificates just because Chrome did
This created a confusing situation where a signed PDF might validate in Acrobat (using AATL) but fail in a web-based validator (using the browser trust store)

note.simpara: The Symantec case illustrates an important distinction: PDF trust stores and web browser trust stores are not the same thing. Adobe Acrobat maintains its own trust list (AATL), which is updated on its own schedule, with its own criteria. A certificate can be distrusted by Chrome but still trusted by Acrobat — or vice versa. This is both a feature (PDF signatures are not automatically affected by browser trust decisions) and a bug (trust status becomes inconsistent across different validation contexts).

section.simpara: The transition to DigiCert mitigated some of the damage — DigiCert re-issued certificates under its own roots, and existing Symantec customers could obtain replacement certificates. But for documents already signed with Symantec-chain certificates, the question of long-term trust remained unresolved.

chapter.title: Let’s Encrypt: The Free CA’s Growing Pains

section.title: The 2020 Mass Revocation
section.simpara: Let’s Encrypt, the free, automated CA that has issued over three billion certificates since its launch in 2015, does not issue document signing certificates. Its certificates are exclusively for TLS (web server) use. So why does a Let’s Encrypt incident appear in a book about PDF signatures?
Because the 2020 mass revocation event demonstrated something important about the fragility of CA infrastructure at scale — a fragility that applies equally to every CA in the PDF signing ecosystem.

On March 3, 2020, Let’s Encrypt announced that it had discovered a bug in its CAA (Certificate Authority Authorization) checking code. CAA is a DNS-based mechanism that allows domain owners to specify which CAs are authorized to issue certificates for their domain. Before issuing a certificate, a CA must check the domain’s CAA records and refuse issuance if the domain specifies a different CA.

The bug: Let’s Encrypt’s validation system checked CAA records when a domain was first validated, but did not re-check them when issuing a certificate if the validation result was cached. This meant that if a domain’s CAA record changed after the initial validation (for example, to explicitly prohibit Let’s Encrypt from issuing certificates), Let’s Encrypt would still issue the certificate based on the stale cached result.
This violated the CA/Browser Forum’s Baseline Requirements, which mandate that CAA records be checked within 8 hours of certificate issuance.

section.title: The Chaos
section.simpara: Let’s Encrypt identified approximately 3,048,289 certificates that had been issued without a compliant CAA check at issuance time. The Baseline Requirements required these certificates to be revoked within 5 days.

Let’s Encrypt gave certificate holders 24 hours to renew their certificates before revocation. For the ACME-based automated renewal ecosystem that Let’s Encrypt had built, this should have been manageable — the whole point of Let’s Encrypt is automated certificate management.

In practice, it was chaos. Not every certificate holder had functional automated renewal. Not every server was properly configured for ACME. Some certificates were on devices that could not easily be updated. The announcement came on a Tuesday evening (US time), giving operators one business day to respond.

section.title: Relevance to PDF Signing
section.simpara: The Let’s Encrypt incident did not directly affect any PDF signatures. But it demonstrated three principles that every PDF signing architect should internalize:

listitem.simpara: Scale amplifies errors. A single bug in a CA’s issuance pipeline can affect millions of certificates. The larger the CA, the larger the blast radius. When choosing a CA for document signing, understand that the CA’s operational quality affects every certificate they issue — including yours.

Revocation at scale is operationally brutal. Even with 24 hours notice and automated tooling, millions of certificates could not be renewed in time. Now imagine the same scenario with document signing certificates, where "renewal" means re-signing every document that was signed with the revoked certificate. There is no automated tool for that.

Compliance requirements drive revocation timelines. The Baseline Requirements gave Let’s Encrypt no discretion — the certificates had to be revoked within 5 days, regardless of the impact. EU regulations impose similar requirements on Qualified Trust Service Providers. If a QTSP discovers a compliance issue, it may be required to revoke certificates on a timeline that does not allow for graceful migration.

caution.simpara: If your QTSP discovers a compliance issue and is required to revoke your signing certificate, every PDF you signed with that certificate becomes suspect — unless you embedded LTV data at signing time. This is not a far-fetched scenario. It has happened to web certificates. It will happen to document signing certificates. Plan accordingly.

chapter.title: Certificate Transparency: The Response to CA Failures

section.title: What Certificate Transparency Is
section.simpara: Certificate Transparency (CT) was invented by Google engineers Ben Laurie and Adam Langley in 2012 (published as RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile companion document RFC 6962, and later updated in RFC 9162), directly in response to the DigiNotar and Comodo breaches.

The core insight: if CAs cannot be trusted to only issue authorized certificates, we need a public audit mechanism that makes every issued certificate visible to domain owners and the broader community.
CT works as follows:

listitem.simpara: When a CA issues a certificate, it submits the certificate to one or more CT logs — append-only, cryptographically verifiable public databases
The CT log returns a Signed Certificate Timestamp (SCT) — a promise that the certificate will be included in the log within a specified time (the "Maximum Merge Delay," typically 24 hours)
The SCT is embedded in the certificate (as an X.509 extension), in the TLS handshake (as a TLS extension), or in the OCSP response (as an OCSP extension)
Browsers verify that certificates come with valid SCTs — if a certificate has no SCT, Chrome treats it as untrusted (since April 2018)
Domain owners (and anyone else) can monitor CT logs for certificates issued for their domains — if an unauthorized certificate appears, they can demand revocation

section.title: How CT Detects Rogue Certificates
section.simpara: CT does not prevent a compromised CA from issuing rogue certificates. It makes rogue certificates detectable.

If the DigiNotar attack had happened in a CT world, the fraudulent google.com certificate would have appeared in public CT logs within 24 hours of issuance. Google’s CT monitoring infrastructure would have detected it immediately — not because a user in Iran happened to be running Chrome, but because Google continuously monitors every CT log for certificates mentioning google.com.
The detection would have been systematic, automatic, and fast.

section.title: CT and PDF: Limited but Growing
section.simpara: Certificate Transparency was designed for the web PKI ecosystem. Its adoption in the document signing world has been slower:

listitem.simpara: Document signing certificates are not required to have SCTs. The CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements mandate CT for TLS certificates, but no equivalent mandate exists for document signing certificates.
Adobe’s AATL does not require CT. CAs in the AATL are not required to log their document signing certificates in CT logs.
The EU Trusted List does not reference CT. The eIDAS regulatory framework does not mention Certificate Transparency.

section.simpara: However, CT is increasingly relevant for PDF signatures:

listitem.simpara: Some CAs voluntarily log their document signing certificates in CT logs
Organizations building their own signature validation can incorporate CT log monitoring as an additional trust signal
The EUDI Wallet infrastructure (Chapter 27) may incorporate CT-like transparency mechanisms for wallet-issued certificates

section.title: Trust Is Fragile and Revocable
section.simpara: The PKI model assumes that root CAs are trustworthy. DigiNotar proved that assumption can fail catastrophically. Symantec proved it can fail gradually. Comodo proved it can fail and be recovered from — but only if the CA responds competently.

For PDF signature architects, the lesson is: design for CA failure. Assume that any CA in your signing certificate’s chain might be distrusted at some point in the future. Your architecture should handle that scenario gracefully.

section.title: Long-Term Validity Requires Embedded Evidence
section.simpara: The single most important defense against CA breach impact is embedding revocation data at signing time.
A PAdES-B-LTA signature includes:

listitem.simpara: The complete certificate chain (signer, intermediates, root)
OCSP responses for every certificate in the chain, obtained at signing time
CRLs as fallback revocation evidence
A signature timestamp proving when the signature was created
A document timestamp proving when the validation data was embedded

section.simpara: With all this data embedded, a validator can determine that the signing certificate was valid at the time of signing — even if the CA is later revoked. The embedded OCSP response from 2024 proves the certificate was not revoked in 2024, regardless of what happens to the CA in 2025.
Without this data, the validator is left with an impossible question: was this certificate valid when the signature was created? If the CA is gone, there is no one to ask.

section.title: You Cannot Predict CA Longevity
section.simpara: DigiNotar: bankrupt in 4 weeks. Symantec CA: sold and sunset in 18 months. Let’s Encrypt: a compliance bug affecting 3 million certificates.
When you select a CA for your document signing certificates, you are making a bet on that CA’s continued trustworthiness. History suggests this bet is riskier than most organizations realize.
Practical mitigations:

listitem.simpara: Use PAdES-B-LTA for anything that matters. The 5 seconds of extra processing time to fetch and embed revocation data is negligible compared to the cost of re-signing thousands of documents after a CA revocation.
Have a certificate migration plan. Know how you will transition to a new CA if your current CA is compromised, acquired, or distrusted. This includes having backup certificates from a second CA.

Monitor your CA’s compliance status. Subscribe to CA/Browser Forum mailing lists, Google’s security blog, and Mozilla’s CA program communications. You want to know about problems with your CA before your customers do.

Re-timestamp archived documents periodically. A PAdES-B-LTA archival timestamp can be refreshed by adding a new document timestamp — extending the signature’s verifiability beyond the original TSA’s certificate lifetime.

table.title: Major CA security incidents and their impact on digital signatures

entry.simpara: VeriSign issues fraudulent Microsoft code-signing certs

Two certificates issued to an impersonator claiming to be Microsoft. Revoked quickly, but demonstrated that even the largest CA could be socially engineered. Led to improved identity verification procedures.

A Debian-specific OpenSSL bug caused all keys generated on Debian/Ubuntu systems for 18 months to be predictable. Affected SSL certificates, SSH keys, and potentially signing keys. Not a CA breach per se, but CAs had to revoke certificates whose keys were generated on affected systems.
9 fraudulent certificates issued via compromised RA accounts. Revoked within hours. CA survived. Attacker claimed Iranian motivation.

531+ fraudulent certificates. Used for mass MITM surveillance of Iranian citizens. CA bankrupt within 4 weeks. Dutch government PKI severely disrupted. All DigiNotar-chained PDF signatures became unverifiable.

Trustwave issued a subordinate CA certificate to a private company for use in DLP (Data Loss Prevention) traffic inspection. Technically allowed the company to issue certificates for any domain. Caused industry debate about acceptable CA practices.
A French government intermediate CA (operated by ANSSI) issued unauthorized certificates for Google domains. Google detected via CT/pinning. Limited scope but raised concerns about government CAs.

India’s National Informatics Centre (NIC), operating under the Indian Controller of Certifying Authorities (CCA), issued unauthorized certificates for Google and Yahoo domains. Google detected via CT. Indian CCA root was constrained in trust stores.

China Internet Network Information Center (CNNIC) delegated certificate issuance to MCS Holdings (an Egyptian company), which issued unauthorized certificates for Google domains via a MITM proxy. Google and Mozilla distrusted CNNIC’s root certificates.

Thousands of certificates issued without proper authorization. Progressive distrust by Chrome and Firefox over 2 years. Symantec CA sold to DigiCert for $950M. Massive impact on document signing certificates issued under Symantec, VeriSign, Thawte, and GeoTrust brands.

Chinese CA WoSign acquired StartCom (Israeli CA) without disclosure, backdated SHA-1 certificates to avoid browser deadlines, and made false statements to browser vendors. Both CAs distrusted by all major browsers.
3 million certificates revoked due to CAA checking non-compliance. 24-hour notice to certificate holders. Demonstrated operational fragility of CA infrastructure at scale.

Investigative reporting linked TrustCor (a Panamanian CA) to a company involved in distributing spyware. Mozilla, Google, and Microsoft removed TrustCor from their trust stores. No evidence of fraudulent certificate issuance, but trust was revoked on the basis of the CA operator’s associations.

Turkish CA e-Tugra suspended from the Common CA Database (CCADB) after security researchers discovered critical vulnerabilities in its infrastructure, including exposed admin panels and improperly secured HSMs.

note.simpara: This timeline only includes publicly known incidents. The actual number of CA compromises is certainly higher — not every breach is detected, and not every detected breach is publicly disclosed. The incidents we know about are the ones where detection mechanisms worked. The ones we should worry about are the ones where they did not.

chapter.title: Impact on PDF Signature Validation: What Happens in Practice
chapter.simpara: When a CA is revoked, the practical impact on PDF signature validation depends on several factors:

section.title: The Validator’s Trust Store
section.simpara: Different validators use different trust stores:

listitem.simpara: Adobe Acrobat (Windows): Uses AATL plus optionally the Windows certificate store
Adobe Acrobat (macOS): Uses AATL plus optionally the macOS Keychain
Foxit Reader: Uses the operating system’s trust store
LibreOffice: Uses the operating system’s trust store (via NSS on Linux)
EU DSS validation service: Uses the EU Trusted List (EUTL)
Custom Java applications: Use the JVM’s cacerts trust store or a custom TrustStore

section.simpara: When a CA is revoked, the timing and scope of the revocation varies across these stores. AATL updates are published by Adobe on their own schedule. OS trust store updates come via system updates. The EUTL is updated when a member state’s supervisory body modifies its trusted list.

This means a signed PDF can show different validation results in different viewers during the transition period — "valid" in one that has not yet received the revocation update, "invalid" in one that has.

section.title: Whether LTV Data Is Present
section.simpara: If the signature includes embedded revocation data (PAdES-B-LT or B-LTA), a competent validator can perform historical validation: it checks the embedded OCSP response or CRL to determine the certificate’s status at the time of signing, rather than its status now.
If the signature does not include LTV data, the validator must check the certificate’s current status — and if the CA has been revoked, the chain cannot be validated at all.

section.title: The Certificate’s Position in the Chain
section.simpara: If only an intermediate CA is revoked (as in the Comodo case), the impact is limited to certificates issued through that specific intermediate. Other intermediates under the same root may be unaffected.
If the root CA is revoked (as in DigiNotar’s case), every certificate in the chain is affected — there is no way to validate the chain without a trusted root.

listitem.simpara: CA breaches retroactively undermine PDF signatures — the math does not change, but the trust does
DigiNotar (2011) showed that a small CA’s failure can have outsized impact — 531 rogue certs, mass surveillance, bankruptcy in 4 weeks
Comodo (2011) showed that fast, transparent response can save a CA — 9 rogue certs, revoked in hours, CA survived
Symantec (2015-2018) showed that even the largest CA is not too big to distrust — 30% of TLS certificates, distrusted over 2 years
Let’s Encrypt (2020) showed that compliance bugs at scale force brutal revocation timelines — 3 million certs, 24-hour notice
Certificate Transparency makes rogue certificates detectable but does not prevent them
PAdES-B-LTA is the only reliable defense — embedded revocation data at signing time proves certificate validity regardless of what happens to the CA later
Always have a CA migration plan — your CA’s trustworthiness is not guaranteed for the lifetime of your signed documents
Monitor your CA’s status — you want to know about problems before your validators do
Re-timestamp archived documents periodically to extend their verifiability beyond any single CA’s lifetime

partintro.simpara: Every technology has its dark corners — the design decisions that made sense in 1999 but haunt us today, the interoperability failures that no spec can fix, the political arrangements that masquerade as technical standards. PDF digital signatures have more dark corners than most.

This chapter is a tour of the things that will make you mutter unprintable words during implementation. Not bugs, exactly — bugs get fixed. These are structural annoyances: the problems baked into the specifications, the ecosystem, and the economics of PDF signing. Each one has wasted thousands of engineering hours across the industry. Understanding them will not make them go away, but it will stop you from wasting your own hours rediscovering what everyone else already knows and nobody bothered to write down.

chapter.title: The Adobe Approved Trust List Monopoly

section.title: What the AATL Is
section.simpara: Adobe Acrobat is the world’s dominant PDF viewer. When a user opens a signed PDF in Acrobat, the first thing they see is a blue or green banner at the top of the document. Green means "Signed and all signatures are valid." Blue means "At least one signature has problems." Yellow means "Signed, but the signature cannot be verified."

The difference between green and yellow, in the vast majority of cases, is not whether the signature is cryptographically correct. It is whether the signing certificate chains to a root in the Adobe Approved Trust List (AATL).

The AATL is a curated list of root certificates that Adobe trusts for document signing. It is published at https://helpx.adobe.com/acrobat/kb/approved-trust-list2.html and ships embedded in Acrobat. If your signing certificate chains to an AATL root, you get the green checkmark. If it does not — even if the certificate is perfectly valid, issued by a reputable CA, chains to a trust anchor in the operating system’s trust store, and was created with impeccable cryptographic rigor — you get yellow.
Yellow, in the real world, means "the customer calls support."

section.title: The Economics of Green
section.simpara: Getting your root certificate into the AATL requires meeting Adobe’s technical requirements (documented in the "AATL Technical Requirements" PDF, which is itself signed with an AATL certificate — recursion intended) and paying a fee. The requirements include:

listitem.simpara: The root must be at least 4096-bit RSA or equivalent
The CA must carry an annual WebTrust for CAs audit or equivalent (ETSI EN 319 411)
The CA must use HSMs for root key protection
Document signing certificates must include specific key usage extensions
The CA must have a published CPS (Certification Practice Statement)

section.simpara: These are reasonable requirements for a public trust anchor. The problem is not the technical bar — it is the gatekeeping.

Adobe is a private company. The AATL is Adobe’s private trust list. Adobe decides who gets in, Adobe decides who stays in, and Adobe decides the criteria. There is no appeals process, no regulatory oversight, and no obligation to accept any particular CA.

For CAs, inclusion in the AATL is not optional — it is a commercial necessity. Enterprise customers demand the green checkmark. Government procurement specifications require it. A CA that is not in the AATL effectively cannot sell document signing certificates at competitive prices, because the certificates will produce yellow warnings in the software that 80% of users use to view PDFs.

important.simpara: The AATL creates a two-tier market for document signing certificates. AATL-listed certificates cost significantly more than non-AATL certificates — typically $200-$500 per year for an individual signing certificate, versus $30-$50 for a certificate from a non-AATL CA. The price premium has nothing to do with cryptographic quality and everything to do with the color of a banner in one company’s software.

If you are building a signing service, the single most important commercial decision you will make is whether your use case requires the green checkmark. If your recipients will open signed PDFs in Acrobat (and they almost certainly will), you need AATL. If your recipients use a custom validation workflow that checks against the EU Trusted List or your own trust store, you do not.

section.title: The EUTL Alternative
section.simpara: The EU Trusted List (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS), Article 22) is the European counterpart to the AATL. Every Qualified Trust Service Provider in the EU is listed on the EUTL, and a certificate chaining to a QTSP on the EUTL qualifies for the highest eIDAS tier regardless of whether Adobe has heard of the CA.

But here is the catch: Acrobat does not validate against the EUTL by default. Acrobat uses the AATL. There is an option to enable EUTL validation in Acrobat (Edit > Preferences > Signatures > Verification > select "European Union Trusted Lists"), but it is off by default, and the vast majority of users never touch it.

This means a signature with a qualified certificate from a QTSP that is not in the AATL will show a yellow warning in Acrobat — even though it is legally a qualified electronic signature with the same standing as a handwritten signature under EU law.
The legal framework says: this is a QES, as valid as ink. The software says: "The validity of the document certification is UNKNOWN."
The user believes the software.

tip.simpara: If you are distributing signed PDFs to recipients who use Acrobat, and your QTSP is on the EUTL but not the AATL, include instructions for enabling EUTL validation in Acrobat. Better yet, embed the full certificate chain and LTV data so that validation depends on the trust store configuration rather than online lookups. Best of all: choose a QTSP that is on both the AATL and the EUTL. Most major European QTSPs have pursued AATL membership precisely because of this problem.

section.title: The Political Dimension
section.simpara: The AATL gives Adobe significant influence over the digital signing ecosystem. Adobe can, at its discretion, adjust requirements, add fees, or remove CAs. This is not a theoretical concern — Adobe has adjusted AATL requirements multiple times, each adjustment requiring CAs to re-certify and sometimes change their infrastructure.

Some in the industry argue that this is appropriate: someone needs to curate trust, and Adobe has a legitimate interest in ensuring that signatures validated by its software are trustworthy. Others argue that a single company should not be the gatekeeper for a technology used in legal, governmental, and financial transactions across the globe.

The emergence of the EUDI wallet (Chapter 27) may shift this dynamic. If QES becomes a consumer technology available to every EU citizen, the volume of qualified signatures will dwarf the current market — and the EUTL, not the AATL, will be the authoritative trust anchor for those signatures. Whether Adobe adjusts its defaults accordingly remains to be seen.

section.title: The Problem
section.simpara: Take a signed PDF — any signed PDF — and open it in five different viewers. You will get five different answers about whether the signature is valid.
This is not an exaggeration. It is the daily reality of PDF signature interoperability.

The fundamental problem is that ISO 32000-2:2020 defines the structure of a PDF signature but leaves enormous latitude in how viewers should validate that structure. The specification says what a /Sig dictionary must contain, what the /ByteRange must cover, and how the CMS SignedData must be embedded. It does not say, with sufficient precision, what a viewer should do when the trust chain is incomplete, when the timestamp is missing, when the revocation data is stale, or when the certificate uses an algorithm the viewer does not support.
