ETSI EN 319 142 (PAdES) adds more precision, but it constrains signing more than it constrains validation. A PAdES-B-LTA signature is well-defined in terms of what it must contain. What a validator must do with each piece of that content — and what it should report to the user when something is missing or ambiguous — is still underspecified.

section.title: The Comparison
section.simpara: The following table shows how five common validators handle the same edge cases. These are real behaviors observed in testing, not theoretical predictions.

table.title: Signature validation behavior across viewers (observed, not specified)

entry.simpara: Valid CMS, expired cert, no timestamp
Valid CMS, EUTL cert, not in AATL

warning.simpara: The most dangerous row in this table is "Incremental update after signing." This is exactly the scenario that the Bochum Incremental Saving Attack (Chapter 24) exploits. If a viewer does not detect that the file was modified after signing, it will show a valid signature on a document whose content has been changed. As of 2026, most major viewers have patched this — but "most" is not "all," and not every installed version has been updated.

section.title: Why Viewers Disagree
section.simpara: The disagreements stem from several root causes:

Different trust stores. Acrobat uses AATL. Foxit uses the OS trust store. LibreOffice uses NSS (on Linux) or the OS store (on Windows/macOS). macOS Preview uses the Keychain. A certificate trusted by one store may not be trusted by another.
Different algorithm support. Some viewers still accept SHA-1; others warn or reject. Some support ECDSA; others are RSA-only. Some parse CAdES extensions; others only understand basic CMS.

Different revocation policies. Acrobat actively checks revocation status (OCSP and CRL) if the information is available. Foxit and LibreOffice are more lenient — they may skip revocation checks if the data is not embedded. macOS Preview performs minimal revocation checking.

Different byte range verification. Acrobat carefully verifies that the byte range covers the entire file except the /Contents gap. Some other viewers are less rigorous — they check that the CMS signature verifies over the byte range data, but do not verify that the byte range actually covers the whole file.

Different handling of incremental updates. This is the critical one. A proper validator must compare the signed byte range to the total file size and, if there are bytes after the signed range, analyze the incremental update to determine whether it contains permitted changes (adding signatures, annotations) or impermissible modifications (changing page content). Not all viewers perform this analysis with equal rigor.

section.title: What You Can Do About It
section.simpara: If you are a signature creator, your goal is to produce signatures that validate consistently across all major viewers:

listitem.simpara: Use PAdES-B-LTA. It is the most broadly supported profile.
Use SubFilter ETSI.CAdES.detached. All modern viewers support it, and it enables PAdES validation.
Use SHA-256 or SHA-384. Never SHA-1. Avoid SHA-512 only because some older viewers have edge-case issues with it.
Embed the full certificate chain. Do not rely on the viewer fetching missing intermediates — some viewers will not.
Embed OCSP responses and CRLs. If the revocation data is in the DSS, viewers that check revocation will find it; viewers that do not check will simply ignore it.
Allocate sufficient space for /Contents. We discuss this in detail later in this chapter.
Test in at least three viewers. Acrobat, Foxit/LibreOffice, and one programmatic validator (PDFBox or EU DSS). If it validates in all three, it will validate almost everywhere.

section.simpara: If you are a signature validator, your goal is to implement the most rigorous checks and report the most precise results:

listitem.simpara: Verify the byte range covers the entire file minus the /Contents gap. This catches the Signature Wrapping Attack (Chapter 24).
Detect incremental updates after signing. Compare the signed byte range to the file size. If there is trailing data, parse it and determine whether it represents permitted or impermissible changes.
Check revocation status. Use embedded OCSP/CRL data if available; fall back to online checks if not; report clearly when revocation data is unavailable.
Report the PAdES profile level. Tell the user whether the signature is B-B, B-T, B-LT, or B-LTA — this is much more informative than a binary "valid/invalid."

section.title: The Five SubFilter Values
section.simpara: The /SubFilter key in a signature dictionary tells the viewer what format the signature value (in /Contents) uses. Over the history of PDF, five SubFilter values have been defined:

table.title: SubFilter values defined across PDF versions and PAdES

entry.simpara: The original. CMS SignedData where the signed content is the byte range data (not included in the CMS structure). Still widely used. Supported by every viewer. Not PAdES-compliant on its own.

The SHA-1 hash of the entire document is embedded as the CMS EncapsulatedContentInfo. Deprecated in ISO 32000-2:2020. SHA-1 is broken for collision resistance since the SHAttered attack (2017). Do not use.
Raw RSA signature over SHA-1 hash. The certificate is in a separate /Cert key, not embedded in CMS. Extremely legacy. Most modern viewers cannot validate it. Do not use.

The PAdES SubFilter. CMS SignedData with CAdES extensions (ESS-signing-certificate-v2, optionally timestamp attributes). Required for all PAdES profiles. The recommended choice for any new implementation.

Document-level timestamp. The /Contents contains an RFC 3161 TimeStampToken instead of a regular CMS signature. Used for archive timestamps in PAdES-B-LTA. Not a "signature" in the user-facing sense — it proves the document existed at a specific time.

section.title: Which One Should You Use?
section.simpara: If you are implementing a new signing feature in 2026, the answer is simple: ETSI.CAdES.detached for signatures and ETSI.RFC3161 for document-level timestamps.

There is no reason to use adbe.pkcs7.detached in new implementations unless you have a specific compatibility requirement with a legacy system that cannot parse CAdES extensions. The practical difference between adbe.pkcs7.detached and ETSI.CAdES.detached is the presence of the ESS-signing-certificate-v2 signed attribute (Chapter 17) — adding it makes the signature PAdES-B-B compliant at zero additional cost.

adbe.pkcs7.sha1 is deprecated and cryptographically broken. Do not use it. If you encounter it in a legacy document, validate it with the understanding that SHA-1 collision resistance has been publicly demonstrated to be insufficient since February 2017 (the SHAttered attack by Stevens et al.).

adbe.x509.rsa_sha1 is a relic of PDF 1.3. It separates the certificate from the signature in a way that no modern library handles well. If you encounter it, you are looking at a document from the early 2000s.

section.title: What Happens with Unrecognized SubFilter Values
section.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.1 states that if a viewer encounters a SubFilter value it does not recognize, it should not attempt to validate the signature. In practice, viewers handle this inconsistently:

listitem.simpara: Acrobat: displays "The signature format is not supported" — a clear and honest message
Foxit: may attempt validation anyway and produce confusing results
LibreOffice: typically shows "Signature could not be verified" — accurate but unhelpful
macOS Preview: ignores the signature entirely — the document appears unsigned

section.simpara: If you are implementing a validator and encounter an unknown SubFilter, the correct behavior is to report that the signature format is not recognized. Do not attempt to guess the format. Do not silently skip the signature. Report it.

note.simpara: A common source of SubFilter confusion in the wild: some signing tools produce a SubFilter of adbe.pkcs7.detached but include CAdES extensions (ESS-signing-certificate-v2) in the CMS signed attributes. Technically, this is a valid adbe.pkcs7.detached signature that also happens to be PAdES-compliant — because PAdES compliance is about the content of the CMS, not just the SubFilter label. But some validators treat the SubFilter as a strict indicator: if it says adbe.pkcs7.detached, they skip CAdES-specific checks. The result: a signature that is PAdES-compliant by content but not recognized as such by label.
For maximum compatibility, use the SubFilter that matches your intent. If you are producing PAdES signatures, use ETSI.CAdES.detached.

section.title: Two Standards, One Problem
section.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 defines how to embed a digital signature in a PDF. ETSI EN 319 142 (PAdES) defines additional requirements for that signature to be legally meaningful in Europe.
These two standards should be perfectly aligned. They are not.

The divergence is not dramatic — PAdES is a strict subset of ISO 32000-2:2020, so a PAdES-compliant signature is always a valid ISO 32000-2:2020 signature. But the two standards were developed by different organizations (ISO 32000-2:2020 by ISO TC 171, PAdES by ETSI ESI), on different timelines, with different constituencies. The result is a set of subtle mismatches that catch developers in the gap.

section.title: Where They Disagree
section.simpara: Signed attributes. ISO 32000-2:2020 requires the CMS SignedData to include content-type and message-digest signed attributes (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) §11.1). PAdES additionally requires ESS-signing-certificate-v2 (ETSI EN 319 122-1 §5.7.3). A signature that satisfies ISO 32000-2:2020 but omits ESS-signing-certificate-v2 is a valid PDF signature but not a valid PAdES signature. This is the single most common PAdES compliance failure.

SubFilter semantics. ISO 32000-2:2020 treats SubFilter as informational — it tells the viewer what format to expect, but the viewer is expected to parse the CMS and determine the format from the content. PAdES treats ETSI.CAdES.detached as a requirement — a signature claiming PAdES compliance must use this SubFilter. A technically identical CMS blob wrapped in adbe.pkcs7.detached is not PAdES-compliant by label, even if the CMS content is identical.

Timestamp handling. ISO 32000-2:2020 defines document-level timestamps (SubFilter ETSI.RFC3161) and signature timestamps (unsigned attribute id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken in the CMS). PAdES defines the same mechanisms but adds requirements about timestamp token format, the relationship between signature timestamps and document timestamps, and the order in which they must be embedded in PAdES-B-LTA. The PAdES requirements are stricter — a timestamp arrangement that is valid under ISO 32000-2:2020 may not satisfy PAdES-B-LTA’s requirements for archive timestamps.

DSS dictionary. The Document Security Store (DSS) dictionary — where certificates, OCSP responses, and CRLs are stored for LTV validation — is defined in ISO 32000-2:2020 §12.8.4.3 and also referenced in PAdES. The specifications agree on the structure but differ slightly on when DSS data is mandatory. ISO 32000-2:2020 treats DSS as optional enrichment. PAdES-B-LT and B-LTA require DSS data — without it, the signature cannot claim the LT or LTA profile level.

section.title: The Practical Consequence
section.simpara: If you are building a signing service that needs to produce signatures valid in both the ISO 32000-2:2020 sense and the PAdES sense (and you almost certainly are), always target PAdES. PAdES is the stricter standard. A PAdES-B-LTA signature satisfies both standards simultaneously. A signature that targets only ISO 32000-2:2020 without PAdES awareness will fail PAdES validation — and PAdES validation is what matters for EU compliance, for AATL-listed CAs, and for most enterprise validation workflows.

important.simpara: The EU DSS validation framework (Chapter 23) validates signatures against PAdES profiles, not against raw ISO 32000-2:2020. If your signatures validate in EU DSS, they satisfy both standards. If you are building your own validator, validate against PAdES — it is the superset of requirements that covers both.

section.title: The Problem
section.simpara: A PAdES-B-T signature requires a signature timestamp — an RFC 3161 TimeStampToken embedded as an unsigned CMS attribute. The timestamp proves when the signature was created.
To obtain the timestamp, you must:

listitem.simpara: Create the CMS SignedData (including the signed attributes and the signature value)
Hash the signature value
Send the hash to a Time Stamp Authority (TSA)
Receive the TimeStampToken from the TSA
Embed the token as an unsigned attribute in the CMS SignedData

section.simpara: Here is the problem: the CMS SignedData is the /Contents value in the signature dictionary. The /Contents value is a hex string with a fixed length — the space for it was allocated when the signature dictionary was written into the PDF, before the CMS was created.

So the size of /Contents must be determined before you know how large the CMS will be. And the size of the CMS depends on whether you include a timestamp — and how large that timestamp is. And you cannot obtain the timestamp until you have the CMS signature value. And you cannot have the signature value until you have the byte range. And the byte range depends on the size of /Contents.
This is the chicken-and-egg.

section.title: The Standard Solution
section.simpara: The standard solution, used by every major signing library, is to pre-allocate a fixed-size /Contents field, create the CMS signature, obtain the timestamp, embed the timestamp in the CMS, and then pad the CMS to fit the pre-allocated space.

The padding is simple: after the CMS SignedData DER encoding, the remaining bytes in /Contents are filled with 0x00. The hex encoding of these zero bytes produces 00 characters. The viewer knows to ignore trailing zeros after the DER-encoded CMS — the DER encoding is self-delimiting, so the parser can determine where the CMS ends and the padding begins.

section.title: The 32 KB Problem
section.simpara: How much space should you pre-allocate? This is a more consequential question than it appears.
The CMS SignedData contains:

listitem.simpara: The DER-encoded SignedData structure itself (~100-300 bytes of overhead)
The signer’s certificate (~1-2 KB)
Any intermediate certificates (~1-2 KB each, typically 1-3 intermediates)
The signature value (~256-512 bytes for RSA-2048/4096, ~64-72 bytes for ECDSA)
Signed attributes (~200-500 bytes)
The signature timestamp token (~3-8 KB, depending on the TSA)

section.simpara: A typical PAdES-B-T signature with one intermediate certificate and a timestamp requires approximately 8-12 KB. A PAdES-B-LTA signature with embedded OCSP responses and CRLs in the CMS (rather than in the DSS) can require 15-30 KB.
Most implementations pre-allocate 32 KB (32,768 bytes, producing 65,536 hex characters in /Contents). This is sufficient for the vast majority of signatures.
But not all.

entry.simpara: 4-5 intermediates, each 2 KB. Happens with some government CAs.
Some TSAs include their own certificate chain in the TimeStampToken. If the TSA has 3 intermediates, the token alone can be 10-15 KB.
If a CRL is embedded directly in the CMS (as an unsigned attribute), it can be 50-200 KB. This is unusual for new implementations (CRLs go in the DSS instead), but some legacy signing tools do this.
Government PKIs that cross-certify with multiple roots can produce chains with redundant certificates, inflating the CMS.
Some workflows add both a signature timestamp and a content timestamp, doubling the timestamp overhead.

section.simpara: If the CMS exceeds the pre-allocated /Contents size, the signing operation fails. The failure mode depends on the library:

listitem.simpara: PDFBox: throws an IOException with a message about the signature being too large for the placeholder
iText: throws a similar exception
OpenPDF: throws an exception

section.simpara: The fix is simple: increase the pre-allocated size. But the fix means you allocated more space that is filled with zero padding — wasted bytes in every PDF you sign. There is a tension between allocating enough space to handle edge cases and not bloating every PDF with unused padding.

tip.simpara: A practical compromise: pre-allocate 32 KB for PAdES-B-T signatures and 64 KB for PAdES-B-LTA signatures. If you know your signing chain is unusually long (government PKIs, cross-certified CAs), increase to 96 KB or 128 KB. The wasted space is insignificant compared to the risk of a signing failure in production.
If you want to be precise, you can estimate the CMS size before allocating:

listitem.simpara: Build a "dry run" CMS with a dummy signature value and a dummy timestamp of the expected size
Measure its DER-encoded length
Add a 50% safety margin
Use that as your pre-allocation size

tip.simpara: This is more complex but avoids both undersizing and excessive oversizing.

section.title: The Taxonomy of Silence
section.simpara: PDF signing can fail in ways that produce a valid-looking PDF with an invalid signature. The document opens. The pages render. But the signature is broken — and the failure may not be obvious until someone tries to validate it.

These silent failures are particularly dangerous because they often are not detected at signing time. The signing code runs without exceptions. The PDF is saved. It is emailed, archived, or submitted to a government portal. And then, days or weeks later, someone discovers that the signature is invalid.

section.title: Empty /Contents
section.simpara: The most egregious silent failure: the /Contents value is present but contains only zero bytes. No CMS structure at all.

This happens when the signing pipeline fails to inject the CMS into the /Contents placeholder after the byte range is computed and the CMS is generated. The PDF has the correct structure — the signature dictionary exists, the byte range is set, the /Contents key is there — but the value is all zeros.

Some signing libraries have had bugs where exceptions during CMS generation were swallowed (caught and not re-thrown), leaving the /Contents empty. The resulting PDF looks signed — it has a signature field, possibly even a visual appearance — but the signature is cryptographically void.

caution.simpara: After every signing operation, always verify the result. Open the output PDF, extract the /Contents value, and confirm it contains a valid DER-encoded CMS SignedData. This adds a few milliseconds to your signing pipeline and catches the most catastrophic failure mode.

section.title: Bad Byte Range
section.simpara: The /ByteRange array must specify two ranges that together cover the entire file except the /Contents hex string. A bad byte range — one that does not start at 0, does not end at the file’s last byte, or has gaps other than the /Contents region — produces a signature that covers only part of the file.
This can happen when:

listitem.simpara: The signing library miscalculates the offset of the /Contents value in the file
The file is modified (even trivially — whitespace changes, metadata updates) between byte range calculation and signature injection
A streaming writer flushes partial content, causing offset shifts

section.simpara: The PDF opens fine. The signature exists. But a proper validator will detect that the byte range does not cover the entire file and report the signature as invalid or the document as modified.

section.title: Missing Certificate Chain
section.simpara: A CMS SignedData should include the signer’s certificate and all intermediate certificates needed to build a chain to a trusted root. If the intermediates are missing, the validator must fetch them — and may not be able to.
Missing intermediates are common in testing (developers use self-signed certificates and forget to include the chain) but also happen in production when:

listitem.simpara: The signing code includes only the signer’s certificate, not the intermediates
The PKCS#12 keystore contains only the signer’s certificate (the intermediates were not imported)
A library upgrade changed the default behavior for chain inclusion

section.simpara: The signature validates if the validator can fetch or already has the missing intermediates. It fails if it cannot. This is a Heisenbug — the signature works on the developer’s machine (where the intermediates are in the trust store) but fails on the customer’s machine (where they are not).

section.title: Expired Timestamp
section.simpara: A signature timestamp proves when the signature was created. But the timestamp itself is signed by the TSA’s certificate — and that certificate can expire.

If the TSA certificate expires and the timestamp token does not include sufficient chain data for historical validation, validators may reject the timestamp. The signature itself may still verify, but the timestamp — which was the proof of when the signature was created — becomes unverifiable.

For PAdES-B-LTA, this is handled by the archive timestamp mechanism: a document-level timestamp covers the entire file (including the DSS with revocation data for the signature timestamp). As long as the archive timestamp chain is valid, the signature timestamp can be verified historically.

For PAdES-B-T signatures without LTV data, an expired TSA certificate means the timestamp can no longer be verified. The signature degrades from B-T to effectively B-B — you lose the proof of when it was created.

warning.simpara: Monitor the expiration dates of the TSAs you use. TSA certificates typically have 10-15 year validity periods, so this is not an urgent problem — but it is a problem that will arrive eventually. When it does, any PAdES-B-T signature without LTV data that used the expired TSA will lose its timestamp verifiability.
This is another reason to prefer PAdES-B-LTA: the archive timestamp mechanism provides a renewable chain that survives individual certificate expirations.

section.title: What Reader-Enabling Is
section.simpara: Adobe Reader (the free version) historically could not save filled-in forms. To allow Reader users to fill in and save a form, the PDF author had to "enable" the PDF for Reader using Adobe Acrobat Pro. This was done by applying a special usage rights signature — a certification-level signature with a specific set of usage rights extensions that told Reader which features to unlock.

The usage rights signature is stored in the document’s permissions dictionary (/Perms) and uses a special Adobe-issued certificate. When Reader opens a document with a valid usage rights signature, it enables the specified features (fill-in, save, comment, etc.).

section.title: The Conflict with Document Signing
section.simpara: The problem arises when a Reader-enabled PDF needs to be signed.

The usage rights signature is a certification signature — it was the first signature on the document, and it claims /DocMDP permissions. Adding an approval signature after the certification signature requires an incremental update, which is permitted under the DocMDP permissions level set by the usage rights signature.
But here is the catch: some PDF editors, when they save the document after adding a signature, perform operations that invalidate the usage rights signature. This happens because:

listitem.simpara: The editor modifies objects that the usage rights signature’s byte range covers
The editor performs a full save instead of an incremental save, destroying the incremental structure that the usage rights signature depends on
The editor adds or modifies AcroForm entries in a way that the usage rights signature’s /FieldMDP transform does not permit

section.simpara: When the usage rights signature is invalidated, Reader reverts to its restricted mode — the user can no longer save the form. And if the document’s approval signature depends on the AcroForm structure (which it does, since signature fields are form fields), the interplay between the invalidated usage rights and the new signature can produce validation errors in Acrobat.

section.title: The Historical Context
section.simpara: Reader-enabling was a significant source of revenue for Adobe: Acrobat Pro was the only tool that could create the usage rights signature (because it used an Adobe-controlled certificate). Third-party libraries could not Reader-enable PDFs.

ISO 32000-2:2020 deprecated the /Perms usage rights mechanism. PDF 2.0 assumes that all viewers can save forms without special enablement. But the real world is not PDF 2.0 — millions of Reader-enabled PDF 1.7 documents are still in circulation, and enterprises still generate new ones because their workflows depend on the Reader experience.

note.simpara: If your signing service processes documents that may be Reader-enabled, test the signing workflow with Reader-enabled PDFs specifically. Ensure your signing code performs an incremental save (not a full save) and does not modify objects covered by the usage rights signature. PDFBox’s PDDocument.saveIncremental() is designed for this — but you must be careful not to load and re-save objects that the usage rights signature covers.

section.title: The Modern Alternative
section.simpara: The practical alternative to Reader-enabling is to stop caring about it. Adobe Acrobat Reader DC (the current free version) can save filled-in forms without usage rights. The limitation existed in older versions of Reader (pre-DC) and was removed as Adobe shifted to a subscription model for Acrobat.

If your user base has upgraded to Reader DC (and by 2026, most have), Reader-enabling is no longer necessary. If you must support legacy Reader versions, the only safe approach is to Reader-enable the PDF after all signatures have been applied — which is usually not possible, since the usage rights signature must be the first signature.
The real answer is: test your specific workflow, document the behavior, and warn your users if they are operating in a scenario where Reader-enabling and signing conflict.

section.title: The Spec Says
section.simpara: ISO 32000-2:2020 §7.5.5 states that a PDF file must end with %%EOF. Anything after the last %%EOF marker is not part of the PDF.
In an incrementally saved PDF (which every signed PDF is — signing requires an incremental save), each revision ends with its own %%EOF. The last %%EOF in the file marks the end of the last revision.

section.title: The Reality
section.simpara: In practice, many tools append data after %%EOF:

listitem.simpara: Some PDF generators append a newline or carriage return after %%EOF (harmless but technically non-conformant)
Some content management systems append metadata or tracking information
Some signing tools, due to buffer sizing bugs, write a few extra bytes after the CMS injection
Some email systems append MIME boundary markers when the PDF is transmitted as an attachment

section.simpara: These trailing bytes create a problem for signature validation: the signed byte range (as specified by /ByteRange) covers the file up to and including the last byte as calculated at signing time. If additional bytes appear after that, the file is longer than the signed byte range. A strict validator will flag this as "the document was modified after signing" — because, technically, it was, even if the modification was just a trailing newline.

tip.simpara: When your validator detects bytes beyond the signed byte range, examine what those bytes actually are before reporting "modified after signing." A few bytes of whitespace or a line break after %%EOF is cosmetically messy but not a security concern. A complete incremental update after the signed range is a genuine modification that needs careful analysis.
Distinguish between "there are trailing bytes that are not an incremental update" (cosmetic) and "there is an incremental update that was not covered by any signature" (potentially malicious).

listitem.simpara: The AATL is a private trust list controlled by Adobe that determines whether Acrobat shows a green or yellow banner — this gatekeeping creates a two-tier market for signing certificates
Validation results differ across viewers — the same PDF can show valid, invalid, or unknown depending on trust stores, algorithm support, revocation policies, and byte range verification rigor
Use ETSI.CAdES.detached for all new signatures — it is PAdES-compliant and universally supported
PAdES is stricter than ISO 32000-2:2020 — always target PAdES, because a PAdES signature satisfies both standards
Pre-allocate 32-64 KB for /Contents — undersizing causes signing failures; oversizing wastes a few KB of padding
Always verify the result after signing — check that /Contents contains valid CMS, the byte range is correct, and the chain is complete
Monitor TSA certificate expiration — expired TSA certs degrade B-T signatures to effectively B-B
Reader-enabled PDFs conflict with signing — test specifically if your workflow involves Reader-enabled documents
Trailing bytes after %%EOF are common and usually harmless, but your validator should distinguish cosmetic trailing data from actual post-signature modifications

partintro.simpara: For three decades, qualified electronic signatures have been a niche technology — expensive, complicated, and reserved for enterprises, notaries, and government agencies. In 2024, the European Union decided to change that. The revised eIDAS regulation puts a qualified signature creation capability in the pocket of every EU citizen, on a device they already own: their smartphone.

This is not incremental improvement. This is a category shift. If it works — and the "if" is substantial — QES moves from a tool that a few thousand specialized users understand to a feature that 450 million Europeans can use as casually as they use contactless payment.

This chapter covers the regulatory, architectural, and technical dimensions of the EUDI wallet and its impact on PDF digital signatures. We start with the political context (because regulation drives everything in this space), move through the architecture, and end with what you — the developer building signing or validation software — need to prepare.

section.title: The Regulatory History
section.simpara: The original eIDAS regulation (Regulation (EU) No 910/2014 (eIDAS), Chapter 19) entered into force in 2014 and became fully effective in 2016. It was a success in the narrow sense: it created a functioning framework for qualified trust services, established the EU Trusted List, and enabled cross-border recognition of QES.
It was a failure in the broad sense: almost nobody used it.

The adoption numbers tell the story. By 2022, eight years after eIDAS entered into force, fewer than 14% of EU citizens had ever used a qualified electronic signature. The number who used QES regularly was a fraction of that. In some member states, the number of active QES users was in the low thousands — in countries with populations of tens of millions.
The reasons for low adoption were structural:

listitem.simpara: Cost. A qualified signing certificate costs 50-300 EUR per year. Most citizens have no reason to pay that.
Complexity. Creating a QES required a smart card or USB token, driver software, and middleware that worked reliably on exactly zero consumer devices.
No killer application. There was no consumer use case compelling enough to justify the cost and complexity. Tax filing, the most common candidate, was solved by other means in most member states.

eID fragmentation. Each member state had its own electronic identity scheme, with its own technology stack, its own enrollment process, and its own level of adoption. Cross-border recognition of eIDs was technically possible under eIDAS but practically rare.

section.simpara: The European Commission recognized by 2020 that voluntary adoption was not going to achieve the digital single market it wanted. Something more forceful was needed.

section.title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1183
section.simpara: On April 11, 2024, the revised eIDAS regulation — Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0), commonly called eIDAS 2.0 — entered into force. The revision keeps the trust services framework from eIDAS 1.0 largely intact (the three-tier signature model, QTSPs, the EU Trusted List — all unchanged) but adds a transformative new component.
The headline change: every EU member state must provide a European Digital Identity Wallet to its citizens and residents.
The timeline:

entry.simpara: European Commission publishes the first set of implementing acts defining technical specifications
Large-Scale Pilots (LSPs) across the EU test wallet implementations in real-world scenarios
Member states must make the EUDI wallet available to all citizens and residents
Mandatory acceptance of the wallet by public services and, for certain use cases, private sector reliance parties

section.title: The Political Context
section.simpara: eIDAS 2.0 did not emerge from a vacuum. It was shaped by several converging political forces:

COVID-19 and the Digital COVID Certificate. The EU Digital COVID Certificate (2021) demonstrated that a pan-European digital credential system could be built and deployed at scale in months. Over 1.8 billion certificates were issued across 46 countries. The technical infrastructure — QR codes, digital signatures, trust frameworks — proved that citizens would adopt digital credentials if the use case was compelling enough. The political lesson was clear: mandates work, voluntary adoption does not.

Strategic autonomy. The European Commission increasingly frames digital identity as a matter of strategic autonomy. European citizens authenticate to online services using Google, Apple, and Facebook identity providers — American companies, subject to American law, storing European identity data on American infrastructure. The EUDI wallet is, in part, an assertion that European identity should be managed by European institutions.

The digital single market. The EU’s single market depends on cross-border transactions. Those transactions require identity verification. If every member state has its own identity system, cross-border identity verification remains fragmented — exactly the problem eIDAS 1.0 was supposed to solve and did not.

Article 45 and the browser wars. The original eIDAS 2.0 proposal included a controversial provision (Article 45) that would have required web browsers to trust Qualified Website Authentication Certificates (QWACs) issued by QTSPs, without the ability to apply additional security requirements. Browser vendors — particularly Mozilla and Google — objected strenuously, arguing that this would undermine browser security by forcing them to trust certificates that did not meet their own security standards. The final text was amended to address some of these concerns, but the episode illustrates the tension between regulatory mandates and technical security communities.

section.title: What the Wallet Is
section.simpara: The EUDI wallet is a smartphone application — provided by or on behalf of a member state — that allows citizens and residents to:

listitem.simpara: Store government-issued identity data (PID): name, date of birth, nationality, and a unique persistent identifier — the digital equivalent of a national ID card or passport

Store qualified electronic attestations of attributes (QEAAs): digitally signed attestations of specific attributes — university degrees, professional qualifications, driving licenses, age verification, right to work
Present credentials selectively: share only the specific attributes required for a transaction (e.g., "I am over 18" without revealing date of birth or name)
Create qualified electronic signatures: sign documents with QES, using a remote QSCD accessed through the wallet

section.simpara: The wallet is not a single application developed by the European Commission. Each member state is responsible for providing its own wallet implementation. The Commission defines the technical specifications and ensures interoperability; member states build or procure the implementations.

section.title: What the Wallet Is Not
section.simpara: The EUDI wallet is not a blockchain. It is not decentralized in the cryptocurrency sense. It is not self-sovereign identity (SSI), although it borrows some concepts from that space.

The wallet is a government-issued credential system. The identity data comes from authoritative government sources (civil registries, passport offices). The trust model is top-down: you trust the wallet because you trust the member state that issued it, and other member states trust it because the EU framework mandates mutual recognition.

The wallet is also not mandatory for citizens. eIDAS 2.0 requires member states to offer the wallet; it does not require citizens to use it. However, Article 5b requires that a wide range of public and private sector entities accept the wallet — which creates strong incentives for adoption even without an explicit mandate.

note.simpara: The voluntary nature of the wallet is politically important. A mandatory government identity app on every citizen’s phone would face significant political resistance, particularly in countries with strong privacy traditions (Germany, Austria, the Netherlands). By making the wallet optional for citizens but mandatory for reliance parties, eIDAS 2.0 creates adoption through demand-side pressure rather than supply-side compulsion.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: The Architecture Reference Framework (ARF) is the technical blueprint for the EUDI wallet ecosystem. Published and maintained by the European Commission, the ARF defines the architecture, protocols, data models, and trust framework for the wallet.
As of early 2026, the ARF is at version 1.4 and still evolving. The core architecture is stable, but specific protocol choices and data formats are being refined based on pilot feedback.
The ARF defines four main roles:

entry.simpara: The citizen or resident who holds the wallet on their device
The member state (or entity acting on its behalf) that issues and manages the wallet application
The authoritative source that issues identity data (PID) or attribute attestations (QEAAs) into the wallet
The entity (public or private sector) that requests and validates credentials from the wallet

section.title: The Trust Model
section.simpara: The EUDI wallet trust model has multiple layers:

listitem.simpara: Device trust. The wallet runs on the user’s smartphone. The device’s secure element or Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) provides hardware-backed key storage. The wallet checks that the device meets minimum security requirements (OS version, no root/jailbreak).

Wallet trust. Each wallet implementation is certified by the member state that issues it. The certification confirms that the wallet meets the ARF’s security and functional requirements. Wallet instances are registered with the member state’s infrastructure.

PID/QEAA trust. PID and QEAAs are digitally signed by the issuing authority (the PID Provider for identity data, the Attestation Provider for attributes). The signature uses a key that chains to the member state’s entry in the EU Trusted List. Relying parties validate the attestation signatures against the EUTL, just as PDF signature validators check the signer’s certificate against a trust store.

Relying party trust. Relying parties that request credentials from the wallet must be registered and authenticated. The wallet verifies the relying party’s identity before releasing any data — the user knows who is requesting their data, and the wallet ensures the request is legitimate.

important.simpara: The trust model for the EUDI wallet is fundamentally the same trust model as PDF digital signatures: X.509 certificates, certificate chains, trusted lists, and CMS/COSE signatures. If you understand how PDF signature validation works (Chapters 16-17), you understand the core of the wallet’s trust model. The wallet adds selective disclosure and user consent on top, but the cryptographic foundations are identical.

section.title: Person Identification Data (PID)
section.simpara: The PID is the wallet’s core credential — the digital equivalent of a national ID card. It is issued by the member state’s PID Provider (typically the civil registry or passport authority) and contains a minimum set of identity attributes.

entry.simpara: A persistent, unique identifier assigned by the member state
Boolean: is the holder over 18? (Derived attribute for age verification without revealing birth date)
The authority that issued the PID
The member state that issued the PID (ISO 3166-1 alpha-2)

section.simpara: The PID is signed by the PID Provider using a key that chains to the member state’s trust infrastructure. The signature format is currently specified as either ISO/IEC 18013-5 mdoc (the same format used for mobile driving licenses) or SD-JWT (Selective Disclosure JSON Web Token, IETF draft). The ARF supports both formats, with ongoing discussion about whether to mandate one or allow both.

section.title: Qualified Electronic Attestations of Attributes (QEAA)
section.simpara: QEAAs are the wallet’s extensibility mechanism. While the PID provides core identity, QEAAs attest to specific attributes beyond identity:

listitem.simpara: Educational credentials: university degrees, professional certifications
Professional qualifications: medical licenses, legal bar admissions, engineering certifications
Organizational identity: employment status, company role, powers of representation
Financial attributes: bank account verification, creditworthiness attestations
Travel documents: digital driving licenses, digital travel credentials

section.simpara: A QEAA is issued by a Qualified Trust Service Provider (QTSP) or a government authority. When issued by a QTSP, it carries the same legal weight as the paper credential it replaces — a QEAA attesting a university degree, issued by a QTSP after verification with the university, is legally equivalent to the paper diploma.

The "qualified" designation is critical. An Electronic Attestation of Attributes (EAA) without the "qualified" prefix does not enjoy the same legal presumptions. The distinction mirrors the QES/AdES distinction: qualified attestations carry a legal presumption of correctness; non-qualified attestations are evidence to be evaluated on their merits.

section.title: The Privacy Problem
section.simpara: Traditional digital credentials have a privacy problem: they are all-or-nothing. If your credential contains your name, date of birth, address, and nationality, presenting it to verify your age requires revealing all four attributes — even though the relying party only needs to know that you are over 18.
The EUDI wallet solves this through selective disclosure — the ability to present only a subset of the attributes in a credential, without invalidating the cryptographic signature.

section.title: How SD-JWT Works
section.simpara: The ARF specifies SD-JWT (Selective Disclosure for JWTs) as one of the credential formats for selective disclosure. SD-JWT is an IETF specification (currently in the RFC track) that extends JSON Web Tokens with selective disclosure capability.
The mechanism works as follows:

listitem.simpara: Issuance. The issuer creates a JWT containing all attributes, but each disclosable attribute is replaced with a hash of the attribute value combined with a random salt. The original attribute values and their salts are provided to the wallet holder as "disclosures" — separate, individually presentable data items.

Presentation. When the wallet presents the credential to a relying party, it includes the JWT (with the hashed placeholders) plus only the disclosures for the attributes the relying party needs. The relying party can verify that each disclosed attribute matches its hash in the JWT, confirming it was part of the original signed credential — but attributes whose disclosures are withheld remain hidden.

Verification. The relying party verifies the JWT signature (confirming it was issued by a trusted authority), then verifies each disclosed attribute against its hash in the JWT. Attributes that were not disclosed cannot be reconstructed from the hashes (the random salts ensure this).

section.title: The ISO/IEC 18013-5 mdoc Alternative
section.simpara: The ARF also supports the mdoc format defined in ISO/IEC 18013-5 (originally for mobile driving licenses). mdoc uses CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation) instead of JSON and COSE (CBOR Object Signing and Encryption) instead of JOSE.

Both formats support selective disclosure. The choice between them is still under discussion in the ARF, with mdoc favored for proximity (NFC/BLE) use cases and SD-JWT favored for remote (online) use cases.

For PDF signing purposes, the credential format does not directly matter — what matters is that the wallet can authenticate the user and authorize a signing operation. The credential format affects how the wallet proves the user’s identity to the signing service, not how the PDF signature itself is structured.

tip.simpara: If you are building a service that accepts signed PDFs and also needs to verify the signer’s attributes (e.g., a medical prescription service that needs to confirm the signer is a licensed doctor), you will need to handle QEAA validation in addition to PDF signature validation. The QEAA is presented separately from the PDF — it is not embedded in the signature’s CMS. Your validation workflow will have two parallel tracks: verify the PDF signature, and verify the QEAA.

section.title: The Signing Flow
section.simpara: The most consequential feature of the EUDI wallet for this book’s audience is its ability to create qualified electronic signatures. eIDAS 2.0 Article 5a(4)(d) requires that the wallet support QES creation. Article 45e goes further: member states must ensure that QES creation through the wallet is offered to natural persons free of charge for non-professional purposes.
Free QES for every EU citizen. That sentence alone justifies this chapter.
The signing flow works as follows:

listitem.simpara: The user initiates a signing request. This could be from within the wallet app itself, or from an external application (a web portal, a desktop application, a government service) that redirects to the wallet via a protocol defined in the ARF (OpenID4VP — OpenID for Verifiable Presentations).

The wallet authenticates the user. The user confirms their identity using the wallet’s authentication mechanism — typically biometric (fingerprint or face recognition) or device PIN. This is the "sole control" requirement from eIDAS Article 26(c) and Article 29.

The wallet presents the document hash to a remote QSCD. The wallet does not contain the private signing key on the device itself. Instead, it communicates with a remote QSCD operated by a QTSP. The wallet sends a hash of the document to be signed (not the document itself — the hash is small and privacy-preserving).

The remote QSCD creates the signature. The QSCD — an HSM in the QTSP’s data center, certified as a Qualified Signature Creation Device — signs the hash with the user’s private key. The private key was generated on and never leaves the QSCD.

The signature is returned to the requesting application. The CMS SignedData (or the raw signature value, depending on the integration pattern) is returned to the application that initiated the signing request. The application embeds the signature in the PDF.

entry.simpara: Application presents document to user and requests signature (via OpenID4VP)
Wallet displays document summary and requests user authentication (biometric/PIN)
Wallet sends document hash to remote QSCD at QTSP (over mTLS, authenticated by wallet instance credentials)
QSCD signs the hash with user’s private key; returns CMS SignedData with qualified certificate and signature timestamp
Application receives CMS, embeds it in PDF /Contents, adds DSS data for LTV, and saves the signed PDF

section.title: Remote QSCD: Why the Key Is Not on the Phone
section.simpara: A natural question: why not put the signing key on the phone itself? Modern smartphones have secure elements and TEEs that can generate and store cryptographic keys.
The answer is QSCD certification.

A QSCD must be certified under Common Criteria EAL4+ (or equivalent) against specific Protection Profiles (EN 419 211 or EN 419 221). Smartphone secure elements are certified for payment (EMVCo) and some for FIDO authentication, but as of 2026, no smartphone secure element has been certified as a QSCD under the eIDAS Protection Profiles.

The certification gap is not primarily technical — modern secure elements are arguably as secure as certified smart cards. The gap is procedural: QSCD certification requires extensive evaluation against specific attack models, documentation of the manufacturing process, and ongoing compliance monitoring. Smartphone vendors have not pursued this certification because, until eIDAS 2.0, there was no market demand for it on consumer devices.

This may change. The ARF explicitly contemplates the possibility of "local QSCD" on a device — a future scenario where smartphone secure elements achieve QSCD certification and the signing key can reside on the phone. But for the initial wallet deployments, the architecture is remote QSCD.

note.simpara: Remote QSCD is not a compromise — it is the dominant model for QES today. Even before the EUDI wallet, most QES in the EU were created using remote QSCDs. Services like DocuSign (through its European qualified signing infrastructure), Swisscom, InfoCert, and D-Trust all operate remote QSCDs. The wallet extends this existing model to a new client (the smartphone) but does not fundamentally change the architecture.

The Cloud Signature Consortium (CSC) API — the industry-standard REST API for remote signing — is being adapted for wallet integration. If your application already supports CSC-based remote signing (Chapter 22), adapting it for wallet-initiated signing is a relatively small change.

section.title: The Sole Control Assurance Problem
section.simpara: eIDAS requires that the signing key be under the signatory’s "sole control" (Article 26(c)). When the key is on a smart card in the signer’s wallet, sole control is straightforward: the signer possesses the card and knows the PIN.

When the key is in a remote QSCD, sole control is more complex. The signer does not physically possess the key. The key is in an HSM in a data center, managed by a QTSP. Sole control must be established through authentication mechanisms that ensure only the authorized signer can trigger a signing operation with that key.
The SCAL (Sole Control Assurance Level) framework, defined in CEN EN 419 241-1, distinguishes two levels:

listitem.simpara: SCAL1: The QTSP relies on an external authentication mechanism (e.g., the wallet app’s biometric authentication) to authorize signing. The QTSP trusts the authentication assertion from the wallet.

SCAL2: The QTSP independently verifies the signer’s authorization — the signer must authenticate directly to the QTSP’s infrastructure (e.g., through a separate OTP sent by the QTSP), in addition to any wallet-level authentication.

section.simpara: SCAL2 is required for QES. This means the remote QSCD must have its own authentication of the signer, not just trust the wallet’s claim that the user authenticated.

In practice, this is implemented through a combination of: - Wallet-level biometric authentication (proves the user is holding the phone) - A cryptographic challenge from the QSCD that the wallet’s secure element signs (proves the specific wallet instance is authorized) - An additional PIN or biometric confirmation presented to the QSCD’s authentication service (proves the user explicitly authorized this specific signing operation)
The user experience goal is to make this feel like a single authentication step (touch fingerprint, confirm signing), while the underlying protocol satisfies SCAL2’s multi-factor requirement.

section.title: What Changes
section.simpara: The EUDI wallet does not change the PDF signature format. A PDF signed through the wallet contains the same structures as any other PAdES signature: a /Sig dictionary with /SubFilter ETSI.CAdES.detached, a /ByteRange covering the file, a /Contents value containing CMS SignedData, and (for PAdES-B-LTA) a DSS dictionary with certificates and revocation data.
What changes is the volume, the source, and the validation path.

Volume. Free QES for 450 million EU citizens will produce orders of magnitude more qualified signatures than the current market. Your validation code must handle this volume. If your validation involves synchronous EUTL lookups for every signature, you need caching. If your trust store is updated manually, you need automation.

Source. Wallet-generated signatures will come from certificates issued by QTSPs participating in the wallet ecosystem. These QTSPs will be on the EUTL. They may or may not be on Adobe’s AATL. If they are not on the AATL, Acrobat users will see yellow warnings on legitimate QES — which will create pressure on Adobe to either auto-consume the EUTL or expand the AATL.

Validation path. Wallet-generated signatures chain through a trust path that includes: the signer’s qualified certificate (issued by a QTSP) → the QTSP’s intermediate/root certificate → the EUTL entry for the QTSP. This is the same chain as any other QES. The novelty is that the signer is a consumer on a smartphone, not an enterprise user with a signing portal.

section.title: PAdES-B-LTA from Mobile
section.simpara: The wallet ecosystem targets PAdES-B-LTA as the default profile for wallet-generated signatures. This is the correct engineering decision: wallet-generated signatures will be used for contracts, government submissions, and official documents that require long-term verifiability.
Producing a PAdES-B-LTA signature from a mobile device requires:

listitem.simpara: Signature creation (CMS SignedData with ESS-signing-certificate-v2, via remote QSCD)
Signature timestamp (RFC 3161 TimeStampToken from a TSA, embedded as unsigned CMS attribute)
DSS population (certificates, OCSP responses, CRLs for the entire chain)
Document timestamp (a separate signature dictionary with SubFilter ETSI.RFC3161 covering the entire file including DSS)

section.simpara: Steps 1-2 are performed by the QSCD/QTSP infrastructure. Steps 3-4 are performed by the application that embeds the signature in the PDF — which may be a server-side service, not the mobile device itself.
The typical architecture:

listitem.simpara: The mobile app sends the document hash to the QTSP via the wallet
The QTSP signs the hash on the QSCD and obtains a signature timestamp from its TSA
The CMS (with timestamp) is returned to the application server
The server embeds the CMS in the PDF, populates the DSS, and adds a document timestamp
The signed PDF is returned to the user or submitted to the relying party

section.simpara: The mobile device never needs to perform the PDF manipulation itself — that is handled by the server. This is a practical necessity: PDF signing with LTV data is computationally modest but requires PDF parsing libraries that are not available on all mobile platforms.

warning.simpara: The code above is simplified for illustration. A production implementation must handle: pre-allocating the /Contents placeholder, coordinating the byte range computation with the CMS injection, error handling for TSA timeouts, retry logic for remote QSCD communication failures, and verification of the completed signature. Chapter 29 covers the full PDFBox signing pipeline.

section.title: The Large-Scale Pilots
section.simpara: The European Commission is funding four Large-Scale Pilots (LSPs) that test the EUDI wallet in real-world scenarios across multiple member states. Each pilot covers different use cases and involves different member states.

table.title: EUDI wallet Large-Scale Pilots (as of early 2026)

entry.simpara: Government services (eID authentication), banking (customer onboarding), mobile driving license, SIM registration, QES signing
Norway, Denmark, Sweden, Finland, Iceland, Latvia, Italy
Cross-border payment account opening, PID issuance and verification, age verification, organizational identity
Sweden, Finland, France, Germany, Czech Republic, Croatia, Italy, Austria, Greece, Romania
Travel (digital travel credentials), education (diploma attestation), digital payments, organizational wallet use cases
Spain, Austria, Belgium, France, Germany, Italy, Luxembourg, Poland, Romania, Sweden
Education (European Digital Credentials for Learning), social security (Professional Qualifications, Portable Document A1)

section.title: What the Pilots Are Testing
section.simpara: The signing-relevant findings from the pilots (as of early 2026, with pilots still running):

QES from mobile works. The core signing flow — user authentication, hash submission to remote QSCD, CMS return, PDF embedding — has been demonstrated successfully across multiple implementations. The user experience typically involves a single biometric confirmation (fingerprint or face recognition) with a clear "sign this document" prompt.

Cross-border validation is the challenge. Creating the signature is (relatively) straightforward. Validating it across borders — ensuring that a signature created by a French citizen’s wallet validates correctly in a German government portal — requires all parties to consume the EUTL, support the same PAdES profiles, and handle certificate chains from unfamiliar QTSPs. This is exactly the same interoperability problem discussed earlier in this chapter, amplified by scale.

TSA integration needs standardization. Different wallet implementations use different TSAs, with different timestamp token sizes, different certificate chains, and different response times. The pilots have exposed edge cases where timestamp tokens from one TSA cause validation issues in validators that are not prepared for the TSA’s specific certificate chain length.

User comprehension is limited. In pilot user testing, most participants understood "sign this document" but did not understand what a qualified electronic signature is, how it differs from a basic electronic signature, or why it has legal equivalence to a handwritten signature. This is not a technical problem, but it has technical consequences: if users do not understand the significance of QES, they may not treat the signing authentication with appropriate care.

listitem.simpara: Support wallet-initiated signing flows. The wallet communicates with your application via OpenID4VP (OpenID for Verifiable Presentations). You will need to implement the RP (Relying Party) side of the OpenID4VP protocol to receive signing requests from the wallet.

Handle remote QSCD signatures. Your application will receive a CMS SignedData blob from the QTSP, not generate it locally. Your signing pipeline must support "external CMS" injection — computing the byte range, sending the hash for external signing, receiving the CMS, and embedding it in the PDF.

Pre-allocate generously. Wallet-generated CMS may include longer certificate chains than you are accustomed to (government PKI chains are often deeper than commercial CA chains). Pre-allocate at least 64 KB for PAdES-B-LTA.

Add DSS and document timestamps server-side. The mobile device will not populate the DSS or add document timestamps. Your server must do this, which means your server needs access to OCSP responders and CRL distribution points for the signer’s certificate chain, plus a TSA for the document timestamp.

Consume the EUTL programmatically. Wallet-generated signatures chain to QTSPs on the EUTL. If your validator uses a static trust store, it will reject these signatures. Implement automated EUTL consumption — fetch the LOT (List of Trusted Lists), parse each member state’s trusted list, extract the trust anchors, and update your trust store on a regular schedule (daily is typical).

Cache the EUTL. With millions of wallet-generated signatures to validate, you cannot fetch the EUTL for every validation. Cache the parsed trust anchors with a TTL (time-to-live) and refresh periodically.

Support deep certificate chains. Government PKI chains can be 4-5 certificates deep (signer → intermediate 1 → intermediate 2 → root). Ensure your chain-building code handles this without performance degradation.

Report the PAdES profile level. Wallet-generated signatures should be PAdES-B-LTA. If a wallet-generated signature is missing LTV data (B-T or B-B instead of B-LTA), this indicates a problem in the signing pipeline. Report the profile level to help the signer or their application diagnose the issue.

Prepare for QEAA validation. If your application needs to verify not just who signed but what qualifications the signer has (e.g., a medical prescription signed by a doctor), you will need to validate QEAAs in addition to the PDF signature. This is a separate validation path: the QEAA is a signed credential (SD-JWT or mdoc) that you verify against the issuing QTSP’s certificate on the EUTL.

Update your trust store management. If your enterprise trust store is managed manually (a Java cacerts file, a Windows certificate store), automate it. Wallet-generated signatures will come from QTSPs that may not be in your current trust store.

Budget for EUTL integration. Consuming the EUTL is not trivial: the LOT and individual trusted lists are XML documents signed with specific certificates, using a specific schema (ETSI TS 119 612). The EU DSS framework (Chapter 23) provides a reference implementation, but integrating it into your architecture requires engineering effort.

Plan for volume. If your system currently validates hundreds of qualified signatures per day, prepare for thousands or tens of thousands. The wallet will democratize QES, and your systems will see the increase.

Test with pilot certificates. The EUDI wallet pilots issue test certificates that can be used for integration testing. Engage with the pilot programs relevant to your member state and obtain test credentials for your validation pipeline.

chapter.title: The Open Questions
chapter.simpara: The EUDI wallet is ambitious, unprecedented, and not yet fully defined. Several significant questions remain open as of early 2026:

Will users actually adopt it? Mandating reliance parties to accept the wallet creates demand, but the wallet still needs a compelling user experience. If the signing flow feels cumbersome — multiple authentication steps, slow QSCD response times, confusing consent screens — users will avoid it.

Will Apple and Google cooperate? The wallet runs on smartphones, which means it needs integration with iOS and Android. Both platforms control access to NFC, secure elements, and biometric APIs. The EU Digital Markets Act (DMA) requires gatekeepers to allow third-party wallet apps to access device hardware, but the implementation details are being negotiated, and the mobile platforms have commercial interests in their own wallet ecosystems (Apple Pay, Google Pay).

What happens with AATL? If hundreds of millions of qualified signatures are created through the wallet but the signing certificates are not in Adobe’s AATL, Acrobat users will see yellow warnings on legitimate QES. This creates political pressure on Adobe to either accept the EUTL as a trust anchor by default or face regulatory action. The outcome will shape the PDF signature ecosystem for years.

Will the credential format converge? The current ARF supports both SD-JWT and mdoc. Supporting two formats doubles the implementation effort for relying parties. The industry is watching to see if one format emerges as dominant or if the dual-format approach persists.

How will cross-border QES work in practice? The technical framework is defined. The legal framework mandates mutual recognition. But whether a German government portal will actually accept a Romanian wallet-generated QES without friction — that depends on whether all the implementation details are correct across two different member state implementations, two different QTSP certificate chains, and two different validation stacks.

The answers to these questions will determine whether the EUDI wallet becomes the most significant development in digital signing since eIDAS itself — or another ambitious European digital project that works in theory and disappoints in practice.

note.simpara: The EUDI wallet is a moving target. The ARF is being updated, implementing acts are being published, and pilots are generating findings that feed back into the specifications. If you are reading this in 2027 or later, some of the specifics in this chapter may have changed. The architectural principles — remote QSCD, selective disclosure, EUTL-based trust, PAdES-B-LTA as the target profile — are stable. The protocol details and credential format choices may have evolved.

listitem.simpara: eIDAS 2.0 (Regulation (EU) 2024/1183 (eIDAS 2.0)) mandates that every EU member state offer a digital identity wallet to citizens by end of 2026, with QES creation capability included
The EUDI wallet is a smartphone application that stores government-issued identity data (PID) and qualified attribute attestations (QEAAs) and can create qualified electronic signatures
Remote QSCD is the signing architecture: the private key lives in a certified HSM operated by a QTSP, not on the phone itself — the wallet provides authentication and sole control
Selective disclosure (via SD-JWT or mdoc) allows users to share only the attributes a relying party needs, without revealing their full identity
Free QES for citizens (Article 45e) will dramatically increase the volume of qualified signatures — plan your infrastructure for orders-of-magnitude growth
PAdES-B-LTA is the target profile for wallet-generated signatures — server-side DSS population and document timestamping are required
EUTL consumption is essential — wallet-generated signatures chain to QTSPs that may not be in your current trust store; automate EUTL updates
Four Large-Scale Pilots (POTENTIAL, NOBID, EWC, DC4EU) are testing the wallet across sectors and member states; engage with them for test credentials
Open questions remain about user adoption, platform cooperation (Apple/Google), AATL integration, credential format convergence, and real-world cross-border validation
The architectural principles are PDF-native — if you understand PAdES, certificate chains, and the EUTL, you understand the cryptographic foundation of the EUDI wallet

partintro.simpara: Before we write a single line of signing code, we need to talk about the tools we’re using — where they came from, why they exist, and what baggage they carry. If you’ve ever wondered why there are three different "iText" libraries on Maven Central, why PDFBox’s API changed so dramatically between version 2 and version 3, or why a library from the year 2000 with known bugs is still running in Fortune 500 production systems — this chapter is for you.

The Java PDF ecosystem is not just a collection of libraries. It is a story of open-source idealism, licensing betrayal (depending on who you ask), corporate acquisitions, community forks, and the kind of technical debt that accumulates when enterprises build critical infrastructure on a dependency and then the rules change. Understanding this history is not optional background reading — it directly affects the code you write, the signatures you produce, and the compatibility problems you will encounter.

chapter.title: The iText Saga: From PhD Project to Corporate Licensing Drama

section.title: Bruno Lowagie and the Birth of iText (2000–2009)
section.simpara: The story of Java PDF processing begins, for practical purposes, with Bruno Lowagie. In 2000, Lowagie — a Belgian mathematician working at a printing company — needed to generate PDF documents from Java. The options at the time were essentially nonexistent. Adobe had published the PDF specification, but the only tools that could create PDFs were Adobe’s own products (Acrobat, Distiller) and a handful of expensive commercial libraries. There was nothing usable in the open-source Java world.

So Lowagie wrote his own library. He called it iText, released it under the Mozilla Public License (MPL) and later the GNU Lesser General Public License (LGPL), and published it on SourceForge — the GitHub of its era. The LGPL license was critically important: it allowed companies to use iText in proprietary software without open-sourcing their own code, as long as they didn’t modify the iText library itself. This is the license model that made iText ubiquitous.

The timing was perfect. Java was exploding in enterprise environments. J2EE application servers were the dominant architecture. Companies needed to generate invoices, reports, contracts, and forms as PDFs — millions of them per day, in automated workflows. iText was free, it worked, and its API was straightforward:

This simplicity was revolutionary. You didn’t need to understand PDF internals. You didn’t need to manipulate dictionaries or worry about cross-reference tables. You told iText what you wanted on the page, and it produced a valid PDF. The library handled fonts, images, tables, forms, encryption, and — crucially for our purposes — digital signatures.

By the mid-2000s, iText was everywhere. It was embedded in JBoss, Alfresco, Jasper Reports, BIRT, and hundreds of other Java projects. Large banks, insurance companies, government agencies, and healthcare organizations had built their document generation pipelines on iText. The Maven coordinates com.lowagie:itext were among the most downloaded artifacts in the Java ecosystem.

section.title: The LGPL-to-AGPL Switch: The Day the Rules Changed (2009)
section.simpara: On December 1, 2009, Bruno Lowagie changed the license of iText from LGPL to AGPL (GNU Affero General Public License), starting with version 5.0.0.

This was, depending on your perspective, either a reasonable business decision or an act of betrayal against the community that had made iText successful. Both perspectives have merit, and understanding the controversy matters because its consequences are still playing out in production systems today.

What AGPL means in practice: The AGPL is like the GPL, but with one additional requirement: if you use an AGPL-licensed library in software that users interact with over a network (which includes web applications, SaaS products, and API services), you must release the entire source code of your application under the AGPL. Not just your modifications to iText — your entire application.

For the thousands of companies using iText in their proprietary Java backends — generating invoices, processing insurance claims, signing contracts — this was devastating. They had exactly three options:

listitem.simpara: Open-source their entire application under AGPL — unthinkable for most enterprises
Buy a commercial license from iText Group NV (the company Lowagie founded to monetize iText) — expensive, typically €5,000–€50,000+ per year depending on deployment scale
Stay on iText 2.1.7 — the last LGPL-licensed version, released in 2009 — forever

section.simpara: Most chose option 3.

Why Lowagie made the switch: Lowagie’s stated reasons were straightforward: he had spent nearly a decade maintaining a complex library for free, while companies worth billions used it to generate revenue without contributing back. He wanted to build a business. The AGPL created a forcing function: use iText for free in open-source projects, or pay for a commercial license. This is a perfectly legitimate business model — MongoDB, Elasticsearch, and Redis would later make similar moves (though with different licenses).

Why the community was angry: The anger wasn’t about the business model itself — it was about the trust violation. Enterprise architects had chosen iText because of its LGPL license. They had written it into procurement documents, gotten legal approval, and built systems that depended on it. The license change felt like a bait-and-switch: attract an enormous user base with a permissive license, then change the terms once everyone is locked in.

The fact that existing versions remained LGPL was cold comfort. iText 2.1.7 was already showing its age in 2009. By 2012, it had known bugs in font handling, incomplete PDF 1.7 support, and no support for the newer CMS structures that PAdES was beginning to require. But upgrading to iText 5.x meant either paying or going AGPL.

tip.simpara: If you encounter a PDF signed by a Java application that uses com.lowagie.text.pdf.PdfSignatureAppearance in its imports — that’s iText 2.x. The class was renamed to com.itextpdf.text.pdf.PdfSignatureAppearance in iText 5.x (note the package change from com.lowagie to com.itextpdf). The package name tells you which era of iText produced the signature.

section.title: The CMS Structure Problem: Why Old iText Signatures Look Different
section.simpara: Here’s where the licensing drama directly impacts signature compatibility.

iText 2.x (the LGPL version that much of the world is still using) produces CMS SignedData structures that are technically valid per RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS), but differ from what modern validators expect in several ways:

Encapsulated vs. Detached content: Early iText versions could produce CMS signatures with encapsulated content — meaning the signed hash was embedded inside the SignedData structure’s encapContentInfo field rather than being referenced externally via the PDF byte range. This was not wrong per the CMS spec, but it created problems:

listitem.simpara: Modern PAdES validators expect detached signatures (SubFilter adbe.pkcs7.detached or ETSI.CAdES.detached), where the signed content is the byte range in the PDF itself — not a copy inside the CMS
Some validators would see encapsulated content and assume the signature was standalone (not bound to the PDF), failing validation even though the cryptographic math was correct
The double-hash problem: if the signed bytes in the CMS didn’t match what a byte-range-aware validator computed from the PDF file, the signature appeared invalid

section.simpara: Missing signed attributes: iText 2.x did not always include the ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute that PAdES requires. Without this attribute, the signature is valid per ISO 32000-2 but not PAdES-compliant — which means it fails validation in any system that enforces PAdES profiles (including EU government validation services and Adobe Acrobat in its stricter validation modes).

SHA-1 default: iText 2.1.7 defaulted to SHA-1 for the message digest. SHA-1 was already theoretically weakened by 2009 (Wang and Yu’s collision attacks were published in 2005), and it was definitively broken in 2017 when the SHAttered attack produced actual colliding files. PDFs signed with iText 2.x before SHA-256 became the norm carry SHA-1 digests, which modern validators increasingly flag or reject.

warning.simpara: If your organization has a corpus of PDFs signed between 2005 and 2015 by Java applications, there is a significant chance they were signed with iText 2.x using SHA-1 and potentially using encapsulated (not detached) CMS content. These signatures may fail validation in modern tools.
To verify: extract the CMS SignedData from the /Contents entry and check:

listitem.simpara: Is encapContentInfo.eContentType set with actual content? (encapsulated — old style)
Is the digest algorithm SHA-1 (OID 1.3.14.3.2.26)? (deprecated)
Is the ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute present in signedAttrs? (missing = not PAdES-compliant)

warning.simpara: Chapter 31 provides code to perform all three checks.

section.title: iText 5.x, 7.x, and 8.x: The Commercial Evolution
section.simpara: After the AGPL switch, iText evolved rapidly — but behind a paywall for commercial use.

iText 5.x (2009–2016): Maintained the original API design but added PDF 2.0 features, improved CMS handling, and better PAdES support. The package name changed from com.lowagie to com.itextpdf. If you see both package prefixes in the same project’s classpath — and this happens more often than you’d think — you’re looking at a project that tried to use both the old LGPL version and the new AGPL version simultaneously, which is a recipe for classloading nightmares.

iText 7.x (2016–2023): A complete rewrite with a modular architecture. The API was entirely different from iText 5.x — migration was not trivial. iText 7 introduced separate modules for core PDF operations, forms, signing, and other features. The signing module (itext7-sign) included comprehensive PAdES support, including B-LTA profiles with embedded timestamps and revocation data. The commercial license became the primary offering, with AGPL remaining for open-source projects.

iText 8.x (2023–present): The current generation, continuing the modular architecture with added support for PDF 2.0 features, improved accessibility tagging, and enhanced signing capabilities. iText Group NV (now iText Software, headquartered in Ghent, Belgium, with offices in the US and Singapore) is a substantial commercial entity with paying enterprise customers worldwide.

The commercial iText is, by all accounts, a high-quality library. Its signing implementation is mature, well-tested, and PAdES-compliant. But at commercial license prices that can reach tens of thousands of euros per year for enterprise deployments, it is not an option for every project — which is why the open-source alternatives exist.

section.title: The IBM Connection
section.simpara: IBM’s involvement with iText is a lesser-known but important part of the story. In the early 2000s, IBM was one of the largest users of iText — embedding it in several of their enterprise products, including IBM Content Manager, IBM Filenet, and various WebSphere-based document processing systems.

IBM contributed patches and improvements back to the project during the LGPL era, which was entirely within the LGPL’s collaborative spirit. However, when the license changed to AGPL, IBM — like most large enterprises — was unwilling to either open-source their proprietary products or pay iText’s commercial licensing fees for a dependency that had previously been free.

This created an awkward situation: IBM’s products contained iText 2.x code, their customers expected continued PDF functionality, and the upgrade path required either legal procurement (commercial iText license) or engineering investment (migrate to a different library). IBM’s response, like most enterprises in this position, was a combination of staying on old versions and gradually migrating new development to Apache PDFBox — which was, by then, becoming mature enough for production use.

The IBM story is representative of what happened across the industry: hundreds of enterprises had iText deeply embedded in their systems, and the license change created a slow-motion migration that, in many cases, is still incomplete in 2026.

chapter.title: OpenPDF: The Community Fork That Refuses to Die

section.title: The Fork (2016)
section.simpara: When it became clear that iText 5.x was permanently AGPL for commercial use, and that iText 2.1.7 was accruing unfixed bugs and missing modern features, a group of developers forked iText 2.1.7 under the name OpenPDF (initially called LibrePDF).

The fork was hosted on GitHub under the LibrePDF/OpenPDF repository, and it was explicitly positioned as the LGPL-licensed continuation of the original iText codebase.
