The initial stated goals were modest: fix bugs, add basic modern features (SHA-256 support, better font handling), and keep the library usable for projects that needed LGPL licensing.

section.title: What OpenPDF Is (and Isn’t)
section.simpara: OpenPDF today (version 2.0.x, as of 2026) is a capable PDF generation library that maintains API compatibility with iText 2.x while adding features that the original 2.1.7 never had:

listitem.simpara: SHA-256/384/512 message digests for signing (critical — SHA-1 is no longer acceptable)
AES-256 encryption support
Improved font handling, including better CJK support and TrueType subsetting
Bug fixes for dozens of issues that were never patched in iText 2.1.7

section.simpara: However, OpenPDF also carries significant limitations:

listitem.simpara: It is built on the iText 2.x architecture, which was designed for PDF 1.7 and earlier
No PDF 2.0 support — cross-reference streams, tagged PDF improvements, and other 2.0 features are absent
Limited PAdES support — basic PAdES-B-B signatures are possible, but B-LT and B-LTA profiles (which require DSS dictionary construction and archival timestamps) are not natively supported
No built-in LTV (Long-Term Validation) — you’ll need to construct the DSS dictionary yourself
The contributor community is small — a handful of maintainers, not the large team that iText Group NV employs
Documentation is sparse — the iText 2.x book (Lowagie’s "iText in Action") is the closest thing to comprehensive documentation, and it describes an API from 2008

note.simpara: OpenPDF lives at com.github.librepdf:openpdf on Maven Central. Do not confuse it with com.lowagie:itext (the original iText 2.1.7) or com.itextpdf:itextpdf (the AGPL iText 5.x). All three exist on Maven Central. All three have different licenses, different capabilities, and different bugs. Getting the wrong one in your dependency tree is a common source of confusion — and potential legal liability.

section.title: Should You Use OpenPDF?
section.simpara: For PDF generation (creating invoices, reports, forms) where you need an LGPL-licensed library and don’t require PDF 2.0 features: OpenPDF is a reasonable choice.

For digital signatures: OpenPDF can produce basic CMS signatures, but for PAdES compliance, long-term validation, and the kind of robust signing infrastructure this book teaches, PDFBox is the better choice. PDFBox’s signing API is more actively maintained, better tested against the Bochum attack scenarios, and has a larger community contributing security fixes.

That said — if you have an existing codebase built on iText 2.x that you cannot easily migrate, OpenPDF is the path of least resistance for getting off the unmaintained 2.1.7 version while keeping your existing API calls intact.

section.title: Origin Story: Ben Litchfield and the Apache Incubation
section.simpara: Apache PDFBox started as PDFBox — a standalone project created by Ben Litchfield in 2002. Litchfield, working as a Java developer, needed to read existing PDF documents — not just create them (which was iText’s primary strength at the time). He needed to extract text, parse structures, and manipulate the low-level PDF object model. iText was designed as a PDF writer; PDFBox was designed as a PDF reader that could also write.
This fundamental design difference — reader-first vs. writer-first — is still visible in the APIs today:

listitem.simpara: iText/OpenPDF excels at high-level document construction: "add a paragraph here, put a table there, generate the PDF"
PDFBox excels at low-level document manipulation: "give me the COS dictionary for object 42, let me modify the annotation rectangle, show me the raw font encoding"

section.simpara: For digital signature work, PDFBox’s reader-first architecture is a significant advantage. Signing a PDF requires understanding the existing document structure, computing byte ranges, parsing existing signatures, and manipulating specific dictionaries — all tasks where low-level access is essential.

In 2008, PDFBox entered the Apache Software Foundation incubator process, and in 2009 it graduated as a top-level Apache project under the Apache License 2.0 — one of the most permissive open-source licenses available. Unlike iText’s LGPL→AGPL journey, PDFBox has been Apache-licensed since inception and will remain so. This licensing certainty is one of the reasons enterprises migrated to PDFBox.

section.title: PDFBox 2.x: The Workhorse (2016–2023)
section.simpara: PDFBox 2.0 was the version that brought PDFBox into mainstream production use. It featured:

listitem.simpara: A complete COS (Carousel Object System) model for low-level PDF manipulation
Text extraction with position information
PDF rendering (via PDFRenderer)
AcroForm support (creating, filling, and flattening forms)
Digital signature support via PDDocument.addSignature()
Font handling (Type1, TrueType, CID fonts, subsetting)

section.simpara: The signing API in PDFBox 2.x was functional but had rough edges. The SignatureInterface required you to provide a sign(InputStream) method that received the bytes to sign and returned the CMS SignedData as a byte array. The actual CMS construction was your responsibility — typically using BouncyCastle (we’ll discuss this later). PDFBox handled the PDF-specific parts: creating the signature dictionary, computing the byte range, reserving space for /Contents, and performing the incremental save.

This separation of concerns — PDFBox handles the PDF, you handle the cryptography — is elegant in theory but demanding in practice. Getting the CMS structure right for PAdES compliance requires deep knowledge of signed attributes, certificate embedding, and timestamp handling. Chapters 29 and 30 provide the complete implementation.

section.title: PDFBox 3.x: The Breaking Change (2024)
section.simpara: PDFBox 3.0 was released in 2024 as a major version bump with breaking API changes. The migration was significant enough to catch many projects off guard:

warning.simpara: PDFBox 3.x is not a drop-in replacement for 2.x. If you’re upgrading, budget significant development time. The changes are pervasive and affect every area of the API.

section.simpara: The most impactful changes for signing:

The signing API itself (PDDocument.addSignature(), SignatureInterface, PDSignature) remained structurally similar, but the surrounding changes — especially in document loading, font handling, and content stream creation — mean that any signing code that also creates visual signature appearances needs substantial rework.

tip.simpara: Migration strategy: Don’t try to upgrade PDFBox in-place. Create a new branch, update the dependency, let the compiler tell you every break, and fix them systematically. The PDFBox team maintains a migration guide that covers the major changes, but the edge cases (especially around font encoding and content stream APIs) will require reading the 3.x Javadoc carefully.

section.title: The PDFBox Signing Infrastructure: What’s Under the Hood
section.simpara: PDFBox’s signing implementation is one of the most impressive pieces of open-source PDF engineering. It’s worth understanding the high-level architecture, because when something goes wrong during signing — and it will — you need to know where to look.
The signing process in PDFBox involves these internal steps:

listitem.simpara: Signature dictionary creation: PDFBox creates the /Sig dictionary with the properties you set (/Filter, /SubFilter, /Name, /M, etc.)

Space estimation: PDFBox needs to know how much space to reserve for the /Contents hex string before the signature is computed — because the signature covers the bytes outside the /Contents gap, and the file must be written before the signature can be calculated. The default is 16KB (32,768 hex characters), but PAdES-B-LTA signatures with embedded timestamps and revocation data can easily exceed this.
Byte range computation: PDFBox calculates the /ByteRange array — the two regions of the file that the signature covers (everything before and after the /Contents value)
Incremental save: The modified PDF is written as an incremental update — appended to the original file, preserving previous revisions
Signing callback: PDFBox passes the byte range data (as an InputStream) to your SignatureInterface.sign() implementation
Signature injection: The returned CMS bytes are hex-encoded and written into the reserved /Contents space

section.simpara: If the CMS signature is larger than the reserved space, the signing fails. If it’s smaller (which is normal), the remaining space is filled with zeros — the zero-padding that you see in the hex dump of any signed PDF’s /Contents entry.

This architecture means that PDFBox does not construct the CMS signature itself. It provides the bytes to sign, and you provide the signature. This is both a strength (maximum flexibility — you can use any CMS library, any algorithm, any certificate) and a burden (you must get the CMS right yourself). That’s why the next chapter is dedicated entirely to BouncyCastle CMS construction.

chapter.title: The Legion of the Bouncy Castle

section.title: Why BouncyCastle Exists
section.simpara: Java has a built-in cryptographic provider — the SunJSSE and SunJCE providers that ship with every JDK. So why does almost every Java PDF signing project use BouncyCastle instead?
Three reasons:

listitem.simpara: CMS support: The JDK’s built-in providers do not include a CMS (SignedData) implementation. They provide the raw cryptographic primitives (RSA, ECDSA, SHA-256, etc.), but not the higher-level CMS container format that PDF signatures require. BouncyCastle’s bcpkix module provides CMSSignedDataGenerator, CMSSignedData, and all the ASN.1 infrastructure needed to construct and parse CMS structures.

Algorithm breadth: BouncyCastle supports algorithms that the JDK providers don’t — including EdDSA (Ed25519, Ed448), GOST, SM2, and various other national or emerging algorithms. While PDF signatures currently use RSA and ECDSA almost exclusively, the ability to work with less common algorithms is valuable for government and specialized applications.

ASN.1 library: BouncyCastle’s ASN.1 library (bcutil and bcprov) is the de facto standard for Java ASN.1 encoding/decoding. When you need to parse a certificate, construct a signed attribute, or decode an OCSP response, BouncyCastle’s ASN.1 classes are what you use.

section.title: The BouncyCastle Ecosystem
section.simpara: BouncyCastle is not a single JAR — it’s a family of related libraries:

table.title: BouncyCastle modules relevant to PDF signing

entry.simpara: Core cryptographic provider: symmetric/asymmetric ciphers, hashes, key generation, random number generation. Register via Security.addProvider(new BouncyCastleProvider())
CMS SignedData construction/parsing, PKCS#10 certificate requests, OCSP, timestamp protocol (RFC 3161), certificate path validation. This is the module you need for PDF signing.
PEM reader/writer, ASN.1 dump utilities, base64 encoding. Pulled in as a transitive dependency.

important.simpara: You must explicitly register the BouncyCastle provider before using it. If you forget this step, you’ll get cryptic NoSuchAlgorithmException errors at runtime.

In Spring Boot applications, put this in a @Configuration class with a @PostConstruct method. In standalone applications, put it in your main() method. Forgetting this registration is the #1 cause of "it works in my IDE but fails in production" signing issues.

section.title: BouncyCastle Version History and the -jdk18on Suffix
section.simpara: BouncyCastle’s artifact naming has changed over the years, which causes dependency confusion:

listitem.simpara: bcprov-jdk15 — for JDK 1.5 (ancient, do not use)
bcprov-jdk15on — for JDK 1.5 and later (the long-lived artifact ID)
bcprov-jdk18on — for JDK 1.8 (Java 8) and later (current, use this)

section.simpara: The jdk18on variants (introduced with BouncyCastle 1.71) dropped support for Java versions before 8 and added Java module system (module-info.java) support. If you’re on Java 11 or later (and for PDF signing with PDFBox 3.x, you should be on Java 17+), use the jdk18on artifacts exclusively.

warning.simpara: Do not mix jdk15on and jdk18on artifacts in the same classpath. BouncyCastle uses a provider registration model, and having two versions of the provider registered simultaneously causes unpredictable failures — the wrong version may handle a given algorithm, producing incompatible ASN.1 structures.

chapter.title: EU DSS: The European Commission’s Signing Framework

section.title: What EU DSS Is
section.simpara: There is one more library that deserves its own section — not because it replaces PDFBox or BouncyCastle, but because it builds on top of them and adds a layer that would take months to implement yourself.

EU DSS (Digital Signature Services) is an open-source Java framework developed and maintained by Nowina Solutions under contract from the European Commission. It is the reference implementation for creating and validating digital signatures that comply with the European eIDAS regulation — which means it implements the full PAdES, CAdES, XAdES, and JAdES signature profiles, including the complex long-term validation (LTV) workflows that individual libraries like PDFBox cannot do alone.
The project lives at github.com/esig/dss and is released under the LGPL 2.1 license. The Maven coordinates follow the pattern eu.europa.ec.joinup.sd-dss:dss-*.

section.title: Why EU DSS Matters for PDF Signatures
section.simpara: If your application needs to produce PAdES-B-LTA signatures — the gold standard for long-term archival signatures that remain verifiable for decades — you have two choices:

listitem.simpara: Build it yourself: Use PDFBox for the PDF layer, BouncyCastle for CMS construction, write your own timestamp client (RFC 3161), implement OCSP and CRL fetching, construct the DSS (Document Security Store) dictionary, embed the VRI (Validation Related Information), and manage archival timestamp chains. This is what Chapters 29–31 of this book teach. It works. It is also hundreds of hours of development and testing.

Use EU DSS: Import the dss-pades module, configure a PAdESSignatureParameters object, provide a SignatureTokenConnection (which wraps your certificate and private key), and let the framework handle everything — CMS construction, signed attribute inclusion, timestamp requests, revocation data embedding, DSS dictionary creation, and incremental save.

section.simpara: Here’s what a PAdES-B-LTA signature looks like with EU DSS:

Compare this to implementing the same B-LTA profile manually with PDFBox + BouncyCastle — the EU DSS version handles the timestamp embedding, OCSP/CRL fetching, DSS dictionary construction, and VRI population automatically.

section.title: EU DSS Architecture
section.simpara: EU DSS is modular. You don’t need to import the entire framework — pick the modules relevant to your signature format:

table.title: EU DSS modules relevant to PDF signing

entry.simpara: PAdES signature creation and extension (B-B → B-T → B-LT → B-LTA). Uses PDFBox internally for PDF manipulation.
CAdES signature creation. The PAdES module delegates CMS construction to this.
Core document model (DSSDocument, InMemoryDocument, FileDocument) and service interfaces.
Online data loaders — HTTP clients for TSA, OCSP, and CRL fetching.
EU Trusted List (EUTL) parsing and validation. Essential for determining whether a signing certificate is qualified under eIDAS.
Generates detailed validation reports following ETSI TS 119 102-2 — the same validation report format that EU government validation services produce.
SignatureTokenConnection implementations: PKCS#12 file, PKCS#11 (HSM), JKS keystore, MS-CAPI (Windows certificate store).

section.title: EU DSS vs. Rolling Your Own
section.simpara: The honest comparison:

listitem.simpara: Compliance out of the box: EU DSS is the European Commission’s reference implementation. If you use it correctly, your signatures will pass validation on the EU’s official validation service (DSS demonstration webapp) and on member-state validation services. This is not a guarantee you get from hand-rolling with PDFBox + BouncyCastle.

LTV complexity hidden: Long-term validation requires fetching OCSP responses, downloading CRLs, embedding them in the PDF’s DSS dictionary, creating VRI entries, and adding archival timestamps. EU DSS does all of this. Implementing it yourself is a significant effort.

Trusted List integration: The dss-tsl-validation module can load and parse the EU Trusted List, letting you programmatically determine whether a signing certificate is qualified. Building this yourself means implementing ETSI TS 119 612 XML parsing and certificate matching — nontrivial.
Validation reports: EU DSS generates ETSI-compliant validation reports that are accepted by auditors and compliance officers.

simpara.emphasis: Advantages of rolling your own (PDFBox + BouncyCastle):

listitem.simpara: Full control: You understand every byte. When something fails, you know exactly where to look because you wrote every layer.

No framework weight: EU DSS pulls in a substantial dependency tree. If you only need PAdES-B-B (baseline signature without timestamps or LTV), you can do it with PDFBox + BouncyCastle in about 200 lines of code — EU DSS adds a megabytes of framework for something achievable with direct library calls.
Learning: Building it yourself teaches you the spec. Using EU DSS teaches you the framework’s API. For a book like this one, understanding comes from building.

Customization: EU DSS makes assumptions about how signatures should be constructed. If your use case deviates (unusual SubFilter values, custom signed attributes, non-standard timestamp configurations), you may fight the framework rather than benefit from it.

tip.simpara: Our recommendation: Learn to build signatures with PDFBox + BouncyCastle first (Chapters 29–31). Then, when you need PAdES-B-LTA or EU Trusted List integration in production, use EU DSS — you’ll understand what it’s doing under the hood, and you’ll be able to debug it when it behaves unexpectedly.
Chapter 23 provides a complete walkthrough of EU DSS for PAdES signing and validation.

section.title: EU DSS Version History
section.simpara: EU DSS follows a rapid release cycle, with major versions roughly annually:

listitem.simpara: DSS 5.x (2017–2022): The long-lived stable series. Most documentation and blog posts you’ll find online reference 5.x APIs.

DSS 6.x (2023–present): The current generation, with Java 17+ as the minimum, updated dependencies (including PDFBox 3.x for the PAdES module), and improved validation reporting. The API changed significantly between 5.x and 6.x — if you’re following tutorials, make sure they match your version.

warning.simpara: EU DSS 6.x uses PDFBox 3.x internally. If your project also uses PDFBox directly (for example, for document manipulation before signing), you must ensure version alignment. Having DSS pull in PDFBox 3.0.0 while your code uses PDFBox 3.0.1 can cause subtle NoSuchMethodError exceptions at runtime. Use Maven’s <dependencyManagement> to lock the PDFBox version across your entire project.

chapter.title: Analysis and Debugging Tools
chapter.simpara: A signing implementation is only as good as your ability to inspect what it produces. The following tools are indispensable for PDF signature development.

section.title: RUPS: The PDF Object Explorer
section.simpara: RUPS (Reading and Updating PDF Syntax) is a Java Swing application that lets you browse the internal object structure of a PDF file. It was originally developed by Bruno Lowagie as a companion to iText, and it shows every COS object, dictionary key, stream, and cross-reference entry in the file.
For signature work, RUPS is invaluable because it lets you:

listitem.simpara: Inspect the /Sig dictionary and all its keys (/ByteRange, /Contents, /SubFilter, /Filter)
Navigate the field tree to see how signature fields are structured
View the /AcroForm dictionary and its /SigFlags
Examine /Lock and /SV (Seed Value) dictionaries
Browse incremental updates to see what changed between revisions
Inspect the raw byte content of the /Contents hex string

section.simpara: RUPS is available on GitHub at LibrePDF/OpenPDF (it’s bundled with the OpenPDF project). It’s also available as a standalone download. The interface is spartan — it’s a developer tool, not a user-facing product — but once you learn to navigate the tree view, it becomes the fastest way to understand what’s actually inside a PDF.

tip.simpara: When debugging a signature failure, the first thing you should do is open the PDF in RUPS and check:

listitem.simpara: Does the /Sig dictionary exist? (Navigate: Root → AcroForm → Fields → [signature field] → V)
Is the /ByteRange array plausible? (Four integers, the first should be 0, the last should be close to the file size)
Is /Contents non-empty? (Should be a long hex string, not all zeros)
What is the /SubFilter? (Should be adbe.pkcs7.detached or ETSI.CAdES.detached)

tip.simpara: If any of these are wrong, you’ve found your problem before even looking at the CMS.

section.title: veraPDF: The Compliance Validator
section.simpara: veraPDF is the industry-standard PDF/A validation tool, developed by the Open Preservation Foundation and the PDF Association. While its primary mission is PDF/A compliance checking, it includes PDF analysis capabilities that are useful for signature work:

listitem.simpara: Policy-based validation — you can define custom validation profiles that check signature-specific requirements
Feature extraction — enumerate all signatures, certificates, and cryptographic properties in a PDF
Structural analysis — verify that the PDF structure conforms to the specification

section.simpara: veraPDF is available as a CLI tool, a Java library (which you can embed in your validation pipeline), and a GUI application. It’s the validator that national archives and government agencies use to verify PDF/A compliance — if your signed PDFs also need to be PDF/A compliant (as many government document workflows require), veraPDF is essential.

section.title: QPDF: The Swiss Army Knife
section.simpara: QPDF is a C++ command-line tool (created by Jay Berkenbilt) that can inspect, transform, and repair PDF files at the structural level. It’s not Java, but every Java PDF developer should have it installed.
Key capabilities for signature work:

listitem.simpara: qpdf --show-xref — displays the cross-reference table, showing where every object lives in the file. Critical for understanding byte range issues.
qpdf --show-object=N — displays a specific object by number. Faster than navigating a GUI when you know the object number from an error message.
qpdf --check — validates the PDF’s structural integrity. If this reports problems, your signing code has a bug.

qpdf --qdf — produces a "QDF" (QPDF data format) output where the PDF is restructured for human readability, with objects in order and comments showing object numbers. Invaluable for understanding what incremental saves changed.
qpdf --linearize — linearizes the PDF for fast web viewing. Do not linearize after signing — it destroys the signature by reorganizing the file.

caution.simpara: Several QPDF operations will destroy existing signatures:

listitem.simpara: --linearize — reorganizes the entire file
--normalize-content=y — reprocesses content streams
--object-streams=generate — restructures object storage
Any operation that produces a non-incremental output

caution.simpara: Always use --copy-encryption=source.pdf and check whether the operation preserves incremental structure before running it on a signed PDF.

section.title: MuTool (from MuPDF)
section.simpara: MuTool is the command-line companion to MuPDF (Artifex’s open-source PDF renderer). Its mutool show command provides quick access to PDF objects:
section.programlisting: mutool show document.pdf trailer # Show trailer dictionary mutool show document.pdf xref # Show cross-reference table mutool show document.pdf 42 0 # Show object 42, generation 0 mutool show document.pdf outline # Show document outline/bookmarks
section.simpara: MuTool is lightweight and fast — useful for quick inspections when you don’t want to launch a GUI tool.

section.title: OpenSSL: CMS Verification Outside the PDF Context
section.simpara: For verifying the CMS signature independently of the PDF wrapper, OpenSSL is the standard tool:
section.programlisting:
# Extract the raw signature bytes from /Contents (after hex-decoding) # and save as signature.der
# Verify the CMS structure openssl cms -inform DER -in signature.der -noout -cmsout -print
# Verify the signature against the signed data openssl cms -inform DER -in signature.der -verify \ -content signed-data.bin \ -CAfile ca-bundle.pem \ -out /dev/null
section.simpara: This separates the CMS verification from the PDF byte-range logic — if OpenSSL says the CMS is valid but your PDF validator says the signature is invalid, the problem is in the byte range handling, not the cryptography.

section.title: ASN.1 Dump Tools
section.simpara: When debugging CMS issues, being able to read the raw ASN.1 structure is essential. Several tools help:

listitem.simpara: openssl asn1parse — parses DER-encoded ASN.1, showing the tag-length-value tree
BouncyCastle’s ASN1Dump — org.bouncycastle.asn1.util.ASN1Dump.dumpAsString(obj) — prints any ASN.1 object as a readable tree from Java code
dumpasn1 (Peter Gutmann) — the original ASN.1 dumper, still the most detailed output
Web-based tools — several websites parse ASN.1/DER online; useful for quick checks but do not upload production signatures to third-party websites

chapter.title: Choosing the Right Library for Your Project
chapter.simpara: After all this history, here is the practical guidance:

table.title: Library selection for PDF signature projects

section.title: Our Recommendation
section.simpara: For the projects this book targets — production signing services, validation pipelines, signature analysis tools — Apache PDFBox 3.x with BouncyCastle is the default recommendation:

listitem.simpara: Apache 2.0 license: No surprises, no AGPL obligations, no license fees. This will not change.

Low-level access: Signing requires understanding and manipulating the raw PDF structure. PDFBox’s COS model gives you direct access. iText and OpenPDF abstract this away, which is convenient until you need to debug a byte-range computation or inspect a malformed signature dictionary.
Active maintenance: PDFBox has a dedicated Apache community, regular releases, and responsive handling of security issues. The Bochum attacks were patched quickly.
Separation of concerns: PDFBox handles the PDF, BouncyCastle handles the crypto. You understand both layers because you built both layers. When something breaks, you know exactly where to look.

section.simpara: The trade-off is that PDFBox makes you build more yourself — especially the CMS layer. That’s what Chapters 29–31 are for.

tip.simpara: The best way to learn is to build it yourself. Using a library that does everything for you (like commercial iText) gets you to production faster, but leaves you helpless when something goes wrong. With PDFBox + BouncyCastle, you understand every byte of the signature because you put it there. When a customer reports that their signature "shows invalid in Adobe but valid in Foxit," you’ll know exactly where to look — because you wrote the code that constructed the CMS, computed the byte range, and injected the signature.

chapter.title: The Ecosystem at a Glance
chapter.simpara: To close this chapter, here is a summary of every tool and library we’ve discussed, organized by function:

entry.simpara: Default choice for this book. Reader-first architecture.
Community fork of iText 2.x. Good for legacy migration.
De facto standard for Java CMS. Register provider explicitly.
European Commission reference implementation. PAdES-B-B through B-LTA, validation reports, EU Trusted List integration.
GUI object browser. First tool to reach for.
Industry-standard validator. Also useful for general PDF analysis.
CMS verification independent of PDF context.

chapter.simpara: Every tool in this table will appear at some point in the remaining chapters. Install them now — when you’re debugging a signature failure at 2 AM, you don’t want to be downloading tools.

listitem.simpara: iText’s license change (LGPL → AGPL in 2009) split the Java PDF world in two. The consequences — old versions in production, community forks, enterprise migrations — are still visible in 2026.
OpenPDF is the LGPL continuation of iText 2.x. Useful for legacy migration but limited for modern PAdES signing.
PDFBox + BouncyCastle is the recommended stack for new signing projects. Apache-licensed, actively maintained, gives you low-level control.
PDFBox 3.x broke API compatibility with 2.x. Budget migration time. Key changes: Loader.loadPDF(), new font constructors, content stream API changes.
BouncyCastle provides the CMS layer that PDFBox doesn’t. Always register the provider explicitly. Use jdk18on artifacts.
RUPS, QPDF, veraPDF, OpenSSL — know your tools. PDF signature debugging is 50% knowing what to look at and 50% having the right tool to look at it.
The library you choose is a 10-year decision. Choose based on license stability, community health, and technical fit — not just today’s feature checklist.

partintro.simpara: Chapter 28 gave us the lay of the land. Now we write code. This chapter is a complete, production-quality walkthrough of PDF signing with Apache PDFBox 3.0.0 and BouncyCastle 1.80 on Java 21. Every code listing compiles, every class is self-contained, and every pitfall we describe is one we have hit in production — or watched someone else hit at 2 AM on a Friday.

We start with the core PDFBox signing API — PDDocument.addSignature(), SignatureInterface, and SignatureOptions — then build outward: the PDSignature dictionary and its fields, visible and invisible signature appearances, multi-signature workflows, external/HSM signing, certification signatures with DocMDP, timestamp embedding, and the migration traps waiting for anyone upgrading from PDFBox 2.x to 3.x.

By the end of this chapter, you will have a complete, production-ready signing class that produces PAdES-B-B and PAdES-B-T compliant signatures. Chapter 30 goes deeper into the BouncyCastle CMS construction that powers the actual cryptography.

chapter.title: The PDFBox Signing API: Three Moving Parts
chapter.simpara: PDFBox signing revolves around three objects that you wire together before calling saveIncremental(). Understanding what each one does — and what happens when you get the order wrong — is essential.

section.title: PDSignature: The Signature Dictionary
section.simpara: PDSignature is PDFBox’s Java representation of the PDF /Sig dictionary (ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.8.1). When you create a PDSignature and set its properties, you are building the dictionary that will be written into the PDF file.
section.programlisting: PDSignature signature = new PDSignature(); signature.setFilter(PDSignature.FILTER_ADOBE_PPKLITE); // /Filter signature.setSubFilter(COSName.getPDFName("ETSI.CAdES.detached")); // /SubFilter signature.setSignDate(Calendar.getInstance()); // /M signature.setName("Alice Smith"); // /Name signature.setReason("I approve this document"); // /Reason signature.setLocation("Berlin, Germany"); // /Location signature.setContactInfo("alice@example.com"); // /ContactInfo
section.simpara: Every setter maps directly to a key in the /Sig dictionary. The following table lists all the fields you can set, what they map to, and when you should care.

entry.simpara: The signature handler. Almost always Adobe.PPKLite. PDFBox provides FILTER_ADOBE_PPKLITE as a constant. Other values (Entrust.PPKEF, CICI.SignIt, VeriSign.PPKVS) exist in the spec but are rarely seen in the wild.

The encoding format of the signature value. For PAdES, use ETSI.CAdES.detached. For legacy compatibility, adbe.pkcs7.detached. The deprecated adbe.pkcs7.sha1 embeds the document hash as CMS content — do not use it in new code.

Claimed signing time. This is not the authoritative timestamp — it is the signer’s local clock. A TSA timestamp (PAdES-B-T) provides cryptographic proof of time. Set it anyway, because viewers display it when no TSA timestamp exists.
Signer’s name as a display string. PAdES validators typically extract the name from the signing certificate’s CN instead. Still useful for the human reader.
Why the document was signed. Free text. Some workflows require specific values ("Approved", "Reviewed").
Where the document was signed. Free text. "Berlin, Germany" or "Server Room 3" — whatever is meaningful.
Contact information for the signer. Rarely used in practice, but mandated by some government workflows.
The byte ranges of the signed content. You do not set this yourself. PDFBox computes it during saveIncremental(). If you manually set it, you will break the signature.

warning.simpara: Do not call setByteRange() manually. PDFBox calculates the byte range during the incremental save process. If you set it yourself, the byte range will not match the actual file offsets after writing, and the signature will be invalid. We have seen production code that does this — it always fails.

section.title: SignatureInterface: The Cryptographic Callback
section.simpara: SignatureInterface is a single-method interface:

PDFBox calls your sign() method during saveIncremental(). The InputStream parameter contains the bytes identified by the /ByteRange — that is, the entire PDF file minus the gap reserved for the /Contents hex string. Your job is to read those bytes, produce a CMS SignedData structure (or whatever your /SubFilter requires), and return the DER-encoded result.

The returned byte[] is hex-encoded and written into the /Contents field of the signature dictionary. If the returned bytes are larger than the space reserved for /Contents, PDFBox throws an exception. If they are smaller, the remaining space is zero-padded.

important.simpara: The InputStream you receive in sign() is the concatenation of the two byte ranges — the bytes before the /Contents gap and the bytes after it. You must read all of them with content.readAllBytes() (or equivalent). If you only read part of the stream, you are signing incomplete data, and the signature will fail validation.

section.simpara: Here is the simplest possible implementation — it signs the content with RSA/SHA-256 and produces a basic CMS SignedData:

callout.para: The false parameter means "detached signature" — the content is not embedded inside the CMS structure. This is mandatory for PDF signatures: the content lives in the PDF file itself, not inside the CMS blob.

section.title: SignatureOptions: Container Size and Visual Appearance
section.simpara: SignatureOptions controls two things: the size of the /Contents container (how many bytes are reserved in the PDF for the CMS signature) and the visual appearance stream.
section.programlisting: SignatureOptions sigOptions = new SignatureOptions(); sigOptions.setPreferredSignatureSize(32768); // 32 KB for the CMS container
section.simpara: The preferredSignatureSize is the number of bytes reserved for the hex-encoded signature value in /Contents. Since the hex encoding doubles the size, a 32768-byte container can hold a CMS blob of up to about 16 KB.

entry.simpara: A CMS SignedData with one signer, RSA-2048, and a 3-certificate chain typically occupies 4-8 KB. 32 KB gives ample room.
The timestamp token adds another CMS SignedData structure (the TSA’s signature, its certificate chain, and the TSTInfo). 64 KB handles even large TSA responses.
CRL responses can be very large (100 KB+). If you embed revocation data in the CMS structure rather than the DSS, you need a correspondingly large container.

warning.simpara: If your CMS blob exceeds the reserved container size, saveIncremental() throws an IOException with a message like "can’t write signature, not enough space." The fix is to increase preferredSignatureSize. But do not set it to absurd values (e.g., 10 MB) because the entire container is written as hex zeros into the PDF — a 10 MB container adds 20 MB of hex padding to the file.

chapter.title: Complete Basic Signing Example
chapter.simpara: Let us put the three pieces together into a complete, compilable example. This is the minimum code needed to sign a PDF with PDFBox 3.0.0.

callout.para: PDFBox 3.x uses Loader.loadPDF(byte[]). The old PDDocument.load() is gone. See the migration section later in this chapter.
addSignature() registers the signature dictionary, the callback, and the options. Nothing is written yet.

saveIncremental() writes the PDF as an incremental update, computes the byte range, calls your sign() method, hex-encodes the result, and patches it into the /Contents field. This is where all the magic happens.

tip.simpara: Always use saveIncremental(), never save(). A regular save() rewrites the entire PDF, which destroys any existing signatures. saveIncremental() appends a new revision to the file, preserving all previous signatures. This is not optional — it is the only correct way to save a signed PDF.

chapter.title: The InputStream in sign(): What You Are Actually Signing
chapter.simpara: The InputStream passed to your sign() method is one of the most misunderstood parts of the PDFBox signing API. Let us be very precise about what it contains.

When PDFBox writes the PDF during saveIncremental(), it creates the file with a placeholder for the /Contents value. The byte range is computed as two ranges that together cover the entire file except the /Contents hex string:
Where:

listitem.simpara: Range 1: bytes 0 through offset1 (everything before the /Contents hex string)
Range 2: bytes offset2 through offset2 + length2 (everything after the /Contents hex string)

chapter.simpara: The InputStream you receive is the concatenation of these two ranges. You do not see the gap. You do not need to parse the byte range. PDFBox handles all of that.
Your sign() method receives a continuous stream of bytes, and you sign all of them.

callout.para: You must read the entire stream. If you read only part of it (e.g., using a fixed-size buffer and stopping early), the hash you compute will not match the hash a validator computes over the full byte range, and the signature will be invalid.

caution.simpara: A common bug in PDFBox 2.x code (which people copy into 3.x projects) is using IOUtils.toByteArray() from Apache Commons IO with a size limit. The byte range content can be large — for a 100 MB PDF, the stream is approximately 100 MB. If your byte array buffer is smaller, you get a truncated hash and a broken signature that validates in your tests (small PDFs) but fails in production (large PDFs).

chapter.title: Visible Signature Appearances
chapter.simpara: An invisible signature is fine for server-side signing workflows, but many use cases — contracts, approvals, notarizations — require a visible signature block on a specific page. This section shows how to create visible appearances with PDFBox.

section.title: The Form XObject Approach
section.simpara: A signature appearance is a Form XObject — a self-contained mini-page with its own coordinate system, resources, and content stream. You build it, attach it to the signature field’s widget annotation, and PDFBox includes it in the signed content.

callout.para: In PDFBox 3.x, PDType1Font.HELVETICA does not exist. You must use new PDType1Font(Standard14Fonts.FontName.HELVETICA). This is one of the most common migration errors. See the migration section at the end of this chapter.

form.getStream().createOutputStream() returns an output stream that writes directly into the Form XObject’s content stream. This is the correct way to write appearance content in PDFBox 3.x. You cannot use PDPageContentStream with a Form XObject.

section.title: Image-Based Appearances
section.simpara: For handwritten signature images (e.g., from a drawing canvas or a scanned signature):

The content stream uses the standard PDF imaging operators: q (save graphics state), cm (concatenate transformation matrix), Do (paint XObject), Q (restore graphics state). The transformation matrix [drawW 0 0 drawH xOffset yOffset] scales and positions the image within the appearance rectangle.

chapter.title: Multi-Signature Support
chapter.simpara: A PDF can contain multiple signatures. Each signature is an independent signature field with its own /Sig dictionary and its own byte range. The key insight: each subsequent signature covers the entire file as it existed after the previous signature, including all previous signatures.

section.title: How Multiple Signatures Work
section.simpara: Consider a document signed by Alice, then by Bob:

listitem.simpara: Alice signs the original document. Her byte range covers the entire file (minus her /Contents gap). PDFBox writes this as an incremental update — the original file plus a new cross-reference section and trailer.

Bob signs the document after Alice. His byte range covers the entire file as it now exists — including Alice’s incremental update and her signature. Bob’s signature is written as a second incremental update appended after Alice’s.

section.simpara: Each signature’s byte range covers everything that existed when that signature was created. Alice’s signature does not cover Bob’s changes (because Bob had not signed yet), but Bob’s signature covers Alice’s.

section.title: Code: Adding a Second Signature

callout.para: Each call to saveIncremental() appends a new revision. The file grows with each signature. A document with three signatures has four revisions (original + three incremental updates).

important.simpara: The order in which signatures are applied matters for validation. Adobe Acrobat validates each signature against its revision — it checks that the bytes covered by Alice’s byte range have not been modified. If someone modified Alice’s revision after she signed (other than by appending a new incremental update), Acrobat flags Alice’s signature as invalid, even if Bob’s is fine.

chapter.title: External Signing: HSM and Remote Key Flows
chapter.simpara: In many production environments, the private key never leaves a Hardware Security Module (HSM), a cloud KMS, or a remote signing service. The key cannot be exported, so you cannot pass it to a JcaContentSignerBuilder. Instead, you need to split the signing process: compute the hash locally, send the hash to the remote system, receive the raw signature value, and assemble the CMS structure yourself.

section.title: The Hash-Signing Pattern
section.simpara: The flow has three steps:

listitem.simpara: Prepare: Create the PDF with a placeholder signature, compute the byte range, and extract the bytes to be signed.
Hash: Compute the SHA-256 digest of the byte range content.
Sign remotely: Send the hash (not the document) to the HSM/cloud service, receive the raw signature bytes, and embed them into the CMS structure.

section.simpara: PDFBox supports this through a two-phase API using ExternalSigningSupport:

callout.para: saveIncrementalForExternalSigning() writes the PDF to the output stream with a placeholder /Contents and returns an ExternalSigningSupport handle.
getContent() returns the byte range content as an InputStream — the same data that would be passed to sign() in the callback-based approach.
This is the only operation that touches the external key. You send a 32-byte SHA-256 hash and receive a raw signature value (typically 256 bytes for RSA-2048 or 64 bytes for ECDSA P-256).
setSignature() hex-encodes the CMS bytes and patches them into the /Contents placeholder.

note.simpara: Building the CMS structure with a pre-computed raw signature is more involved than using CMSSignedDataGenerator. You need to construct the SignerInfo manually with the external signature value. Chapter 30 shows the complete code for this.

chapter.title: Certification Signatures with DocMDP
chapter.simpara: A certification signature (also called a "certifying signature" or "MDP signature") is a special first signature that declares permission levels for future modifications to the document. It uses the /DocMDP transform method (ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.8.2.2).

section.title: The Three DocMDP Levels

table.title: DocMDP Permission Levels — ISO 32000-2:2020 S12.8.2.2, Table 257

entry.simpara: No modifications whatsoever. Even filling in form fields invalidates the signature.
Form fields may be filled, signature fields may be signed, and pages may be instantiated from templates. No other changes.
Everything in level 2, plus annotations may be added, modified, or deleted.

section.simpara: Level 2 is by far the most common in practice — it allows a document to be certified by the author and then signed by additional parties.

section.title: Code: Applying a Certification Signature

callout.para: The /P value is the permission level: 1, 2, or 3.
The /Perms dictionary in the document catalog must reference the certification signature’s /Sig dictionary. This is how viewers discover that the document has been certified.

warning.simpara: A document can have at most one certification signature, and it must be the first signature applied. If you try to add a certification signature to a document that already has one, the existing certification is invalidated. If you apply it as a second signature (after an approval signature), the result is undefined — some viewers accept it, some reject it, and Adobe Acrobat shows a confusing error.

chapter.title: PDFBox 2.x to 3.x Migration
chapter.simpara: PDFBox 3.0.0 introduced breaking API changes that affect every piece of signing code. If you are migrating from PDFBox 2.x (which many projects still use), these are the changes you need to know.

section.title: Document Loading
section.simpara: The Loader class is in org.apache.pdfbox.Loader. The old PDDocument.load() static methods were removed entirely — not deprecated, removed.

section.title: Standard 14 Fonts
section.simpara: This is the single most common compilation error in PDFBox 2.x-to-3.x migration.
The Standard14Fonts.FontName enum lives in org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.font.Standard14Fonts.

callout.para: The method was renamed from isNeedAppearances() to getNeedAppearances(). A small change, but it breaks at compile time.

formalpara.title: PDFBox 2.x — could use PDPageContentStream for Form XObjects

para.programlisting: // This approach no longer works for Form XObjects in 3.x

formalpara.title: PDFBox 3.x — use the stream’s output stream directly

table.title: PDFBox 2.x to 3.x Breaking Changes Relevant to Signing

tip.simpara: When migrating a large codebase, search for every occurrence of PDDocument.load, PDType1Font.HELVETICA (and all other font constants), and isNeedAppearances. These three account for about 90% of compilation failures in signing code.

chapter.title: Common Pitfalls
chapter.simpara: We have collected these from production incidents, Stack Overflow questions, and our own debugging sessions. Each one has cost someone hours.

section.title: Pitfall 1: Not Registering the BouncyCastle Provider
section.simpara: Without this, you get a NoSuchAlgorithmException or a NoSuchProviderException at runtime. The error message varies depending on which BouncyCastle class triggers first, making it confusing to diagnose. In Spring Boot applications, put this in a @PostConstruct method of a @Configuration class:

section.title: Pitfall 2: Using save() Instead of saveIncremental()
section.simpara: A regular save() rewrites the entire PDF from scratch. This changes every byte offset in the file, which means every existing signature’s byte range is now wrong. Every signature becomes invalid. Use saveIncremental(). Always.

section.title: Pitfall 3: /Contents Container Too Small
section.simpara: If the CMS blob returned by your sign() method exceeds the space reserved by preferredSignatureSize, you get:
section.screen: java.io.IOException: Can't write signature, not enough space
section.simpara: The fix is straightforward — increase the container size. But be aware that the container size is in bytes of hex encoding, not bytes of CMS data. A 32768-byte container holds approximately 16 KB of CMS data (since each byte becomes two hex characters).
When in doubt, use 65536 (64 KB). The extra space is filled with hex zeros and adds about 64 KB to the file size — negligible for most PDFs.

section.title: Pitfall 4: Not Closing the PDDocument
section.simpara: PDDocument implements Closeable. If you do not close it, temporary files and memory-mapped buffers may not be released. In a server application that signs thousands of documents, this leads to OutOfMemoryError or file handle exhaustion.

section.title: Pitfall 5: Signing After Closing the Document
section.simpara: addSignature() only registers the signature. The actual signing happens inside saveIncremental(). If you close the document before calling saveIncremental(), the document’s internal structures are released, and the save fails.

section.title: Pitfall 6: Reusing a PDSignature Object
section.simpara: A PDSignature is a COS dictionary tied to a specific document. After it is added to doc1 and saved, it belongs to doc1. Create a new PDSignature for each document.

chapter.title: Timestamp Embedding
chapter.simpara: A timestamp proves when a signature was created. Without it, you have a PAdES-B-B signature. With it, you have PAdES-B-T.

section.title: Embedding a TSA Timestamp as an Unsigned Attribute
section.simpara: The timestamp token is an RFC 3161 TimeStampToken embedded as an unsigned attribute (id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken, OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.14) in the CMS SignerInfo.
The flow is:

listitem.simpara: Generate the CMS SignedData (without the timestamp)
Extract the signer’s raw signature value from the SignerInfo
Hash the signature value with SHA-256
Send the hash to the TSA, receive a TimeStampToken
Attach the token as an unsigned attribute on the SignerInfo
Return the modified CMS SignedData

note.simpara: The timestamp is embedded after the CMS signature is generated. This is important: the timestamp covers the signature value, not the document content. The document content is covered by the CMS signature itself. The timestamp proves when that signature was created. This two-layer structure is fundamental to PAdES-B-T.

chapter.title: Complete Production-Ready Signing Class
chapter.simpara: Here is a complete signing class that handles all the scenarios discussed in this chapter: invisible and visible signatures, PAdES-B-B and PAdES-B-T profiles, certification signatures, and multi-signature workflows.

This class covers the full production workflow. It produces PAdES-B-B compliant signatures by default (with ESS-signing-certificate-v2 and ETSI.CAdES.detached), upgrades to PAdES-B-T when a TSA URL is provided, supports visible and invisible appearances, and handles certification signatures with DocMDP.

In the next chapter, we go deep into the BouncyCastle CMS construction that powers the PadesCmsCallback — every signed attribute, every ASN.1 structure, every decision point that determines whether your signature is a basic CMS blob or a fully PAdES-compliant SignedData.

partintro.simpara: Chapter 29 showed how PDFBox orchestrates the signing process — creating the signature dictionary, computing the byte range, calling your sign() method, and writing the result into /Contents. This chapter goes inside that sign() method. We are now in BouncyCastle territory, building the CMS SignedData structure that is the actual cryptographic heart of every PDF signature.

If you have ever looked at a CMS signature in an ASN.1 dump and wondered what each field means, why certain attributes are mandatory for PAdES, or how a timestamp token gets attached — this chapter answers all of those questions with complete, compilable code.

We cover: CMSSignedDataGenerator and its components, provider registration, building the ContentSigner, the four mandatory signed attributes for PAdES-B-B, the ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute in detail, algorithm negotiation between RSA and ECDSA, timestamp tokens as unsigned attributes, debugging CMS with ASN1Dump and openssl, and complete builder classes for PAdES-B-B and PAdES-B-T.

chapter.title: The CMS SignedData Structure: A Quick Refresher
chapter.simpara: Before we write code, let us recall what we are building. A CMS SignedData structure (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) S5) contains:
chapter.screen: SignedData ::= SEQUENCE { version CMSVersion, digestAlgorithms SET OF AlgorithmIdentifier, encapContentInfo EncapsulatedContentInfo, certificates [0] IMPLICIT CertificateSet OPTIONAL, crls [1] IMPLICIT RevocationInfoChoices OPTIONAL, signerInfos SET OF SignerInfo }
chapter.simpara: For a PDF signature:

listitem.simpara: version is 1 (one signer, detached content)
digestAlgorithms contains SHA-256 (or SHA-384, SHA-512)
encapContentInfo has contentType = id-data and no content (detached signature — the content lives in the PDF)
certificates contains the signer’s certificate and the chain (up to but not including the root)
crls is typically empty (revocation data goes in the DSS for PAdES-B-LT)
signerInfos contains exactly one SignerInfo with signed attributes, the signature value, and optionally unsigned attributes (the timestamp)

chapter.simpara: The SignerInfo is where most of the action is:
chapter.screen: SignerInfo ::= SEQUENCE { version CMSVersion, sid SignerIdentifier, digestAlgorithm AlgorithmIdentifier, signedAttrs [0] IMPLICIT SignedAttributes OPTIONAL, signatureAlgorithm AlgorithmIdentifier, signature SignatureValue, unsignedAttrs [1] IMPLICIT UnsignedAttributes OPTIONAL }
chapter.simpara: The signedAttrs (signed attributes) are hashed and signed — they form the binding between the document content and the signer’s identity. The unsignedAttrs (unsigned attributes) are not covered by the signature — this is where the timestamp goes, because the timestamp is created after the signature.

chapter.title: BouncyCastle Provider Registration
chapter.simpara: Every BouncyCastle crypto operation requires the provider to be registered with the JCA (Java Cryptography Architecture). Without it, calls to JcaContentSignerBuilder, JcaDigestCalculatorProviderBuilder, and JcaCertStore fail with NoSuchProviderException.
In a Spring Boot application, the cleanest approach is a configuration class:

warning.simpara: If you register the provider in a static block, it races with other static initializers. In complex applications with multiple classloaders (application servers, OSGi), the provider may not be visible where you need it. The @PostConstruct approach avoids both problems.

tip.simpara: You can verify the registration by calling java.security.Security.getProvider("BC"). If it returns null, BouncyCastle is not registered. If it returns a Provider object, check its version: provider.getVersion() should return 1.80 (or whatever version you have on the classpath).

chapter.title: Building a Basic CMS Signature
chapter.simpara: Let us build a CMS SignedData from scratch, step by step. We will start with the simplest possible version and then add PAdES requirements.

section.title: Step 1: The ContentSigner
section.simpara: The ContentSigner is BouncyCastle’s abstraction over the private key and signature algorithm. It provides an OutputStream that you write data into, and when you are done, it produces the raw signature bytes.

The JcaContentSignerBuilder takes a JCA algorithm string and a private key. The .setProvider("BC") ensures BouncyCastle handles the actual signing, not the JDK’s default provider (which may not support all algorithms or may behave differently for edge cases like PSS padding).

section.title: Step 2: The DigestCalculatorProvider
section.simpara: BouncyCastle needs a way to compute message digests. The DigestCalculatorProvider is the factory for digest calculators:
This provider is used internally by the signer info generator to compute the message digest of the signed content and the signed attributes.

section.title: Step 3: The SignerInfoGenerator
section.simpara: The SignerInfoGenerator combines the ContentSigner, the DigestCalculatorProvider, and the signer’s certificate into a component that can produce a SignerInfo:

At this point, the signerInfoGen knows which key to use, which algorithm, which certificate identifies the signer, and how to compute digests. It does not yet know about signed attributes — we will add those next.

section.title: Step 4: The CMSSignedDataGenerator
section.simpara: Now we wire everything together:

callout.para: The certificates are embedded in the CMS certificates field. Include the signer’s certificate and all intermediate certificates. Do not include the root CA certificate — validators are expected to have the root in their trust store already, and including it wastes space without adding trust.

The second parameter false means detached signature. For PDF signatures, this must always be false. If you pass true, the content is embedded inside the CMS structure, which doubles the size and does not match what PDF validators expect for ETSI.CAdES.detached or adbe.pkcs7.detached.

chapter.title: Signed Attributes: What PAdES Requires
chapter.simpara: A basic CMS signature (without signed attributes) signs the content directly — the hash of the content is what the signature covers. When signed attributes are present, the signature covers the hash of the signed attributes instead, and the signed attributes contain the hash of the content. This indirection is how CMS allows metadata (signing time, content type, certificate binding) to be included in the signed data.
For PAdES-B-B compliance (ETSI EN 319 142-1 V1.1.1 (2016-04)), four signed attributes are required.

entry.simpara: Identifies the type of content being signed. Must be id-data (OID 1.2.840.113549.1.7.1). BouncyCastle adds this automatically.
The hash of the signed content (the byte range bytes). BouncyCastle computes and adds this automatically.
The claimed signing time. BouncyCastle adds this automatically when using DefaultSignedAttributeTableGenerator. Note: PAdES validators prefer the TSA timestamp over this value.
Binds the signature to a specific certificate by its hash. This is the one you must add manually. Without it, you have a valid CMS signature but not a PAdES-B-B signature.

note.simpara: BouncyCastle’s DefaultSignedAttributeTableGenerator automatically adds content-type, message-digest, and signing-time. You only need to explicitly construct ESS-signing-certificate-v2 and pass it in. The generator merges your custom attributes with the automatic ones.

chapter.title: Creating ESS-signing-certificate-v2
chapter.simpara: The ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) and RFC 5035) prevents certificate substitution attacks. It contains a hash of the signer’s certificate, so a validator can verify that the certificate embedded in the CMS certificates field is the one the signer intended to use.
The ASN.1 structure:
Here is the complete code to build this attribute:

callout.para: certificate.getEncoded() returns the DER-encoded X.509 certificate. We hash this entire byte sequence, not just the public key or the subject name. This ensures that any change to the certificate (even adding an extension) changes the hash.

The hash algorithm identifier must match the algorithm used to compute the hash. SHA-256 is the standard choice. If you use SHA-384 or SHA-512 for the signature algorithm, you may use the matching digest here, but SHA-256 is always acceptable.

The SigningCertificateV2 wraps an array of ESSCertIDv2. For a single signer, there is one entry. If your signing certificate was issued by an intermediate CA, you may optionally include the intermediate’s hash as well, but this is not required by PAdES.

The OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.47 identifies this as the ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute. Do not confuse it with the older ESS-signing-certificate (v1, OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.12), which uses SHA-1. PAdES requires v2.

caution.simpara: Using ESS-signing-certificate v1 (with SHA-1) instead of v2 (with SHA-256) is a PAdES compliance failure. Some older code examples on the internet still use v1. SHA-1 is considered cryptographically broken for collision resistance since the SHAttered attack (2017). Always use v2 with SHA-256 or stronger.

section.title: Wiring ESS-signing-certificate-v2 into the Generator
section.simpara: Now we pass the attribute table to the SignerInfoGenerator:

callout.para: DefaultSignedAttributeTableGenerator accepts an initial AttributeTable and merges it with the attributes it generates automatically (content-type, message-digest, signing-time). Your ESS-signing-certificate-v2 is included alongside the automatic attributes. You do not need to manually construct the other three.

chapter.title: Algorithm Negotiation: RSA vs. ECDSA
chapter.simpara: The signature algorithm depends on the private key type. You must match the algorithm string to the key, or the ContentSigner construction fails.

callout.para: RSA-PSS (Probabilistic Signature Scheme) is increasingly preferred over PKCS#1 v1.5 padding (SHA256withRSA). Some EU validation services flag PKCS#1 v1.5 as deprecated. If your key was generated as RSASSA-PSS, you must use the PSS algorithm string.

Ed25519 and Ed448 (Edwards curves) use the algorithm name directly — there is no separate hash algorithm because the hash is built into the signature scheme. These are not yet widely supported by PDF validators (Adobe Acrobat does not validate Ed25519 signatures as of 2026), but they are valid per ISO 32000-2:2020 and will gain support over time.

entry.simpara: RSA-PSS. Better security properties. Some legacy validators do not support it.
Compact signatures (64 bytes vs. 256 for RSA-2048). Widely supported.
Modern curve. Limited validator support (as of 2026).
Higher security Ed curve. Very limited support.

tip.simpara: For maximum compatibility in 2026, use SHA256withRSA (RSA-2048 key) or SHA256withECDSA (P-256 key). These work with every PDF validator we have tested, including Adobe Acrobat, Foxit Reader, and the EU DSS validation service.

chapter.title: Timestamp Token as Unsigned Attribute
chapter.simpara: A PAdES-B-T signature contains a timestamp token as an unsigned attribute on the SignerInfo. The timestamp proves when the signature was created.

The attribute is identified by OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.14 (id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken), and its value is a complete CMS SignedData structure — the timestamp is itself a CMS signature, created by the TSA.

listitem.simpara: Generate the CMS SignedData for the document signature (PAdES-B-B)
Extract the raw signature value (SignerInfo.signature)
Hash the signature value with SHA-256
Send the hash to the TSA in an RFC 3161 TimeStampRequest
Receive the TimeStampResponse, extract the TimeStampToken
Encode the token as an ASN.1 structure
Attach it as an unsigned attribute on the SignerInfo
Replace the SignerInfo in the CMSSignedData
Return the modified CMSSignedData

section.title: Complete Implementation

callout.para: getSignature() returns the raw signature bytes from the SignerInfo — this is the value that was produced by the ContentSigner. For RSA-2048, this is 256 bytes. For ECDSA P-256, it is approximately 72 bytes (DER-encoded).

setCertReq(true) tells the TSA to include its signing certificate in the response. This is important: without the TSA’s certificate, a validator cannot verify the timestamp without separately fetching the TSA’s certificate.

validate() checks that the response corresponds to our request (matching nonce, hash algorithm, and hash value). If the TSA returned a response for a different request, this throws a TSPValidationException.
The timestamp token is wrapped in a DERSet and assigned OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.14. This is the standard way to embed a timestamp in CMS (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)).

replaceUnsignedAttributes() creates a new SignerInformation with the same signed attributes and signature value, but with the specified unsigned attributes. The original SignerInformation is immutable.

chapter.title: Debugging CMS Structures
chapter.simpara: When a signature fails validation, you need to look inside the CMS blob. There are two indispensable tools: BouncyCastle’s ASN1Dump (Java) and OpenSSL (command line).

section.title: ASN1Dump: Java-Side Inspection

callout.para: The true parameter enables verbose mode, which shows the full content of OCTET STRING and BIT STRING values. Without it, large values are truncated.

section.simpara: The output looks like this (truncated for readability):
section.screen: SEQUENCE OBJECT IDENTIFIER 1.2.840.113549.1.7.2 (signedData) [0] SEQUENCE INTEGER 1 (version) SET SEQUENCE OBJECT IDENTIFIER 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1 (SHA-256) SEQUENCE OBJECT IDENTIFIER 1.2.840.113549.1.7.1 (data) [0] (certificates) SEQUENCE ... (X.509 certificate) SET SEQUENCE (SignerInfo) INTEGER 1 (version) SEQUENCE (sid - issuerAndSerialNumber) ... SEQUENCE (digestAlgorithm) OBJECT IDENTIFIER 2.16.840.1.101.3.4.2.1 (SHA-256) [0] (signedAttrs) SEQUENCE OBJECT IDENTIFIER 1.2.840.113549.1.9.3 (content-type) ... SEQUENCE OBJECT IDENTIFIER 1.2.840.113549.1.9.4 (message-digest) ... SEQUENCE OBJECT IDENTIFIER 1.2.840.113549.1.9.5 (signing-time) ... SEQUENCE OBJECT IDENTIFIER 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.47 (ESS-signing-cert-v2) ... SEQUENCE (signatureAlgorithm) OBJECT IDENTIFIER 1.2.840.113549.1.1.1 (RSA) OCTET STRING (signature value) ... [1] (unsignedAttrs) SEQUENCE OBJECT IDENTIFIER 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.14 (timestamp) ...
section.simpara: When debugging, check for:

listitem.simpara: Is ESS-signing-certificate-v2 (OID …​16.2.47) present in the signed attributes? If not, the signature is not PAdES-B-B compliant.
Is message-digest present? If not, the signature structure is malformed.
Is the timestamp (OID …​16.2.14) in the unsigned attributes? If not, it is not PAdES-B-T.
Are certificates present in the [0] IMPLICIT set? If not, validators cannot build the chain.

section.title: OpenSSL: Command-Line Inspection
section.simpara: Extract the CMS blob from the PDF (it is the hex-encoded content of /Contents), decode it, and use OpenSSL:
section.programlisting:
# Decode the hex string from /Contents to binary xxd -r -p signature-hex.txt > signature.der
# Parse the CMS structure openssl cms -inform DER -in signature.der -cmsout -print
# Verify the CMS signature (requires the signed content) openssl cms -verify -inform DER -in signature.der \ -content signed-bytes.bin -noverify -out /dev/null
# Extract and examine the signing certificate openssl cms -inform DER -in signature.der -cmsout -print \ | grep -A 50 "cert:"
# View the timestamp token (if present) openssl ts -reply -in timestamp-token.der -text

tip.simpara: To extract the /Contents hex string from a PDF, search for /Contents < in the raw file. The hex string starts after the < and ends at >. It is zero-padded — strip trailing zeros before decoding. You can also use PDFBox’s PDSignature.getContents(byte[]) to extract the raw bytes programmatically.

chapter.title: Complete PAdES-B-B Builder
chapter.simpara: Here is a self-contained class that builds a PAdES-B-B compliant CMS SignedData:

chapter.title: Complete PAdES-B-T Builder
chapter.simpara: The PAdES-B-T builder extends B-B by adding a timestamp after the signature is generated:

callout.para: TSA status 0 = granted, 1 = grantedWithMods. Any other status (2 = rejection, 3 = waiting, 4 = revocationWarning, 5 = revocationNotification) means the TSA refused to issue a timestamp. Handle this gracefully — some TSAs reject requests during maintenance windows.

chapter.title: CMS Construction for Remote Signing (CSC API)
chapter.simpara: When using a remote signing service via the CSC API (Chapter 22), you cannot use BouncyCastle’s CMSSignedDataGenerator in the normal way — because you do not have the private key locally. The generator expects a ContentSigner that performs the signing operation, but with CSC, the signing happens on a remote HSM. You must build the CMS SignedData structure manually using BouncyCastle’s low-level ASN.1 classes.

section.title: The Challenge
section.simpara: The remote signing flow splits the CMS construction into three phases:

listitem.simpara: Build signed attributes (local) — content-type, message-digest, ESS-signing-certificate-v2
Hash and send to CSC (remote) — the server signs the DER-encoded signed attributes hash
Assemble CMS (local) — combine the signed attributes, the remote signature value, and the certificate chain into a valid SignedData structure

section.simpara: Phase 2 returns raw signature bytes — not a CMS structure, not a PKCS#7 blob, just the naked RSA or ECDSA signature value. You must wrap those bytes into a complete CMS SignedData yourself.

section.title: Manual CMS Assembly
Algorithm Detection for CSC Credentials
section.simpara: The CSC credential info response tells you the key algorithm (RSA or EC) but not always the exact signing algorithm OID. Use the key algorithm OID to determine the correct signing algorithm:

section.title: Adding an Unsigned Timestamp to the Remote CMS
section.simpara: After assembling the CMS with the remote signature value, you can add an RFC 3161 timestamp as an unsigned attribute to achieve PAdES-B-T. This requires parsing the CMS back, adding the unsigned attribute, and re-encoding:

note.simpara: This pattern — build CMS without unsigned attributes, then parse and add the timestamp — is the standard approach for remote signing. You cannot add the timestamp during initial CMS construction because the timestamp must cover the signature value, which does not exist until the CSC API returns it. The two-phase assembly is inherent to the remote signing model.

For local signing (where you have the private key), BouncyCastle’s CMSSignedDataGenerator can add the timestamp in a single pass. The manual assembly shown here is only needed when the signing operation is split between local CMS construction and remote cryptographic operation.

chapter.title: Putting It All Together
chapter.simpara: Here is how you use the PAdES-B-B and PAdES-B-T builders with PDFBox:

The PadesBBBuilder and PadesBTBuilder classes are production-ready. They handle algorithm negotiation, ESS attribute construction, certificate embedding, and timestamp integration. Drop them into your project alongside the BouncyCastle and PDFBox dependencies from Chapter 28, register the BouncyCastle provider, and you have a complete signing pipeline.

In Chapter 31, we reverse the process: given a signed PDF, we verify the CMS signature, validate the certificate chain, check the timestamp, and extract every piece of information that these structures contain.

partintro.simpara: Signing a PDF is a matter of following a recipe. Verifying a signed PDF is where things get interesting — and where most implementations get things wrong. PDFBox gives you every primitive you need: signature extraction, byte range access, CMS parsing, content stream reading. But it deliberately does not give you a "verify this signature" method. You build the pipeline yourself, step by step, and you understand every failure mode because you wrote the code that handles it.

This chapter walks through a complete verification implementation using Apache PDFBox 3.0.0 and BouncyCastle 1.80. We start with extracting signatures from a PDF document, proceed through each verification step from Chapter 16, and end with a production-ready five-step pipeline that handles the edge cases real-world PDFs throw at you. If Chapter 16 described what to verify, this chapter shows you how — with code you can compile and run.

chapter.title: Extracting Signatures from a PDF Document

section.title: The Extraction Path: AcroForm to PDSignature
section.simpara: Every PDF signature lives inside the AcroForm field tree. The extraction path follows the object hierarchy we dissected in Chapter 12: the document’s /AcroForm dictionary contains a /Fields array, which includes PDSignatureField entries, each of which references a PDSignature dictionary.
PDFBox models this hierarchy directly. The key classes are:

listitem.simpara: PDAcroForm — the form dictionary, accessed via PDDocument.getDocumentCatalog().getAcroForm()
PDSignatureField — a form field of type /Sig, extends PDTerminalField
PDSignature — the actual /Type /Sig dictionary containing /Contents, /ByteRange, /SubFilter, etc.

section.simpara: There are two ways to access signatures in PDFBox. The first walks the field tree:
The second approach bypasses the field tree and goes directly to the signature dictionaries:

tip.simpara: Use the first approach (field tree walk) when you need the full picture — field name, widget annotation, visual appearance, page location. Use the second approach when you only need the cryptographic payload — for pure verification, it is faster and simpler.

section.title: Reading Signature Metadata
section.simpara: Once you have a PDSignature, you can extract the metadata that drives the verification pipeline:
The /SubFilter value tells you what kind of signature you are dealing with:

listitem.simpara: adbe.pkcs7.detached — Classic PKCS#7 (CMS) detached signature
ETSI.CAdES.detached — PAdES CAdES-based signature (the modern standard)
adbe.pkcs7.sha1 — Legacy: SHA-1 hash embedded in PKCS#7 (deprecated, avoid)
adbe.x509.rsa_sha1 — Legacy: bare RSA signature with X.509 cert (deprecated, avoid)

section.simpara: For any new code, you should handle adbe.pkcs7.detached and ETSI.CAdES.detached. The legacy formats exist in old documents, and you may encounter them, but you should never produce them.

section.title: Extracting the Raw CMS Bytes
section.simpara: The /Contents value in the signature dictionary is a hex-encoded byte string containing the DER-encoded CMS SignedData structure. PDFBox provides this as a byte array:
section.programlisting: byte[] cmsBytes = signature.getContents();
section.simpara: This single line hides a meaningful amount of work. PDFBox reads the hex string from the PDF (which can be 16KB to 64KB or more of hex characters), decodes it from hex to binary, and strips any trailing zero padding. The result is the raw DER-encoded CMS SignedData that BouncyCastle can parse.

section.title: Parsing with BouncyCastle
section.simpara: To parse the CMS structure, you also need the signed bytes — the byte range segments from the PDF file. For a detached signature (which is what adbe.pkcs7.detached and ETSI.CAdES.detached are), the signed content is not inside the CMS; it is the external byte range data.

warning.simpara: A common mistake is to construct CMSSignedData with only the /Contents bytes (without the signed content). This will parse the CMS structure, but any subsequent verification call will fail because BouncyCastle cannot compute the message digest without the signed data. Always pass both the signed content and the CMS bytes.

section.title: Navigating the CMS Structure
section.simpara: Once parsed, the CMSSignedData object exposes the full CMS tree from RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS):

section.title: The Three Checks
section.simpara: Byte range verification (Step 1 from Chapter 16) requires three checks:

listitem.simpara: The /ByteRange array has exactly four elements
The first offset is zero (the signed region starts at the beginning of the file)
The second segment extends to the end of the file (or the end of the revision for multi-signature documents)

important.simpara: Trailing bytes are not always an attack. In a multi-signature document, each signature covers only up to the end of its own incremental revision. Subsequent revisions (containing later signatures) appear as "trailing bytes" from the perspective of the earlier signature. Chapter 8 covers how incremental updates work, and Chapter 13 covers the byte range geometry in multi-signature scenarios.

However, trailing bytes on the last signature in a document are suspicious and may indicate a Shadow Attack (Chapter 24). Your verification code should distinguish between "trailing bytes that are a legitimate later revision" and "trailing bytes that are unexplained."

section.title: Validating the Contents Gap
section.simpara: The gap between the two segments should contain exactly the /Contents hex string. You can validate this by checking that the gap bytes, when decoded from hex, produce valid DER-encoded data:

section.title: Building the SignerInformationVerifier
section.simpara: This is Step 3 from Chapter 16 — verifying that the CMS SignedData was actually created by the claimed signer’s private key. BouncyCastle’s SignerInformation.verify() method performs this check, but you need to provide the correct SignerInformationVerifier:

section.title: What verify() Actually Does
section.simpara: When you call signerInfo.verify(verifier), BouncyCastle performs the following internally:

listitem.simpara: Computes the digest of the signed content (the byte range data you passed when constructing CMSSignedData) using the algorithm from SignerInfo.digestAlgorithm
Checks that this computed digest matches the messageDigest attribute in the signed attributes
DER-encodes the signed attributes
Verifies the RSA/ECDSA/DSA signature over the DER-encoded signed attributes using the public key from the signer’s certificate

section.simpara: If signed attributes are absent (which is technically allowed by CMS but rare in PDF signatures), BouncyCastle instead verifies the signature directly over the content digest.

note.simpara: The verify() method performs only the cryptographic verification — Steps 2 and 3 combined. It does not check certificate validity, revocation status, or trust. Those are separate steps you must implement yourself.

section.title: The Java PKIX CertPath API
section.simpara: Java’s java.security.cert package provides a complete PKIX certificate path validation engine via CertPathBuilder and CertPathValidator. This is the standard way to validate certificate chains in Java, and it handles the complexity of chain building, validity checking, basic constraints verification, and key usage checking.

section.title: Building the Trust Anchor Set
section.simpara: The TrustAnchor set represents your trust store — the root certificates you are willing to trust. In a real application, this might come from the Java default trust store (cacerts), a custom keystore, or the AATL/EUTL (see Chapter 16, Step 5).

section.title: Key Usage Validation
section.simpara: The PKIX CertPathValidator checks basic constraints and key usage automatically. But if you want to verify specific requirements — for example, that the signer’s certificate has the digitalSignature or nonRepudiation key usage — you can check explicitly:

section.title: Extracting the Timestamp Token
section.simpara: A signature timestamp is an unsigned CMS attribute with OID 1.2.840.113549.1.9.16.2.14 (id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken). The attribute value is itself a CMS SignedData structure — a timestamp is a signature on a signature:

section.title: Verifying the Timestamp Covers the Signature
section.simpara: A valid timestamp is not just a signed time — it must also prove that the signature value existed at that time. The timestamp’s message imprint digest must match the hash of the signature value from the SignerInfo:

section.title: Putting It All Together
