section.simpara: Here is the complete verification pipeline that combines every step from this chapter. Each step is independent — a failure at one step does not prevent the other steps from running. The pipeline produces a comprehensive VerificationReport with per-step results:

section.title: The VerificationReport Model
The ValidationResult Enum
Why Multi-Signature Documents Need Revision Comparison
section.simpara: When a PDF has multiple signatures, each signature covers a specific revision of the document — a snapshot of the file at the point when that signature was applied. Later signatures add incremental updates (Chapter 8) that extend the file. From the perspective of the first signature, the second signature’s bytes are "trailing bytes" beyond its byte range.
The critical question is: did the incremental update between revisions only add the new signature, or did it modify existing content?

If someone added a new page, changed text, or altered form field values between signing events, the earlier signature’s byte range still verifies (the signed bytes haven’t changed), but the document’s visible content has changed. This is the basis of the Shadow Attacks described in Chapter 24.

section.title: Extracting Individual Revisions
section.simpara: PDFBox allows you to extract the bytes of each revision by using the byte range information:

caution.simpara: Revision comparison is inherently incomplete. A sophisticated attacker can modify content streams in ways that are difficult to detect by comparing PDF objects. The comparison above catches obvious changes (page count, form fields, annotations), but it is not a security guarantee. For high-security applications, consider using the EU DSS framework, which implements the full ETSI modification detection specification.

chapter.title: Edge Cases: Self-Signed, Expired, and Missing Chain

section.title: Self-Signed Certificates
section.simpara: A self-signed certificate is one where the issuer and subject are the same entity — the certificate signs itself. Self-signed signatures are cryptographically valid (the CMS verifies), but the certificate chain is trivially short: one certificate, no intermediates, no root CA.

For self-signed certificates, the chain validation in Step 4 will fail unless you explicitly add the self-signed certificate as a trust anchor. Most applications handle self-signed signatures by reporting: "The signature is cryptographically valid, but the identity of the signer could not be verified because the certificate is self-signed."

This is the correct behavior. A self-signed signature proves that someone possessing that private key signed the document. It does not prove who possesses that key, because no trusted third party (CA) has vouched for the identity.

section.title: Expired Certificates
section.simpara: Certificate expiration is time-dependent — the same signature can be "valid" today and "expired" tomorrow. The handling depends on whether a timestamp is present:

section.title: Missing Intermediate Certificates
section.simpara: The most common real-world chain validation failure is a missing intermediate certificate. The CMS SignedData should include all intermediate certificates between the signer’s certificate and the root, but some signing implementations omit them.
When intermediates are missing, you have two options:

listitem.simpara: AIA chasing: Follow the Authority Information Access (AIA) extension in the signer’s certificate to download the missing intermediate from the CA’s server
Local certificate cache: Maintain a local cache of known intermediate certificates

tip.simpara: In production, always cache downloaded certificates. AIA chasing adds network latency and introduces a dependency on external servers — exactly the kind of dependency that LTV (Chapter 18) is designed to eliminate. If you are building a signing service, embed all intermediate certificates in the CMS at signing time, so validators never need to chase AIA.

chapter.title: Algorithm OID Mapping Utility
chapter.simpara: Throughout this chapter, we reference a mapOidToAlgorithm() helper. Here is a complete implementation that covers the algorithm OIDs you will encounter in real PDF signatures:

listitem.simpara: PDFBox provides signature extraction via two paths: the AcroForm field tree (full metadata) or getSignatureDictionaries() (fast, crypto-only)
CMS parsing requires both the /Contents bytes and the signed content (byte range data) — always pass both to CMSSignedData
The five-step pipeline is: byte range check, hash verification, CMS cryptographic verification, certificate chain validation, trust evaluation
SignerInformation.verify() performs Steps 2 and 3 combined — but not chain validation or trust evaluation
Java’s PKIX CertPathBuilder handles chain construction, validity checking, and basic constraints automatically
Timestamps shift the validation time from "now" to "when the signature was created" — critical for expired certificates
Revision comparison detects modifications between signatures but is not a complete security guarantee
Self-signed signatures are cryptographically valid but untrusted — always report the distinction clearly
Missing intermediates are the most common chain failure — implement AIA chasing or embed all intermediates at signing time
The VerificationReport model gives callers granular per-step results rather than a binary pass/fail

partintro.simpara: Testing PDF digital signatures is one of the hardest testing problems in software engineering. A signature that is valid today may be invalid tomorrow because a certificate expired. A test that passes on your machine may fail on CI because the TSA server is unreachable. A verification test that ran perfectly for three years suddenly breaks because a CRL distribution point returns HTTP 404. Time, network, and third-party infrastructure are all embedded in your test surface — and they are all moving targets.

This chapter covers how to build a test infrastructure that is deterministic, fast, and comprehensive. We generate our own keystores, mock external services, freeze time, and construct a test matrix that exercises every meaningful signature scenario. By the end, you will have a test suite that runs in seconds, needs no network access, and covers the full signing-to-verification round trip including PAdES compliance.

chapter.title: Why Testing PDF Signatures Is Hard

section.title: The Three Enemies of Deterministic Signature Tests
section.simpara: Enemy 1: Time. Certificate validation is time-dependent. A certificate that is valid right now has a notBefore and notAfter date range. If your test generates a certificate valid for one year and your test suite runs 13 months later, every test that validates that certificate will fail. Timestamps make this worse — a timestamp proves the signature was created at a specific time, and the TSA certificate has its own validity period.

Enemy 2: Network. Real signing workflows depend on external services: TSA servers for timestamps, OCSP responders for revocation checks, CRL distribution points for certificate revocation lists. Any of these can be slow, unreachable, or returning unexpected responses. A test that calls a real TSA is a test that depends on the internet — and the internet is not deterministic.

Enemy 3: State. PDF files are stateful. A signed PDF is a specific sequence of bytes, and any change — even adding a single trailing newline — invalidates the signature. Tests that generate PDFs must produce identical byte sequences across runs, or the test assertions become meaningless. Different versions of PDFBox may serialize objects in different orders, produce different padding sizes, or use different default values — any of which changes the output bytes.

section.title: The Testing Strategy
section.simpara: Our approach addresses all three enemies:

listitem.simpara: Generate test keystores programmatically with explicit, long-lived validity periods (100 years — we are not testing certificate expiration unless we want to)
Mock all external services (TSA, OCSP) using WireMock or BouncyCastle’s in-process TSA
Freeze time using a Clock abstraction so certificate and timestamp validation happens at a controlled instant
Generate reference PDFs as part of the test setup rather than shipping binary PDF files in the repository

chapter.title: Test Infrastructure: JUnit 5 and Test Keystore Generation

section.title: The Test Keystore Generator
section.simpara: Every test needs cryptographic key material: a signing certificate, an intermediate CA, a root CA, and a TSA certificate. We generate all of these programmatically using BouncyCastle:

section.title: JUnit 5 Test Base Class
section.simpara: We use a shared base class that sets up the test PKI and provides utility methods:

section.title: Generating Signed PDFs Programmatically
section.simpara: Rather than shipping pre-built signed PDFs (which become stale and opaque), we generate them as part of each test. This ensures every test controls its own input and can vary specific parameters:

section.title: Scenario Generators
section.simpara: Each test scenario generates a specific type of signed PDF:

chapter.title: Test Matrix: Scenario to Expected Result
chapter.simpara: The following table defines every test scenario, what it tests, and the expected outcome at each verification step. This is the specification for your test suite — every row should be a test method:

entry.simpara: Same as PKCS#7 but with PAdES SubFilter
CMS is valid but chain has no trusted root
Timestamp proves signing was within validity
First sig has trailing bytes (second revision)
Chain cannot be built without intermediate
SubFilter says PAdES but ESS attr is missing

chapter.simpara: Each row in this matrix translates directly to a test method. The "--" entries mean that step is not reached because an earlier step failed fatally.

section.title: In-Process TSA with BouncyCastle
section.simpara: For timestamp tests, we need a TSA that returns deterministic timestamp tokens without hitting the network. BouncyCastle provides everything you need to build one in-process:

section.title: Mocking OCSP with WireMock
section.simpara: For revocation checking tests, we use WireMock to simulate an OCSP responder. This lets us return "good," "revoked," or "unknown" responses without any real PKI:

section.title: The Clock Abstraction
section.simpara: The most reliable way to handle time-dependent tests is to inject a Clock into any component that checks certificate validity or timestamp times:

section.title: Testing Expired Certificates
section.simpara: With the Clock abstraction, testing expired certificate scenarios becomes straightforward:

chapter.title: Complete Signing-to-Verification Round-Trip Test
chapter.simpara: This is the most important test in the suite — it exercises the entire pipeline end-to-end:

chapter.title: PAdES Compliance Test
chapter.simpara: A PAdES-compliant signature must include specific signed attributes beyond what plain CMS requires. We test for these explicitly:

chapter.title: Reference PDF Corpus: Naming Convention
chapter.simpara: For reference PDFs that you ship with your test suite (for regression testing or interoperability testing), use a consistent naming convention that encodes the signature properties in the filename:

The _v1 suffix is a version number. When you regenerate reference PDFs (because you changed the test keystore or signing code), increment the version number. This ensures old and new reference files can coexist temporarily during migration.

tip.simpara: Store reference PDFs in src/test/resources/reference-pdfs/ and regenerate them with a dedicated test utility class. Never edit reference PDFs by hand — they should always be produced by code. Commit the generated PDFs to your repository so tests can run without a generation step, but include the generator so anyone can rebuild them.

listitem.simpara: PDF signature testing is hard because of three enemies: time, network, and state — address all three explicitly
Generate test keystores programmatically with long validity periods (100 years) to avoid time-rot
Use an in-process TSA built from BouncyCastle instead of hitting real TSA servers
Mock OCSP responders with WireMock for deterministic revocation testing
Inject a Clock abstraction into verification services so you can freeze or advance time in tests
Build a test matrix that maps every scenario to expected per-step results
The round-trip test (generate PDF, sign, extract, verify) is the single most important test in the suite
Test PAdES compliance explicitly: check for ESS-signing-certificate-v2, proper SubFilter, and SHA-256+
Use a consistent naming convention for reference PDFs that encodes all signature properties in the filename
Generate reference PDFs from code, not by hand — commit both the generator and the generated files

appendix.title: Complete Code Listings
appendix.simpara: This appendix collects the complete, compilable source code for the major classes developed throughout Parts 5 and 6. Each listing is self-contained — you can copy it into your project, adjust the package name, and compile. All code targets Java 21, Apache PDFBox 3.0.0, and BouncyCastle 1.80.

chapter.title: SigningService: Complete PDF Signing Implementation
chapter.simpara: This is the full signing service from Chapters 29 and 30, consolidated into a single class. It handles both adbe.pkcs7.detached and ETSI.CAdES.detached signatures, with optional RFC 3161 timestamps.

chapter.title: VerificationService: Complete Signature Verification
chapter.simpara: The full verification pipeline from Chapter 31, as a single compilable class.

chapter.title: Signature Appearance Builder
chapter.simpara: A minimal but complete appearance builder for visible signatures. Generates an appearance stream with signer name, date, and reason inside the signature widget rectangle.

chapter.title: Test Keystore Generator: Full Implementation
chapter.simpara: The complete generator from Chapter 32, including all certificate generation methods.

appendix.title: ASN.1 Structure Reference
appendix.simpara: This appendix is a reference card for the ASN.1 structures you will encounter when working with PDF digital signatures. Every CMS SignedData blob inside a /Contents value, every X.509 certificate in the chain, every OCSP response and timestamp token — they are all ASN.1 structures encoded in DER. When your BouncyCastle code throws a ParseException at 2 AM, this is the appendix you reach for.

We present the OID table first (because OIDs are the keys to everything), then the ASN.1 grammar for each major structure, with annotations showing where each field appears in the context of PDF signatures.

chapter.title: OID Reference Table
chapter.simpara: Object Identifiers (OIDs) are the universal labels of the ASN.1 world. Every algorithm, every attribute, every extension is identified by an OID. The following table covers the OIDs you will encounter in PDF signature processing:

entry.simpara: Deprecated — do not use for new signatures
Modern RSA padding, preferred over PKCS#1 v1.5

table.title: CMS signed and unsigned attribute OIDs
X.509 v3 extension OIDs relevant to PDF signatures

chapter.title: CMS SignedData Structure (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS))
chapter.simpara: The CMS SignedData is the core structure inside every PDF /Contents value. This is the complete ASN.1 grammar from RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS) Section 5.1, annotated with PDF-specific notes:

note.simpara: For PDF detached signatures, the eContent field inside EncapsulatedContentInfo is absent. The signed content is the byte range data from the PDF file, which is passed externally to the CMS verifier. This is why you must provide the byte range bytes when constructing a CMSSignedData in BouncyCastle — without them, the verifier cannot compute the digest.

chapter.title: X.509 v3 Certificate Structure (RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile)
chapter.simpara: Every certificate in the CMS certificates collection, every CA in the chain, and every TSA certificate follows this structure:

chapter.title: OCSP Response Structure (RFC 6960 — Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP))
chapter.simpara: OCSP responses are embedded in the DSS (Document Security Store) for PAdES-B-LT and PAdES-B-LTA signatures. The structure you will find in /DSS /OCSPs:

chapter.title: Timestamp Token Structure (RFC 3161 — Internet X.509 PKI Time-Stamp Protocol (TSP))
chapter.simpara: The timestamp token (TST) is the structure inside the id-aa-signatureTimeStampToken unsigned attribute. It is itself a CMS SignedData containing a TSTInfo as the encapsulated content:

important.simpara: The messageImprint in the TSTInfo is the hash of the signature value (the raw bytes of the SignerInfo.signature field), not the hash of the signed document. This is a critical distinction. The timestamp proves that a specific signature value existed at a specific time. The signature value, in turn, covers the document bytes (via the signed attributes and byte range). The chain of trust is: timestamp proves signature time, signature proves document integrity.

chapter.title: Algorithm Identifier Structure
chapter.simpara: The AlgorithmIdentifier appears throughout all the structures above. For most algorithms, the parameters field is either absent or NULL:

appendix.title: Glossary & Spec References
appendix.simpara: This appendix collects every acronym, term, and specification referenced throughout the book into two lookup tables. When you encounter an unfamiliar term in a chapter, this is the place to find a concise definition. When you need the precise specification for a standard we reference, the spec table gives you the document number, title, and where to find it.

entry.simpara: Adobe Approved Trust List. Adobe’s curated list of root CA certificates trusted by Acrobat and Reader for signature validation. CAs must apply and meet Adobe’s requirements to be listed. If a signer’s certificate does not chain to an AATL root, Acrobat shows a warning even if the signature is cryptographically valid.
Interactive Form Dictionary. The PDF structure (/AcroForm in the document catalog) that contains all form fields, including signature fields. Defined in ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 12.7.

Authority Information Access. An X.509 v3 certificate extension (RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile Section 4.2.2.1) that provides URLs for the OCSP responder and the issuer’s certificate. Used to fetch missing intermediate certificates during chain building.

Abstract Syntax Notation One. A standard for describing data structures used in X.509 certificates, CMS, OCSP, and TSP. Structures are encoded using DER (Distinguished Encoding Rules) for cryptographic applications.

The /ByteRange array in a PDF signature dictionary. Specifies which bytes of the PDF file are covered by the signature. Always four integers: [offset1 length1 offset2 length2]. See ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 12.8.1.
Certificate Authority. An entity that issues X.509 certificates. CAs form a hierarchy: root CAs are self-signed and explicitly trusted; intermediate CAs are signed by a root or another intermediate.

CMS Advanced Electronic Signatures. ETSI standard (EN 319 122) defining enhanced CMS signature profiles for long-term validity and legal compliance. PAdES builds on CAdES for the CMS portion of PDF signatures.

Cryptographic Message Syntax. The standard (RFC 5652 — Cryptographic Message Syntax (CMS)) for signing and encrypting data. PDF signatures use CMS SignedData as the cryptographic payload inside the /Contents value. Successor to PKCS#7.

Certificate Revocation List. A signed list published by a CA containing the serial numbers of revoked certificates. Defined in RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile Section 5. Checked during certificate chain validation.
Distinguished Encoding Rules. A deterministic binary encoding of ASN.1 structures. All cryptographic ASN.1 (certificates, CMS, OCSP, TSP) uses DER encoding.

Document Security Store. A PDF dictionary (/DSS in the document catalog) defined in ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 12.8.4.3 that stores certificates, CRLs, and OCSP responses needed for offline (LTV) validation.

Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm. A signature algorithm based on elliptic curve cryptography. Uses smaller keys than RSA for equivalent security (e.g., 256-bit ECDSA approximates 3072-bit RSA).

Electronic Identification, Authentication, and Trust Services. EU Regulation (No 910/2014) that establishes a legal framework for electronic signatures, seals, and timestamps across EU member states. Defines three signature levels: simple, advanced, and qualified.
Extended Security Services. Defined in RFC 2634 and RFC 5035. The ESS-signing-certificate-v2 attribute is required in PAdES signatures to bind the CMS signature to a specific signer certificate.

European Telecommunications Standards Institute. The standards body that publishes PAdES (EN 319 142), CAdES (EN 319 122), and the validation standards (TS 119 102) referenced by the eIDAS regulation.
European Union Trusted List. The official registry of Qualified Trust Service Providers (QTSPs) maintained by EU member states. Analogous to Adobe’s AATL but for the EU regulatory framework.
Hardware Security Module. A physical device that stores private keys and performs cryptographic operations. Used in production signing environments to protect key material from extraction.

A mechanism defined in ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 7.5.6 that appends new or modified objects to the end of a PDF file without rewriting the entire file. Every PDF signature is applied via an incremental update.

Long-Term Validation. The practice of embedding all data needed for signature validation (certificates, CRLs, OCSP responses) within the PDF itself, so the signature can be validated offline, indefinitely, without contacting external servers.

Modification Detection and Prevention. A PDF mechanism (ISO 32000-2:2020 Section 12.8.2.2) that specifies what changes are permitted after a certification signature is applied. Three permission levels exist (P=1, P=2, P=3).

Online Certificate Status Protocol. A protocol (RFC 6960 — Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP)) for checking certificate revocation status in real time. An OCSP responder returns good, revoked, or unknown for a given certificate serial number.
Object Identifier. A globally unique dotted-decimal number (e.g., 1.2.840.113549.1.7.2) that identifies algorithms, attributes, extensions, and other standardized objects in ASN.1 structures.
PDF Advanced Electronic Signatures. ETSI standard (EN 319 142) that defines constrained profiles for PDF signatures to meet EU legal requirements. Four baseline profiles: B-B, B-T, B-LT, B-LTA.

Public-Key Cryptography Standards #7. The predecessor to CMS. PDF signatures historically used the term "PKCS#7" (and the SubFilter adbe.pkcs7.detached), though modern implementations use CMS. The terms are often used interchangeably.
Public-Key Cryptography Standards #12. A file format for storing private keys and certificates together, typically with .p12 or .pfx extension. Protected by a password.

Public-Key Infrastructure (X.509). The set of standards and protocols for managing X.509 certificates and building certificate chains. In Java, CertPathBuilder and CertPathValidator implement the PKIX algorithm.

Qualified Trust Service Provider. Under eIDAS, a trust service provider that has been granted qualified status by an EU member state’s supervisory body. Only QTSPs can issue qualified certificates and qualified timestamps.
Rivest-Shamir-Adleman. The most widely used public-key algorithm for digital signatures. Common key sizes for PDF signatures are 2048 and 4096 bits.
Secure Hash Algorithm, 256-bit. The recommended minimum hash algorithm for new PDF signatures. Produces a 32-byte (256-bit) digest. Part of the SHA-2 family.

The /SubFilter entry in a PDF signature dictionary that specifies the CMS encoding format. Values: adbe.pkcs7.detached (classic), ETSI.CAdES.detached (PAdES), adbe.pkcs7.sha1 (deprecated), adbe.x509.rsa_sha1 (deprecated).
Timestamp Authority. A trusted server that issues RFC 3161 timestamp tokens. The timestamp token proves that specific data existed at a specific point in time.
Time-Stamp Protocol. The protocol defined in RFC 3161 — Internet X.509 PKI Time-Stamp Protocol (TSP) for requesting and receiving timestamp tokens from a TSA.
Time-Stamp Token. The CMS SignedData structure returned by a TSA containing a TSTInfo with the timestamp time and the hash of the timestamped data.
Validation Related Information. A sub-dictionary of the DSS that groups certificates, CRLs, and OCSP responses for a specific signature. Keyed by the SHA-1 hash of the signature value.
The ITU-T standard for public-key certificates. Version 3 (X.509 v3) is used universally in PDF signatures. Defined in RFC 5280 — Internet X.509 PKI Certificate and CRL Profile.
XML Advanced Electronic Signatures. ETSI standard (EN 319 132) for XML-based signatures. Not directly relevant to PDF signatures, but part of the same eIDAS framework as PAdES and CAdES.

tip.simpara: The RFCs are freely available and are the definitive reference for CMS, X.509, OCSP, and TSP. The ETSI standards are available free of charge from ETSI’s website after registration. ISO 32000-2 is a paid standard, but much of the PDF signature specification is reproduced (with commentary) in this book and in Adobe’s freely published PDF Reference documents.
